Overview

Your PhD-funding command center for Sept-2027 entry. Everything is filtered to your profile (Italian/EU, first-gen, CS/AI/ML + computational biology).

Strategy: Cambridge is one application → 5 funding shots (Gates · Trust · Harding · Math Genomics · CCAIM). Oxford Clarendon is auto-considered — apply the December field. Compbio/dry-lab framing unlocks EMBL-EBI + Sanger (nationality-blind, no bio degree). Hard wall: Sanger 27 Nov 2026 — front-load it and start supervisor emails in Jul–Aug.

Only accessible (EU-eligible, funded) borse are must-apply. Conditional = UKRI/EPSRC home-fee-capped, overseas funding not guaranteed. 48 filtered out as ineligible (US-only, Master's-only, refugee-only…).

Best PIs for you — email these first

Adji Bousso Dieng↗high reply
Princeton University · Vertaix
Diversity-aware generative model (extending Vendi Score framework) for exploring chemical or genomic sequence space.
Ana Namburete↗high reply
University of Oxford · OMNI Lab — Oxford Machine Learning in NeuroImaging (omni.cs.
Self-supervised deep learning for automated brain age/maturation estimation from low-resource fetal ultrasound.
Ard Louis↗high reply
University of Oxford · Louis Group (theoretical biophysics and ML)
Characterising inductive bias of GNNs on biological sequence-to-function (genotype-phenotype) mappings.
Avlant Nilsson↗high reply
Karolinska Institutet · Deep Learning Models of Cancer Mechanisms (Avlant Nilsson&#x
Extend the mechanistic multi-omics deep learning cancer model to incorporate patient-derived single-cell or spatial data for drug-resistance prediction.
Bartlomiej Papiez↗high reply
University of Oxford · Papiez Lab (biomedical image analysis)
Graph-based representation of cell/tissue morphology from microscopy for disease classification, linking image features to a cell-graph GNN.
Caroline Uhler↗high reply
MIT · Uhler Lab
Causal generative models linking 3D genome organization (Hi-C/imaging) to gene regulatory state changes in single cells.
Charlotte Bunne↗high reply
EPFL · AIMM -- Artificial Intelligence in Molecular Medicine (aimm.
Generative/diffusion or optimal-transport models for perturbation prediction, cell-state trajectories, or an 'AI virtual cell' benchmark using public multi-omics perturba
Charlotte Deane↗high reply
University of Oxford · Oxford Protein Informatics Group (OPIG)
Equivariant/graph neural network for antibody-antigen interface prediction using geometric representations of residue contact graphs.

Causal Structure-Aware Graph Neural Networks for Multi-Omics Treatment-R
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship

Read the ceilings carefully. The "≥1" numbers multiply independent odds, but real applications share your CV, references and timing, so they are correlated — the true figure sits well below the ceiling and closer to your strongest single target. Your chances

Honest, calibrated estimates of your admission + full-funding odds per university and scholarship, given your CV (the Liò connection is weighted heavily). likely target reach

University of Cambridge

target
30-45% (admission+full funding combined); admission alone with Liò backing ~65-75%
admission + full funding, given your CV
Best PI: Pietro Liò (Computer Lab) — already his existing RA supervisor; this is the single biggest lever. Secondary co-supervisi↗
PI reply odds: 85-95% — not a true cold email; he is already an RA in Liò's lab, so this i
Best programme: PhD in Computer Science, Department of Computer Science and Technology (the Computer Laboratory) — Liò group. Strong fun↗
Why
Two separate gates must both clear: (1) an academic offer, (2) full tuition+stipend. On gate 1 he is exceptionally well-positioned: he is already a Research Assistant in Pietro Liò's Computer Lab on the exact topic (sheaf-based geometric DL), plus IFOM sheaf-GNN cancer modelling. At Cambridge/Oxford supervisor buy-in is the dominant admission signal, and he effectively has it — a known supervisor vouching outweighs the missing first-author paper, which is normal for his early stage. So admission alone is roughly 65-75%. Gate 2 is the real problem. Post-Brexit, an Italian/EU citizen is OVERSEAS fee status, so UKRI/EPSRC/DTP money is largely closed (see funding-strategy facts). Full funding must come from nationality-blind central scholarships — Gates Cambridge (~1-3% success, brutal), Cambridge Trust/CIS (more places, still competitive), Harding — OR from a supervisor-held departmental studentship/grant, OR the fully-funded Wellcome Mathematical Genomics 4yr programme. Each of these is a lottery on its own; stacked in parallel they give a realistic ~40-55% funding-conditional-on-offer. Multiplying the two gates lands the combined admission+full-funding probability at ~30-45%. This is a genuine TARGET, not a reach, precisely because the Liò relationship converts what is normally the hardest part (getting a top-lab supervisor to want you) into a near-solved problem — but it is not "likely" because he does not control the money and the central scholarships are savage.
Boosters:
  • Get Liò to explicitly commit to supervise AND check whether he holds a departmental/grant-funded studentship — a supervisor-funded place bypasses the Gates/Trust lottery entirely and is by far the highest-leverage move
  • Ship a first-author arXiv preprint of the sheaf-GNN p53/cancer work before the Dec 2026 deadlines — converts the weakest part of the file into a strength and strengthens every scholarship app
  • Apply in parallel to Gates Cambridge + Cambridge Trust + Harding on the SAME PhD application (they are not mutually exclusive) to maximise the funded-offer probability
  • Add the Wellcome Mathematical Genomics & Medicine 4yr programme as a second funded route — nationality-blind, fully funded, comp-bio framing fits, and does not require a first-author paper

EPFL (École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne)

target
25-40%
admission + full funding, given your CV
Best PI: Maria Brbić (IC / EDCB) — machine learning for single-cell genomics and biological foundation models; young, actively-gr↗
PI reply odds: 35-50%
Best programme: EDCB (Computational & Quantitative Biology) as primary — best fit for GNN/GDL-for-genomics and marginally more forgiving↗
Why
He is a top-decile applicant on paper: multiple summa cum laude degrees, ~4.0 at Bocconi+Humanitas, and — decisively — hands-on research in exactly the target niche (sheaf-based GNNs at Liò's Cambridge lab, p53-cancer sheaf GNN modelling at IFOM, real genomics/HPC work). That research-topic alignment with Brbić is rare and is his single biggest lever at EPFL. The structural reality tempers it: EPFL doctoral-school selection is centralized and brutal, and the one gap that matters most to EDIC/EDCB committees — a first-author paper or preprint at a recognized venue — he does not yet have. Grades saturate quickly in this pool; nearly every shortlisted candidate has top marks, so they do not differentiate him much. The Liò connection is worth a lot as a reference letter and a credibility signal, but its outsized value is at Cambridge/ELLIS/ETH where Liò is directly embedded; at EPFL it functions as a strong external letter, not an inside track. Net: clearly above the base rate but not a lock — a genuine target, not a likely. If he lands even one first-author preprint before applying, this moves toward 40-50% and into strong-target territory.
Boosters:
  • Get one first-author preprint out (arXiv/bioRxiv) from the IFOM p53 sheaf-GNN or Cambridge work — the single highest-impact move
  • Secure a strong, specific letter from Pietro Liò explicitly endorsing his independent research capacity
  • Send a technical, tailored email to Maria Brbić referencing her recent papers and proposing a concrete GDL-for-genomics direction — get informal pre-backing before the formal deadline
  • Apply to EDCB and EDIC in parallel to hedge the doctoral-school gate

Karolinska Institutet (KI)

target
30-42% (of landing at least one fully-funded, well-matched doktorand position across a full application cycle; ~15-22% for any single advertised position)
admission + full funding, given your CV
Best PI: Sten Linnarsson (single-cell & molecular systems biology, heavily computational, chronically needs strong ML) as primary↗
PI reply odds: 45-60% (Swedish academia is responsive and non-hierarchical; a targeted, project
Best programme: Doktorand position in computational biology / bioinformatics (KI Doctoral Programme in Bioinformatics), ideally in a Sci↗
Why
KI is a medical university, not an ML-methods school — pure geometric deep learning lives at KTH/Stockholm University, not KI. So the number of KI positions that truly fit "GNNs/geometric DL for genomics" is limited, which caps the practical chance regardless of how strong he is. Where he DOES fit (single-cell, cancer genomics, systems biology groups that want ML applied to their data) he is close to an ideal hire: PyTorch Geometric + scVI/Scanpy + GNN cancer modelling (IFOM p53/Sheaf-GNN) + genomics/HPC is exactly the rare skill stack these groups struggle to recruit. His grades are irrelevant-to-mildly-positive here (Swedish hiring weights fit, references, and demonstrated research over GPA). The Liò connection is worth much less at KI than at Cambridge/ELLIS — useful mainly as a strong reference letter, not as a co-supervision magnet. Missing first-author paper is a moderate, not fatal, gap: normal at Swedish PhD entry, but the strongest competing applicants for prime comp-bio slots increasingly arrive with a preprint. Net: not a reach (he clears the bar comfortably for matched positions), but genuinely fit- and timing-dependent because the right funded project must be open when he applies — hence target, not likely.
Boosters:
  • Ship a bioRxiv preprint (first or strong co-author) from the IFOM p53/Sheaf-GNN cancer work before applying — single biggest lever for KI comp-bio slots
  • Secure a strong reference letter from Pietro Liò plus IFOM PIs (Buffa/Tangherloni) — references outweigh grades in Swedish hiring
  • Target the right environment: SciLifeLab-affiliated single-cell/cancer-genomics groups and pursue a KI(biology)+KTH(methods) co-supervision rather than pure clinical KI departments
  • Cold-email matched PIs 3-6 months before positions post, pitching a concrete project tied to their data (single-cell/cancer genomics) — position offers are often shaped around a candidate already in dialogue

University of Oxford

reach
12-20%
admission + full funding, given your CV
Best PI: Michael Bronstein (DeepMind Professor of AI)↗
PI reply odds: Bronstein cold email: ~10-15% (flooded, rarely replies to unsolicited mail). Wit
Best programme: DPhil in Computer Science (ML/AI group), Department of Computer Science↗
Why
the applicant is an academically elite, unusually well-prepared candidate: multiple summa cum laude degrees, a Cambridge Computer Lab RA position under Pietro Lio doing exactly sheaf-based geometric deep learning, IFOM modelling work on Sheaf GNNs for p53, real HPC genomics experience, and a modern ML stack (PyTorch Geometric, scVI, Pyro). His research topic maps almost perfectly onto Oxford's single strongest asset in this space — Bronstein's group, whose own sheaf-diffusion work (Bodnar, a Lio-lineage student) is the intellectual sibling of what the applicant already does. That fit is genuine and rare. Two things pull the number down to "reach": (1) no first-author paper at a top ML venue, which is close to the implicit bar for Bronstein-tier supervisors who can pick from a global pool of published applicants; and (2) the Oxford funding structure, where an EU/international candidate needs a competitive studentship to cover fees, and the offer is effectively conditional on winning that funding. The Lio connection is a real multiplier but it is weaker at Oxford than it is for Cambridge or an ELLIS placement — it converts a cold application into a warm, credibly-referred one and materially raises the odds of a supervisor actually engaging, but it does not by itself clear the funding hurdle. Net: a strong reach, not a target. He can realistically expect to be taken seriously and get interviews, but converting to a fully-funded offer against published competitors is the hard part.
Boosters:
  • Land a first-author (or strong co-first) paper at a top ML venue or workshop (NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR/LoG) before applying — single biggest lever
  • Get Pietro Lio to make a direct introduction/referral to Bronstein and to write a strong letter — turns a cold app into a sponsored one
  • Preprint the IFOM p53 Sheaf-GNN work on arXiv/bioRxiv to make the research concrete and citable
  • Target the AIMS CDT and Health Data Science CDT in parallel with the direct DPhil route to widen funding pathways

Harvard University

reach
6-11%
admission + full funding, given your CV
Best PI: Marinka Zitnik (Harvard Medical School / Dept of Biomedical Informatics) — near-perfect fit: graph neural networks and g↗
PI reply odds: 15-20% cold; 60%+ if Liò makes a warm intro
Best programme: Harvard SEAS PhD in Computer Science (primary); Bioinformatics & Integrative Genomics (BIG) PhD as the compbio-framed al↗
Why
Calibrated, not flattering. Harvard is a genuine reach for him despite an exceptional academic record. Why the grades don't carry it: at the Harvard/MIT/Stanford CS-PhD tier, near-4.0 GPAs and multiple masters are the baseline, not the differentiator. The committee sorts on research output, letters, and fit — and here his profile is strong but not yet at the modal admit. The decisive gap is the missing first-author paper at a top ML venue (NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR); many geometric-DL admits arrive with 1-3 such papers or strong preprints. His real research substance (Liò's lab on sheaf-based geometric DL, IFOM p53-in-cancer via Sheaf GNNs, HPC genomics) is a serious asset that most applicants lack, and it maps almost exactly onto Zitnik's agenda — but unpublished/early-stage work reads weaker to a committee than to a supervisor. The Liò connection is valuable but worth LESS here than it is for Cambridge/ELLIS: US admission is not PI-gated, so a strong personalized Liò letter helps as a letter (and letters are heavily weighted), not as a decision-maker who can pull him in. Net: exceptional coursework + excellent-but-unpublished research + a great letter + perfect topical fit with Zitnik/Weber = a credible but low-probability shot. 6-11% is the honest band for the CS PhD; the BIG/compbio route sits a couple points higher because the applicant pool is marginally shallower and his dry-lab compbio framing fits it well.
Boosters:
  • Land a first-author paper (or at minimum an arXiv/bioRxiv preprint) at a top ML venue or strong workshop from the Liò/IFOM sheaf-GNN work — single biggest lever, can move him from reach toward the fat part of the admit distribution
  • Get a strongly personalized letter from Pietro Liò plus a second US-visible recommender; letters are decisive in US committees
  • Ask Liò for a warm intro to Zitnik and/or Weber — converts a 15-20% cold-email into a 60%+ engaged conversation and gets an internal advocate on the committee
  • Write a razor-sharp research statement that turns the p53/cancer Sheaf-GNN work into a concrete, testable biological question aligned to Zitnik's or Weber's current projects

Stanford University

reach
3-7%
admission + full funding, given your CV
Best PI: Jure Leskovec (GNNs / SNAP / biomedical graph ML) is the textbook fit but among the hardest advisors to land anywhere. M↗
PI reply odds: 10-20% for a sharply tailored cold email; ~5% for Leskovec specifically (flooded
Best programme: Stanford CS PhD (AI track) is the headline target given the sheaf/geometric-DL agenda; the Biomedical Data Science (BMDS↗
Why
Honest read: this is a reach bordering on lottery, not because he is weak but because the bar at Stanford CS-AI is set by applicants who already have first-author NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR/LoG papers plus similar elite pedigrees. His transcript is genuinely top-decile (five degrees, multiple summa cum laude, ~4.0 at Bocconi+Humanitas) but grades are close to a non-differentiator at this tier — nearly every serious applicant has them. His real assets are the research surface: a Research Assistant post in Pietro Liò's Cambridge lab on sheaf-based geometric deep learning, IFOM work modelling p53 via Sheaf GNNs, and a coherent, unusual, defensible research identity (sheaf/geometric DL for genomics) that reads as a real agenda rather than a buzzword collage. The decisive gap is the missing first-author top-venue publication; without it he lacks the one signal Stanford committees weight most for AI admits, and letters/grades cannot fully substitute. The Liò connection is a strong recommendation-letter asset and genuinely huge for Cambridge/ELLIS/European routes, but it transfers only partially to Stanford — Liò is not a Stanford-network insider, so its value here is mainly a credible, senior letter attesting to research maturity, not a back-channel admit. Because funding is automatic on admission, the % is a pure admission probability, not further discounted. Net: 3-7%, reach.
Boosters:
  • Land a first-author paper at NeurIPS / ICML / ICLR / LoG or a strong comp-bio venue (RECOMB / ISMB / Cell Systems) before the December deadline — by far the largest single lever, could roughly double the odds
  • Convert the Liò/IFOM Sheaf-GNN work into a public arXiv/bioRxiv preprint with a recognizable co-author, so reviewers can see the output not just the affiliation
  • Broaden targets within Stanford: apply to BMDS PhD and ICME alongside CS, not CS-AI alone — same PIs reachable, softer applicant pool
  • Secure a genuinely strong, specific letter from Pietro Liò plus ideally one US-based collaborator the committee will recognize

MIT (Massachusetts Institute of Technology)

reach
3-7%
admission + full funding, given your CV
Best PI: Caroline Uhler (EECS/IDSS, Broad Institute) — geometry + causal inference for single-cell genomics, near-exact overlap w↗
PI reply odds: 10-20%
Best programme: MIT CSB (Computational and Systems Biology) PhD — slightly less saturated than pure EECS ML and a cleaner fit for GNN/ge↗
Why
His academics are top-decile and his research exposure (Liò's Cambridge lab on sheaf-based geometric DL, IFOM sheaf-GNN p53 modelling, HPC genomics) is genuinely on-theme for Uhler/Jaakkola/Berger. That clears the "is this person real" filter. But MIT's ML admit pool is filled by candidates who already have first-author top-venue papers, and he has none yet — that is the exact currency the committee weighs most, and its absence is decisive at this tier. The Liò connection, which is huge for Cambridge/ELLIS (Liò IS the network there), converts to far less leverage at MIT: it matters only through the strength of his letter, not through direct placement power in the US committee system. US admissions are committee-based, so a warm PI does not pull you in the way a European supervisor can; cold emails inform but rarely decide. Funding is not a separate hurdle — MIT funds every PhD admit, so admission = full funding. Net: a strong, legible applicant who is one publication short of the profile MIT's ML committee actually selects. Realistic reach, not a target.
Boosters:
  • Land a first-author (or strong co-first) paper at NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR/LMRL/MLCB, or at minimum a solid arXiv preprint + workshop paper before Dec 2026 — single highest-leverage move
  • Get Liò to write a letter that explicitly benchmarks him against past students who reached MIT/ETH, not a generic strong letter
  • Apply to CSB rather than pure EECS ML to sit in a less saturated committee while keeping the same PIs
  • Secure a short US-facing collaboration or remote project (Broad/Uhler-adjacent) to create an internal advocate

Caltech

reach
6-12%
admission + full funding, given your CV
Best PI: Matt Thomson (ML + single-cell dynamical systems) is the best combined biology+ML fit. Lior Pachter (computational genom↗
PI reply odds: 10-18%
Best programme: Computing + Mathematical Sciences (CMS) PhD, machine-learning / AI-for-science track. Secondary route: Biology & Biologi↗
Why
Grades are elite and the research portfolio (Cambridge/Lio sheaf GNNs, IFOM p53 modelling, HPC genomics) is unusually deep for an applicant, so he is genuinely competitive on preparation and topical fit. But two structural problems cap the odds at Caltech specifically. First, the Lio connection, a huge lever for Cambridge and the ELLIS/ETH network, buys little at Caltech: Caltech is not an ELLIS node and its committee weights US-legible signals (letters from US-known faculty, top-venue first-author output). Second, and decisive at this tier, he has no first-author NeurIPS/ICML/Nature-Methods-class paper yet, whereas the marginal admits at Caltech ML/comp-bio typically do. Add tiny-cohort variance (a subfield may admit only 2-4 people, and whether a matched PI is recruiting that cycle dominates the outcome) and this is a clear reach, not a target. Funding is not a separate hurdle: US PhD admission carries full funding, so admission is effectively admission+funding. Best realistic path runs through a specific PI (Thomson, Pachter, or Anandkumar) who wants exactly his GDL-for-genomics skill set in the cycle he applies.
Boosters:
  • Get a first-author preprint out (arXiv/bioRxiv) from the IFOM or Cambridge sheaf-GNN work before applications, ideally submitted to a top venue
  • Secure a co-supervision or explicit endorsement from a US-recognizable faculty name to complement the Lio letter
  • Email 1-2 target PIs (Thomson, Pachter, Anandkumar) with a specific proposal tied to their current open problems, confirming they are recruiting for 2027
  • Write a razor-sharp SOP connecting geometric deep learning to a concrete Caltech PI's research gap, not a generic GNN pitch

Princeton University

reach
8-15%
admission + full funding, given your CV
Best PI: Olga Troyanskaya (deep learning for functional genomics) — strongest genomics fit. Ellen Zhong (geometric/generative DL ↗
PI reply odds: 25-35%
Best programme: Quantitative and Computational Biology (QCB) PhD — rotation-based, ideal for methods-driven comp-bio. CS PhD (ML track) ↗
Why
US PhD admission is committee-based, not PI-funded, so a cold email moves the needle far less than at ELLIS/ETH where the Liò link is decisive. Princeton admits to the program; rotations (QCB) or an ML committee (CS) decide, and full funding is automatic on admission — so admission = funding. His academics are elite (multiple summa, ~4.0) and his research stack (Cambridge/Liò sheaf-GNNs, IFOM p53 modelling, genomics HPC) is an exceptionally clean fit for Troyanskaya/Zhong/Raphael. The binding constraint is the publication bar: top-tier US ML/comp-bio cohorts increasingly show 1-3 first-author or strong co-author papers at NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR/RECOMB. He has none yet. Against applicants who have both the grades AND the papers, he lands in the reach band. Grades and pedigree get the file read seriously; the missing first-author paper is what keeps this below target. The Liò connection helps mainly through a strong, specific letter and any warm intro Liò can make to Princeton faculty he knows — real but secondary leverage in the US system.
Boosters:
  • Land a first-author (or strong co-author) paper at NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR/RECOMB/ISMB before Dec 2026 — single biggest lever
  • Get Liò to write a specific, superlative letter AND make a direct intro to Troyanskaya, Zhong, or Raphael
  • Preprint the IFOM sheaf-GNN p53 work on bioRxiv/arXiv to show a concrete first-author artifact
  • Name 2-3 Princeton PIs explicitly in the SOP with a concrete project idea per PI

UC Berkeley

reach
~8% (best single route: CCB). Route-split: CCB ~10-12%, Statistics ~5-7%, EECS/CS ~2-3%. "With funding" is not a separate hurdle at Berkeley — admitted PhD students are funded by default via GSR/GSI/departmental packages, so admission ≈ funding.
admission + full funding, given your CV
Best PI: Yun S. Song (Professor, EECS + Statistics; Director, Center for Computational Biology; https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/↗
PI reply odds: ~25% to a cold email. US admissions are committee-gated, not supervisor-gated; B
Best programme: PhD in Computational Biology — Center for Computational Biology (CCB), UC Berkeley. Dept root (high confidence): https:/↗
Why
Calibrated estimate, not a flattering one. Start from base rates: Berkeley EECS PhD admits ~3-4% of applicants and the international slice is lower still; Statistics is comparable; CCB is the softest of the three, plausibly ~10-15% overall, because the pool is smaller and explicitly interdisciplinary. Now adjust for him. UPWARD: the Cambridge/Lio RA position is a genuine, verifiable, top-of-field research signal in exactly the method Berkeley has faculty working on; the IFOM p53 work is a second independent line; the engineering stack is real; the grades are unimpeachable and legible to a US reader. Two serious research environments with two serious letter writers puts him above the median CCB applicant. DOWNWARD, and this is the part that decides the number: no first-author top-venue paper. In the pool he is competing against — international applicants to a top-5 US programme in ML-for-biology — a first-author NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR or Nature-family paper is close to the modal credential, not an exceptional one. He is bringing potential where his competitors bring output. Committees discount potential heavily because they see thousands of promising CVs and can only rank on evidence of completed, owned work. The second downward factor is framing risk: his five-degree European CV is genuinely unusual to a US committee and the default read is drift, not depth. That read is wrong but it is the one that happens unless the SOP forces the alternative. The structure of US admissions is the crux of the whole assessment and cuts both ways for him. Because it is committee-based, Lio cannot simply take him the way a Cambridge or ETH supervisor could — the connection degrades from 'decision-maker' to 'strong signal in a rubric', which is a large loss relative to his Cambridge option. But symmetrically, no single professor gets to look at his file, note the absent first-author paper, and personally decide he is not ready; the rubric weighs letters, fit, statement, and record together, so the gap costs him points rather than vetoing him. Net: it compresses his distribution — lowers his ceiling at Cambridge-like odds, raises his floor above zero. Combining: CCB ~10-12%, Statistics ~5-7%, EECS ~2-3%. Best-route headline: ~8%. Funding is NOT a separate filter at Berkeley — US PhD admission comes with a funding package (GSR/GSI/fellowship), which is the one structural respect in which Berkeley is CLEANER than Cambridge for him, where admission and money are two separate lotteries. His EU citizenship locks him out of NSF GRFP, NDSEG, DOE CSGF, and NIH T32s — none of these are options and none should be in his plan; the indirect cost is that domestic competitors carrying their own federal fellowship are cheaper to admit. Berkeley's internal fellowships appear open to international students by nomination at admission (moderate confidence, verify autumn 2026). VERDICT: reach, worth exactly one application to CCB, not worth restructuring his strategy around. One caution on the field data: Nilah Ioannidis, who would have been the single most natural PI match (ML for genomics, EECS+CCB), shows signals of having moved to UC Santa Cruz — flagged, low confidence, must be re-verified before he writes any name into an SOP. Nir Yosef has left for Weizmann. This is a live reminder that the Berkeley faculty list must be re-checked in autumn 2026 rather than trusted from any snapshot, including this one.
Boosters:
  • Get a first-author preprint on arXiv/bioRxiv by summer 2026 and submit it to NeurIPS 2026 or ICLR 2027. This is the single highest-leverage action available and it roughly doubles his CCB odds and is the only thing that makes EECS non-farcical. A workshop paper (NeurIPS GLFrontiers / MLCB / LMRL) is a weaker but real substitute if the main-track timeline slips — get SOMETHING with his name first on it, timestamped before December 2026.
  • Ask Pietro Lio to email Yun Song and Aditi Krishnapriyan directly in October 2026 — not a forwarded CV, a personal note saying 'this person did X in my group and it worked'. In a committee system, a known-name advocate inside the room is worth more than any cold email he can write himself.
  • Apply to CCB as the primary, not EECS. He should treat EECS as a lottery ticket he buys only if a paper lands. If Berkeley allows only one application, CCB is the answer — better fit, smaller pool, values his biology.
  • Rewrite the SOP so the five degrees are ONE argument, not five facts: 'I learned physiology, then molecular biology, then genomics, then geometry, because the object I want to model — a cell's regulatory state — is a structured biological system and I refused to model it without understanding it.' Then close on sheaf GNNs and p53. First paragraph must state the research question, not the biography.

Imperial College London

target (reach on the full-funding leg)
22-32%
admission + full funding, given your CV
Best PI: Ben Glocker (BioMedIA, ML for biomedical data) as the closest well-known anchor; also scope the Dept of Computing ML fac↗
PI reply odds: 40-55%
Best programme: UKRI CDT in AI for Healthcare (Dept of Computing / Bioengineering) as first choice — near-perfect fit for his health-foc↗
Why
Academically he is in the top percentile: multiple summa-cum-laude MScs, Bocconi/Humanitas data-science-for-health, and real research at Cambridge (Liò) and IFOM on Sheaf GNNs with a production ML stack (PyTorch Geometric, scVI, Scanpy). For a UK PhD, admission is supervisor-driven, so a strong Liò reference plus a targeted proposal makes getting a supervisor to WANT him quite plausible (~45-55%). Three things pull the combined admission+full-funding number down to ~22-32%: (1) no first-author top-venue paper, which is the standard discriminator at this tier; (2) the Liò/ELLIS connection is huge for Cambridge/Oxford but gives NO structural inside-track at Imperial — Bronstein, who built the GDL legacy there, has left for Oxford; (3) international-fee funding is scarcer than home-fee funding, so even a keen supervisor may lack a studentship that covers his full cost. Net: he clears the academic bar easily; the real gate is landing money as an international candidate in a field where Imperial isn't his sharpest fit.
Boosters:
  • Land a first-author preprint/workshop paper (e.g. NeurIPS/ICML workshop, LoG, MLCB) before applying — single biggest lever
  • Get Liò to write a strong, specific letter and, if possible, to intro him to an Imperial PI directly
  • Target the President's PhD Scholarship and any international-eligible CDT places explicitly to solve the funding gate
  • Send early, hyper-specific cold emails (Sept-Oct) tying his sheaf-GNN + genomics work to a named PI's current projects

ETH Zurich

reach (edges toward target only if the ellis route with liò as co-supervisor is used)
12-22% (best case via ELLIS + Liò co-supervision; ~8-12% for a cold direct-hire to a top group)
admission + full funding, given your CV
Best PI: Valentina Boeva (Computational Systems Biology / cancer genomics + ML) — tightest fit given the p53-in-cancer and genomi↗
PI reply odds: ~30-40% to a well-targeted cold email that names Liò, cites the specific sheaf-G
Best programme: ELLIS PhD @ ETH Zurich with Pietro Liò as co-supervisor↗
Why
His grades are effectively maxed (multiple 110L summa, ~4.0 Bocconi) and his skill stack (PyTorch Geometric, sheaf GNNs, scVI/scanpy, HPC genomics) maps exactly onto ETH comp-bio-ML groups — but at this tier, top grades are table stakes, not a differentiator. Everyone shortlisted has them. The binding constraint is the missing first-author top-venue paper: he is competing against applicants who already have that signal, and ETH PIs use publications as the primary de-risking proxy for research independence. His genuine edge is the Pietro Liò connection: Liò is a heavyweight in geometric deep learning and an ELLIS insider, so a strong letter plus a co-supervision commitment partially substitutes for the missing paper by pre-solving the "will a credible senior researcher vouch for and co-own this student" question. That is why the ELLIS route roughly doubles his realistic odds versus a cold direct application. Net: a strong reach. Real but minority chance, entirely swingable by publication output and how actively Liò advocates over the next ~12-18 months before the 2027 cycle.
Boosters:
  • Land a first-author paper — even a workshop paper at a top venue (LoG, NeurIPS/ICML GDL or MLCB workshop) — from the sheaf-GNN / p53 work. Single biggest lever.
  • Get an arXiv preprint of the IFOM p53 sheaf-GNN project up NOW to create a citable artifact before the cycle.
  • Convert Liò from letter-writer to active advocate: secure a warm introduction to Boeva/Rätsch and an explicit ELLIS co-supervision commitment.
  • Apply specifically through the ELLIS PhD program with ETH as hub — do not rely solely on a cold direct application.

Pre-application action plan

The combinatorial set of moves to make before each application — sequenced, with what can run in parallel and the critical path. Pick a target.

ELLIS PhD (academic track) with primary supervisor at the ETH Zurich ELLIS unit + Pietro Lio (ELLIS Cambridge) as the mandatory second-country co-supervisor. Single ELLIS portal; the true deadline is ~31 Oct 2026 AoE (NOT Dec/Jan) with reference letters due ~25 Nov 2026 and interviews Jan-Feb 2027. Core structural requirement: two advisors from two different European countries, at least one an ELLIS fellow — Lio (UK) + ETH fellow (CH) satisfies it exactly.

Jul 2026 (wk 1)
Read the ELLIS PhD 2027 call end-to-end and lock the real timeline: portal opens ~Sep/Oct, application deadline ~31 Oct 2026 AoE, references due ~25 Nov, interviews Jan-Feb 2027. Verify the mandatory rule: 2 advisors from 2 different European countries, >=1 ELLIS fellow, plus a min 6-month exchange with the second advisor.
The deadline is ~2 months earlier than the Dec/Jan he assumed. Every downstream date must be back-planned from 31 Oct, and the two-country/exchange rules dictate the whole supervisor strategy.
effort: 4
Jul 2026 (wk 1-2)
Formalize the pairing strategy: PRIMARY advisor = an ETH ELLIS fellow (top targets Gunnar Ratsch, biomedical informatics/ML-for-health; or Valentina Boeva, computational genomics; Andreas Krause as ETH ELLIS unit anchor); SECOND advisor = Pietro Lio (ELLIS Cambridge, UK). CH+UK = two countries, both ELLIS units. This is the single highest-leverage design decision.
ELLIS admission is advisor-driven, not central-committee-driven. Lio is already his PI, so the second slot is de-risked; the only open variable is landing an enthusiastic ETH primary. Naming CH+UK also auto-satisfies the two-country rule and the exchange requirement (he can do the 6-month exchange in Lio's Cambridge lab he already knows).
effort: 3
Jul 2026 (wk 2)
Sit down with Pietro Lio (in person / call, leveraging the current Research Assistant role) and secure three explicit commitments: (1) he will co-supervise as the Cambridge/second-country advisor on an ELLIS application, (2) he will endorse/introduce the applicant to a specific ETH ELLIS fellow, (3) he will write one of the three reference letters. Leave with a named ETH target and, ideally, an intro email teed up.
This is THE critical-path move and the applicant's unfair advantage: an ELLIS fellow who already knows his work committing to the mandatory second slot AND warm-introducing the primary. A Lio intro converts a cold ETH email into a near-guaranteed reply.
effort: 6
Jul-Aug 2026
Shortlist and deeply read 3-4 ETH ELLIS fellows whose agenda overlaps geometric DL for genomics/health (Ratsch, Boeva, Krause, Julia Vogt). For each, read 3-5 recent papers, identify an open problem his sheaf-GNN / p53-in-cancer / transposable-element work plugs into, and note current funding/openings.
The outreach email and SOP must speak the ETH group's language and propose a project at the Lio x ETH intersection. Generic interest gets ignored; a specific, informed hook gets a call.
effort: 12
Aug 2026
Draft a 1-2 page research proposal sketch bridging Lio's sheaf-based geometric DL with the ETH primary's program (e.g. sheaf/GNN models over multi-omic cancer graphs with Ratsch-style clinical ML, or Boeva-style comp-genomics). Include a concrete first-year aim and why the two labs are jointly necessary.
Doubles as the outreach attachment AND the seed of the final ELLIS research statement. Framing it as intrinsically two-lab justifies the co-supervision to the admission committee.
effort: 20
late Aug - early Sep 2026
Send the tailored outreach email to the ETH primary target (ideally via / cc Lio's intro), attaching CV + proposal sketch, requesting a 30-min call. Aim to actually talk before the portal opens.
ELLIS explicitly expects candidates to have contacted prospective advisors. A pre-deadline call is what turns 'application in a pile' into 'candidate my ETH fellow is already championing.'
effort: 6
Sep 2026
Convert the ETH call into an informal pre-commitment: the ETH fellow agrees to be listed as primary advisor and to co-review the project. If target #1 is non-responsive/full by mid-Sep, pivot to the #2 ETH fellow immediately (branch built into the shortlist).
Without a willing ETH primary the ELLIS application is effectively dead on arrival. The mid-Sep pivot rule protects the ~31 Oct deadline from a single unresponsive professor.
effort: 5
Jul-Sep 2026 (parallel, start NOW for lead time)
Assemble the mechanical dossier in parallel: order official transcripts from all five programs (Bocconi/Humanitas, Pavia, Insubria, Insubria/two Insubria, sport/human-movement); check English-proficiency requirement and, if not waived by English-medium MSc or interview, book TOEFL/IELTS early; prepare an academic CV.
Transcripts and (if needed) a language test have multi-week lead times and are pure dependencies — a missing official transcript or unbooked test can sink an otherwise strong file at the last minute. Fully parallelizable, so front-load it.
effort: 10
Aug-Oct 2026 (parallel, high value)
Push the Cambridge/IFOM sheaf-GNN work (p53 in cancer, sheaf geometric DL) to a bioRxiv/arXiv preprint with the applicant as first author, even if not yet peer-reviewed.
His one real gap is 'no first-author top-venue paper.' A first-author preprint on exactly the ELLIS-relevant method converts that gap into demonstrated output and gives referees + the ETH fellow something concrete to point to. Runs alongside everything else.
effort: 40
Sep 2026
Confirm three referees and brief them: (1) Pietro Lio (Cambridge), (2) IFOM PI (Francesca Buffa or Andrea Tangherloni), (3) a Bocconi/Humanitas or Pavia faculty member. Send each the CV, proposal, target-advisor names, and the ~25 Nov portal deadline. Register them in the ELLIS portal as soon as it opens so the system emails them.
ELLIS references are submitted directly by referees via the portal by ~25 Nov; a late referee is the most common self-inflicted failure. Briefing them to name the geometric-DL-for-biology fit makes the letters reinforce the application's thesis.
effort: 5
Sep-Oct 2026
Write and iterate the ELLIS motivation letter / SOP, explicitly structured around the Lio (Cambridge) x ETH-fellow pairing: why this exact two-lab, two-country combination, what he brings (sheaf GNNs, PyG, scVI/Scanpy, HPC genomics), and the joint project. Run >=2 revision rounds with Lio's feedback.
In advisor-matched programs the SOP is read as 'does this map onto a real project these two advisors want.' Tailoring to the named pair is what separates admits from rejects.
effort: 25
early-mid Oct 2026
Finalize the research proposal from the August sketch, incorporating ETH-fellow feedback so it reads as a project both labs have already discussed.
A proposal the primary advisor has visibly shaped signals a real, fundable collaboration rather than a speculative pitch.
effort: 15
~25-28 Oct 2026 (before 31 Oct AoE)
Submit the ELLIS application via the single portal, listing the ETH fellow as first-preference advisor and Lio as second, with proposal + SOP + CV + transcripts. Confirm all three referees have received their portal links; chase any who have not started. Submit 2-3 days early to absorb portal glitches.
One portal, one hard AoE deadline. Submitting early guards against upload/portal failures and gives referees runway to the ~25 Nov reference cutoff.
effort: 4
Nov 2026 - Jan 2027
After submission, prep for the Jan-Feb 2027 ELLIS interviews: rehearse a crisp research pitch, be ready to whiteboard the sheaf-GNN math and defend the joint ETH-Cambridge project, and do 2-3 mock interviews with Lio and/or lab peers.
ELLIS shortlists then interviews; the interview is where the two-lab fit and technical depth are stress-tested. Preparation is fully parallelizable with the post-submission wait.
effort: 12
Can run in parallel:
Assemble mechanical dossier (transcriptsPush first-author sheaf-GNN preprint to Deep-read ETH ELLIS fellows' recentConfirm and brief the three referees — SInterview preparation and mock interview
Critical path:
Read ELLIS 2027 call, lock the ~31 Oct 2Lock the pairing strategy: ETH ELLIS felSecure Lio's co-supervision commitmOutreach to the ETH primary target with Convert the call into an informal ETH prWrite the pairing-tailored SOP and finalSubmit the ELLIS application by ~28 Oct ELLIS interview Jan-Feb 2027

Cambridge PhD in Computer Science with Pietro Liò (Dept of Computer Science & Technology / Computer Lab) — Sept 2027 entry, funding deadlines Gates 3 Dec 2026 & Harding 8 Dec 2026

Jul 2026
Lock explicit written supervision commitment from Liò: get him to confirm in email he will act as your named PhD supervisor for Sept-2027 entry, AND which funding routes he will actively back (Gates Cambridge, Cambridge Trust/CIS, Harding, plus any departmental/DeepMind studentship). You are already his RA — convert that into a named-supervisor agreement now.
Cambridge admission is supervisor-gated: the department will not make an offer without a supervisor who confirms capacity. Everything else (proposal shape, funding scheme choice, referee framing) hangs on his yes and on which schemes he names. This is the single de-risking move.
effort: 3
Jul-Aug 2026
Co-define ONE concrete, fundable research question with Liò — e.g. sheaf/geometric GNNs for p53-network modelling in cancer genomics (bridges your IFOM Buffa/Tangherloni work + Liò's sheaf-DL lab). Write a 1-page problem statement he signs off on.
Memory strategy note: must lock a concrete biological question by Jul-Aug 2026 or you miss the Dec funding walls. The compbio dry-lab framing also widens funding eligibility (nationality-blind). A vague topic kills competitiveness for Gates/Harding.
effort: 10
Jul-Aug 2026 (parallel)
Book and sit IELTS Academic (or confirm exemption). Cambridge CS PhD requires IELTS 7.5 overall (7.0 each). As an Italian/EU applicant taught partly in English you may qualify for a waiver — verify with the department first; if not, sit it before Oct so results are in-portal.
English evidence is a hard admission condition; missing it makes any offer conditional and can void funding. Test slots fill up — booking late is a self-inflicted critical-path risk. Fully parallel to the proposal thread.
effort: 25
Aug-Sep 2026 (parallel)
Convert your current RA + IFOM sheaf-GNN work into a preprint (arXiv) and/or a workshop submission (GRL+, LoG, or a NeurIPS/ICLR workshop). Aim for at least an arXiv preprint with Liò as co-author before December.
Your gap is no first-author top-venue paper. A preprint/workshop with Liò's name is the highest-leverage fix and directly strengthens Gates/Harding (which reward research promise + endorsement). Runs in parallel; does not block submission but materially raises win probability.
effort: 60
Aug 2026 (parallel)
Line up and brief 3 referees: (1) Liò as academic supervisor-referee, (2) IFOM PI (Buffa or Tangherloni), (3) Pavia genomics/HPC PI or a Bocconi/Humanitas MSc supervisor. Send each your CV + the locked research question so references are specific.
Cambridge requires 2-3 academic references submitted through the portal by the deadline; late/generic references are a common silent failure. Briefing them early with the concrete topic makes letters concrete. Independent of the proposal thread.
effort: 2
Sep 2026
Draft the research proposal / statement (Cambridge CS: ~2-3 pages research statement) around the locked question. Iterate at least one full round with Liò.
The proposal is the core assessed document for a research-degree offer and is read by Gates/Harding panels. It depends on the Jul-Aug question lock and Liò's input, so it sits on the critical path between topic and submission.
effort: 20
Sep-Oct 2026 (parallel)
Assemble the supporting dossier: tailored CV, personal statement, all transcripts and degree certificates (Bocconi+Humanitas, Insubria, Pavia, prior degrees) with certified English translations where needed.
Transcript/translation gathering across 5 institutions is slow and is a frequent last-minute blocker. Doing it in parallel keeps the December wall clear. Required for a complete portal submission.
effort: 12
Oct 2026
Register the Cambridge Applicant Portal, select the correct course (PhD in Computer Science, Dept of Computer Science & Technology), name Liò as proposed supervisor, and tick the funding boxes: Cambridge Trust/CIS (auto-considered), Gates Cambridge, Harding Distinguished Postgraduate Scholars. Confirm each scheme's mechanics inside the one portal.
Unlike Sanger (separate portal) or ELLIS (external route), Cambridge central scholarships (Trust, Gates, Harding) are applied for by ticking boxes in the single University portal — but each has its own extra requirements/essays. Getting the course code and supervisor field right is prerequisite to everything downstream.
effort: 2
Oct-Nov 2026
Write the Gates Cambridge extra components (leadership statement + reasons-for-study / how you'll use your degree to improve others' lives), and any Harding-specific fields. These are additional essays layered on top of the standard application inside the same portal.
Gates is decided on a distinct rubric (leadership + social commitment), not just research fit — reusing only the research statement fails it. These essays gate the 3 Dec Gates deadline, the earliest Cambridge funding wall.
effort: 10
Nov 2026
Have Liò pre-flag your application to the departmental admissions/funding committee — a short note from him confirming supervision capacity and endorsing you for Gates/Harding shortlisting.
Your existing-supervisor status is the biggest asset for THIS target; an internal nudge from Liò before committees meet materially improves shortlisting odds. Low effort on your side, high leverage. Depends on the July commitment being in place.
effort: 1
Early Dec 2026 (submit by 1 Dec)
Submit the complete application — proposal, references requested, transcripts, English evidence, Gates + Harding components — before the earliest wall. Gates international deadline 3 Dec 2026; Harding 8 Dec 2026. Build a ~1 Dec buffer.
The 3 Dec Gates deadline is the binding constraint for the whole Cambridge track; missing it forfeits the top scholarship even if admission succeeds. Submitting to the earliest deadline auto-covers the later ones (Harding 8 Dec, Trust auto). This is the terminal node of the critical path.
effort: 4
Can run in parallel:
IELTS Academic prep + sitting (Jul-Aug) Preprint/workshop paper with Liò (Aug-SeReferee line-up + briefing (Aug) indepenTranscript/certificate gathering + certiGates/Harding extra essays (Oct-Nov) can
Critical path:
Jul 2026: Lock Liò written supervision cJul-Aug 2026: Co-define and sign off theSep 2026: Draft research proposal with oOct 2026: Register portal, select PhD CoOct-Nov 2026: Write Gates leadership + rEarly Dec 2026: Submit complete applicat

EPFL doctoral (primary EDIC, backup/parallel EDCB) with Maria Brbić (IC, ML4science/single-cell foundation models) or Charlotte Bunne (comp bio, optimal transport/generative single-cell) — autumn 2026 cycle, EPFL portal deadlines ~Dec 2026, separate ELLIS PhD portal deadline ~mid-Nov 2026, interviews Jan–Feb 2027.

Jul 2026 (wk1)
Confirm program mechanics and eligibility: EDIC vs EDCB exact deadlines (EDIC typically ~15 Dec, EDCB ~1 Dec) on official EPFL pages; verify TOEFL/IELTS waiver (Bocconi MSc is English-taught → usually waived, get it in writing); confirm 3-referee requirement, whether program is admit-first-then-advisor (EDIC/EDCB both admit to program, advisor secured after). Note EPFL programs are program-based, NOT direct-to-supervisor — but supervisor interest is decisive.
Every downstream artifact (SoP count, letters, test) depends on the real requirements; kills wrong assumptions early.
effort: 4
Jul 2026 (wk1-2)
Deep-map both target labs. Pull Brbić's 2024-2026 work (single-cell foundation models, cross-tissue/cell-type annotation, ML4science) and Bunne's (optimal transport for perturbation/spatial, generative single-cell). Extract 2-3 flagship papers each; write a one-page overlap memo linking each to your Sheaf-GNN p53 / geometric-DL-on-genomics work.
Specificity is the entire signal in supervisor emails, SoP, and interviews; generic contact gets ignored.
effort: 12
Jul 2026 (wk2)
Activate the Liò connection. Ask Pietro Liò (Cambridge, ELLIS member) for a warm intro to Brbić and Bunne. Frame it around ELLIS co-supervision: Liò as external co-supervisor at Cambridge + Brbić/Bunne at EPFL. Give Liò your one-line pitch + CV so he can forward.
A warm ELLIS-network intro moves reply odds from ~10% to ~70% and directly seeds the co-supervision structure that IS the ETH/ELLIS dream path.
effort: 2
Aug 2026 (wk1)
Send tailored emails to Brbić and Bunne (separately, no CC). 150-200 words: one concrete idea connecting your sheaf/geometric-DL work to a specific recent paper of theirs, the Liò link, MSc Data Analytics & AI for Health + genomics/HPC background, CV attached, ask for a short chat and whether they take students in the autumn cycle.
Converts warm intro into stated supervisor interest — the true gate for EPFL admission behind the program formality.
effort: 6
Aug–Sep 2026
Ship a tangible research artifact BEFORE December: a preprint (arXiv/bioRxiv) OR a polished public GitHub repo + short technical report on the Sheaf-GNN p53 (IFOM) / Cambridge work. Even a workshop-style preprint counts. Coordinate with Liò/Buffa/Tangherloni on authorship + release permission.
No first-author top-venue paper yet is the one real weakness; a citable preprint is the single highest-leverage fix and materially strengthens both letters and SoP.
effort: 70
Aug 2026 (wk2-3)
Lock 3 referees and brief them: Liò (Cambridge), Buffa or Tangherloni (IFOM), plus one Bocconi/Humanitas or Pavia professor. Confirm availability for the whole cycle (EPFL + ELLIS + any others), send each your CV, draft SoP, and a bullet list of what to emphasize per referee.
Letters are submitted directly by referees via portals; late/uneven letters sink otherwise strong files. Briefing makes them specific and aligned to Brbić/Bunne.
effort: 4
Sep–Oct 2026
Draft the Statement of Purpose, then produce program-specific variants (EDIC = CS/ML framing; EDCB = computational-biology/genomics framing). Arc: geometric DL / GNNs → sheaf-based methods → single-cell & genomics problems → named Brbić/Bunne projects → long-term ELLIS/co-supervision vision.
Core narrative document for every portal; tailoring per program and per supervisor is what admissions committees reward.
effort: 20
Oct 2026 (early)
If TOEFL/IELTS is NOT waived (per step 1), book and sit the test now with buffer for score reporting to EPFL. If waived, obtain the official waiver confirmation document and skip.
Test scores have reporting lag; a December scramble is a common avoidable rejection cause.
effort: 15
Oct–Nov 2026
Prepare and submit the ELLIS PhD application on its SEPARATE portal (hard deadline ~15 Nov 2026 — earlier than EPFL). Rank Brbić and Bunne as host supervisors, list Liò as co-supervisor, reuse tailored SoP + research statement + referee letters.
Distinct portal and earliest hard deadline; it is the highest-leverage track for the co-supervised ELLIS model and must not be missed while focused on EPFL's later date.
effort: 8
Nov 2026
Interview prep for informal supervisor chats and ELLIS matching interviews: rehearse the p53 Sheaf-GNN work, be able to critique one Brbić and one Bunne paper, prepare 2-3 concrete project proposals per lab, and questions about compute/rotations/funding.
Supervisor conviction is formed in these calls; concrete proposals separate you from applicants who only admire the lab.
effort: 10
Nov–Dec 2026
Assemble and submit EPFL doctoral application(s) before the ~Dec deadline: EDIC as primary (both supervisors reachable via IC), EDCB as the comp-bio-flavored alternative if a second application is permitted. Upload transcripts (all 5 degrees), CV, program-specific SoP; trigger referee links early.
Primary formal target; EDIC maximizes reach to both Brbić and Bunne, EDCB hedges toward the genomics framing.
effort: 8
Dec 2026 (mid)
Verify all 3 referee letters are actually submitted on every portal (EPFL + ELLIS); send reminders 1 week before each deadline. Confirm transcripts/test docs marked received.
Missing letters are the most common silent disqualifier and are outside your direct control — active follow-up is required.
effort: 3
Jan–Feb 2027
Faculty/committee interview round: targeted prep per supervisor, close the ELLIS co-supervision arrangement with Liò + EPFL host, and negotiate rotation/start logistics on admission.
Final conversion step from admitted-to-program to a funded, supervised offer under Brbić or Bunne.
effort: 12
Can run in parallel:
Preprint/artifact build (Aug-Sep) runs cSupervisor emails run concurrently with TOEFL/IELTS (if needed) runs concurrentlLab mapping memo and referee briefing caELLIS application assembly reuses SoP/le
Critical path:
Confirm program mechanics + eligibility Deep-map Brbić/Bunne labs + overlap memoLiò warm intro to both supervisors (Jul Tailored supervisor emails → secure statShip preprint/artifact to fix the no-firSubmit ELLIS PhD on separate portal — eaSubmit EPFL EDIC/EDCB before ~Dec 2026 dVerify letters submitted (mid-Dec 2026)

Oxford DPhil (supervisors: Christopher Yau, Chris Holmes, Yarin Gal) + Clarendon Scholarship, entry Sept 2027 — application submitted autumn 2026 by the December gathering-field / Clarendon-eligible deadline. Clarendon is nationality-blind, full fees + stipend, and requires NO separate application: it is auto-considered when the DPhil form is submitted by the course's December deadline with the funding box ticked. So the single binding wall is the DPhil December deadline, not a separate scholarship portal (contrast Sanger's separate 27 Nov portal / Imperial's supervisor-nomination gate). The whole plan is supervisor-capacity gated: Oxford will not admit without a named supervisor willing to take you, so the Liò bridge is the highest-leverage move.

Jul 2026 (wk 1-2)
Lock one concrete, testable thesis question bridging geometric deep learning and genomics/single-cell (e.g. sheaf/Bayesian GNNs for spatial transcriptomics or single-cell perturbation, with calibrated uncertainty). Write it as a 1-paragraph pitch + 3 candidate sub-aims.
Every supervisor email, the research proposal, and the SOP all derive from this. Oxford DPhil (esp. Statistics) is proposal-driven; without a locked question nothing downstream can start. Also the compbio framing is what escapes the UKRI/EPSRC home-fee bottleneck.
effort: 12
Jul 2026 (wk 2)
Map exact fit per supervisor and pick the primary department + DPhil route. Yau (Christopher Yau, statistical ML for genomics / single-cell, Big Data Institute) and Holmes (Bayesian stats + genomics/health, Turing) => DPhil in Statistics. Gal (OATML, Bayesian deep learning / uncertainty) => DPhil in Computer Science or AIMS. Decide Statistics as primary home (Yau+Holmes both there, best compbio+funding fit), Gal as co/second.
The department chosen dictates which application portal, which December deadline, and which proposal format apply. Statistics and CS have different dates and requirements. Choosing wrong forces a redo.
effort: 6
Jul-Aug 2026
Ask Pietro Liò (existing Cambridge RA supervisor) for warm-intro emails / endorsement to Yau, Holmes and Gal. All three sit in the ELLIS / geometric-DL / Bayesian-compbio orbit Liò is embedded in — he is a direct bridge, not a cold contact.
Oxford DPhil admission is supervisor-capacity gated. A warm intro from Liò converts a cold c, ignorable email into a real conversation and dramatically raises the odds a supervisor commits capacity — the single biggest de-risking move. Also pre-positions Liò as lead referee.
effort: 3
Aug 2026 (early)
Send 3 tailored supervisor emails (Yau, Holmes, Gal): CV + 1-page research-fit note mapping your sheaf-GNN / p53-IFOM work to each lab's current agenda + Liò referral line. Ask explicitly whether they have DPhil capacity for Oct-2027 entry and would consider supervising.
You need a named supervisor's soft commitment BEFORE submitting. Sending in early Aug beats the Sept-Oct rush when inboxes fill and capacity fills. Three parallel targets hedge against any one being full.
effort: 8
Aug-Sep 2026
Hold intro calls; co-refine the project to each supervisor's actual grants/students/data. Confirm at least one (ideally Yau or Holmes) will support your application and be named on the form.
Aligning the proposal to real lab capacity and funded projects both strengthens the academic case and materially helps Clarendon (judged on academic merit + fit). Converts soft interest into a concrete supervisor commitment.
effort: 10
Sep 2026 (early)
Secure and confirm 3 referees: Liò (lead, geometric-DL RA), IFOM (Buffa/Tangherloni, p53 GNN modelling), and Bocconi+Humanitas MSc supervisor. Get written commitment to submit by mid-Nov and send them the proposal + brag sheet.
Oxford requires 3 references uploaded by the deadline; a missing/late reference voids Clarendon auto-consideration. Italian-institution referees are slow — lock them early. Parallelizable with proposal writing.
effort: 2
Sep-Oct 2026
Write the research proposal (Statistics DPhil ~2-3 pages; CS similar). Iterate 2-3 rounds with the committed supervisor. Frame the biological question first, then the geometric/Bayesian method.
Core academic assessment document and the main thing Clarendon and the department judge beyond grades. This is the longest single item on the critical path — start once the supervisor call fixes direction.
effort: 30
Sep 2026
Draft the Statement of Purpose / personal statement tailored to Oxford and to the specific supervisor(s), threading the first-gen narrative (narrative only, not a funding key) and the coherent compbio+geometric-DL trajectory.
Required and assessed component. Parallelizable with proposal; different document, same source material from Step 1.
effort: 10
Sep 2026
Order official transcripts + degree certificates from Bocconi, Humanitas, Pavia, Insubria (MSc + BSc), plus certified English translations where the originals are Italian.
Oxford requires certified transcripts uploaded by the deadline; Italian universities are slow to issue and translate. A missing transcript blocks submission regardless of everything else being ready. Runs fully in parallel with writing.
effort: 4
Sep 2026
Resolve English-language requirement: confirm whether the Bocconi/Humanitas English-taught MSc grants Oxford's exemption; if not, book and sit IELTS Academic / TOEFL. VERIFY Oxford's exact rule for your course.
Mandatory. Either you need documentary proof of exemption or a valid test score in the file by the deadline. If a test is needed, booking slips fast in autumn — decide in Sept. Parallelizable.
effort: 3
Oct 2026
Confirm on the official Oxford Graduate Admissions page the EXACT chosen-course portal and the December (Clarendon-eligible / 'gathered field') deadline for the DPhil in Statistics vs CS — they differ. Note that Clarendon has NO separate form or deadline.
Clarendon auto-consideration is triggered ONLY by submitting the course application by its relevant deadline (typically an early-December gathered-field date) with the funding box ticked. Missing that date forfeits Clarendon even if the DPhil itself has a later January deadline. This is the one wall to defend.
effort: 2
Nov 2026
Assemble and internally review the full package (proposal, SOP, CV, transcripts, English proof, 3 references, writing sample if CS route). Chase referees to upload by mid-Nov. Do a supervisor/mentor read-through.
Buffer before the December wall; references and translations are the items most likely to slip. Review catches formatting/eligibility gaps while there is still time to fix them.
effort: 8
Early Dec 2026 (by the gathered-field deadline)
SUBMIT the Oxford DPhil application through the single course portal, ticking 'apply for all available funding including Clarendon'. No separate Clarendon application exists.
This one submission is the binding event: it triggers Clarendon auto-consideration AND the department admission decision. Everything upstream exists to make this deadline with a complete, supervisor-backed file.
effort: 3
Dec 2026 - Jan 2027
Prepare for and attend the DPhil interview (likely Yau/Holmes/Gal + panel). Rehearse defending the proposal, method choices, and fit; line up Liò for a mock.
The offer and the Clarendon nomination are both contingent on the interview. A strong, proposal-grounded interview is what converts a supervisor-backed application into a funded place.
effort: 10
Can run in parallel:
Supervisor emails to Yau, Holmes and GalResearch proposal (Step 7) and SOP (StepTranscript/certificate procurement (StepReferee lock-in (Step 6) runs in paralleDeadline/portal confirmation (Step 11) c
Critical path:
Step 1 — Lock concrete research questionStep 2 — Choose department/DPhil route (Step 3 — Liò warm intros to Yau/Holmes/GStep 4 — Tailored supervisor emails (earStep 5 — Supervisor calls + secure namedStep 7 — Write + iterate research proposStep 12 — Assemble + internal review, reStep 13 — Submit DPhil by December gathe

Imperial College London — PhD (Dept of Computing / I-X, geometric deep learning & GNNs for computational biology) + Imperial President's PhD Scholarship (full overseas tuition + stipend, nationality-blind, SUPERVISOR-GATED). Entry Sept/Oct 2027; apply autumn 2026; hard deadline window Dec 2026–Jan 2027.

Jul 2026 (wk1)
Read the President's PhD Scholarship rules end-to-end and extract the exact mechanics: confirm the MANDATORY pre-requisite that you secure a potential supervisor's written agreement to support the application BEFORE the scholarship form opens; note whether the department nominates or you apply direct, the number of awards, eligibility (overseas OK), the scholarship deadline, and whether a separate PhD admissions application must be submitted/held first. Build a one-page requirements checklist + dated timeline.
The whole plan hinges on the supervisor-agreement gate and on there being TWO separate submissions (admissions + scholarship). Getting the mechanics wrong loses the cycle. Traps memory already flags Imperial CDTs as home-only routes — the President's Scholarship is the only nationality-blind money here, so its exact rules must be locked first.
effort: 6
Jul 2026 (wk1–2)
Lock ONE concrete, fundable research question in geometric DL / GNNs for genomics or single-cell, reusing the IFOM sheaf-GNN p53-in-cancer work and the Liò Cambridge sheaf-based geometric DL line. Write a 1-page mini-proposal (question, method, why-now, why-Imperial) framed as DRY-LAB compbio to widen fit.
Supervisors and the scholarship both judge research potential + a specific biological question. This artifact is the reusable core of every downstream email, proposal, and statement. Memory: must lock a concrete biological question by Jul–Aug 2026 or the whole autumn cascade slips.
effort: 20
Jul–Aug 2026
Ask Pietro Liò (existing supervisor) for two things in one conversation: (a) a warm introduction / named suggestions of Imperial faculty doing graph ML / geometric DL / ML-for-biology (Dept of Computing, I-X, BioMedIA, Life Sciences), and (b) a firm commitment to serve as lead academic referee for Imperial. Give him the 1-page mini-proposal to react to.
The Liò connection is the single biggest asset. A warm intro converts cold supervisor emails into meetings, and his reference name-recognition in geometric DL directly lifts the 'research potential' score the President's Scholarship weights. Note: Michael Bronstein left Imperial for Oxford, so the graph-ML cluster there is thinner — a Liò-brokered intro to the right person matters more than a cold blast.
effort: 3
Aug 2026
Identify and email 3–5 target Imperial supervisors (shortlist from Liò's suggestions + own scan of Dept of Computing / I-X / BioMedIA / Dept of Life Sciences). Each email tailored: 1 paragraph on their specific work, the 1-page pitch, CV, and an explicit ask for a call. Track responses in a simple sheet.
Securing a supervisor who will support the scholarship is THE gate — no agreement, no eligibility. Emailing 3–5 in parallel de-risks non-response and gives leverage. Must start early because August/September academic availability is patchy and the agreement must be firm before the scholarship form.
effort: 12
Aug–Sep 2026
Convert supervisor interest into an EXPLICIT written agreement to support the President's Scholarship application. Hold 1–2 calls, iterate the mini-proposal to fit their lab/grant, and get it in writing (email is fine) that they will back the nomination.
This is the hard pre-condition the scholarship rules impose. Everything after this (co-developed proposal, admissions app naming the supervisor, scholarship form) is unlockable only once this exists. Highest-risk single node on the critical path.
effort: 8
Sep 2026
Co-develop the FULL research proposal with the agreed supervisor, aligned to their funding/lab direction (methods, feasibility, 3-year plan, data access). Iterate to their sign-off.
The scholarship is decided largely on proposal quality + supervisor endorsement; a supervisor-co-authored proposal both scores higher and cements their advocacy. Must be near-final before the admissions and scholarship forms open.
effort: 25
Sep 2026
Formally line up 2–3 referees and brief them: Pietro Liò (lead), Buffa and/or Tangherloni (IFOM), plus a Bocconi/Humanitas academic. Send each the final CV, proposal, Imperial portal instructions, and the deadline. Confirm they can submit by early Dec.
References are a submission-blocking component and referees are slow in the autumn. Briefing them with the actual proposal makes letters concrete and aligned to the geometric-DL-for-biology narrative.
effort: 4
Sep–Oct 2026
Resolve English-language evidence: check whether your English-taught Bocconi/UK research experience grants an Imperial exemption; if not, book and sit IELTS Academic / TOEFL early (Imperial has high band requirements incl. per-component minima).
Missing English proof blocks the ADMISSIONS decision (and thus scholarship consideration) even with a perfect proposal. Test slots fill up in autumn — book now or confirm exemption now. Parallel to the supervisor track so it never becomes the bottleneck.
effort: 15
Oct 2026
Draft the personal statement / research statement + Imperial-tailored CV, explicitly hitting the scholarship's two axes: academic excellence (110L summa cluster, ~4.0 Bocconi GPA, Ghislieri/Bocconi awards) and research potential (Cambridge/Liò RA, IFOM sheaf-GNN, HPC genomics). Reuse the mini-proposal for the research vision.
The scholarship panel scores on excellence + potential; a tailored statement is worth more than any single credential. Depends on the locked research question; can be drafted in parallel with the proposal.
effort: 15
Oct–Nov 2026
Assemble and, where needed, certify/translate documents: all degree transcripts and certificates (Insubria, Bocconi/Humanitas, Pavia, Sport Sci, BSc), Italian→English certified translations, and an ECTS/Italian-to-UK grade conversion note.
Italian transcript translation + grade conversion is slow and admissions-blocking; starting in October avoids a December scramble. Fully parallel to the supervisor and writing tracks.
effort: 8
Nov 2026
Submit the PhD PROGRAMME (admissions) application via the Imperial portal, naming the agreed supervisor, attaching proposal, statement, CV, transcripts, English proof; trigger referee requests.
You typically must have applied for / hold a PhD place for the President's Scholarship to consider you — admissions is the upstream submission. Naming the supervisor ties the two together.
effort: 4
Nov–Dec 2026
Submit the President's PhD Scholarship application (the separate route/form), referencing the supervisor's support and the admissions application. Verify the exact scholarship deadline from step 1 and submit with buffer.
Terminal node on the critical path — the actual money application. Separate from admissions, so it is easy to miss if you assume one submission covers both.
effort: 4
Aug–Nov 2026 (parallel)
Push a preprint or workshop paper from the Liò / IFOM sheaf-GNN work (arXiv + a NeurIPS/ICLR/ICML workshop or a compbio venue) to convert 'no first-author top-venue paper yet' into demonstrable output.
The one competitive weakness is the missing first-author paper; even a workshop/preprint materially lifts 'research potential' and the referee letters can cite it. Runs entirely in parallel — boosts every application, blocks none.
effort: 40
Dec 2026
Prepare for interview: rehearse the research vision, technical depth on sheaf/geometric GNNs, and 'why Imperial + this supervisor', with a mock led by Liò or a labmate.
Shortlisted President's candidates are interviewed; being fluent on the specific proposal and fit converts the shortlist into an award. Low effort, high leverage, off the submission critical path.
effort: 8
Can run in parallel:
Preprint/workshop-paper push (Aug–Nov) rEnglish-language test / exemption check Transcript gathering, certified translatPersonal/research statement drafting (OcReferee briefing (Sep) overlaps proposalEmailing all 3–5 supervisors simultaneou
Critical path:
Confirm President's Scholarship mecLock concrete GNN/geometric-DL research Liò warm intro to Imperial supervisors (Email 3–5 target Imperial supervisors (ASecure EXPLICIT written supervisor agreeCo-develop full research proposal to supSubmit PhD admissions application namingSubmit President's PhD Scholarship

EMBL-EBI International PhD (via EMBL International PhD Programme / EIPP) — single EMBL-wide application selecting EMBL-EBI Hinxton groups; autumn/winter 2026 call, deadline ~Dec 2026-Jan 2027. NOTE: EMBL-EBI is NOT the Sanger PhD (separate portal, Nov deadline) despite shared Wellcome Genome Campus; EIPP requires NO supervisor pre-nomination (unlike Imperial), but pre-contacting PIs and ranking preferred groups is decisive.

Jul 2026 (week 1)
Open the EIPP application page and confirm the exact autumn/winter-round timeline for THIS cycle: portal open date, hard submission deadline, and separate referee-submission deadline. Historically the winter round opens mid-Aug and can close as early as mid/late Oct — treat Dec-Jan as the outer bound and target full readiness by early Oct so you are never caught by an earlier close. Confirm EMBL-EBI (Hinxton) is a selectable site in this round.
The single biggest risk is assuming a Dec-Jan deadline when the winter EIPP round may close in October; every downstream date anchors to the verified deadline. Also disambiguates EMBL-EBI/EIPP from the Sanger and Cambridge-EBI routes so effort is not wasted on the wrong portal.
effort: 3
Jul 2026 (week 1-2)
Map target EMBL-EBI groups for geometric DL / GNNs on genomics and read 2-3 recent papers each: Oliver Stegle (statistical + ML methods for molecular variation across individuals/space/time — strongest fit for generative/graph models on genomics; note he is split EMBL-EBI/DKFZ Heidelberg), John Marioni (single-cell + spatial, ML-motivated statistical models of cell fate), and one of Pedro Beltrao (evolution of cellular/protein networks — natural GNN substrate) or Nick Goldman (phylogenetics). For each, draft a concrete project idea that plugs in Sheaf/geometric GNNs.
EIPP lets you name and rank preferred groups; a specific, paper-grounded fit per group is what converts your Cambridge/IFOM Sheaf-GNN work into an obvious hire. Drives the PI emails, the motivation letter, and the ranking.
effort: 12
Jul 2026 (week 2)
Ask Pietro Liò (your Cambridge Computer Lab PI) for two things: (a) a warm email introduction to Stegle and/or Marioni — Cambridge and Hinxton are ~15 km apart and collaborate heavily, so this is a high-probability warm path — and (b) confirmation he will serve as a referee. Give him your one-paragraph pitch and target-group shortlist so the intro is specific.
The Liò connection is your strongest lever for EMBL-EBI specifically: a warm intro from a nearby, well-known ML-for-bio PI massively raises the odds a target PI engages, and Liò is your most credible reference for geometric deep learning. Everything on the warm-outreach and reference tracks depends on this.
effort: 2
Jul-Aug 2026
Email 2-3 target PIs (using the Liò intro where available): 4-6 sentences — who you are, the specific project fit citing one of their papers, one concrete GNN/geometric-DL angle, CV attached, and a request for a 20-min call. Follow up once after ~10 days if silent.
EIPP is programme-not-PI admission, but PIs flag candidates they want and it lets you rank groups from real signal rather than guesswork. A recorded interest from Stegle/Marioni is a strong tailwind at interview-week group selection.
effort: 8
Aug 2026 (early)
Lock in 2-3 referees (EIPP requires a minimum of two academic references): Liò (Cambridge, geometric DL) + an IFOM PI (Buffa or Tangherloni, Sheaf-GNN p53 / cancer modelling) + optionally an MSc supervisor. Send each your CV, draft research statement, the target groups, and the exact referee deadline. Ask them to reserve time.
References are submitted by referees through the portal and are a common failure point; early briefing protects against a missed referee deadline that silently voids an otherwise complete application. Two of your three referees directly attest to GNN-for-genomics ability — maximally on-target.
effort: 3
Aug 2026
Build a computational-genomics-tailored CV: lead with Cambridge RA (Liò, sheaf-based geometric DL) and IFOM (Sheaf-GNN p53 in cancer), then HPC genomics/transposable-elements (Pavia), single-cell stack (Scanpy/AnnData/scVI/Pyro), and PyTorch Geometric. Foreground methods over the long degree list; compress the five degrees.
EMBL-EBI is a pure computational institute; the CV must read as a methods person who already does GNNs on biological data, not a generalist with many MScs. Feeds the PI emails and the submission.
effort: 6
Aug 2026 (early, in parallel)
Request and collect official transcripts + degree certificates for ALL degrees (Bocconi+Humanitas ongoing incl. transcript-to-date, Pavia postgrad, Insubria x2, plus BSc). Italian university registrars are slow — file requests now; scan to PDF as received.
Document procurement is the classic bureaucratic long-pole that has nothing to do with your merit but can block submission at the deadline. Starting in August absorbs registrar delays.
effort: 5
Aug-Sep 2026
Draft the motivation letter + research-interest statement: a coherent thesis (geometric DL / GNNs for genomics), evidence you already do it (Cambridge + IFOM Sheaf-GNNs, HPC genomics), and a per-group paragraph naming your 2-3 ranked EMBL-EBI groups with a concrete project for each. Keep an ELLIS/ETH-Liò through-line so the narrative is consistent across your parallel applications without copy-paste.
This is the core intellectual deliverable and the main differentiator at shortlisting; it can only be written well after the group mapping and PI conversations. Longest single item on the critical path.
effort: 15
Sep 2026
Confirm English-proficiency handling: EIPP typically does not require TOEFL/IELTS (English-taught Bocconi MSc + Cambridge RA usually suffice and proficiency is assessed at interview) — verify on the portal and assemble evidence only if the form requests it.
Cheap to close, expensive to discover late. Prevents a last-minute scramble for a test that is probably not needed but occasionally is.
effort: 2
Sep 2026 (mid-late)
Send the motivation letter + research statement to Liò and one other mentor for critique; revise for a sharper thesis and tighter per-group projects.
External critique from someone who knows EMBL-EBI PIs converts a solid statement into a competitive one; must precede final submission.
effort: 6
Sep-Oct 2026
Complete the EIPP online form early: enter the 'general info' and 'references' sections FIRST so referees receive their automated requests with weeks of runway, then upload CV, statement, transcripts, and rank your EMBL-EBI groups. Nudge referees once they are triggered.
EMBL explicitly advises entering referees first because their letters are the slowest-moving dependency; front-loading the form removes deadline-day risk.
effort: 4
Oct 2026 (or the verified portal deadline, whichever is earlier)
Final proofread, confirm ALL referees have actually submitted, then SUBMIT well before the cutoff. Keep a dated copy of every uploaded file.
Endpoint of the critical path; an unsubmitted reference is the most common way a strong application dies silently, so verify referee status before you rely on the deadline.
effort: 3
Oct-Nov 2026 (post-submission, while waiting)
Prepare for EMBL Interview Weeks: build a crisp talk on your Sheaf-GNN / geometric-DL work (Cambridge + IFOM), rehearse a tailored fit pitch for each ranked group, prepare sharp questions for Stegle/Marioni/Beltrao, and run 1-2 mock interviews. Confirm format (in-person hub vs virtual) once invited.
Shortlisted candidates are assessed and matched to groups during interview week; strong per-group performance is what secures your preferred EMBL-EBI supervisor. Runs in parallel with the waiting period so no time is lost.
effort: 15
Can run in parallel:
Transcript/certificate procurement (ItalCV tailoring runs alongside PI outreach PI outreach emails and follow-ups run coEnglish-proficiency verification runs inReferee briefing runs in parallel once LInterview-week preparation runs in paral
Critical path:
Verify exact autumn/winter EIPP deadlineSecure Liò warm intro + referee commitmeMap target EMBL-EBI groups and read theiPI outreach to Stegle/Marioni/Beltrao anDraft motivation letter + per-group reseMentor critique + revision (Sep)Complete online form with referees enterConfirm referees submitted + final SUBMI

Wellcome Sanger Institute 4-Year PhD Programme (2027 entry) — separate Sanger portal, ~27 Nov 2026 deadline (earliest wall of the cycle), fully funded for ALL nationalities, rotation-based first year

Jul 2026 (week 1)
Lock ONE concrete biological question bridging geometric deep learning -> genomics, framed for Sanger's data (e.g. sheaf/graph representation learning over single-cell atlases or somatic-mutation graphs). Write it as a 3-sentence thesis pitch.
