Sanger's hard gate is genuine biological motivation + methods-to-data fit, not just ML skill. Every downstream asset (emails, statement, interview) reuses this narrative. Rotation model means the question must be broad enough to survive 2-3 rotation hosts yet specific enough to prove intent.
effort: 6-10
Jul 2026 (week 1-2)
Shortlist 4-5 Sanger groups/themes that fit: Cellular Genetics (single-cell, Human Cell Atlas lineage), computational/generative genomics groups, Cancer/Ageing & Somatic Mutation. Note which run dry-lab rotations. Map each to his scanpy/scVI/AnnData + PyG/sheaf-GNN stack.
The Sanger application asks you to express/rank research interests across themes; rotation-based admission judges breadth-of-fit, not one PI. This list drives who to email and how to tailor the statement.
effort: 5-8
Jul-Aug 2026
Send 2-3 short, individualized informal emails to Sanger faculty in the shortlisted dry-lab groups. Lead with the IFOM p53 Sheaf-GNN + Liò Cambridge single-cell work; ask about rotation availability and fit. Attach 1-page CV.
Sanger is rotation-based so no supervisor nomination is REQUIRED (unlike Imperial) — but a warm faculty signal materially helps shortlisting and secures a likely rotation host. The Liò/Cambridge tie is direct credibility on the same Genome Campus ecosystem (EMBL-EBI next door).
effort: 6-9
Aug 2026 (early)
Formally request 2-3 references and give referees the thesis pitch + CV + deadline. Priority: Pietro Liò (Cambridge, nationality-blind flagship credibility), Buffa/Tangherloni (IFOM), a Bocconi+Humanitas MSc supervisor.
Sanger requires referees to submit via its portal by the deadline; referee lag is the single most common self-inflicted miss. Liò's letter is the highest-leverage asset for a nationality-blind, methods-heavy applicant. Requesting in Aug leaves buffer before the 27 Nov wall.
effort: 2-3
Aug 2026
Check English-language requirement and, if not exempt, book IELTS Academic / TOEFL now. Confirm whether his English-taught Bocconi MSc grants a waiver.
Sanger students register with the University of Cambridge, which enforces an English-language condition; test slots fill and scores take ~2 weeks. A hidden blocker that can void an otherwise-complete application or delay the offer.
effort: 3-6 (plus test day)
Aug-Sep 2026
Draft the Sanger personal/research statement tailored to the ROTATION model: show 2-3 distinct rotation directions across the shortlisted themes, all unified by the geometric-DL-for-genomics narrative. Avoid single-PI tunnel vision.
Statement is the core differentiator and must read as rotation-ready (curious across groups) while proving depth. Reuses the locked pitch and group shortlist; feeds interview.
effort: 12-18
Sep 2026
When the cycle opens, create the account on Sanger's OWN application portal (separate from the Cambridge Postgraduate Applicant Portal) and pre-fill static sections; log the exact 2026 deadline (verify it is ~27 Nov).
Sanger runs standalone admissions on a separate system with an earlier deadline than the university-wide Dec deadlines. Registering early surfaces required fields/uploads and prevents a last-week portal surprise. Confirm the date because it is the earliest wall of the whole cycle.
effort: 2-4
Sep-Oct 2026
Tailor a computational-genomics CV: foreground Python/PyTorch-Lightning/PyG, Scanpy/AnnData/scVI/Pyro, HPC genomics (Pavia), Cambridge RA + IFOM. De-emphasize wet-lab/sport-science except as summa-cum-laude signals.
Sanger dry-lab reviewers scan for exactly this stack; the compbio dry-lab framing is what makes him nationality-blind competitive here. Parallelizable with statement drafting.
effort: 4-6
Oct 2026
Send the near-final statement + CV to Liò (and one IFOM PI) for a review pass; incorporate edits.
A Sanger-credible reviewer catches domain-motivation weaknesses and calibrates ambition. Doing this in Oct leaves time to revise before the Nov wall.
effort: 3-5 (his time; days of turnaround)
Nov 2026 (by ~20 Nov)
Submit the full Sanger application; then confirm each referee has actually uploaded and that theme-preferences/uploads registered. Chase any missing reference.
Submitting ~1 week before the ~27 Nov deadline absorbs referee delay and portal issues. Sanger's is the FIRST deadline of the cycle, so a slip here cascades into Gates (3 Dec) / Harding (8 Dec) prep collisions.
effort: 3-5
Dec 2026 - Jan 2027
Prep the Sanger interview: rehearse a research/rotation presentation, prepare to discuss 2-3 rotation ideas per theme, and reconnect with any faculty who replied in Jul-Aug. Expect a multi-panel / possibly two-day interview.
Sanger shortlists then interviews (typically Jan/Feb), often with a presentation and multiple panels. Warm faculty contacts and a crisp rotation plan convert shortlist to offer.
effort: 10-15
Can run in parallel:
Faculty emails (Jul-Aug) run parallel toReference requests (Aug) run parallel toEnglish test booking/sitting (Aug-Oct) fCV tailoring (Sep-Oct) parallel to stateSanger portal registration/pre-fill (SepGroup shortlist (Jul) can be built along
Critical path:
Lock biological question / thesis pitch Shortlist Sanger groups/themes (Jul wk1-Send faculty emails to seed fit + rotatiDraft rotation-tailored statement (Aug-SLiò review pass on statement (Oct)Submit + confirm references uploaded befInterview prep + presentation (Dec-Jan)Offer

US backbone — fully-funded PhD at Harvard / Stanford / MIT. Funding = guaranteed on admission (full tuition + 12-month stipend via program fellowship/RA/TA); there is NO separate funding application. Therefore every pre-application action targets ADMISSION, not a scholarship. Concrete program set: Harvard BIG (Bioinformatics & Integrative Genomics) / Systems Biology / SEAS CS; MIT EECS + CSB (Computational & Systems Biology); Stanford CS + BMI (Biomedical Informatics). Best-fit faculty (the leverage): Marinka Zitnik (Harvard, GNNs for biomedicine — near-perfect match), Bonnie Berger & Caroline Uhler & Manolis Kellis (MIT), Jure Leskovec & Anshul Kundaje (Stanford). One portable nationality-blind external exists: Knight-Hennessy Scholars (Stanford). NSF GRFP / Hertz / Soros are US-citizen/PR-only — ineligible, skip.

Jul 2026
Build target matrix: 3 schools x 2-3 programs x 2-3 named faculty each. Map the applicant's sheaf/geometric-DL + genomics profile onto specific labs — Marinka Zitnik (Harvard, GNNs for biomedicine), Bonnie Berger + Caroline Uhler + Manolis Kellis (MIT CSB/EECS), Jure Leskovec + Anshul Kundaje (Stanford). Record each program's deadline, TOEFL/GRE policy, essay set, letter count.
US committee admissions still weight faculty fit heavily, especially bio/BMI programs. This decides which programs to apply to, which essays to write, and who to email. Nothing downstream is coherent without it.
effort: 12
Jul 2026
Book and take TOEFL iBT (or IELTS). First check each program's waiver rule — a degree taught in English (Cambridge RA / possibly Bocconi track) may waive it; VERIFY per program before paying. If not waived, take by mid-Sept so ETS scores land before Dec 1 deadlines.
International (Italian) applicants need a valid English score on file or a documented waiver; a missing/late score is a hard submission blocker. ETS reporting takes 1-2 weeks.
effort: 15
Jul-Aug 2026
Lock the 3 recommender slate and ask now with a Nov 15 internal deadline: (1) Pietro Liò (Cambridge, geometric-DL RA — primary), (2) Buffa or Tangherloni (IFOM, p53 / Sheaf-GNN cancer modelling), (3) a Bocconi/Humanitas MSc research supervisor. Give each a one-page brag sheet + target list.
Three research-heavy letters gate every US submission; a strong Liò letter is the single biggest asset. Early ask beats the autumn letter-crush and lets weak links be swapped.
effort: 4
Jul-Aug 2026
Leverage the Liò connection for warm intros. Ask Liò to email-introduce the applicant to US faculty in his network — prioritise Marinka Zitnik (Harvard) and Jure Leskovec / Caroline Uhler. One intro line from Liò converts a cold email into an advocate.
For committee-based programs a faculty champion can flag your file; for bio/BMI programs faculty input directly shapes admits. Warm intro >> cold email. This is the applicant's structural edge over other applicants.
effort: 3
Aug-Oct 2026
Lock ONE concrete research thesis: a crisp biological question + geometric-DL method (e.g. sheaf GNNs for single-cell multi-omics integration, or gene-regulatory-network inference), directly extending the IFOM p53 / Liò sheaf work.
This single sentence is the spine of the SoP AND the hook in every faculty email. US SoPs are judged on a specific, credible research vision, not a CV recap. Domain-motivation gate is the same trap as the EU/UK plan.
effort: 15
Aug-Sept 2026
Send tailored pre-contact emails to 2-3 target faculty per school. Each: one paragraph on shared research (cite one of their papers), one on the sheaf-GNN / IFOM work, one concrete idea building on their lab. Short. Track replies.
Signals fit and can surface your file to the committee; for Stanford BMI / Harvard BIG a positive faculty reply materially raises admit odds. Also surfaces which labs are actually taking students in the 2027 cohort.
effort: 10
Sept-Oct 2026
Push a first-author preprint or NeurIPS/ICLR-workshop paper from the IFOM p53 Sheaf-GNN or Liò sheaf work onto arXiv/bioRxiv. Target NeurIPS 2026 workshop deadlines (typically late Sept-early Oct).
The one documented gap is no first-author top-venue output. A public first-author artifact converts 'promising' to 'proven' and is the highest-leverage single move on admit probability at these three schools. Boosts every application even though it formally blocks none.
effort: 60
Oct 2026
Complete the Knight-Hennessy Scholars application — SEPARATE portal, ~mid-October deadline (earlier than every department), nationality-blind, funds any Stanford PhD. Needs its own essays + recommenders. Must ALSO submit the Stanford department application separately.
It is the only fully-portable, internationally-eligible funding lever for the US set (NSF GRFP / Hertz / Soros are all US-only). Separate portal + early Oct wall = the US analogue of the Sanger/Gates trap; missing it is invisible until too late.
effort: 20
Sept-Nov 2026
Order official transcripts + certified English translations from ALL institutions (Bocconi, Humanitas, Pavia, Insubria, Sport Sciences, BSc) + Cambridge verification. Check whether any program requires a WES/course-by-course evaluation and start it early.
Six Italian institutions = slow, bureaucratic, long-lead. Missing/late official transcripts is a silent submission blocker. Independent of essays, so run it in parallel from September.
effort: 10
Oct-Nov 2026
Draft the SoP, then tailor one version per program (~7-8 essays across the 3 schools). Write the separate Personal History / diversity statement required by Harvard and Stanford (first-gen narrative belongs HERE, not in the SoP).
The SoP is the core admission document and each committee wants program-specific fit (named faculty, why-this-program). First-gen is a narrative asset in the personal statement, not a funding key.
effort: 40
Nov 2026
Finalise CV/portfolio, clean and pin GitHub repos (sheaf GNN, IFOM, PyG work) with READMEs, and file fee-waiver requests where offered. Confirm GRE is not required (Stanford CS / MIT EECS / most targets waive it) and drop it as a decision.
Reviewers open GitHub for CS/compbio applicants; a clean portfolio substantiates the skill claims. Fee waivers cut cost and sometimes need lead time. Confirming no-GRE frees ~40h that would otherwise be wasted.
effort: 8
Late Nov - Dec 2026
Submit all department applications before the walls: Harvard BIG / Systems Biology ~Dec 1; MIT CSB ~Dec 1, EECS ~Dec 15; Stanford BMI ~Dec 1, CS ~Dec 2. VERIFY each exact 2026-cycle date when portals open; submit 3-5 days early to absorb portal/transcript failures.
The Dec 1-15 deadline wall is the terminal gate; admission = guaranteed funding, so on-time submission is the whole ballgame. Buffer protects against last-minute transcript/letter/portal breakage.
effort: 10
Can run in parallel:
TOEFL booking + test (Jul-Sept) — indepeRecommender ask + brag sheets (Jul-Aug) Liò warm intros to US faculty (Jul-Aug) Faculty pre-contact emails (Aug-Sept) — First-author preprint / NeurIPS workshopKnight-Hennessy Scholars application (OcTranscripts + certified translations froCV/portfolio + GitHub cleanup + fee waiv
Critical path:
Jul: build target matrix (schools x progAug-Oct: lock ONE concrete research thesOct-Nov: draft + tailor SoP and personalLate Nov-Dec: submit before Dec 1-15 dep

MSCA Doctoral Network (Horizon Europe) Doctoral Candidate position — geometric deep learning / GNNs for computational biology & genomics. Positions from 2025-call networks open autumn 2026; individual host deadlines Dec 2026–Jan 2027.

Jul 2026 (wk 1)
Run a personal MSCA mobility + eligibility audit: build a 36-month residence table (Italy + Cambridge/UK + any secondment) and mark, for every candidate host country, whether you exceed the 12-months-in-36 residence limit at the expected recruitment date. Confirm you are within the first 4 years of research career and hold no doctorate.
MSCA-DN eligibility is per host country, not per person. Cambridge time makes UK-based DC slots (incl. Sanger, Imperial) legally risky. This gates which networks you can even apply to; getting it wrong wastes every downstream effort.
effort: 3
Jul 2026 (wk 1-2)
Meet/email Pietro Liò with one question: is Cambridge (or he personally) a beneficiary/partner in any funded or 2025-call-pending MSCA Doctoral Network in geometric DL / GNNs / comp-bio / genomics — and will he name-flag you to the coordinating and partner PIs when DC posts open? Ask for the network acronyms and coordinator names.
This is the single highest-leverage move. DN DC hiring is fit-driven and semi-informal; an insider PI knows which networks won, when each partner recruits, and can convert you from an unknown applicant to a pre-endorsed one. Liò is your live connection into exactly this topic area.
effort: 3
Jul–Aug 2026
Systematically search CORDIS (cordis.europa.eu) for HORIZON-MSCA-DN projects from the 2023/2024 (funded) and 2025 (results due ~2026) calls filtered on graph neural networks, geometric/topological deep learning, genomics, single-cell, computational oncology. For each, log: acronym, coordinator, beneficiary list, topic, expected recruitment window. Build a ranked target table (10–15 networks).
You do not apply to the EU — you apply to individual beneficiaries once a network is funded. You must first know which networks exist in your niche and who the partners are. CORDIS is the authoritative funded-project database.
effort: 10
Jul 2026 (wk 2)
Set standing EURAXESS alerts (keywords: MSCA Doctoral Network, doctoral candidate, GNN, geometric deep learning, computational biology, genomics) plus alerts on findaphd, and bookmark each shortlisted beneficiary's own jobs portal (each recruits on its own site with its own deadline — e.g. Sanger runs a separate portal with an early-Nov cutoff, not the generic EURAXESS date).
DC vacancies are advertised per-beneficiary with staggered, host-set deadlines and are legally required to be posted on EURAXESS. Missing a portal = missing the only application channel. Sanger-style separate-portal/early-deadline traps are common.
effort: 2
Aug 2026
Convert the IFOM sheaf-GNN p53 work (with Buffa/Tangherloni) into an arXiv + bioRxiv preprint, even as a workshop-length manuscript. Push any Cambridge sheaf/geometric DL result the same way.
Your one gap is no first-author top-venue paper. A citable preprint materially strengthens the CV, is directly on-topic for these networks, and is the kind of concrete output DN selection committees weight. Slow to produce, so start early and run in parallel.
effort: 40
Aug 2026
Build a master academic CV (2-page + long form) and a modular research statement with swappable topic paragraphs, foregrounding: 5 degrees with honours, Liò-lab sheaf geometric DL, IFOM p53 SGNN, HPC genomics/transposable elements, and the PyTorch Geometric / scVI / Scanpy stack.
Every DC application reuses a common core; a strong master version lets you tailor fast when staggered deadlines hit in a narrow window. Depends on preprint status being known so publications can be listed correctly.
effort: 12
Sep 2026
Line up 3 referees and brief them now: Pietro Liò (Cambridge), Buffa/Tangherloni (IFOM), and a Bocconi/Humanitas MSc supervisor. Give each your CV, target-network list, and a one-page bullet brief on what to emphasise; confirm they will respond fast to host-triggered reference requests.
MSCA-DN hosts often trigger reference requests on short notice after shortlisting. Pre-briefed referees who know the networks and your framing produce sharper, faster letters. Fully parallelizable with document work.
effort: 4
Sep 2026
Email 5–8 target PIs (prioritising any Liò-endorsed ones) with a short tailored expression of interest: one paragraph on fit to their specific DC project, CV attached, and a direct question on recruitment timing and deadline. Reference the network acronym and DC topic explicitly.
DN hiring rewards demonstrated fit and prior contact; PIs frequently steer strong candidates to apply and flag them internally. Early contact also surfaces exact per-host deadlines before they publish. Depends on the CORDIS shortlist and, ideally, Liò's intros.
effort: 10
Sep–Oct 2026
Assemble and, where required, certify/translate the credentials pack: all degree certificates + diploma supplements, transcripts, proof of English (Europass language passport or certificate), passport/ID. Produce scanned, named, PDF-standardised copies.
Hosts across different EU countries demand certified/translated documents; assembling these under deadline pressure is a classic failure point. Independent of the research track — run fully in parallel.
effort: 10
Oct 2026
For each shortlisted DC position, produce a tailored motivation letter + research-fit statement mapping your sheaf/GNN and genomics experience onto that project's stated objectives and secondment/industrial partner. Keep a per-position tracker (host, portal, deadline, docs required, referees notified).
Generic applications lose in DN selection; explicit project-objective alignment is what committees score. The tracker prevents missing staggered, host-specific deadlines. Depends on CV, shortlist, PI intel, and preprint status.
effort: 20
Oct–Nov 2026
Monitor EURAXESS + beneficiary portals as posts open; immediately confirm the exact deadline and required documents for each, and separate the early-deadline/separate-portal cases (e.g. Sanger, ~Nov) from the Dec–Jan cluster.
Deadlines are host-set and staggered, not a single EU date; the earliest ones (often UK institutes) can close a month before the rest. Catching this early prevents losing a target to a calendar surprise.
effort: 6
Nov 2026
Submit early-deadline applications first (any ~Nov separate-portal hosts), with referees pre-notified for those specific posts.
These close first and use their own portals; batching them first protects against the crunch when the Dec–Jan cluster hits.
effort: 8
Dec 2026 – Jan 2027
Submit the main cluster of tailored applications across shortlisted networks, respecting each host's deadline, and log confirmations. Notify referees per submission.
This is the terminal action the whole plan feeds; volume across multiple eligible, well-matched networks maximises offer probability given per-position competition.
effort: 16
Can run in parallel:
Preprint of IFOM sheaf-GNN p53 work (AugLining up + briefing referees (Sep)Assembling and translating/certifying crSetting up EURAXESS/findaphd alerts and CORDIS network search can proceed while
Critical path:
Mobility/eligibility audit (Jul wk1)Liò conversation on DN membership + warmCORDIS funded-DN shortlist in your nichePI outreach / expression of interest to Per-position tailored motivation + reseaSubmissions on host-set deadlines (Nov e

Shared cross-application prep — proposal, references, English test, CV, statement — for autumn-2026 PhD cycle (Sep-2027 entry). Dream: ELLIS PhD at ETH Zurich with Pietro Liò co-supervision; parallel targets Cambridge, Imperial (President's, supervisor-gated), Wellcome Sanger (27 Nov 2026 wall), EMBL-EBI, Oxford Clarendon, Gates/Harding.

Jul 2026 (wk1)
Lock the ONE concrete biological question + method thesis (e.g. sheaf/geometric GNNs for single-cell p53 / cancer transcriptomics on genomics data) as a 1-paragraph pitch. This is the seed reused across proposal, statement, and every supervisor email.
Every downstream asset (proposal, SoP, supervisor emails) references this. Compbio dry-lab framing is what unlocks nationality-blind funding (EMBL-EBI/Sanger/Cambridge Math Genomics) vs the UKRI home-fee trap.
effort: 8
Jul 2026 (wk1)
Email Pietro Liò to secure explicit informal commitment to CO-SUPERVISE the ELLIS/ETH application, and ask him to name a specific ETH-side ELLIS PI to pair with. Confirm he will also be a referee.
ELLIS is dual-institution; the ETH+Liò pairing is the single biggest differentiator and only works if Liò confirms AND an ETH host PI exists. He is already the applicant's RA supervisor — leverage the standing relationship now, before his autumn load.
effort: 3
Jul 2026 (wk2)
Build a per-program requirements matrix: for each of ELLIS/ETH, Cambridge, Imperial, Sanger, EMBL-EBI, Oxford — capture deadline, #references, proposal length/format, SoP prompt, English-test requirement + WAIVER eligibility, separate-portal quirks. Note Sanger 27 Nov (earliest wall), ELLIS ~31 Oct, EMBL closes mid/late Oct, Gates 3 Dec, Harding 8 Dec.
Prevents late surprises (Sanger's separate portal + Nov deadline; Imperial supervisor-nomination gate). Determines whether an English test is even needed and which sitting date is safe.
effort: 6
Jul 2026 (wk2)
Resolve English-test question: check waiver eligibility (Bocconi MSc taught in English usually satisfies ETH/Cambridge/Imperial medium-of-instruction waiver). If any target refuses the waiver, book IELTS Academic / TOEFL for an early-Sept sitting NOW.
Test slots fill; results take ~13 days and are valid 2 years. Booking now keeps results in hand well before the Nov-Dec wall. A waiver removes ~20h of prep entirely — confirm before spending it.
effort: 2
Jul 2026 (wk3-4)
Draft master academic CV (2-page) + long-form CV: 5 degrees (110L summa x3), Cambridge/Liò RA, IFOM p53-sheaf-GNN work, Pavia HPC genomics, full stack (PyTorch Geometric, Scanpy, scVI, Pyro). Quantify everything.
CV is the most reusable asset — feeds every portal and every referee packet. Doing it early lets referees write specific letters.
effort: 8
Jul 2026 (wk4)
Formally ask 3 referees (Liò — Cambridge; Buffa & Tangherloni — IFOM; a Pavia genomics/HPC supervisor as #3) with deadline list and a request to write. Confirm each says yes in writing.
Referees need 8-12 weeks lead. Sanger (27 Nov) needs letters uploaded early; a late referee is the most common self-inflicted failure. Three distinct angles (geometric DL / cancer-bio modelling / genomics) cover every program's motivation gate.
effort: 3
Aug 2026 (wk1-2)
Write research-proposal v1 (~2-4 pp, modular): abstract, background, gap, aims, methods (sheaf/geometric GNNs on single-cell/genomics), feasibility, references. Structure it so the core is program-agnostic and only the 'fit' paragraph swaps.
The proposal is the long pole and the strongest signal for supervisor-gated routes (Imperial President's, Cambridge, ELLIS). Modular design turns one document into six tailored ones.
effort: 35
Aug 2026 (wk2)
Send referees a briefing packet (final CV, proposal v1, per-referee bullet list of what to emphasize + each program's deadline/portal).
Turns generic letters into targeted ones and lets each referee start early. Removes the referee from the critical path.
effort: 4
Aug 2026 (wk3-4)
Send tailored supervisor/PI emails: ETH ELLIS host (from Liò), Imperial potential supervisors (President's is supervisor-gated — must secure a nomination), Sanger group leaders, EMBL-EBI groups. Attach 1-page proposal summary + CV.
Imperial President's and Sanger/EMBL are supervisor-gated: no nomination = no funding regardless of proposal quality. This is the highest-risk gate and needs weeks of back-and-forth before Nov.
effort: 12
Sep 2026 (wk1)
Sit English test if no waiver (early-Sept slot). Order score reports sent to all target institutions.
Score is a hard admissions gate; sitting in early Sept keeps a buffer for a resit before the Nov-Dec wall if the score misses a threshold.
effort: 6
Sep 2026 (wk1-2)
Incorporate Liò's feedback → proposal v2. Lock the shared 80% core; create the per-program 'fit / why-here' inserts.
Supervisor-reviewed proposal is dramatically stronger and signals genuine co-supervision for ELLIS. v2 is the version everything else tailors from.
effort: 15
Sep 2026 (wk2-3)
Draft master statement of purpose / motivation letter: narrative arc (multidisciplinary path → geometric DL for compbio → specific question → why this lab/program). Include first-gen as narrative colour only, not as a funding claim.
SoP reuses proposal + CV content but adds the 'why me / why now / why here' human layer required by Gates/Harding/Clarendon and central scholarships. First-gen is narrative-only for an international applicant (not a funding key).
effort: 16
Sep 2026 (wk3)
Confirm ETH-side ELLIS host PI locked and Imperial nomination secured; if either is stalling, escalate or reallocate effort. Register on Sanger's SEPARATE application portal and note the 27 Nov hard deadline.
ELLIS ~31 Oct and Sanger 27 Nov are the earliest walls. Sanger's portal is separate from university systems — registering late is a classic miss. Gating decisions made now avoid wasted November tailoring.
effort: 4
Oct 2026 (wk1-2)
Produce tailored proposal + SoP variants per program from the locked core; run each through a critical read (self + one peer/mentor). Nudge referees to upload for the earliest-deadline programs.
Tailoring is fast off a locked core; front-loading the earliest deadlines (ELLIS ~31 Oct, EMBL mid/late Oct) prevents a November pile-up colliding with the Sanger wall.
effort: 18
Oct 2026 (wk3-4)
Submit ELLIS/ETH (~31 Oct) and EMBL-EBI (mid/late Oct). Verify each referee has uploaded.
These are the first hard walls; clearing them frees full attention for the Nov-Dec cluster.
effort: 6
Nov 2026 (wk1-4)
Submit Wellcome Sanger by 27 Nov via its separate portal (proposal + SoP + confirmed supervisor + references). Prep Gates (3 Dec) and Harding (8 Dec) packages.
27 Nov is the single earliest UK wall and uses a distinct portal + earlier referee upload — miss it and Sanger is gone for the cycle.
effort: 10
Dec 2026 - Jan 2027
Submit Cambridge (+ Gates 3 Dec, Harding 8 Dec, CIS/Trust), Oxford (Clarendon auto — apply in DEC field), Imperial (President's with nomination). Final proof-read + referee/score verification per portal.
This is the main deadline cluster; all shared assets are already built and tailored, so December is assembly + submission, not writing.
effort: 14
Can run in parallel:
CV drafting (Jul wk3-4) runs alongside tEnglish test booking/sitting runs fully Referee asks + briefing packet proceed iSupervisor/PI outreach emails (Aug) run Statement of purpose drafting overlaps pPer-program proposal and SoP tailoring vSanger separate-portal registration is i
Critical path:
Jul wk1: Lock the concrete biological quJul wk1: Secure Liò co-supervision commiAug wk1-2: Research proposal v1 (35h lonSep wk1-2: Proposal v2 after Liò feedbacSep wk2-3: Master statement of purpose oOct wk1-2: Per-program tailored proposalOct wk3-4: First-wall submissions (ELLISNov: Sanger submission by 27 Nov (separa

Timeline — Jul 2026 → Feb 2027

Month-by-month plan to the December–January deadline wall.

Jul-Aug 2026

  • Lock concrete biological question
  • Start supervisor/PI emails for EMBL, Sanger, CCAIM, Math Genomics
  • Register on EMBL PhD portal when it opens mid-Aug
  • Lock ONE concrete biological/computational question (dry-lab) as your research pitch spine
  • Draft master supervisor-outreach email + 1-page research summary + CV
  • Build target list: Oxford/Cambridge/Imperial + EMBL-EBI/Sanger/CCAIM/Cambridge Math Genomics supervisors
  • Send first wave of supervisor emails (5-10) to Cambridge/Oxford/Imperial + Sanger/EMBL-EBI PIs
  • Request 3 referees now; brief them on timeline (Sanger 27 Nov is the wall)
  • Draft core SoP
  • Identify + email prospective supervisors (Imperial nomination requires this earliest)
  • Confirm referees
  • Write Gates Cambridge extra statement + gather leadership evidence
  • Secure Imperial supervisor nomination commitment
  • Finalise scheme-specific statements
  • Brief referees on Gates leadership + submit reference requests
  • Submit Imperial application ahead of internal nomination deadline
  • Polish Cambridge + Oxford applications
  • Submit Cambridge Postgraduate Application (Gates/Trust/Harding) by course-with-funding deadline
  • Submit Oxford application with Clarendon box ticked
  • Watch for and download the Fulbright Italy bando when published
  • Take/refresh TOEFL or IELTS
  • Build target list of guaranteed-funding US departments and identify prospective advisors
  • Email each target dept/grad-admissions office to confirm English-test exemption status for your specific Italian degree (mark VERIFY items)
  • Confirm GRE requirement per US program; confirm ATAS applicability per UK programme CAH code
  • If no English waiver: book IELTS Academic (UKVI) / TOEFL iBT for ~June-July sitting
  • If any US program requires GRE: book GRE General
  • Order certified English translations of transcripts/degree (sworn translation)
  • Sit English test; order extra score reports to all target schools
  • Finalize referee list; send each referee brief + CV + deadline table
  • Referees submit letters via portals/Interfolio ahead of autumn 2026 deadlines
  • Ensure certified documents + Diploma Supplement uploaded per application
  • On admission: request CAS (UK) / I-20 or DS-2019 (US) / employment offer (ETH/EPFL/KI)
  • Apply for ATAS certificate (UK STEM) 1-3 months before start
  • Apply for UK Student visa + pay IHS (or US F-1/J-1 + SEVIS, or CH/SE residence permit)
  • Complete pre-enrolment document checks for Sept 2027 entry

Aug-Sep 2026

  • Email prospective US advisors to confirm they take students for Sept 2027
  • Draft Knight-Hennessy essays + record video assessment
  • Draft base SOP and personal history statement
  • Prepare Fulbright application materials per the bando
  • Second wave supervisor emails; follow up wave-1 non-responders after ~10 days
  • Convert positive replies into calls/meetings; refine question with interested PIs
  • Monitor EMBL-EBI/EIPP portal — opens mid-Aug; start application shell
  • Draft reusable personal statement / research proposal master version
  • Draft CV to Cambridge/Oxford academic format; get supervisor feedback

Sep-Oct 2026

  • Finalise EMBL group ranking and submit before mid/late-Oct close
  • Draft and refine research statements for each portal
  • Lock at least 1-2 committed supervisors willing to support/co-develop proposals
  • Finalize EMBL-EBI/EIPP application (portal closes mid/late Oct) — near-complete draft
  • Confirm all 3 referees have portal access and draft letters underway
  • Prepare ELLIS PhD application (route, not funder) — deadline ~31 Oct
  • Begin tailoring proposal per institution (Cambridge/Oxford/Imperial)

October 2026

  • Submit Knight-Hennessy application by its ~mid-October deadline (separate portal)
  • Submit Fulbright Italy application by the bando deadline
  • Confirm recommenders have KH + Fulbright letters in
  • SUBMIT EMBL-EBI/EIPP before mid/late-Oct close
  • SUBMIT ELLIS application by ~31 Oct
  • Begin Cambridge/Oxford course applications (Clarendon = auto via Oxford Dec field; tick funding boxes)
  • Start Wellcome Sanger 4yr application (deadline 27 Nov) — earliest UK wall
  • Confirm Imperial President's supervisor sponsorship in writing
  • Line up Cambridge Trust/CIS + Harding + Gates Cambridge sub-forms

Nov 2026

  • Submit Wellcome Sanger 4-yr application by HARD 27 Nov deadline
  • Ensure referees submit Sanger letters
  • Submit per-department guaranteed-funding US PhD applications (deadlines Dec 1 - Jan 15)
  • Send official TOEFL/transcripts to each program
  • Submit only GRE where still required
  • Ensure all recommender portals are completed per program
  • SUBMIT Wellcome Sanger by 27 Nov (hard deadline, earliest)
  • Finalize Cambridge PhD application + Gates Cambridge (intl 3 Dec) + Harding (8 Dec) components
  • Finalize Oxford application with Clarendon field ticked (Dec deadline)
  • Confirm all referee letters submitted for Sanger and Cambridge/Oxford
  • Prep Cambridge Wellcome Math Genomics + CCAIM (verify 2027 cohort open)

Dec 2026

  • Submit CCAIM initial form and Cambridge Math Genomics programme-specific step
  • Confirm exact programme deadlines
  • SUBMIT Gates Cambridge intl by 3 Dec
  • SUBMIT Harding by 8 Dec
  • SUBMIT Oxford + Clarendon (December field deadline)
  • SUBMIT Cambridge Trust/CIS + Cambridge Math Genomics + CCAIM
  • Confirm every submission received; screenshot confirmations
  • Rest / prep interview talking points for early-cycle responses

Jan-Mar 2027

  • Attend EMBL interview symposium, Sanger interviews, CCAIM and Math Genomics interviews as shortlisted
  • Attend Knight-Hennessy finalist interview if invited
  • Attend Fulbright Commission interview if shortlisted
  • Respond to department interview requests
  • Backstop applications: MSCA-linked positions, Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds, Open Phil AI
  • Apply ETH/EPFL/Karolinska salaried PhD positions (rolling)
  • Prep US applications where cycle still open + Knight-Hennessy planning
  • INPS dottorato estero check (if parent public-sector) + regional bank foundation stackers
  • Begin interview prep for UK shortlists

Feb 2027

  • Attend Sanger/Cambridge/Oxford/EMBL interviews
  • Continue ETH/EPFL/KI direct-position outreach and applications
  • Submit remaining US / Knight-Hennessy / guaranteed-funding programs
  • Track decisions; keep supervisors warm; negotiate offers
  • Consolidate funding stack: confirm full-funding coverage before accepting

Fallback / safety class

Explicit safety net: high-prestige but statistically more accessible than Oxbridge/top-US, Italian/EU-eligible and funded/paid — routes that still ladder you toward a Stanford postdoc. ★ best fit = top matches for your profile · verify = confirm one detail first. Not primary targets — insurance.

Cohere Labs Scholars Program↗8-15% odds

Cohere (Cohere Labs, formerly Cohere For AI) · Remote-first, global (optional in-person at Cohere hubs)
industry-researchhigh prestigemore-accessibleEU: yesverify
Pay: "Competitive" full-time salary (not publicly disclosed in exact figures), plus health benefits and weekly stipends; industry sources suggest roughly $
Ladders to: Cohere Labs research staff, or a published NLP/ML paper + reference that materially strengthens a subsequent PhD/postdoc application; alumni have gone on to Sta
Explicitly remote-first, explicitly does NOT require relocation or visa sponsorship (so EU-based the applicant can do this without any immigration friction), does not require prior publications, and is genuinely one of the most accessible elite-adjacent industry research programs goin
verify: Real, remote-friendly, paid (8-month, full-time, health/dental + stipends), and explicitly does NOT require prior publications — genuinely more accessible than
Open↗

OpenAI Residency↗2-5% odds

OpenAI · San Francisco, USA (in-person required)
industry-researchvery-high prestigenoEU: checkverify
Pay: ~$210,000-220,000/year (reported $18,333/month), plus relocation assistance
Ladders to: Full-time OpenAI research role, or an extremely strong industry credential for any subsequent Stanford/top-lab postdoc or PhD application
Flagging as NOT a good fit despite the prestige/pay: it is US-based, in-person only in San Francisco, and OpenAI's own hiring info sessions state visa sponsorship support is for transfers/OPT/O-1 conversions during the program but green-card-track sponsorship (needed for a f
verify: Real, well-paid ($18,333/month = ~$220k/yr), 6-month, explicitly credential-agnostic (looks for learning velocity/originality over formal PhD), and OpenAI has a
Open↗

Microsoft Research Cambridge Residency↗12-20% odds

Microsoft Research · Cambridge, UK
industry-researchhigh prestigemoderateEU: yes★ best fit
Pay: Not publicly disclosed; UK MSR research roles typically £45-65k/year equivalent for residency-level positions plus visa sponsorship
Ladders to: MSR Cambridge full-time Researcher role, or a UK-based reference/publication record that helps a subsequent Cambridge/Oxbridge PhD application (genuinely useful
Directly relevant and underrated given the applicant's existing Cambridge foothold in Pietro Liò's lab: MSR Cambridge is a short commute/relationship away, sponsors UK skilled-worker visas routinely for EU citizens post-Brexit, is meaningfully more accessible than a fully-fu
Open↗

Isomorphic Labs — Research Scientist (Machine Learning), London or Lausanne↗4-9% odds

Isomorphic Labs (Alphabet subsidiary, DeepMind-spinout drug discovery) · London, UK or Lausanne, Switzerland
industry-researchvery-high prestigemoderateEU: yes★ best fitverify
Pay: ~£90k-160k+ GBP (London) depending on level; Lausanne comparable in CHF; equity/bonus common at this stage of company
Ladders to: Direct pedigree line to DeepMind-adjacent research + AlphaFold-descendant systems; strong springboard to a Stanford/CS-bio postdoc or a full DeepMind Research S
Best-fit fallback: geometric DL + drug discovery is literally the job description; London hires EU nationals under UK Skilled Worker visa (no lottery unlike US H1B); Lausanne office sits inside Swiss/EU labor market. Bar is real (PhD-level publication record often expected for Re
verify: Thematically excellent fit (DeepMind-spinout, AlphaFold-lineage drug discovery, computational biology + ML — closer to his sheaf-GNN/genomics profile than any o
Open↗

Microsoft Research Cambridge — Postdoc / AI Residency (Cambridge Residency Programme)↗14-22% odds

Microsoft Research Cambridge, UK · Cambridge, UK
industry-researchvery-high prestigemore-accessibleEU: yes★ best fit
Pay: ~£45k-65k GBP/year (UK postdoc/residency band), full benefits, 2-year contract
Ladders to: MSR Cambridge → Stanford/Oxbridge postdoc or faculty pipeline is well-trodden (MSRC has strong alumni network into top CS/ML faculty and industry-lab leadership
Directly in the applicant's existing Cambridge network (adjacent to Liò's lab) — huge advantage since MSRC hires heavily via researcher connections/referrals, not blind admissions. UK sponsors Skilled Worker visas routinely for MSR. Explicitly framed as a stepping stone into
Open↗

Google DeepMind Student Researcher / Research Engineer track (via published pathways)↗3-7% odds

Google DeepMind · London, UK
industry-researchvery-high prestigecheckEU: check
Pay: Full Research Engineer roles ~£100k-180k+ GBP; Student Researcher program is a paid part-time/PhD-adjacent placement, not a fallback salary role by itself
Ladders to: Gold-standard prestige; DeepMind alumni move directly into Stanford/CMU faculty or senior industry research roles
Flagging honestly as a stretch, not a true 'safety': DeepMind Research Engineer/Scientist full-time hiring is arguably MORE competitive than an Oxbridge PhD admit, not less — so this doesn't satisfy the 'more accessible than Oxbridge' requirement. Include
Open↗

Recursion Pharmaceuticals — Machine Learning Scientist (Valence/Mila ML hub)↗6-12% odds

Recursion Pharmaceuticals · Montreal or Toronto, Canada (ML hub, ex-Valence/Mila-affiliated)
industry-researchhigh prestigemore-accessibleEU: check★ best fitverify
Pay: ~CAD $110k-160k for ML Scientist level, biotech-typical equity on top
Ladders to: Mila-adjacent GNN/chemistry work + published benchmarks (GNN-Suite-style work) build a first-author record that can support a later Stanford-affiliated postdoc
Not EU/Schengen — Canada, not the EU — so does not satisfy the strict EU-eligibility ask, flagging clearly. Included because Canadian PR/work-permit pathways (Express Entry, LMIA-backed sponsorship) are dramatically more accessible than US H1B lottery for an EU citizen, GNN-for-c
verify: Real, well-paid industry research job (base + bonus + equity) at Valence Labs, with EU-adjacent hub in London plus Montreal, tied to Mila. Company has an active
Open↗

Genentech gRED / Prescient Design — Machine Learning Research Scientist, AI for Drug Discovery↗2-6% odds

Genentech (Roche) — gRED Computational Sciences, Prescient Design · South San Francisco, CA / New York, NY (US only)
industry-researchhigh prestigemoderateEU: no
Pay: ~$140k-220k+ USD base for Scientist/Senior Scientist, strong biotech bonus/RSU stack
Ladders to: Prescient Design is a legitimate publication-generating industry lab (geometric DL, structure-based generative models) — good springboard to a computational-bio
Explicitly flagging as NOT EU-eligible in practice: US-only roles, H1B-lottery-dependent (Genentech does sponsor H1B, ~130/year approved, but it is a lottery with real rejection risk each year, not guaranteed sponsorship). Role and pay are excellent and directly on-thesis (geomet
Open↗

IMPRS-IS (International Max Planck Research School for Intelligent Systems) — Tübingen/Stuttgart↗12-20% odds

MPI for Intelligent Systems + Uni Tübingen + Uni Stuttgart · Tübingen / Stuttgart, Germany
phdhigh prestigemore-accessibleEU: yes
Pay: Employment contract at ~65% TVöD E13 (some tracks 75%); net roughly €1,900-2,400/month depending on tax class, full benefits (health insurance, pension contribu
Ladders to: Feeds directly into ELLIS/CLS postdocs, and MPI-IS/Tübingen alumni have gone on to Stanford, MIT, Berkeley postdocs and faculty jobs — this is the standard pipe
Open↗

ELLIS PhD Program — central call, Tübingen unit↗8-14% odds

ELLIS (European Laboratory for Learning and Intelligent Systems) · Multiple EU sites, Tübingen is the flagship German unit
phdvery-high prestigecheckEU: yes★ best fit
Pay: ELLIS itself does not pay directly — it's a co-supervision meta-program layered on top of a funding PhD (typically IMPRS-IS, CLS, or a university contract)
Ladders to: Explicitly designed to mirror the Oxbridge/Stanford pedigree — ELLIS PhDs co-supervised across two top EU labs are a known feeder into Google DeepMind, Meta FAI

This IS the concrete Stanford postdoc landing spot to target 2-3 years from now, not a fallback to apply to today. Requires PhD in hand, at most 2 years prior postdoc experience, and a host faculty member's pre-agreement before the committee will even consider the applicatio
Open↗

Meta AI Residency Program (1-year paid research residency, pre-PhD or PhD-alternative)↗3-8% odds

Meta AI (FAIR) · USA (primarily), with some international postings historically
industry-researchhigh prestigemore-accessibleEU: check
Pay: Competitive full-time salary (industry AI research scale, historically reported in the ~USD 90,000-130,000/yr range for similar residencies, not officially disc
Ladders to: Meta AI Residency alumni have gone on to top PhD programs (Stanford, MIT, Berkeley, CMU) and directly to industry research roles; explicitly marketed as grad-sc
Explicitly designed as pre-PhD/pre-grad-school preparation: produces NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR-caliber first-author work in 12 months with a FAIR mentor, which directly patches the applicant's current gap (no first-author top-venue paper). Selection is merit/interview-based rather t
Open↗

Google DeepMind Student Researcher Program (part-time/PhD-embedded, not a standalone track)↗6-12% odds

Google DeepMind · London, Zurich, or other DeepMind offices
industry-researchvery-high prestigecheckEU: check
Pay: Paid role, hourly/contract rate not publicly disclosed; reports suggest competitive with full-time entry research salaries pro-rated
Ladders to: DeepMind co-authored papers and staff references substantially strengthen a Stanford Science Fellows or HAI-adjacent postdoc application.
Requires current enrollment in a PhD program (12-24 week in-person commitment), so this is a boost to layer ON TOP OF one of the funded PhDs above (e.g. CLS or EPFL) rather than a standalone fallback -- best used once enrolled at ETH/EPFL/Helmholtz to add a DeepMind co-authorship
Open↗

Konrad Zuse School of Excellence in Reliable AI (relAI) — PhD Program↗22-35% odds

TU Munich + LMU Munich, DAAD-funded, ELLIS-affiliated · Munich, Germany
phdhigh prestigemoderate vs Oxbridge/top-USEU: yes
Pay: Full TV-L E13 German public-sector salary (~€3,200-3,800/month gross before tax, roughly €43-50k/year), 3 years, plus travel/conference grants
Ladders to: ELLIS network position -> ELLIS PhD co-supervision track -> postdoc at ETH/MPI/Stanford via faculty connections
Actual employment contract (not a stipend), directly ELLIS-network-adjacent, geometric DL / GNN faculty present at TUM, statistically far more admits than the ELLIS PhD program itself (~121 slots/4500 applicants) since it is one school's own intake rather than a pan-European
Open↗

Max Planck ETH Center for Learning Systems (CLS) PhD Fellowship↗15-25% odds

Max Planck Society + ETH Zurich · Zurich / Tübingen / Stuttgart / Saarbrücken, Switzerland-Germany
phdhigh prestigemoderate vs Oxbridge/top-USEU: yes★ best fit
Pay: Fully funded via Max Planck support contract + ETH employment contract; Swiss PhD salaries ~CHF 75-85k/year gross (among the highest PhD pay in Europe)
Ladders to: Co-supervised by ETH + MPI faculty -> direct pipeline into ELLIS Institute Tübingen and Stanford/CMU postdoc network (many CLS alumni go to top-US postdocs)
This is your single best fallback: same ETH ecosystem as your ELIAS/Liò dream track, real salaried Swiss contract (not a stipend), smaller globally-scattered applicant pool than the flagship ELLIS PhD call, and MPI-Tübingen has direct GNN/geometric-ML groups.
Open↗

Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds (BIF) PhD Fellowship↗12-20% odds

Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds (private German foundation) · Any strong biomedical lab worldwide (frequently used for Europe/US labs)
fellowshiphigh prestigemore-accessible/moderate vs Oxbridge/top-USEU: yesverify
Pay: ~€1,650/month base + €150/month research allowance + country premium (up to €1,600/month, e.g. Switzerland +€1,600, USA +€1,175) — so ~€2,100-3,250/month depend
Ladders to: Explicitly funds PhD work in top international labs including US destinations — a legitimate direct bridge into a Stanford-affiliated lab stipend if you secure
This is one of the few EU-based fellowships that will pay you to do your PhD in a US lab (e.g., Stanford-adjacent computational biology/genomics group) — i.e., it can literally BE your ladder to Stanford, not just lead to one. Selective but a much smaller, less winner-take-all ap
verify: Real, funds EU and non-EU citizens doing experimental basic biomedical PhD research anywhere in the world (so it could in principle fund a PhD at ETH/Cambridge/
Open↗

MSCA Doctoral Networks (DN) — as a recruited Doctoral Candidate↗20-32% odds

European Commission / Horizon Europe · Consortium-dependent, across multiple EU countries incl. Italy/Germany/Netherlands/Switzerland (associated)
phdhigh prestigemore-accessible/moderate vs Oxbridge/top-USEU: yes
Pay: EU living allowance ~€3,400/month (country-corrected) + mobility allowance (~€600/month) + family allowance if applicable — full employment contract, among the
Ladders to: Structured intersectoral/international secondments (often including non-EU partners) -> strong CV signal for ERC/EMBO next step; some DNs include a US or Sta
Not a single fellowship you apply to broadly — you must find and apply to a SPECIFIC funded doctoral position within an approved MSCA-DN consortium (like applying for an ELLIS PhD, but you search per-project on the MSCA/Euraxess job boards). Strategy: monitor Euraxess for geometr
Open↗

phdvery-high prestigecheckEU: yes

Ladders to: This is literally your dream track (Liò is Cambridge ELLIS unit) — but flagged here to correct scope

Open↗

Compatible professors

Candidate PIs matched to your profile. Confidence chips flag web-verification; always confirm current status + whether they take students before emailing. Set an outreach status on each — it saves in your browser.

Pietro Liò

University of Cambridge · Department of Computer Science and Technology · Computational Biology / AI Group (Liò Lab)
high reply oddsGraph neural networks / geometric deep learningMulti omics and multi scale computational biologyAI for precision/personalised medicineDrug discovery (structural biology)
The single best CS<->bio bridge at Cambridge for this applicant: full CS professor whose entire research programme is ML methods (GNNs, geometric DL) applied to genomics, multi-omics integration, and precision medicine. Ideal for a strong CS/ML background applicant who wants compbio applications without leaving a CS department.
Takes students: yes · high confidence
Funding: Large, well-established group (dozens of PhD students/postdocs historically); part of CCAIM and ELLIS network; typical funding via CS department studentships, EPSRC, and industry/charity partnerships (has supervised students on Cancer Research UK and multi-omics-funded projects).
Approach: Email directly with a short research statement referencing specific recent papers (e.g. on GNNs for multi-omics or drug discovery); he is noted as actively welcoming PhD enquiries. Apply via Cambridge CS PhD admissions naming him as supervisor.
Project seed: Graph neural network framework integrating multi-omics (genomic, transcriptomic, imaging) data for patient-specific disease trajectory prediction.
homepage✓

Mihaela van der Schaar

University of Cambridge · Department of Applied Mathematics and Theoretical Physics · van der Schaar Lab
medium reply oddsMachine learning for medicineAI for scientific discoveryHuman AI alignmentReinforcement/inverse reinforcement learning
Extremely strong for a CS-core applicant: explicitly states no biology/medicine background required, only strong ML/maths foundation. Large, extremely well-funded, high-profile group bridging core ML methodology to biomedical applications — ideal fallback/parallel option if pure compbio labs are too wet-lab-adjacent.
Takes students: yes · high confidence
Funding: Actively recruiting (publicly announced 4 new fully-funded PhD positions for 2026 entry, open to home and international students); very well resourced lab (ERC and multiple industry/foundation grants; directs CCAIM).
Approach: Apply directly through the lab's official 'Join the Lab' application portal (rolling review) rather than cold email; reference specific thrust area (e.g. foundational models or AI for scientific discovery) in the application.
Project seed: Foundation model for longitudinal multi-omic/clinical time-series data enabling counterfactual treatment-response prediction.
homepage↗

Mohammad (Mo) Lotfollahi

University of Cambridge · Wellcome Sanger Institute · Lotfollahi Group
unknown reply oddsDeep learning for single cell/spatial transcriptomicsPerturbation modellingAI driven cell engineeringDrug discovery from omics data
Strong CS-core-to-bio fit: PhD trained under Fabian Theis (a top computational biology ML lab), builds deep learning architectures (biologically-informed autoencoders, foundation models for single-cell) — very close to the applicant's interest in ML methods applied to genomics/single-cell biology.
Takes students: likely · high confidence
Funding: Sanger Institute group leaders are typically fully funded via Wellcome Trust core funding; newer/rapidly growing group (recently started, actively building team per lab site description of a diverse multidisciplinary team).
Approach: Check Sanger Institute PhD programme (Sanger runs its own funded PhD studentship scheme with Cambridge) and email directly; also reachable via CCAIM associate faculty channel.
Project seed: Biologically-structured deep generative model predicting transcriptional response to unseen genetic/chemical perturbations, extending prior pathway-informed causal representation work.
homepage✓

Neil D. Lawrence

University of Cambridge · Department of Computer Science and Technology — DeepMind Professor of Machine Learning · Machine Learning Group affiliation (CST); personal research programme
medium reply oddsProbabilistic machine learning / Gaussian processesComputational biologyPersonalised health / digital health dataData centric AI for science
High-profile CS-core ML professor (former Amazon Research Cambridge director) with explicit, longstanding stated focus on personalised health and computational biology applications — strong for an applicant with heavier CS/ML skills who wants a foundational-ML supervisor with genuine biomedical application interest.
Takes students: likely · medium confidence
Funding: Well-resourced chair (DeepMind-endowed professorship); CST PhD studentships and access to major funding bodies; also affiliated with Cambridge's broader AI-for-health ecosystem (CCAIM, LCFI).
Approach: Email with a concrete proposal tying probabilistic ML/Gaussian processes to a genomics or health-data problem; also check CST PhD admissions cycle and his personal blog/homepage (inverseprobability.com) for current openings.
Project seed: Uncertainty-aware Gaussian process / deep probabilistic model for integrating sparse longitudinal biomedical data with genomic covariates.
homepage✓

Guillaume Hennequin

University of Cambridge · Department of Engineering — Computational and Biological Learning Lab · Neural Dynamics and Control Group
medium reply oddsComputational neuroscienceTheoretical neural dynamicsMachine learning for neural population dataMotor control modelling
Strong computational-neuroscience fit for the applicant's stated interest in computational neuroscience; heavily mathematical/ML-methods-driven (dynamical systems, optimization) rather than wet-lab, matching a CS-core skill set well.
Takes students: yes · high confidence
Funding: CBL runs a joint PhD admissions process (with Máté Lengyel's group) offering competitive fully-funded 3-year studentships open to all nationalities; well-established, funded lab within Department of Engineering.
Approach: Apply via the joint CBL PhD admissions process (Hennequin/Lengyel), submitting a statement of research interest indicating group preference; shortlisted candidates give a research talk.
Project seed: Recurrent neural network models of cortical dynamics constrained by large-scale neural recordings, linking theory to data-driven inference methods.
homepage✓

Máté Lengyel

University of Cambridge · Department of Engineering — Computational and Biological Learning Lab · Bayesian and Learning Group (Lengyel Group)
medium reply oddsComputational/theoretical neuroscienceBayesian models of learning and inferenceNeural codingStatistical machine learning
Core computational neuroscience PI with a strongly Bayesian-statistics/ML-methodological orientation, well suited to a computer-science trained applicant seeking rigorous theoretical neuroscience work rather than experimental neuroscience.
Takes students: yes · high confidence
Funding: Same joint CBL funded studentship scheme as Hennequin group; fully-funded 3-year positions, open to international applicants.
Approach: Apply through CBL's joint BLG PhD admissions page; contact m.lengyel@eng.cam.ac.uk with statement of interest specifying group preference.
Project seed: Bayesian generative models of hippocampal/cortical circuits benchmarked against large-scale electrophysiology datasets.
homepage✓

Florian Markowetz

University of Cambridge · Cancer Research UK Cambridge Institute · Markowetz Group
medium reply oddsComputational oncologyMachine learning for cancer evolutionChromosomal instability modellingMulti omics and image analysis integration
Strong dry-lab computational biology fit: PhD in computational biology, group is explicitly computational (ML + statistics) working closely with experimental cancer genomics collaborators — good for an applicant wanting genuine biomedical/cancer-genomics grounding with a CS-adjacent PhD training background himself.
Takes students: likely · high confidence
Funding: Senior Group Leader at CRUK Cambridge Institute — funded by Cancer Research UK core programme grants; also holds a Royal Society Wolfson Research Merit Award; co-founder of a genomics spinout (Tailor Bio), indicating strong translational funding base.
Approach: Email directly referencing specific papers on cancer evolution/chromosomal instability ML models; check CRUK-CI PhD programme (separate funded studentship competition) alongside standard Cambridge PhD application.
Project seed: Machine learning model integrating single-cell sequencing and histology imaging to predict chromosomal-instability-driven treatment resistance.
homepage✓

Gos Micklem

University of Cambridge · Department of Genetics — Professor of Computational and Molecular Biology · Micklem Lab / Bioinformatics and Genomics Group
unknown reply oddsIntegrative genomicsBioinformatics data infrastructure (InterMine)Functional genomicsSynthetic biology
Good fit for an applicant wanting a computationally-driven genomics lab embedded in a biology department (rather than CS) — heavy software/data-integration engineering focus (large open-source bioinformatics tools) suits strong CS skills applied directly to genomics.
Takes students: likely · medium confidence
Funding: Group historically funded by Wellcome Trust, EPSRC, NIH and BBSRC over two decades of continuous large-scale collaborative funding; directs the Cambridge Computational Biology Institute, indicating institutional stability.
Approach: Email directly outlining interest in genomic data integration/software tools (e.g. InterMine); note this route requires biology-department PhD application (Genetics) rather than CS — check co-supervision options if applicant wants to remain CS-affiliated.
Project seed: Scalable data-integration framework applying ML-based entity resolution across multi-species functional genomics datasets.
homepage✓

José Miguel Hernández-Lobato

University of Cambridge · Department of Engineering · Machine Learning Group (CBL-adjacent)
medium reply oddsBayesian deep learningDeep generative modelsMolecular machine learningOptimization for chemistry/materials
Bridges ML and molecular/chemical sciences with strong generative-model and probabilistic focus. Good fit for the applicant's PyTorch/Pyro/generative-model skills; leans molecules over genomics.
Takes students: yes · high confidence
Project seed: Geometric generative models over molecular/biological graphs with Bayesian uncertainty for active learning in genomics screens.
homepage✓

Mateja Jamnik

University of Cambridge · Department of Computer Science and Technology · Artificial Intelligence Group
medium reply oddsHuman centric AIExplainable MLML for healthcareNeuro symbolic / concept based models
Same department/group as Liò; works on interpretable ML and health applications. A Liò connection carries real weight here. Fit is methodological (explainability) more than genomics-specific.
Takes students: yes · medium confidence
Project seed: Concept-based / interpretable graph models for clinically actionable multi-omics predictions.

Nicholas D. Lane

University of Cambridge · Department of Computer Science and Technology · Machine Learning Systems Lab (CaMLSys)
medium reply oddsEfficient ML systemsFederated learningOn device/edge MLML for health sensing
Systems/efficiency and federated health-data angle; fits applicant's HPC and physiology (NIRS) experience more than the GNN-genomics core. Secondary target.
Takes students: yes · medium confidence
Project seed: Federated GNN training over distributed hospital genomic/physiological cohorts.

Ferenc Huszár

University of Cambridge · Department of Computer Science and Technology · Artificial Intelligence Group
medium reply oddsMachine learning theoryGenerative modellingCausal representation learningDeep learning foundations
Theory-forward ML in the same group as Liò; good for an applicant wanting stronger theoretical grounding for geometric/generative methods. Not bio-specific.
Takes students: yes · medium confidence

Richard E. Turner

University of Cambridge · Department of Engineering · Machine Learning Group / CBL
medium reply oddsProbabilistic machine learningApproximate inferenceMeta learningML for climate/science
Strong probabilistic-ML methods group; fits applicant's Pyro/scVI probabilistic-modelling skills. Applications are broad rather than genomics-first.
Takes students: yes · medium confidence

homepage✓

Paul D. W. Kirk

University of Cambridge · MRC Biostatistics Unit, University of Cambridge · Kirk Group (Statistical & Machine Learning)
medium reply oddsBayesian machine learningIntegrative clustering of multi omicsStatistical genomicsUnsupervised learning
Bayesian multi-omics integration is a clean bridge between the applicant's scVI/Pyro probabilistic skills and genomics. Strong methodological fit, smaller lab so cold emails land better.
Takes students: yes · medium confidence
Project seed: Bayesian multi-view clustering fused with graph-structured priors across omics layers.
homepage✓

Sarah A. Teichmann

University of Cambridge · Cambridge Stem Cell Institute & Department of Medicine, University of Cambridge · Teichmann Lab
low reply oddsSingle cell genomicsHuman Cell AtlasImmunology/developmentComputational method development for scRNA seq
World-leading single-cell genomics; excellent scientific fit with the applicant's Scanpy/AnnData/scVI stack. But very high-profile and heavily oversubscribed, so a cold email has low odds without an internal referral (a Liò intro could help).
Takes students: yes · medium confidence
Project seed: Graph/geometric deep learning for cell-atlas representation and tissue-architecture inference.
homepage✓

John C. Marioni

University of Cambridge · CRUK Cambridge Institute & EMBL-EBI · Marioni Lab
low reply oddsSingle cell computational biologyStatistical genomicsDevelopmental modellingscRNA seq method development
Excellent computational-genomics fit, but as of 2025 he moved to Genentech and retains only visiting/associate Cambridge appointments — so new PhD supervision capacity at Cambridge is uncertain. Verify before investing effort.
Takes students: uncertain · low confidence
Project seed: Statistical/graph models for single-cell developmental trajectories.
homepage✓

Yarin Gal

University of Oxford · Department of Computer Science · OATML (oatml.cs.ox.ac.uk)
medium reply oddsBayesian deep learningapproximate Bayesian inferenceuncertainty quantificationAI safety/robustness
Core ML/AI theory group but with a strong track record of applying Bayesian deep learning and uncertainty quantification to biomedical and scientific problems (e.g. active learning for molecular/drug discovery, clinical risk prediction). Good fit for a CS-core applicant who wants to build ML depth while keeping a bio-application angle; OATML alumni/collaborations regularly touch genomics and healthcare data.
Takes students: yes · high confidence
Funding: Large, well-funded group; funded via EPSRC/UKRI, industry partnerships (Google DeepMind, etc.), and Oxford CDTs (Fundamentals of AI CDT, AIMS CDT, Health Data Science CDT feed students into OATML). Group explicitly states it mostly takes students through CDTs/central scholarships rather than direct PhD offers.
Approach: Do not email cold asking for a slot — read recent OATML papers (Gal as senior/last author) first, identify a specific technical angle, then apply via one of the CDTs (Fundamentals of AI CDT or AIMS CDT are the main routes) naming Gal/OATML as a preferred group in the application, per the group's own instructions on oatml.cs.ox.ac.uk/apply.html.
Project seed: Uncertainty-aware deep learning for genomic variant effect prediction or clinical time-series risk models, using Bayesian deep learning to flag low-confidence predictions for expert review.
homepage✓

Christopher Yau

University of Oxford · Big Data Institute · Yau Research Group (cwcyau.github.io)
unknown reply oddsmachine learning for high dimensional molecular/omics datastatistical genomicscancer genomicsAI for precision medicine
Excellent CS<->bio bridge: trained in engineering/statistics (DPhil under Chris Holmes at the EPSRC Life Sciences Interface DTC), now Professor of Artificial Intelligence applying ML/statistical methods directly to molecular disease data (e.g. ovarian cancer genomics). Ideal for a computational applicant wanting rigorous ML methodology grounded in real clinical-genomic problems.
Takes students: yes · high confidence
Funding: Turing AI Fellow — funded by a UKRI/EPSRC Turing AI Acceleration Fellowship; also affiliated with the Alan Turing Institute and BDI infrastructure funding. Actively lists open DPhil projects (e.g. AI-based prognostic classification of high-grade serous ovarian cancer).
Approach: Email directly with a specific idea referencing one of his listed DPhil projects on the NDWRH 'find a project' page or a recent Yau-group paper; mention interest in Turing AI Fellowship-supported work.
Project seed: Deep learning-based integration of multi-omic (transcriptomic + genomic) cancer data for prognostic subtype discovery in gynecological cancers.
homepage↗

Chris Holmes

University of Oxford · Department of Statistics · OxCSML (Computational Statistics and Machine Learning group, csml.stats.ox.ac.uk)
medium reply oddsBayesian statisticsstatistical machine learningstatistical genomicsprecision medicine
One of Oxford's most established CS/stats<->genomics bridges: MRC Programme Leader in Statistical Genomics and Alan Turing Institute Faculty Fellow/Programme Director for Health & Medical Science. Strong fit for someone wanting rigorous probabilistic ML methodology with direct genomic/biomedical application and access to the Turing ecosystem.
Takes students: likely · high confidence
Funding: MRC Programme Leader Grant in Statistical Genomics; Alan Turing Institute Faculty Fellow (Turing Health & Medicine programme director) — well-resourced via MRC and Turing funding streams.
Approach: Approach via the Department of Statistics DPhil programme or the Health Data Science CDT (BDI), citing his MRC statistical genomics programme; email with a concrete proposal on Bayesian ML for genomic/biomedical data.
Project seed: Bayesian nonparametric models for integrating genetic and electronic health record data to improve disease risk stratification.
homepage✓

Jerome Kelleher

University of Oxford · Big Data Institute · Kelleher group — tskit / msprime development (jeromekelleher.net)
unknown reply oddspopulation genomics algorithmsancestral recombination graphscoalescent simulationscalable genomic data structures
Strong CS-core fit: his research is fundamentally algorithms and data-structures work (tskit, msprime) applied to population-scale genomics — ideal for an applicant with computer-science strengths who wants to work on the CS-theory side of computational genomics rather than wet-lab-adjacent bioinformatics.
Takes students: likely · high confidence
Funding: Robertson Fellow (Robertson Foundation-funded fellowship) and independent Group Leader in Biomedical Data Science at BDI; tskit/msprime development has been supported by Wellcome Trust and BBSRC/NHGRI-adjacent grants historically.
Approach: Email with a concrete algorithmic proposal (e.g. scaling ARG inference, new tskit functionality) and reference to specific tskit/msprime papers; also listed as a supervisor on the Oxford Medical Sciences Graduate School site — apply via Health Data Science CDT or DPhil in Genomic Medicine and Statistics naming him as a potential supervisor.
Project seed: Scalable ancestral recombination graph inference methods for biobank-scale (UK Biobank-size) genomic datasets.
homepage↗

Nicola Whiffin

University of Oxford · Big Data Institute and Centre for Human Genetics · Whiffin Lab — Computational Rare Disease Genomics (whiffinlab.org)
unknown reply oddscomputational rare disease geneticsnon coding/regulatory variant interpretationlarge scale genomic data analysis (100,000 Genomes/Genomics England)statistical genomics methods development
Strong bio-computational bridge for an applicant with mathematical genomics interest: group is explicitly computational ("we use code to ask big questions of large genomic datasets"), works on regulatory/non-coding variant effect interpretation — a natural fit for ML/data-science methods applied to real clinical genomics questions.
Takes students: yes · high confidence
Funding: Wellcome Trust and Royal Society Sir Henry Dale Fellowship; 2024 Lister Institute Research Prize and Wellcome Career Development Award — well-funded independent group with multiple funding streams.
Approach: Lab website has an active 'Join us' recruitment page — check for open PhD studentships there first; otherwise email directly with a specific proposal tied to their non-coding variant/rare disease work, or apply via Genomic Medicine and Statistics DPhil / Health Data Science CDT naming her as supervisor.
Project seed: Machine learning models to predict pathogenicity of non-coding/UTR variants in rare neurodevelopmental disease using Genomics England data.
homepage↗

Ana Namburete

University of Oxford · Department of Computer Science · OMNI Lab — Oxford Machine Learning in NeuroImaging (omni.cs.ox.ac.uk)
high reply oddsdeep learning for medical image analysiscomputer visionfetal and neonatal neuroimagingultrasound based brain development quantification
Good CS-core <-> neuro/bio fit: based fully within Computer Science (not a joint bio department), applying deep learning/computer vision methods to developmental neuroimaging — well suited to an applicant strong in CS/ML who wants a computational-neuroscience-adjacent application without needing wet-lab background.
Takes students: likely · medium confidence
Funding: Royal Academy of Engineering Research Fellowship funded her independent group setup; past support from Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, Lundbeck Foundation, and Carlsberg Foundation for related work — group appears well-resourced though a specific current PhD funding line was not confirmed on the profile page checked.
Approach: Email with a specific proposal referencing OMNI Lab publications on fetal/neonatal ultrasound brain analysis; apply via CS DPhil or AIMS CDT naming her as a potential supervisor.
Project seed: Self-supervised deep learning for automated brain age/maturation estimation from low-resource fetal ultrasound.

Peter Minary

University of Oxford · Department of Computer Science · Minary group (cs.ox.ac.uk/people/peter.minary)
unknown reply oddsAI/ML and scientific computing for structural biologyCRISPR/genome editing computational methodsepigenetics3D structural modelling of RNA/DNA/proteins
Strong CS-core fit within a genuinely computational-biology group: methods development (AI/ML, applied statistics, algorithms) applied to genome editing and molecular structure — a good match for a CS-skills-first applicant who wants to do method-driven computational biology rather than pure wet-lab-adjacent bioinformatics.
Takes students: yes · medium confidence
Funding: Active group with nine current doctoral students listed on the group page, indicating solid ongoing funding/capacity, though specific grant sources were not disclosed on the profile checked.
Approach: Group page explicitly welcomes DPhil applicants ("We welcome DPhil students... interested in rigorous method development") — email directly with a proposal aligned to CRISPR/epigenetics/structural modelling methods, and apply via the CS DPhil programme.
Project seed: Machine learning-guided prediction of CRISPR off-target effects using 3D chromatin/structural context.

Rafal Bogacz

University of Oxford · MRC Brain Network Dynamics Unit · Bogacz Group (bndu.ox.ac.uk/groups/bogacz-group)
medium reply oddscomputational neurosciencereinforcement learning and decision making modelsneural mechanisms of learningbrain stimulation optimization (Parkinson's/movement disorders)
Strong computational-neuroscience fit for a CS-core applicant: originally trained in computer science (Wroclaw) before moving into computational/theoretical neuroscience, building mathematical/algorithmic models of brain computation (predictive coding, reinforcement learning) — a natural home for someone wanting rigorous modelling work bridging CS and neuroscience.
Takes students: yes · high confidence
Funding: MRC-funded unit; specific MRC-funded DPhil studentships advertised (e.g. FindAPhD listing for MRC-funded computational neuroscience studentship under his supervision); also received a Wellcome Discovery Award.
Approach: Apply via the DPhil in Neuroscience (1+3) or directly via MRC-funded studentship listings referencing his group; email with a proposal on predictive coding, reinforcement learning models, or neurostimulation optimization.
Project seed: Computational modelling of reinforcement learning deficits and optimized deep-brain-stimulation parameters in Parkinsonian basal ganglia circuits.

Pietro Sormanni

Imperial College London · Department of Chemical Engineering · Sormanni Lab (computational antibody & protein design)
medium reply oddsComputational antibody designProtein designAntibody developability predictionMachine learning / deep learning for proteins
Partial, method-transferable but domain-divergent. Applicant does GNN / geometric-DL for GENOMICS (sheaf GNNs, p53-in-cancer, single-cell). Sormanni does computational ANTIBODY/PROTEIN design and developability — immunology/biophysics, not genomics. The methods overlap is real and strong: geometric deep learning, graph/structure representations, and equivariant/GNN modelling map directly onto antibody and protein structures; his group's flagship work (AbNatiV / AbNatiV2 nativeness & pairing models, developability prediction) is deep-learning-heavy though more sequence/structure-VAE than sheaf-GNN. But the biological target (antibodies/proteins) is a hard pivot away from the applicant's genomics/transcriptomics core. To fit, the applicant must reframe as 'geometric/graph deep learning applied to protein & antibody structure' and lean on the PyTorch/Lightning + geometric-DL stack, not on Scanpy/genomics. His overlap with the applicant's RA supervisor world (both ex-Cambridge, protein/structure ML) is a soft networking advantage but does NOT make the science a natural continuation of the sheaf-GNN-for-genomics thread.
Takes students: Likely yes — runs an interdisciplinary group at Imperial (a PI in a growing, newly-relocated lab), RSC prize winner, actively publishing and hiring. Not explicitly confirmed for a Sept 2027 start; verify open positions/funding on his Imperial group page. · high confidence
Project seed: Equivariant / sheaf-style graph neural network on antibody structural graphs (CDR loops as attributed subgraphs) to jointly predict developability liabilities (aggregation, viscosity, poly-reactivity) and binding — extending the geometric-DL machinery the applicant built for sheaf GNNs from gene-interaction graphs to residue-contact graphs, benchmarked against his AbNatiV2 nativeness scores. Framing bridge: 'geometric deep learning on biological graphs' transferred from genomics to antibody design.

Project seed: Feature-learning theory for message-passing/sheaf GNNs: characterizing the spectral/geometric inductive biases that make graph representations generalize on biological networks.

low reply oddsmulti omics & personalized medicinewearables + omics for healthfunctional genomicslarge scale integrative genomics

homepage↗

Malene Lindholm

Stanford University · School of Medicine — Department of Medicine, Division of Cardiovascular Medicine · Director, Human Molecular Athlete "Moonshot", Wu Tsai Human Performance Alliance; runs the MoTrPAC Bioinformatics Center (no eponymous "Lindholm Lab")
medium reply oddsSkeletal muscle / exercise physiologyMulti omics integration (transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, epigenomics)Genomics of physical activity & cardiometabolic healthGenetics of extreme human performance
Thematic overlap (genomics / large-scale multi-omics), methodological gap. Her group is a bioinformatics-and-integration operation on exercise/muscle data — differential analysis, network/pathway methods, consortium-scale pipelines — not a geometric-deep-learning methods lab. The applicant's sheaf/GNN work is not what she builds, but MoTrPAC's multi-tissue, multi-assay data (19 tissues, 25 assays, 35k+ regulated analytes) is a genuinely strong substrate for GNN/graph integration. He would have to reframe: not "I do geometric DL" but "your multi-omic atlas is a graph, and I can build the model that learns across its tissue-analyte edges." Domain (muscle/cardio-metabolic, not cancer/p53) is a stretch from his IFOM background but adjacent enough. She is a data/genomics fit, a weak DL-methods fit.
Takes students: Unclear / likely not as primary supervisor. She is a Sr. Research Engineer (research staff), not tenure-track faculty — at Stanford Med, PhD students are admitted to and supervised by faculty PIs. Realistic path is co-supervision under a faculty PI (e.g. Euan Ashley / Cardiovascular Institute) with her as day-to-day mentor. · medium confidence
Project seed: Graph-based multi-omic integration of the MoTrPAC atlas: build a heterogeneous GNN (or sheaf-diffusion model) over the multi-tissue, multi-assay endurance/resistance-training data (genes-proteins-metabolites-tissues as typed nodes) to predict tissue-specific and sex-biased training-response modules, propagating signals across the analyte-tissue graph rather than analyzing each omic layer separately. Frame it as "learning the transducer network of exercise as a graph."

medium reply oddsalgorithmic genomicslarge language models for biological sequencessingle cell causal inferencestructural biology ML
The clearest CS-core <-> biology bridge at MIT: a mathematician/computer scientist who builds foundational algorithms for biological problems rather than just applying off-the-shelf ML. Excellent fit for someone with strong CS/algorithms skills and a genuine but still-developing compbio interest — the lab has a long history of training students from pure CS/math backgrounds into computational biologists.
Takes students: yes · high confidence
Funding: Senior, well-funded PI (NIH/NSF); currently advises roughly ten PhD students plus postdocs; part of CSAIL's Computation & Biology group with shared infrastructure and close Broad Institute ties.
Approach: Apply to MIT EECS PhD (list CSBi/HST as secondary if relevant) and name her as a point of contact in the statement of purpose — MIT PhD admissions are centralized, not lab-direct. A brief, specific pre-application email referencing recent lab work (e.g., protein/DNA language models or single-cell causal work) is reasonable and can help.
Project seed: Foundation-model representations of regulatory DNA/protein sequence combined with causal inference over single-cell perturbation data.

Funding: Very large, well-funded lab (NIH, multiple center grants, Broad Institute affiliation); lab website has an active positions/openings page.
