medium reply oddscomputational genomicssingle cell RNA seq methodsRNA biologyalgorithms for sequencing data
The single strongest CS<->bio bridge PI at Caltech: Bren Professor of Computational Biology AND Computing and Mathematical Sciences (dual appointment). Develops rigorous computational/algorithmic methods (kallisto, bustools, etc.) for genomics -- ideal for a CS-core applicant who wants to do methods-driven computational biology/mathematical genomics rather than wet-lab work.
Takes students: likely · high confidence
Funding: Well-established, NIH-funded lab with long track record (large group, many trainees historically); exact current grants not itemized on profile page, but is one of the most senior/resourced PIs in the space.
Approach: Apply via Biology/BBE PhD program (or CMS, given joint appointment) and name him specifically in the statement of purpose; email directly referencing specific tool/paper (e.g. kallisto|bustools, single-cell methods) to gauge current openings.
Project seed: Develop new algorithmic/statistical methods for single-cell or spatial genomics data (e.g. improved pseudoalignment, RNA velocity-adjacent inference) with rigorous CS/stats foundations.
homepage↗

Matt Thomson

Caltech · Division of Biology and Biological Engineering · Thomson Lab / SPEC (Beckman Center for Single Cell Profiling and Engineering)
high reply oddsmachine learning for single cell biologycellular reprogramming/differentiationclosed loop ML guided experimentationsystems biology
Explicitly integrates machine learning, mathematical modeling, and single-cell experiments to build predictive/generative models of cell state and control cellular differentiation -- a near-perfect fit for a CS-core applicant wanting to do computational systems biology with real ML content, not just data analysis.
Takes students: yes · high confidence
Funding: PI/director of SPEC (Beckman Center for Single Cell Profiling and Engineering); Investigator, Heritage Medical Research Institute (HMRI); Packard Fellowship (2019); NIH Transformative R01 (2023). Well-funded, actively growing lab -- lab team page lists ~16 PhD students as of verification.
Approach: Email directly with a specific idea connecting ML methods (e.g. generative models, RL, foundation models) to single-cell/tissue engineering; mention SPEC center. Apply through BBE PhD program (Biology or CNS option).
Project seed: Build closed-loop ML models (active learning/RL) that propose perturbations to steer single-cell differentiation trajectories toward target cell states.

David Van Valen

Caltech · Division of Biology and Biological Engineering · Van Valen Lab
high reply oddsdeep learning for bioimage analysiscell segmentation/trackingspatial genomicscomputational cell biology
Builds deep learning software infrastructure (DeepCell/DeepCell-tf) for quantitative cell biology -- a strong fit for a CS/ML-core applicant since the work is fundamentally about scalable deep learning systems (segmentation, tracking, cloud-native deployment) applied to biological imaging data.
Takes students: yes · high confidence
Funding: Verified funders: Rita Allen Foundation, Susan E. Riley Foundation, Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation, Rothenberg Innovation Initiative (Caltech), NIH Human BioMolecular Atlas Program (HuBMAP), NIH Director's New Innovator Award. Lab site has an active 'Openings' page.
Approach: Check the lab's 'Openings' page directly (vanvalen.caltech.edu/openings), then email with specific interest in DeepCell architecture or live-cell/spatial data pipelines. Apply via BBE PhD program.
Project seed: Develop new deep-learning architectures for multiplexed spatial genomics/proteomics image analysis (segmentation + cell-state inference) at scale.

CS-core ML professor (Professor of Computing and Mathematical Sciences) whose group explicitly applies agents/foundation models/closed-loop experiment design to biomedical imaging, neural data, and protein engineering -- exactly the CS<->bio bridge profile requested, and a very natural home for a strong-CS/compbio-curious applicant.
Takes students: likely · high confidence
Funding: Active, well-established CMS lab with continuous funding and publication record; specific current grants not itemized in verified sources.

Caltech · Electrical Engineering / Computing and Mathematical Sciences · Perona Lab / Visipedia
medium reply oddscomputer visioncomputational ethologybehavior analysis in animalscomputational neuroscience adjacent (behavior quantification)
Pioneer of 'computational ethology' -- using computer vision/ML to automatically quantify animal behavior, working with neuroscience/genetics collaborators (Anderson, Meister, Dickinson) to link genes, brains and behavior. Good fit for someone interested in computational neuroscience via a strong CS/ML lens rather than wet-lab neuroscience.
Takes students: likely · high confidence
Funding: Long-established, senior, well-funded lab (endowed chair); NSF/NIH history typical of this group; specific current grants not itemized in verified sources.
Approach: Apply via CMS or EE PhD program; reference specific Visipedia/behavior-analysis projects (e.g. Animal Kingdom dataset, mouse behavior recognition) in statement of purpose.
Project seed: Self-supervised or weakly-supervised computer vision methods for automated animal behavior phenotyping linked to neural/genetic data.

Anima Anandkumar

Caltech · Computing and Mathematical Sciences · Anima AI + Science Lab (tensorlab.cms.caltech.edu)
medium reply oddsmachine learning theoryneural operatorsAI for scientific applications (incl. genomics, protein function, medical devices)
CS-core ML theorist whose 'AI for Science' program has produced genome-scale foundation models for evolutionary dynamics/protein function (Gordon Bell Special Prize, COVID-19 research) -- real bio-relevant work exists, though her current emphasis has shifted heavily toward physical sciences (weather, fusion, materials) rather than biology, so compbio fit is real but narrower than the other candidates. Best suited to an applicant whose core interest is ML methods with bio as one of several application domains.
Takes students: likely · medium confidence
Funding: Co-founder of Caltech's AI for Science initiative (2018); large, extremely well-resourced group with industry ties (part-time role at NVIDIA as of some sources) and major awards (Time100 Impact Award 2025, IEEE Kiyo Tomiyasu Award 2025).
Approach: Apply via CMS PhD program; in statement of purpose, be explicit that the interest is in genome-scale/biological foundation models specifically (not just physics/climate) since that is a minority of current lab output.
Project seed: Extend neural-operator or genome-scale foundation-model approaches to a new biomedical prediction task (e.g. variant-effect or regulatory-genomics prediction).

Project seed: Geometric deep learning on tissue meshes / physics-informed GNNs for spatio-temporal signalling during organogenesis.

Michael Moor

ETH Zurich · Department of Biosystems Science and Engineering · Moor Lab (Medical AI group)
medium reply oddsMedical foundation modelsMultimodal medical AI (vision language)Medical LLMs / agent systemsClinical time series & EHR modelling
Partial / moderate fit — honest read. Moor does medical AI (clinical foundation models, multimodal medical LLMs, EHR/time-series), NOT geometric-DL-for-genomics. The applicant's core (sheaf-based GNNs, p53/cancer, genomics) is not his current thrust, so a naive "I do sheaf GNNs for genomics" pitch would land as off-topic. HOWEVER there is a real bridge: Moor's own PhD was in Borgwardt's lab on topological data analysis, graph kernels and representation learning, so he understands and values structured/graph/geometric methods. The applicant's IFOM cancer-genomics + HPC + microscopy/NIRS multimodal experience maps onto his multimodality and biomedical-data agenda. Best framed as: "structure-aware / graph-based components inside biomedical foundation models" rather than "GNNs for genomics" in isolation. As a co-supervisor (e.g. ELLIS pairing with a more genomics-GNN-focused PI, or with Lio) the fit is workable; as sole thesis home for pure geometric-DL-genomics it is a stretch.
Takes students: Likely yes (inferred). New tenure-track PI building a group since Sept 2024; standard ETH/ELLIS PhD hiring expected. Not individually confirmed — verify via his site/ELLIS call. · high confidence
Project seed: "Structure-aware biomedical foundation models": inject graph/geometric priors (gene-gene, pathway, or patient-similarity graphs; sheaf/message-passing modules) into a multimodal medical foundation model, e.g. grounding an EHR + molecular/genomics LLM on a knowledge graph so it reasons over pathway structure rather than flat token soup. Concretely: a retrieval-augmented, graph-conditioned model for cancer (leveraging the applicant's p53/IFOM genomics + HPC background) that couples a GNN encoder over regulatory/pathway graphs with Moor's foundation-model / RAG stack, benchmarked on a genomics-to-clinical prediction task. This uses the applicant's held skills (PyTorch/Lightning, Scanpy/AnnData, GNNs) while sitting squarely inside Moor's foundation-model / multimodality agenda.

Maria Brbic

EPFL · School of Life Sciences · MLBio Lab (Machine Learning for Biomedicine / Machine Learning for Biomedical Discovery Laboratory)
unknown reply oddsmachine learning for single cell genomicsgraph neural networksgeometric deep learningcell atlas / spatial transcriptomics
Excellent dry-lab ML/AI + computational biology match. Assistant Professor (tenure-track), joined EPFL Sept 2022 from a Stanford postdoc with Jure Leskovec (Chan Zuckerberg Biohub). Develops novel ML methods (e.g. STELLAR geometric deep learning method) applied to single-cell/spatial genomics and large cell-atlas consortia (HuBMAP, Fly Cell Atlas). Close to an ideal profile for an ML/AI-trained applicant pivoting into computational biology — pure methods-development, no wet lab.
Takes students: Yes — PhD students join via EPFL's EDIC (computer science) or EDCB (computational biology) doctoral programs, admission centrally handled by EPFL. Lab page explicitly states it is 'always looking for highly motivated and talented PhD students.' Source: brbiclab.epfl.ch/join-us/ · [certain] role/lab/focus verified via brbiclab.epfl.ch, people.epfl.ch, sib.swiss, and stanford cs pages (2025-2026 search results) confidence
Funding: Awarded SNSF Starting Grant in 2024 (verified, per EPFL/SIB bio) — indicates active grant funding for PhD positions. Also SIB Early Career Bioinformatics Award (2023). Verified 2025/2026 figures, not estimated.
Approach: Apply through EDCB or EDIC doctoral school; also reasonable to email directly referencing specific papers (e.g. STELLAR, DOGMA) given her Stanford ML background makes an ML-major applicant's fit obvious without translation.

Vassily Hatzimanikatis

EPFL · School of Basic Sciences · Laboratory of Computational Systems Biotechnology (LCSB)
unknown reply oddsgenome scale metabolic modelingsystems biologymetabolic engineeringconstraint based modeling (FBA type approaches)
Solid dry-lab computational fit, though closer to chemical/biological engineering than pure ML/AI — good option if applicant wants a systems-biology/metabolic-modeling angle rather than an ML-methods angle. Associate Professor at EPFL since 2006. Long track record (redHUMAN human metabolic models, genome-scale yeast models) shows heavy computational/bioinformatics focus with essentially no wet-lab component in his own group's core work.
Takes students: Yes — LCSB page lists PhD theses under his direction (multiple students verified via search); likely EDCB or EDBB (Biotechnology and Bioengineering) doctoral program route. · [certain] role and focus verified via epfl.ch/labs/lcsb, people.epfl.ch, pubmed-indexed papers, google scholar (search results current as of 2025/2026) confidence
Funding: No specific current 2025/2026 grant figures found in this pass — [Guessing/estimate] that as an established, long-tenured PI with a continuous SNSF-type funding history he likely has funded slots, but this needs direct verification on lcsb.epfl.ch before relying on it.
Approach: Check LCSB thesis/open-positions page directly (lcsb.epfl.ch) for currently advertised PhD projects before cold-emailing; less ML-forward than Brbic/Bitbol so frame outreach around the systems biology / metabolic network angle if applicant's ML background is the selling point.

Marcel Salathé

EPFL · School of Life Sciences · Digital Epidemiology Lab
unknown reply oddsdigital epidemiologyAI/ML for public healthcomputational modeling of disease dynamicsmobile/web data streams for health monitoring
Good dry-lab fit for an ML/AI-first applicant interested in health/epidemiology rather than molecular computational biology — his lab is explicitly computational/software-based ('In code we trust'). Less 'core computational biology' (no molecular/genomic focus) and more population-health/AI, so profile fit is moderate-to-good depending on how strictly the applicant wants classic comp-bio vs. broader AI-for-health. Associate Professor, prominent public voice during COVID-19 (Swiss National COVID-19 Science Taskforce, DP-3T decentralized contact tracing).
Takes students: Likely yes as an active associate professor, but explicit current PhD openings and doctoral-program affiliation were NOT confirmed in this search pass — mark as [Guessing] and verify via digitalepidemiologylab.org or direct email before treating as a live option. · [certain] role, lab focus, and background verified via epfl.ch/labs/salathelab, wikipedia, usenix/itu speaker bios (current 2025/2026) confidence
Funding: No specific current funding figures found in this pass. [Guessing] that AI Center co-directorship implies access to EPFL AI Center funding streams for PhD students, but unverified — check before relying on it.
Approach: If pursued, verify current lab activity/PhD openings at digitalepidemiologylab.org first (EPFL institutional page had limited detail); position outreach around AI/ML for population health rather than molecular biology given applicant's ML/CS-plus-comp-bio profile.

Bruno Correia

EPFL · School of Engineering · Laboratory of Protein Design and Immunoengineering (LPDI)
unknown reply oddsAI/deep learning for de novo protein designcomputational structural biologyvaccine and cancer immunotherapy protein engineeringprotein protein interaction design
Strong AI-for-biology fit but flagged as hybrid, not pure dry-lab: the lab explicitly combines 'computational design methods development and experimental characterization of the designed proteins' — some wet-lab validation is core to the group's output, though an applicant's own contribution as a computational PhD student would likely be dry-lab (deep learning pipelines, structure prediction/design algorithms). Included for very high current relevance (2023 Nature paper on deep-learning protein-interaction design) and because it is formally in STI not SV — flagging this department mismatch versus the requested 'SV, or SIB-affiliated' criterion; his lab IS SIB-affiliated (sib.swiss/bruno-correia-group), which satisfies the alternate criterion given.
Takes students: Yes, historically active PhD recruiter (long track record of lab publications with student co-authors); current openings not confirmed in this pass — verify on LPDI site. · [certain] role/lab/focus verified via sib.swiss/bruno-correia-group, actu.epfl.ch news articles, and people.epfl.ch (2025/2026 current) confidence
Funding: No specific current 2025/2026 grant figures found in this pass — [Guessing] that high-profile Nature-level output implies strong grant funding (likely SNSF/ERC-type), but unverified.
Approach: Because his lab is a computational+experimental hybrid, an ML/AI applicant should explicitly propose a computational-only or computation-heavy thesis angle (e.g., generative models for protein design) when reaching out, and confirm whether a fully dry-lab PhD track is feasible in this group before committing.

Dimitri Van De Ville

EPFL · School of Engineering · Medical Image Processing Lab (MIPLab)
medium reply oddsgraph signal processingbrain network analysisgeometric deep learning on connectomesneuroimaging (fMRI)
One of the strongest geometric/graph-signal-processing groups for biology at EPFL — spectral graph methods on brain networks. Direct methodological overlap with applicant's geometric DL / GNN focus; comp-neuro bridge. Cold email works best if framed around graph/sheaf signal processing on connectomes.
Takes students: yes — via EDEE/EDNE and Neuro-X · high confidence
Project seed: Cellular-sheaf Laplacian for multi-subject brain-network harmonization: model inter-subject connectome variability as sheaf restriction maps to improve group-level GSP inference.

Project ideas to propose

Concrete PhD proposals across CS/ML and dry-lab computational biology, each rated for appeal, the effort it would take you specifically, and 10-year impact. Time-to-result assumes ~20 h/week of active work with Claude and coding agents assisting. ◆ cross-lab workhorse = a flagship you can pitch to many PIs at once; → DeepMind track = what top industrial labs recruit on.

Calibrated Variant-Effect Foundation Models: Uncertainty-Aware DNA Language Models Grounded in Functional Genomics

Foundation models for genomics / DNA language models
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Current DNA language models (Nucleotide Transformer, Evo, GPN, and AlphaGenome, 2025) predict variant effects but give overconfident, poorly-calibrated scores that are unsafe for clinical rare-disease interpretation. This project builds a DNA foundation model that jointly fits self-supervised sequence pretraining and multi-task functional-genomics supervision (ENCODE/Roadmap epigenomic tracks, MPRA readouts), then adds a Bayesian/deep-ensemble prediction head yielding calibrated per-variant uncertainty benchmarked against ClinVar, gnomAD constraint, and saturation-mutagenesis assays. The output is a variant-effect predictor that knows when it does not know, closing the gap between genomic FMs and actionable clinical variant classification.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.25bang-for-buck
Time to result10.5months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: If he owned decision-ready, calibrated clinical variant interpretation he'd be the go-to name where genomics meets the clinic — but leadership here demands FM-pretraining muscle he hasn't yet built.
Verdict: Strong, fundable and DeepMind-relevant, but pursue the calibration layer on frozen public DNA foundation models rather than pretraining from scratch; pitch to clinical-genomics FM groups and AlphaGenome-adjacent labs.
Why fundable: Directly matches Wellcome/genomic-medicine and rare-disease funder priorities (clinical translation, safe AI); Bayesian-uncertainty framing is a Clarendon/Gates-quality 'trustworthy AI for health' angle; uses only public data so cost is low and deliverables are certain within a 3-4 year PhD.
Fits labs:
Christopher Yau (Oxford, BDI)Chris Holmes (Oxford, Bayesian stat genomics)Nicola Whiffin (Oxford, rare-disease/regulatory variants)Yarin Gal (Oxford, Bayesian DL/uncertainty)Anshul Kundaje (Stanford, regulatory genomics DL)Olga Troyanskaya (Princeton, DL genomics/genotype-phenotype)
Fits funding:
Clarendon (Oxford)Gates CambridgeCambridge TrustEMBL-EBI PhD programme
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • Genuine unmet need: clinical rare-disease variant interpretation is bottlenecked by miscalibrated scores, so calibration is a real deliverable, not a vanity metric
  • Rides the 2025 AlphaGenome/Evo/Nucleotide-Transformer wave — a strong base model exists to bolt calibration and functional-genomics supervision onto rather than pretraining from scratch
  • Uncertainty quantification (conformal/Bayesian) maps directly onto his stated interest and is under-explored in genomics vs raw predictive performance
  • Public grounding data is abundant (ClinVar, gnomAD, ProteinGym, ENCODE/GTEx functional tracks) so the supervision signal is available without wet-lab

Hidden caveats

  • Scoop risk from DeepMind/Google — AlphaGenome team can add uncertainty/calibration in a point release and erase the novelty overnight
  • Pretraining or even large-scale finetuning a DNA LM needs GPU budget a first-year dry-lab RA likely lacks; hidden compute barrier
  • Validation gap: 'safe for clinical interpretation' is very hard to demonstrate convincingly without prospective/wet-lab confirmation, so reviewers may see calibration numbers as insufficient
  • Calibration-as-add-on can be framed as incremental engineering rather than a novel method, hurting first-author top-venue prospects

Gets obsolete if…

  • DeepMind/Google ships a calibrated, uncertainty-aware version of AlphaGenome or a successor with per-variant confidence intervals
  • A benchmark/standardization paper defines variant-effect calibration metrics and reports that existing DNA LMs are already well-calibrated after simple temperature scaling
  • Genomics England / clinical consortium releases an uncertainty-aware clinical variant-effect scorer
  • A conformal-prediction-for-genomics method paper lands claiming to solve calibrated variant effect prediction

Gets more feasible if…

  • Open pretrained AlphaGenome / Evo2 weights or a free inference API is released, removing the pretraining cost
  • A distilled or small DNA-LM checkpoint (runs on a single GPU) drops
  • A public variant-effect benchmark adds calibration/uncertainty metrics (ECE, coverage) as standard
  • A conformal-prediction library with genomics tutorials (e.g. MAPIE-style) ships for sequence models
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Long-Context State-Space Genomic Foundation Models for Cross-Species Regulatory Grammar and Generative cis-Regulatory Design

Foundation models for genomics / DNA language models
→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Transformer DNA models are context-limited and species-narrow, so they miss the long-range and evolutionarily-conserved grammar that governs gene regulation. This project trains a state-space (Mamba/Hyena-style) genomic foundation model on multi-species alignments (Zoonomia 240-mammal) with megabase context, learning regulatory grammar directly from evolutionary constraint, then repurposes it generatively to design and score synthetic cis-regulatory elements (enhancers/promoters) with target cell-type activity. It unifies ultra-long-context representation learning, comparative-genomics conservation signals, and generative regulatory design in one model.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact5/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result20months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Mastering long-context SSM genomic design could make him a founder of generative regulatory genomics; realistically it's the hardest bet on the board for someone with no paper and no formal CS/math.
Verdict: Dream-tier and field-defining but the single least tractable option for him; only viable inside a compute-rich FM team with a co-lead, not a solo PhD given no paper, no CS degree and no PyG.
Why fundable: Hits the 'AI for science / generative biology' priorities of Open Phil, ETH/EPFL salaried positions, and US programmes; efficiency (sub-quadratic long-context) is a strong ML-methods contribution that reads well to CS-heavy committees; generative regulatory design has synthetic-biology and therapeutic-delivery relevance funders reward.
Fits labs:
Bonnie Berger (MIT, algorithmic genomics/LLMs for sequences)Manolis Kellis (MIT, regulatory/comparative genomics)David Gifford (MIT, ML regulatory genomics)Brian Hie (Stanford, genome/protein language models/generative biology)Anima Anandkumar (Caltech, ML theory/neural operators/AI for science)Jerome Kelleher (Oxford, population genomics algorithms)
Fits funding:
Open Philanthropy AI (technical AI for science)Knight-Hennessy (Stanford)ETH/EPFL salaried doctoral positionsKarolinska (Jens Lagergren generative/Bayesian)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~9 mo

Strengths

  • Long-range context and cross-species conservation are real, well-documented failure modes of Transformer DNA models (Enformer/Borzoi context limits)
  • State-space (Mamba/Hyena) architectures are hot and demonstrably enable megabase context, giving a credible technical wedge
  • Zoonomia 240-mammal alignments are a rich, underused, publicly available multi-species signal
  • Generative cis-regulatory design is a rising, high-impact framing (synthetic enhancers/promoters)

Hidden caveats

  • Arc Institute's Evo/Evo2 already occupies exactly this niche (long-context SSM genomic FM) — very high scoop and 'already done' risk
  • Megabase-context multi-species pretraining is extremely compute-intensive; the hidden barrier is the largest of all six projects for a solo RA
  • Generative regulatory element design demands MPRA/wet-lab validation to be credible — a validation gap he cannot close in dry-lab alone
  • Crowded and fast-moving; a first-year without a paper risks being permanently behind the frontier compute race

Gets obsolete if…

  • Arc Institute releases Evo3 or a megabase multi-species genomic FM with regulatory-grammar capability
  • An Enformer/Borzoi successor ships megabase context and cross-species training
  • A published SSM genomic FM demonstrates cross-species regulatory grammar transfer at scale
  • A generative cis-regulatory design benchmark is released and shows near-solved performance

Gets more feasible if…

  • Open Evo2 weights plus finetuning/adaptation code become usable on modest hardware
  • Zoonomia alignment preprocessing tooling or a ready multi-species token dataset is released
  • A Hyena/Mamba-for-genomics tutorial or PyG/HF integration lands
  • Large public MPRA / STARR-seq benchmark datasets for regulatory-element scoring drop
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Causal Counterfactual Perturbation Modeling: Predicting Unseen Combinatorial Genetic and Drug Perturbations in Single Cells

Single-cell transcriptomics deep learning
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Current perturbation models (CPA, scGEN, GEARS, scGPT) interpolate well but fail to extrapolate to unseen combinations and dosages because they entangle perturbation effects with confounded cell state. This project builds a causal generative model that disentangles latent cell-state factors from intervention effects using a structural-causal-model decoder over a single-cell foundation-model embedding, enabling calibrated counterfactual prediction of combinatorial CRISPR and drug perturbations (including higher-order genetic interactions) with out-of-distribution uncertainty. Validation uses existing large Perturb-seq and sci-Plex atlases with held-out combinations, benchmarking OOD generalization and epistasis recovery.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result13months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Cracking identifiable causal extrapolation of perturbations would mark him as the person who moved the field past interpolation, contingent on real causal-inference depth he must build.
Verdict: Pursue if he wants drug-discovery pull; pitch to single-cell plus causal-ML and industrial perturbation labs, but he must first close the Pyro and causal-inference gap.
Why fundable: Directly hits Wellcome Sanger / EMBL-EBI and CCAIM priorities on scalable perturbation biology and 'virtual cell' foundation models; combinatorial perturbation prediction is a named strategic goal of the Human Cell Atlas and pharma partners, giving clear translational and target-discovery framing that funders reward. First-gen + EU eligibility aligns with Gates Cambridge / Cambridge Trust and Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds (mechanism-oriented, computationally rigorous).
Fits labs:
Mo LotfollahiCaroline UhlerJames ZouJure LeskovecFlorian Markowetz
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeCambridge TrustWellcome Sanger PhD ProgrammeEMBL-EBI / EMBL International PhD
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • Extrapolation to unseen perturbation combinations/dosages is a genuinely unsolved, widely-acknowledged limitation (GEARS/CPA/scGPT all interpolate)
  • Causal/structural-causal-model disentanglement is a distinctive angle vs the FM-scaling crowd, with a clear methodological story
  • GEARS-style graph priors mean the problem is naturally a GNN task — direct fit for his PyG/GNN skill-building and Lio's lab
  • Large public perturbation corpora now exist (scPerturb, Tahoe-100M, Replogle) to train and benchmark on

Hidden caveats

  • Crowded and accelerating: CPA, scGEN, GEARS, scGPT, plus new scaling efforts compete directly
  • Causal identifiability claims are hard to validate without interventional ground truth — reviewers push hard here
  • Combinatorial perturbation data is sparse/expensive, so the very regime he wants to extrapolate into is under-sampled
  • Benchmarking extrapolation is contested (train/test split leakage debates); easy to publish a result that a benchmark paper later invalidates

Gets obsolete if…

  • A large perturbation foundation model (e.g. Arc's State, scGPT-perturb successor) reports strong unseen-combination extrapolation
  • A model trained on Tahoe-100M ships and claims combinatorial/dose generalization
  • A benchmark paper (PerturBench-style) shows existing FMs already extrapolate well, removing the gap
  • A causal/SCM perturbation-effect paper is published with the disentanglement idea he planned

Gets more feasible if…

  • Open Tahoe-100M / scPerturb data with standardized loaders is released
  • A causal disentanglement / SCM-decoder library for single cell drops
  • PerturBench or a standard perturbation extrapolation benchmark with public leaderboard lands
  • GEARS or CPA release clean, extensible codebases to fork
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Uncertainty-Calibrated Single-Cell Foundation Models for Trustworthy Cross-Atlas Annotation and Active Reference Building

Single-cell transcriptomics deep learning
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Single-cell foundation models (scGPT, Geneformer, scFoundation) transfer cell-type labels across atlases but give overconfident, uncalibrated predictions on novel cell states, batches, and diseased tissue, which blocks clinical and atlas-scale use. This project develops Bayesian and conformal uncertainty layers on top of transformer-based single-cell embeddings to produce calibrated per-cell annotation confidence, flag genuinely novel cell states (out-of-distribution detection), and drive an active-learning loop that tells atlas builders which cells most need expert curation. It is evaluated on cross-tissue and disease-vs-healthy transfer using CELLxGENE Census and the Human Cell Atlas.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you2/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort1.5bang-for-buck
Time to result4.5months @ 20h/wk + AI high AI-leverage
GOAT path: He could become the trusted-uncertainty authority for single-cell annotation, but the contribution is tooling atop others' foundation models, not a new paradigm.
Verdict: Safest and most executable entry with his current Scanpy skills; pitch broadly to atlas and single-cell labs as a reliable win, but it will not dazzle DeepMind.
Why fundable: Trustworthy, uncertainty-aware ML for health is a core priority of Oxford (Yarin Gal / EPSRC AIMS), CCAIM, and Wellcome; calibrated annotation is a recognized bottleneck for clinical single-cell adoption, giving strong impact framing. The methods-plus-atlas contribution suits Clarendon and Cambridge Trust and is portable to ETH/KI salaried positions where atlas and clinical genomics groups sit.
Fits labs:
Yarin GalNeil LawrenceChristopher YauMihaela van der SchaarJulia VogtGunnar Rätsch
Fits funding:
ClarendonCambridge TrustImperial President's PhDCCAIM
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~14 mo

Strengths

  • Calibration/OOD failure of single-cell FMs is a real, blocking problem for clinical and atlas-scale annotation
  • Highest feasibility of the six: builds Bayesian/conformal layers on FROZEN scGPT/Geneformer/scFoundation embeddings — minimal pretraining compute, ideal for a first-year
  • Conformal + active learning is tractable, well-scoped, and directly matches his uncertainty interest
  • Data and tooling are fully open (CELLxGENE Census, scvi-tools, MAPIE), so he can move fast and produce a first-author result

Hidden caveats

  • Perceived novelty is thin — a bolt-on uncertainty layer can read as incremental to a top-venue reviewer
  • The FM teams (scGPT, Geneformer, CZI) can add calibration natively and subsume the contribution
  • Conformal prediction for scRNA is already emerging, so the window to claim novelty is narrowing
  • Defining ground-truth 'novel cell state' for OOD evaluation is subtle and can undermine the headline claim

Gets obsolete if…

  • scGPT / Geneformer / scFoundation release versions with built-in calibrated uncertainty
  • A conformal or Bayesian single-cell annotation benchmark paper is published and reports it solved
  • CZI or a major atlas effort ships a 'trustworthy'/uncertainty-aware single-cell FM
  • An OOD/novel-cell-type detection method for single cell reaches near-saturated benchmark performance

Gets more feasible if…

  • Open scGPT/Geneformer embedding APIs and CELLxGENE Census stay freely queryable (already true)
  • A conformal-prediction library adds single-cell / multiclass annotation recipes
  • scvi-tools or a similar package exposes ready uncertainty/OOD utilities
  • A public cross-atlas benchmark with held-out diseased/novel tissue for OOD evaluation is released
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Resolution-Agnostic Spatial Foundation Model: Unifying Imaging- and Sequencing-Based Spatial Transcriptomics Across Organs

Spatial transcriptomics machine learning
★ flagship
A single self-supervised foundation model that tokenizes spatial transcriptomics at any resolution — subcellular imaging platforms (Xenium, MERFISH, CosMx) and spot/bead sequencing platforms (Visium HD, Slide-seqV2, Stereo-seq) — into a shared tissue-niche embedding space. It learns transferable representations of cellular neighborhoods that generalize across tissues and technologies, enabling zero-shot niche annotation, cross-platform imputation of unmeasured genes, and gene-panel design. The core contribution is a resolution-agnostic geometric tokenizer plus masked-neighborhood pretraining on the large public spatial corpus (HuBMAP, HTAN, Allen, 10x atlases).
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result15months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: A working cross-platform spatial backbone would make him the 'ImageNet-of-spatial' figure, but it's a compute- and data-integration marathon with nothing yet to derisk it.
Verdict: Exciting and fundable but a heavy backbone build with no derisking paper; pitch only to well-resourced spatial-omics foundation-model labs.
Why fundable: Directly matches Sanger/EMBL-EBI and CCAIM strategic priorities on scalable single-cell/spatial foundation models and open reference atlases; a platform-unifying model is high-impact, reusable community infrastructure that funders favor. Clean deliverables (public benchmarks, released weights) fit doctoral funders wanting demonstrable output. Compute-heavy but data-open, so cost story is credible for salaried EU/Swiss positions and industry-linked fellowships.
Fits labs:
Mo LotfollahiDavid Van ValenMarinka ZitnikBen RaphaelPietro LiòLior Pachter
Fits funding:
Wellcome Sanger / EMBL-EBI PhDGates CambridgeCambridge TrustCCAIM (Cambridge Centre for AI in Medicine)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~10 mo

Strengths

  • Spatial transcriptomics is one of the fastest-growing areas and cross-platform unification is a real, named pain point
  • Tissue-niche/cellular-neighborhood embeddings are naturally graph-structured — strong fit for his GNN/PyG focus and sheaf-GNN angle
  • Abundant and growing public data across platforms (Xenium, MERFISH, CosMx, Visium HD, Slide-seqV2, Stereo-seq)
  • A single resolution-agnostic tokenizer is an ambitious, high-impact framing that could anchor a strong thesis if it lands

Hidden caveats

  • Very high compute and engineering scope for a first-year — building a multi-platform foundation model alone is a major hidden barrier
  • Well-funded groups are already racing here (Nicheformer, CellFM/spatial FMs); scoop risk from consortia with more resources
  • Cross-platform batch/technology effects are severe and can dominate the learned embedding, undermining 'shared space' claims
  • Validation of transferable niche representations across organs is hard and easy for reviewers to contest

Gets obsolete if…

  • Nicheformer or a successor releases a unified cross-platform / cross-resolution spatial FM
  • 10x Genomics or a platform vendor ships an official spatial foundation model
  • A published spatial FM demonstrates imaging+sequencing platform unification into one embedding space
  • The HEST-1k or a spatial-niche benchmark is reported as effectively solved

Gets more feasible if…

  • Open weights for Nicheformer or another spatial FM are released to finetune from
  • HEST-1k or a large multi-platform spatial dataset with standardized loaders drops
  • squidpy / spatialdata add cross-platform harmonization and tokenization utilities
  • Large public Visium HD + Xenium paired/adjacent-section datasets are released
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Counterfactual Cell-Cell Communication: Causal, Uncertainty-Aware Inference of Signaling Niches from Spatial Transcriptomics

Spatial transcriptomics machine learning
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Current cell-cell communication methods (CellChat, COMMOT, ligand-receptor scoring) are correlative and give no uncertainty or causal direction. This project builds a Bayesian graph-neural-network framework that treats the spatial tissue as a causal graph and estimates counterfactual signaling effects — what a cell's expression state would be under a perturbed neighborhood — with calibrated posterior uncertainty and explicit identifiability analysis. Output is testable, ranked communication hypotheses (which signals actually drive niche states) rather than correlation heatmaps.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you3/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort1.33bang-for-buck
Time to result8months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: This fuses his sheaf/GNN signature with causal spatial biology — the clearest path for him to own causal cell-cell communication and extend his Lio work into a subfield.
Verdict: Best fit to his sheaf/GNN identity and to Lio himself; pursue this as the flagship and pitch straight to Lio/ELLIS geometric-DL-for-biology labs.
Why fundable: Sits squarely in the causal-ML-for-medicine and trustworthy-uncertainty agendas that van der Schaar, Gal, and CCAIM/Wellcome fund; produces clinically-relevant, interpretable hypotheses (tumor microenvironment signaling, immune niches) attractive to Wellcome and cancer funders. Methodologically rigorous (identifiability, calibration) which appeals to elite methods-focused scholarships, and requires no wet lab, keeping the funding case low-cost and self-contained.
Fits labs:
Mihaela van der SchaarYarin GalChristopher YauSarah FilippiBarbara BraviPietro Liò
Fits funding:
Clarendon (Oxford)Gates CambridgeCCAIMWellcome Trust PhD programmes
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~16 mo

Strengths

  • Best fit of all six for his profile: causal GNN over a spatial tissue graph directly leverages sheaf-GNN / Lio-lab expertise
  • Real conceptual gap — CellChat/COMMOT/ligand-receptor scoring are purely correlative with no direction or uncertainty
  • Less crowded than the foundation-model races, so lower scoop pressure and a clearer first-author path
  • Adding uncertainty and counterfactual/causal framing is a genuinely novel methodological contribution, not a bolt-on

Hidden caveats

  • Causal claims from observational spatial data are hard to validate — there is rarely interventional ground truth for signaling
  • Ligand-receptor databases are noisy/incomplete, so the causal graph is built on shaky priors
  • Validation gap is the core risk: 'counterfactual signaling effect' is difficult to confirm without spatial perturbation experiments
  • May be seen as lower-impact/niche versus the FM projects, so venue prestige could be harder to secure

Gets obsolete if…

  • A causal cell-cell communication method (e.g. causal COMMOT / interventional CCC) is published with the same core idea
  • A perturbation-grounded signaling benchmark with ground-truth CCC effects is released
  • A spatial foundation model subsumes cell-cell communication inference as a downstream capability
  • A Bayesian/uncertainty-aware ligand-receptor inference tool ships and becomes standard

Gets more feasible if…

  • COMMOT / LIANA+ / CellNEST release extensible codebases to build on
  • Spatial perturbation datasets (Perturb-map, spatial CRISPR) become public for validation
  • A GNN-for-CCC library or PyG example for spatial signaling graphs lands
  • Sheaf/geometric GNN tooling matures in PyTorch Geometric, lowering the implementation barrier
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Identifiable Causal Representation Learning for Single-Cell Perturbation Effects

Perturbation modelling & causal representation (single-cell)
◆ cross-lab workhorse★ flagship
Current perturbation predictors (CPA, scGen, GEARS) learn entangled latent shifts that conflate the true regulatory mechanism of a perturbation with confounders like cell cycle, batch, and baseline cell state, so they fail to extrapolate to new cell contexts. This project develops an identifiable causal representation model that treats each genetic/chemical perturbation as a sparse, mechanism-specific intervention on a latent causal graph, using recent identifiability results (sparse mechanism shift, interventional causal representation learning) to disentangle intervention targets from nuisance variation. Trained on genome-wide Perturb-seq (Replogle 2022) and sci-Plex chemical screens, it predicts held-out perturbation effects across unseen cell lines and quantifies which latent factors a perturbation causally moves.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact5/5
Appeal / effort0.8bang-for-buck
Time to result16.5months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: If he mastered interventional identifiability he would own the bridge between causal-representation theory and single-cell biology, but that demands formal identifiability math he does not yet hold.
Verdict: Pursue only as a stretch and shore up the identifiability math first; pitch to causal-rep + single-cell labs (Uhler-adjacent, Theis, Lio).
Why fundable: Directly hits funder priorities for mechanistic, generalizable AI-for-biology rather than black-box prediction: EMBL-EBI/Sanger fund single-cell foundation and perturbation work, CCAIM funds causal ML for medicine, and the identifiability angle appeals to theory-forward panels (Clarendon, Gates Cambridge). Precision-medicine framing (predicting drug/gene effects in patient-derived contexts) maps cleanly to Wellcome and Cambridge Trust health-impact criteria. Fully computational, low cost, high citation potential.
Fits labs:
Mo Lotfollahi (Cambridge/Sanger)Caroline Uhler (MIT)Barbara Bravi (Imperial)Mihaela van der Schaar (Cambridge)Matt Thomson (Caltech)
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeClarendonEMBL-EBI PhD ProgrammeWellcome Sanger / Cambridge Trust
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~18 mo

Strengths

  • Sits at the intersection of two hot waves: identifiability theory in causal representation learning (Schölkopf/Locatello/Uhler lines) and the rapid scaling of Perturb-seq — a clean theory-plus-application story that reads well for a GNN/dry-lab candidate.
  • Concrete, well-articulated mechanistic gap: CPA/scGen/GEARS entangle cell cycle, batch and baseline state with the true regulatory shift, so failure-to-extrapolate is easy to demonstrate empirically.
  • Data is abundant and fully open (Norman, Replogle, sci-Plex, scPerturb) — no wet lab needed, matches his constraints.
  • Natural fit for Lio's lab: gene-regulatory graph priors / sheaf structure can be injected as inductive bias into the latent causal model.

Hidden caveats

  • Identifiability guarantees hinge on strong assumptions (rich interventional support, known intervention targets) that rarely hold in real single-cell data — the theory-to-practice gap is larger than a newcomer expects.
  • Crowded and fast: Theis, Uhler, Marks groups are actively publishing here; high scoop risk on the exact 'identifiable perturbation model' framing.
  • No ground truth for the 'true regulatory mechanism', so evaluating identifiability on real data is contentious and reviewers may reject claims of recovered causal factors.
  • Requires solid causal inference plus measure-theoretic identifiability, not just PyTorch Geometric — steep ramp for someone still learning PyG with no first-author paper yet.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A single-cell foundation model (scGPT/Geneformer successor, or one trained on Tahoe-100M) demonstrates strong zero-shot perturbation extrapolation to unseen cell contexts, making bespoke causal models less compelling.
  • A benchmark paper (PerturBench / scPerturb-style) reports that causal/identifiable methods do not beat simple linear or additive baselines on held-out perturbations.
  • Uhler or Theis lab publishes an identifiable causal perturbation model at scale first, claiming the core novelty.
  • A calibrated, confounder-disentangling pretrained single-cell embedding is released that already separates cell-cycle/batch from perturbation effect.

Gets more feasible if…

  • A massive open perturbation atlas (Tahoe-100M or similar) is released, removing the data-scale barrier for training identifiable models.
  • An identifiable-CRL library or tutorial (e.g., extending CausalBench / scPerturb tooling) lands, lowering the theory-implementation barrier.
  • Open interventional single-cell benchmarks with known intervention targets appear, enabling actual identifiability validation.
  • A PyG-native causal-single-cell example / reference implementation is published.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Optimal-Transport Counterfactuals and Closed-Loop Active Design for Unseen Combinatorial Perturbations

Perturbation modelling & causal representation (single-cell)
★ flagship
The combinatorial perturbation space is astronomically large (pairs, triples of genes/drugs) yet screens only cover a tiny fraction, so the key question is how to predict synergistic/epistatic combination effects and choose which experiments to run next. This project frames single-cell perturbation response as a counterfactual optimal-transport map from control to perturbed distributions, learns a compositional latent model of how single-perturbation OT maps combine (capturing non-additive epistasis), and wraps it in an active-learning loop that proposes maximally informative next combinations under a budget. It is validated retrospectively on Norman 2019 combinatorial Perturb-seq and can drive real closed-loop screening.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort0.8bang-for-buck
Time to result12months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: Owning an OT-algebra of epistasis for combinations would be field-defining, but he stacks two gaps here: OT theory and a wet-lab closed loop he cannot run.
Verdict: Poor solo bet for him given OT math plus wet-lab dependency; pitch only to a lab already running combinatorial perturbation screens.
Why fundable: Combines two funder-favoured themes: generative modelling of cellular state (OT/diffusion) and AI-driven experimental design that cuts wet-lab cost — an explicit efficiency and translational selling point for Sanger, EMBL-EBI, and industry-linked funders (Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds, Open Phil AI for its scalable-oversight/active-learning flavour). Combination-therapy relevance (predicting synergistic drug/gene pairs) gives clear clinical impact for Wellcome, Cambridge Trust, and CCAIM.
Fits labs:
Mo Lotfollahi (Cambridge/Sanger)Matt Thomson (Caltech)Yisong Yue (Caltech)Jure Leskovec (Stanford)James Zou (Stanford)Caroline Uhler (MIT)
Fits funding:
EMBL-EBI PhD ProgrammeGates CambridgeClarendonKnight-Hennessy (Stanford)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~15 mo

Strengths

  • Combinatorial synergy/epistasis prediction is genuinely important and underserved — GEARS handles pairs weakly, so there is real headroom.
  • Optimal transport for single-cell is mature (Waddington-OT, Moscot from Theis lab), giving well-tested tooling to build counterfactual maps on.
  • The closed-loop active-design framing aligns with the fundable self-driving-lab / active-learning trend and yields two natural thesis chapters (OT counterfactual + Bayesian acquisition).
  • Counterfactual OT map framing is mathematically elegant and differentiates from pure regression predictors.

Hidden caveats

  • 'Closed-loop active design' implies wet-lab experimental feedback he does not have (dry-lab only); he must simulate the loop, which weakens the headline story.
  • OT counterfactual maps are compute-heavy and hard to validate — true triple/combination-perturbation datasets are scarce.
  • Moscot already occupies much of the OT-for-single-cell territory; incremental-novelty risk is high.
  • Combinatorial ground truth is sparse, so benchmarks may be too small to support convincing synergy-prediction claims.

Gets obsolete if…

  • Moscot or a successor ships native combinatorial / counterfactual-OT and experimental-design modules, absorbing the contribution.
  • A large open dual/triple-guide Perturb-seq screen enables a plain foundation model to predict synergy well without OT machinery.
  • A GEARS-successor or perturbation foundation model demonstrates strong combination extrapolation on standard benchmarks.
  • An active-learning-for-perturbation benchmark paper concludes simple acquisition functions match sophisticated OT-based design.

Gets more feasible if…

  • A large open dual/triple-guide combinatorial Perturb-seq dataset is released, removing the validation-data barrier.
  • Moscot adds an active-learning / experimental-design tutorial or API.
  • An open benchmark for combinatorial perturbation / self-driving-lab experiment selection launches.
  • A differentiable / GPU OT library tutorial for single-cell counterfactuals drops, cutting the compute-and-engineering cost.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Calibrated Uncertainty for Protein Structure Prediction: From pLDDT to Trustworthy Conformational Ensembles

Protein structure prediction and design
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Modern predictors (AlphaFold3, Boltz-2, ESMFold) emit confidence scores like pLDDT/PAE that are poorly calibrated and collapse multi-state proteins into a single misleading structure. This project builds a Bayesian deep-ensemble and conformal-prediction layer on top of frozen structure predictors to output statistically calibrated per-residue and per-interface confidence, and to flag and sample conformational heterogeneity (disorder, fold-switching, alternative binding poses). Deliverable: a model-agnostic uncertainty head plus a benchmark tying calibration error to downstream decisions (which predicted structures are safe for docking or design).
Appeal3/5
Effort for you3/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result4.5months @ 20h/wk + AI high AI-leverage
GOAT path: Calibration is respected but a supporting role; hard to become a world leader in a bolt-on UQ layer over someone else's frozen model.
Verdict: Safe tractable entry and a fast first-author paper; pitch broadly, but it will not wow an ETH geometric-DL panel.
Why fundable: Directly serves funder priorities in trustworthy/safe AI for science: Open Phil AI (reliability of scientific ML), EPSRC/Oxford AI, and Clarendon which rewards methodological depth transferable across domains. Uncertainty quantification is a recognized bottleneck for using AF-class tools in decision-critical biomedicine, giving a clear impact narrative for Wellcome-aligned health funders.
Fits labs:
Yarin Gal (Oxford)Barbara Bravi (Imperial)Michael Sternberg (Imperial)Ellen Zhong (Princeton)Chris Holmes (Oxford)
Fits funding:
Clarendon (Oxford)Open Phil AIEPSRC DTP / Oxford AIImperial President's PhD
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • pLDDT/PAE miscalibration is a widely acknowledged, real pain point, and conformal prediction offers distribution-free guarantees — a clean, defensible thesis motivation.
  • Builds on FROZEN predictors (AF3/Boltz-2/ESMFold), so compute needs are modest — ideal for a dry-lab candidate with limited resources.
  • Conformal prediction and deep ensembles are well-tooled (MAPIE, etc.), enabling fast prototyping and early results.
  • Multi-state / conformational ensembles is a rising topic (AF-cluster, MSA subsampling), so the ensemble angle is timely.

Hidden caveats

  • A 'frozen predictor + calibration wrapper' can read as incremental engineering rather than novel science — thesis-worthiness scrutiny is likely.
  • Ground-truth conformational ensembles are scarce (few multi-state experimental sets), making ensemble-calibration validation hard.
  • Deep ensembles of AF3-class models remain expensive at inference even with frozen weights, eroding the 'cheap' claim for the ensemble variant.
  • Crowded: many groups now work on AlphaFold ensembles/uncertainty (AF-cluster, Str2Str, distributional confidence), raising scoop risk.

Gets obsolete if…

  • AlphaFold3 or a Boltz successor ships natively calibrated confidence or built-in ensemble outputs, removing the need for a wrapper.
  • A generative ensemble model (AlphaFlow/Str2Str successor, BioEmu) becomes the standard for multi-state prediction, bypassing calibration-on-frozen.
  • A benchmark paper standardizes structure-calibration metrics and shows a simple post-hoc recalibration is sufficient.
  • Boltz/Chai release confidence heads retrained to be calibrated, closing the gap directly.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Boltz/Chai weights and logits are fully open with easy ensemble/uncertainty hooks (already trending open).
  • A conformal-prediction-for-structure library or tutorial lands.
  • An open multi-state / conformational-ensemble benchmark (from PDBe or a new dataset) is released, enabling proper validation.
  • An open recalibration dataset pairing predictions with experimental B-factors / ensemble data appears.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Steering Protein Language Models: Activation Editing for Function-Conditioned Binder Design

Protein structure prediction and design
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Instead of expensive fine-tuning, this project controls generative protein design by discovering and applying steering vectors in the activation space of large protein language models (ESM3, ProGen-class), enabling function-, fold-, and stability-conditioned generation of binders and mini-proteins. Designs pass through an in-silico pipeline (ProteinMPNN inverse folding + RFdiffusion scaffolds + AlphaFold3/Boltz confidence and interface metrics) and, optionally, a small validation round via the Adaptyv Foundry API. Core claim: PLMs contain linearly recoverable directions for biophysical properties that can be composed for controllable design.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.25bang-for-buck
Time to result5.5months @ 20h/wk + AI high AI-leverage
GOAT path: First to make activation-steering work cleanly in protein LMs could own controllable, interpretable design — a genuinely novel and hot niche.
Verdict: Strong DeepMind/Isomorphic ladder and fully in-silico; pitch to protein-design plus interpretability labs, accepting it is a domain pivot from his single-cell base.
Why fundable: Generative biology / protein design is a flagship priority for Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds, Open Phil AI-for-science, and mission-driven scholarships (Gates Cambridge impact framing, Knight-Hennessy at Stanford for the Hie lab). Cheap, interpretable steering aligns with both cost-efficiency and AI-safety/interpretability funder narratives.
Fits labs:
Brian Hie (Stanford)Barbara Bravi (Imperial)Peter Minary (Oxford)Mona Singh (Princeton)Jure Leskovec (Stanford)
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeKnight-Hennessy (Stanford)Boehringer Ingelheim FondsOpen Phil AI
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~10 mo

Strengths

  • Activation steering is red-hot in LLM interpretability; porting it to protein language models is novel, timely, and a strong differentiator from the pure generative-design crowd.
  • Avoids fine-tuning, so it is cheap and fits limited compute — well matched to a dry-lab newcomer.
  • Binder / mini-protein design is high-impact and fundable, and the in-silico pipeline (ProteinMPNN inverse folding + folding check) is standard and doable without wet lab.
  • The interpretability framing (steering vectors, SAE features) gives a mechanistic narrative that reviewers increasingly value.

Hidden caveats

  • Steering vectors may not transfer cleanly to pLMs — they are masked/encoder models, not autoregressive chat LLMs, so controllability is unproven and could simply fail.
  • ESM3 access and EvolutionaryScale licensing/commercial terms may restrict activation access; the 'large pLM' assumption is fragile.
  • In-silico-only validation is weak evidence for actual binder function; reviewers will want wet-lab or at least strong physics/energetics.
  • The activation-steering literature moves fast; a pLM-steering paper landing first would make this derivative.

Gets obsolete if…

  • The ESM3/ProGen team or another lab publishes activation-steering / controllable generation for protein language models first.
  • A cheap LoRA / adapter fine-tuning recipe for pLMs makes steering unnecessary for function-conditioned generation.
  • A dedicated function-conditioned binder generator (next RFdiffusion-AllAtom, BindCraft, or Chai-design update) dominates binder benchmarks.
  • A benchmark paper shows steering vectors give no controllability gain over prompt/conditioning baselines in pLMs.

Gets more feasible if…

  • A fully open ESM3-class pLM with accessible activations is released (ESM-2 is open; an open large successor removes the access barrier).
  • A sparse-autoencoder / interpretable-feature toolkit for protein LMs (InterProt-style) is released with pretrained SAEs.
  • Cheap experimental binder validation (Adaptyv-style foundry, already surfaced as a skill) becomes routinely accessible, adding a wet-lab loop.
  • An open steering-vector library with pLM examples and a binder-design tutorial lands.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Calibrated Active Learning for Multi-Objective Molecular Optimization: Uncertainty-Aware Navigation of the ADMET-Potency Frontier

Molecular / drug-discovery machine learning
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Drug candidates fail late because models extrapolate confidently into chemical regions where they were never trained, and lead optimization must trade off potency against many ADMET liabilities at once. This project builds a Bayesian deep-learning framework that produces trustworthy, calibrated uncertainty on molecular property predictions under scaffold/time distribution shift, then couples it to multi-objective batched active learning (Pareto Thompson sampling / expected hypervolume improvement) that proposes the next round of molecules to synthesize. The output is a decision-making engine for medicinal chemistry that quantifies not just what to make next but how much to trust each prediction.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.75bang-for-buck
Time to result5months @ 20h/wk + AI high AI-leverage
GOAT path: Calibration-under-scaffold-shift is valuable but many pharma-ML groups already chase it, so it is hard to dominate as a signature.
Verdict: Solid and fundable but crowded, applied, and in a chem domain new to him; pitch to drug-discovery ML groups, not as an ETH geometric-DL flagship.
Why fundable: Directly hits Yarin Gal's flagship theme (uncertainty and safe deployment of DL) and the Open Phil / AI-reliability framing of calibration and distribution shift; ADMET failure is the dominant driver of pharma R&D cost, giving clear translational impact for Clarendon/Cambridge Trust panels and industry-linked funders (Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds). Purely computational, benchmark-driven, and reproducible, which de-risks the proposal for review.
Fits labs:
Yarin GalYisong YueMarinka ZitnikMihaela van der SchaarNeil Lawrence
Fits funding:
ClarendonOpen Phil AIKnight-HennessyMSCA
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~18 mo

Strengths

  • Calibration failure under scaffold/temporal shift is a genuine, well-documented drug-ML failure mode, giving a clear and credible motivation.
  • Multi-objective ADMET-vs-potency optimization mirrors real lead optimization, making the work industry-relevant and fundable.
  • Therapeutics Data Commons and Polaris supply ready benchmarks, scaffold/time splits, and oracles — dry-lab friendly (a pytdc skill is even available).
  • Bayesian deep learning and active learning are mature, so a working pipeline is buildable with limited compute.

Hidden caveats

  • Mature and crowded: many QSAR-uncertainty and molecular-AL papers already exist, so the novelty bar is high.
  • Real validation needs prospective experiments; a purely retrospective AL simulation is easy to criticize as unrealistic.
  • Calibration metrics under distribution shift are contentious and easy to overclaim, inviting reviewer pushback.
  • Combining multi-objective optimization + calibration + active learning is a lot of moving parts — scope-creep risk for a first-author-less newcomer.

Gets obsolete if…

  • Polaris or TDC releases a definitive calibrated-uncertainty benchmark that settles method ranking and leaves little headroom.
  • A molecular foundation model ships well-calibrated out-of-distribution uncertainty out of the box.
  • A prominent paper shows conformal prediction or simple ensembles are 'good enough' under scaffold split, closing the methods gap.
  • An industry active-learning study concludes uncertainty-guided acquisition gives no lift over greedy multi-objective selection.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Polaris ADMET benchmark expansion or newly donated industry datasets with temporal splits are released.
  • An open calibrated-uncertainty toolkit for molecules (built on TDC) with tutorials lands.
  • A strong open pretrained molecular property / ADMET foundation model is released for transfer learning.
  • An open multi-objective active-learning benchmark with oracles for lead optimization launches.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Physics-Informed 3D Generative Diffusion for Target-Conditioned De Novo Binder Design

Molecular / drug-discovery machine learning
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Structure-based generative models can hallucinate molecules that look plausible in 2D but bind poorly or are unsynthesizable in 3D. This project develops an SE(3)-equivariant diffusion model that generates ligands directly inside a protein pocket, regularized by geometric and physics-informed priors (steric/electrostatic energy terms and neural-operator surrogates for binding free energy) and constrained toward synthetic accessibility. Generation is closed with a docking/energy-based re-ranking loop so it is coupled to a differentiable estimate of binding quality rather than only chemical likelihood.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact5/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result15months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: SE(3)-equivariant diffusion binder design plays directly to his geometric-DL identity; mastering equivariant generative models could make him a leader, but the execution bar is the highest of the six.
Verdict: His most on-brand geometric-DL flagship and squarely DeepMind/Isomorphic; pitch to Lio/ETH and structure-based design labs, but only if he closes the PyG/e3nn and equivariant-diffusion gap fast.
Why fundable: Sits squarely in the geometric-deep-learning-for-biology programmes championed by Leskovec, Zitnik and Anandkumar, and aligns with the generative-biology thesis funders now actively back; target-conditioned design is a headline capability for Gates Cambridge / Clarendon impact narratives and AI-for-science funders, with strong industry translation for Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds and CCAIM-style programmes.
Fits labs:
Jure LeskovecMarinka ZitnikAnima AnandkumarBrian HiePeter MinaryPietro Liò
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeClarendonKnight-HennessyOpen Phil AI
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~10 mo

Strengths

  • SE(3)-equivariant pocket-conditioned diffusion is a very active area (DiffSBDD, TargetDiff, Pocket2Mol lineage), so the approach is on-trend and well-referenced.
  • Physics-informed regularization directly targets the real 'looks-good-in-2D-binds-poorly' failure, providing a genuine differentiator.
  • Structural data is plentiful (PDBbind, CrossDocked), making it dry-lab feasible.
  • The neural-operator energy surrogate is a fresh ML-plus-physics angle that bridges to Lio-lab geometric-DL strengths.

Hidden caveats

  • Extremely crowded and fast-moving — structure-based generative design is near-saturated, so scoop risk is high.
  • Compute-heavy: training SE(3) diffusion plus a neural-operator surrogate is expensive and may exceed his available resources.
  • Validation gap: docking scores and energy proxies are unreliable, and synthesizability / true binding are rarely tested in silico.
  • CrossDocked is a known gameable benchmark; strong-looking results frequently fail to hold, and reviewers now know this.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A dominant open pocket-conditioned generator (next RFdiffusion-AllAtom, Chai-design, or Boltz-design) ships physics-aware generation, absorbing the contribution.
  • A benchmark paper exposes CrossDocked/PoseBusters gaming and resets the field's evaluation, invalidating prior gains.
  • Co-folding models (AF3/Boltz-2 with a generative-design mode) collapse the separate-generator paradigm.
  • A published SE(3) diffusion model already integrates steric/electrostatic energy priors, claiming the exact physics-informed novelty.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open all-atom equivariant diffusion codebase with pretrained weights is released, removing the training-compute barrier.
  • PoseBusters / PoseBench-style physics-checked validated benchmarks become standard, giving trustworthy evaluation.
  • An open differentiable-physics / neural-operator energy library for ligand-pocket interactions lands.
  • A GPU-efficient / distilled SBDD diffusion model is released that fits a single-GPU budget.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Heterogeneous Graph Neural Networks with Prior-Biology Constraints for Patient-Level Multi-Omics Integration in Precision Oncology

Graph neural networks for multi-omics integration
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Build a heterogeneous GNN that fuses genomics, transcriptomics, methylation and proteomics into a single patient-specific graph whose edges are seeded from curated biological priors (protein-protein interaction, gene-regulatory and pathway networks), then learns to reweight them for outcome prediction. Each omics layer is a typed node set with cross-layer message passing, yielding interpretable subnetworks that drive treatment-response and survival predictions. Attention over biological edges lets clinicians trace a prediction back to specific regulatory modules.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you3/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result5months @ 20h/wk + AI high AI-leverage
GOAT path: World-lead potential only if he makes the biological-prior graph a genuinely learnable/prunable object with calibrated per-patient interpretability — otherwise it is one more MOGONET variant.
Verdict: Pursue as his safe workhorse — closest to his IFOM/sheaf-GNN cancer work — and pitch to comp-onc and multi-omics-integration PIs; needs him to actually acquire PyG HeteroConv fluency.
Why fundable: Matches funder priorities in clinically translatable AI for cancer: Cambridge/CRUK and Gates emphasise health impact plus methodological novelty, and precision oncology is a flagship theme. A dry-lab plan reproducible on public data de-risks the project for stipend-plus-fees funders.
Fits labs:
Pietro LioFlorian MarkowetzMarinka ZitnikChristopher YauOlivier Gevaert
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeCambridge TrustClarendonHarding Distinguished Postgraduate
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~18 mo

Strengths

  • Heterogeneous GNN + biological priors maps cleanly onto PyG HeteroData and Lio-lab expertise; edge reweighting is a natural extension of his sheaf-GNN background (learned restriction maps over typed edges).
  • Fully dry-lab feasible: TCGA, CPTAC, MOFA cohorts give paired multi-omics; STRING/Reactome/KEGG prior networks are free, so no data-generation or compute-cluster barrier.
  • Interpretability/prior-injection framing is funder- and reviewer-friendly in precision oncology; edge attributions give a built-in narrative for clinicians.
  • Clear, bounded scope for a first paper — outcome/subtype prediction with an ablation on priors is a shippable contribution.

Hidden caveats

  • Field is already crowded (MOGONET, MoGCN, SUPREME, Pathformer since 2021); pure 'GNN + priors on TCGA' has thin remaining novelty and is easy to scoop.
  • Sample-starved regime (hundreds of patients per cohort, thousands of features) means GNNs frequently fail to beat a regularized MLP or late-fusion concatenation — the real risk is a negative/null result vs simple baselines.
  • TCGA survival/outcome benchmarks are notorious for train-test leakage and optimistic AUCs; a naive pipeline can look great and not replicate.
  • Prior networks are incomplete and cell-type-agnostic; a reviewer will ask whether reweighting learns biology or just memorizes cohort quirks.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A pan-omics foundation model (scGPT/Geneformer-style but bulk/patient-level) ships turnkey multi-omics patient embeddings that beat bespoke GNNs.
  • A benchmark/reality-check paper shows GNNs do not outperform simple late-fusion baselines on TCGA multi-omics outcome prediction.
  • A major lab releases a calibrated, prior-guided multi-omics integration package (e.g., an extension of MOFA+/MultiVI) covering the same task.
  • TCGA/CPTAC or a consortium ships an official harmonized benchmark that standardizes and effectively 'closes' the patient-level integration task.

Gets more feasible if…

  • A curated, leakage-controlled multi-omics benchmark with a ready PyG HeteroData loader is released.
  • A library lands that auto-builds prior-biology graphs (PPI/GRN/pathway) as PyG objects from gene lists.
  • Pretrained per-omics encoders (methylation, proteomics) drop, removing the need to train modality encoders from scratch.
  • A tutorial on heterogeneous GNNs for multi-omics appears in PyG/scverse docs.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Geometric Deep Learning on Cell-Cell Graphs for Paired Single-Cell Multi-Omics Integration and Perturbation Prediction

Graph neural networks for multi-omics integration
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Develop a graph-based generative model that integrates paired single-cell modalities (scRNA-seq + scATAC-seq / CITE-seq surface proteins) by learning a shared latent manifold over a cell-cell k-NN graph, with GNN message passing enforcing cross-modal consistency and batch invariance. The learned representation predicts single-cell responses to unseen genetic or chemical perturbations by propagating perturbation signals across the cell graph, unifying multi-omics integration and counterfactual perturbation prediction in one geometric framework.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact5/5
Appeal / effort1.25bang-for-buck
Time to result8months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: If he cracks graph-based OOD perturbation prediction on paired single-cell multi-omics he sits at the centre of the virtual-cell movement — a subfield-defining position.
Verdict: His strongest strategic bet — leverages his existing Scanpy/AnnData skills, is DeepMind/Arc-aligned, and pitchable to Lio, ETH and any single-cell ML group; only risk is his still-nascent scVI/Pyro generative fluency.
Why fundable: Central to Sanger/EMBL-EBI and CCAIM priorities (single-cell atlases, perturbation biology, foundation models for cells). Reviewers reward a reusable method plus biological discovery; public Perturb-seq and multiome data make results demonstrable within a PhD timeframe.
Fits labs:
Mo LotfollahiSarah FilippiMarinka ZitnikMihaela van der SchaarMatt ThomsonRickard Sandberg
Fits funding:
EMBL-EBI / Wellcome Sanger PhDCambridge TrustCCAIMBoehringer Ingelheim Fonds
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • Sits on two hot waves at once: paired single-cell multi-omics (10x Multiome, CITE-seq) and perturbation-response prediction (Perturb-seq), both high-visibility at NeurIPS/ICLR and Open Problems in Single-Cell.
  • Cell-cell k-NN graph is a natural GNN substrate and message passing for cross-modal/batch consistency is a clean geometric-DL story aligned with the lab.
  • Abundant public data (CZ CELLxGENE, scPerturb, Tahoe-100M, Replogle) means zero wet-lab dependency.
  • Perturbation prediction is genuinely translational and gets dataset/competition traction, giving natural benchmarks to target.

Hidden caveats

  • Brutally crowded and dominated by resource-rich groups (scVI/scArches, scGPT, GEARS, scGen, State); high scoop risk and hard to differentiate as a solo newcomer.
  • Perturbation-prediction benchmarks have a credibility crisis — multiple 2023-2024 papers show SOTA models barely beat a non-linear mean or 'predict training mean' baseline, so a positive result is hard to defend.
  • Generative + GNN over large atlases is compute-hungry; without cluster access he may be capped to toy subsets.
  • Batch effects and unstable evaluation metrics make results fragile and reviewer-contested.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A single-cell foundation model ships calibrated, well-benchmarked genetic/chemical perturbation prediction (e.g., a new scFM or Arc's State model update) that beats graph-based methods.
  • An Open Problems / benchmark paper shows GNN cell-graph approaches do not beat linear or mean baselines on unseen perturbations.
  • A major lab releases a paired-modality integration tool that 'solves' cross-modal consistency and batch invariance out of the box.
  • A large calibrated perturbation atlas + leaderboard launches, commoditizing the task.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Pretrained scFM embeddings are released openly, letting him condition/fine-tune instead of training from scratch (removes compute barrier).
  • A new large paired multi-omics or Perturb-seq dataset with standardized splits drops.
  • GEARS/scGPT-style perturbation tooling ships an easy PyG-compatible tutorial or wrapper.
  • scverse (squidpy/scvi-tools) adds first-class cell-graph GNN utilities.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Riemannian Flow Matching over SE(3) for Protein Conformational Ensembles from Sparse Experimental Restraints

Geometric deep learning for biological structure
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
AlphaFold gives one static fold, but function lives in the conformational ensemble. This project builds an SE(3)-equivariant flow-matching generative model on the manifold of backbone frames that samples full Boltzmann-weighted ensembles conditioned on sparse experimental restraints (cryo-EM density maps, NMR chemical shifts, HDX-MS, crosslinking-MS), turning partial low-resolution data into calibrated structural distributions with per-residue uncertainty.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact5/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result20months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Boltzmann-weighted SE(3) ensemble generation is a near-Nobel-adjacent frontier — being the person who cracks it beyond static folds would make him a global name.
Verdict: Dream-tier and maximally DeepMind-aligned, but a genuine long shot for him given no formal math/CS, no PyG, no structure background — pursue only with a strong equivariance/geometry co-supervisor to backstop the math.
Why fundable: Directly hits the post-AlphaFold frontier funders now prioritise (dynamics, not single structures); uncertainty-calibrated generative modelling maps cleanly onto Wellcome Sanger / EMBL-EBI open-science methods remits, Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds basic-science molecular focus, and Clarendon/Gates-Cambridge merit criteria. High citation and open-source tooling potential makes it a strong flagship proposal.
Fits labs:
Brian HieEllen ZhongPeter MinaryBarbara BraviMarinka ZitnikJure Leskovec
Fits funding:
Clarendon (Oxford)Gates CambridgeBoehringer Ingelheim FondsEMBL-EBI PhD Programme
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~14 mo

Strengths

  • Targets a widely acknowledged frontier — AlphaFold gives one fold, function needs the Boltzmann ensemble — so the problem framing is compelling and citable.
  • SE(3)-equivariant flow matching is genuinely cutting-edge (FrameFlow, FrameDiff, AlphaFlow, Str2Str); restraint-conditioned ensemble generation is underexplored and offers real first-author novelty.
  • Conditioning on sparse experimental restraints (cryo-EM, NMR, HDX-MS, XL-MS) differentiates it from pure de-novo generators and gives an experimental-grounding angle reviewers like.
  • Strong methods depth signals ambition and fits a geometric-DL lab's identity.

Hidden caveats

  • Steep prerequisite stack (Riemannian geometry, SE(3) equivariance, flow/diffusion on manifolds) is a heavy lift for someone still learning PyTorch Geometric — real risk of a long ramp before any output.
  • Compute-intensive equivariant generative training; without serious GPU access this stalls at toy proteins.
  • Validation gap is severe: ground-truth ensembles barely exist, and comparing against MD or sparse experiments is contentious — easy to produce pretty samples that mean nothing.
  • Competes head-on with extremely well-funded structure groups (Baker, DeepMind, Dror, Microsoft), who can scoop or out-scale quickly.

Gets obsolete if…

  • AlphaFold3's successor or a major release ships native ensemble/dynamics or conformational-diversity generation.
  • A lab releases a restraint-conditioned ensemble generator (e.g., an AlphaFlow/BioEmu variant that ingests cryo-EM/NMR restraints) covering the same niche.
  • Microsoft BioEmu or a Boltzmann-generator-style model ships at scale with open weights, absorbing the emulate-the-ensemble use case.
  • A benchmark paper standardizes ensemble evaluation and shows existing generators already match experimental observables.

Gets more feasible if…
