  • Open pretrained SE(3) flow-matching backbones (FrameFlow/AlphaFlow-style) are released with weights to fine-tune rather than train from scratch.
  • A curated restraint-to-ensemble benchmark dataset (paired sparse restraints + reference ensembles) drops.
  • Riemannian flow-matching tooling matures (e.g., torchcfm / geometric FM libraries) with worked protein examples.
  • An MD ensemble dataset (like mdCATH/ATLAS) is extended with experimental-restraint annotations.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Equivariant Graph Neural Fields for 3D Tissue Architecture: Learning Cell-Neighbourhood Geometry in Spatial Omics

Geometric deep learning for biological structure
◆ cross-lab workhorse★ flagship
Tissue is a 3D geometric object where cell type, morphology, and spatial neighbourhood jointly encode function and disease. This project builds E(3)-equivariant graph neural networks over reconstructed 3D cell graphs from spatial transcriptomics/proteomics (Xenium, MERFISH, CODEX serial sections) that predict niche-level phenotypes and are invariant to rotation/translation of the tissue, learning transferable geometric signatures of tumour microenvironments across patients.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result8.5months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: He could own 3D-equivariant spatial-omics if he pairs true E(3) geometry with real 3D tissue reconstruction — most of the field is still stuck in rotation-breaking 2D pipelines.
Verdict: Underrated fit — uniquely leverages his microscopy/image-analysis background plus geometric DL; pitch to spatial-omics and geometric-DL labs, with equivariance being the skill he must build.
Why fundable: Spatial biology is the hottest experimental modality and starved of principled geometric ML; equivariant modelling of tissue structure fits precision-oncology and health-ML priorities of CRUK-linked (Markowetz), CCAIM, Cambridge Math Genomics, and Harding/Imperial President's remits. Clinical translation angle strengthens Wellcome and CCAIM applications; strong multi-omics story for Gates/Clarendon.
Fits labs:
David Van ValenBen RaphaelPietro LiòFlorian MarkowetzOlivier GevaertMarinka Zitnik
Fits funding:
CCAIM (Cambridge)Cambridge Mathematics of Information / Math GenomicsClarendon (Oxford)Imperial President's PhD Scholarship
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~16 mo

Strengths

  • Spatial omics is booming (Xenium, MERFISH, CODEX) and 3D tissue architecture is markedly less crowded than 2D single-cell integration — more open space for a newcomer.
  • E(3)-equivariance over 3D cell graphs is a principled, defensible fit; rotation/translation invariance for niche prediction is a clean geometric-DL contribution.
  • Growing public 3D/serial-section data (HuBMAP, 10x Xenium releases) plus clinically meaningful niche/phenotype endpoints.
  • Aligns with genomics + geometric-DL profile and can start in 2D then extend to 3D, giving an incremental path to a paper.

Hidden caveats

  • True 3D data is still scarce — most spatial datasets are single 2D sections; serial-section registration/3D reconstruction is a large, underestimated data-engineering burden.
  • Reconstruction noise (alignment errors, dropout, segmentation) can dominate signal, so equivariance gains may be swamped by preprocessing artifacts.
  • Niche/phenotype labels are subjective and weakly standardized, making evaluation soft and reviewer-contested.
  • Equivariant GNNs on large tissue graphs are memory/compute-heavy; scaling to whole-slide 3D is nontrivial.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A spatial-omics foundation model (Nicheformer successor, CellNexus-style) ships niche/phenotype prediction that beats bespoke equivariant GNNs.
  • 10x Genomics or a vendor releases turnkey 3D niche-analysis tooling.
  • A benchmark paper shows E(3)-equivariance gives no gain over non-equivariant baselines on tissue-niche prediction.
  • A standardized large 3D spatial benchmark launches and is quickly saturated by existing methods.

Gets more feasible if…

  • A large aligned 3D spatial atlas (HuBMAP 3D, or a multi-section Xenium release) drops with ready cell graphs.
  • squidpy/spatialdata adds 3D graph construction with a PyG bridge, removing pipeline plumbing.
  • Pretrained spatial/niche embedding models are released for fine-tuning.
  • A tutorial on equivariant GNNs for spatial omics appears in PyG or scverse docs.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Reliable Dynamic Treatment Regimes: Uncertainty-Aware Counterfactual Estimation from ICU Time-Series

Causal ML for clinical decision-making
Build a deep sequential model that estimates individualised, time-varying treatment effects (e.g. when to start vasopressors, ventilation weaning, fluid dosing) from ICU EHR streams while correcting for time-dependent confounding via a marginal-structural / G-computation-inspired neural architecture. Crucially, every counterfactual prediction carries calibrated uncertainty and an explicit 'defer to clinician' abstention signal when the observed data give no support for the proposed action. The system is validated on MIMIC-IV and eICU with prospective-style temporal splits and formal overlap/positivity diagnostics.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.75bang-for-buck
Time to result9months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: Strong clinical-safety story (abstention + positivity-aware causal ML) but methodologically incremental and far from any geometric-DL identity he could lead.
Verdict: Off-brand and low-DeepMind — skip unless he deliberately pivots to clinical causal ML, since it abandons his GNN/geometric strengths and needs an entirely new causal-inference toolkit.
Why fundable: Directly hits CCAIM's mandate (medicine + reliable ML), Open Phil AI's interest in trustworthy/abstaining high-stakes systems, and Wellcome/health-AI priorities on deployable clinical decision support. Uses only public de-identified data, so no wet-lab cost and clear reproducibility — funders like Gates Cambridge and Clarendon reward tangible societal impact plus rigour.
Fits labs:
Mihaela van der SchaarGunnar RätschMarzyeh GhassemiJulia VogtYarin GalChristopher Yau
Fits funding:
CCAIM PhDGates CambridgeCambridge TrustClarendon
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~24 mo

Strengths

  • Fully dry-lab and reproducible: MIMIC-IV and eICU are public, well-trodden ICU datasets, so no data-access wet-lab barrier once credentialed.
  • Uncertainty-aware / calibrated counterfactuals is a differentiator on top of a solid methodological lineage (MSMs, G-computation, RMSN, CRN, TE-CDE) — reviewer-friendly and impactful.
  • Clinically concrete decision problems (vasopressor timing, ventilation weaning, fluids) give a crisp narrative and evaluation targets.
  • Causal + calibration framing ages relatively well compared to fast-moving foundation-model races.

Hidden caveats

  • Weak fit with the lab's geometric-DL / GNN identity and with his sheaf-GNN and PyG skill-building — this is sequential/causal ML, so he'd be building expertise orthogonal to his stated trajectory.
  • Fundamental validation problem: no ground-truth counterfactuals in observational ICU data, so 'reliable' claims are hard to defend and reviewers are skeptical.
  • Direct overlap with the van der Schaar lab (also Cambridge), which dominates exactly this space — serious scoop and 'why not just use their tool' risk.
  • Time-dependent confounding and treatment-selection bias are subtle; easy for a newcomer to get the causal setup subtly wrong and invalidate results.

Gets obsolete if…

  • van der Schaar or a comparable group releases a calibrated dynamic-treatment-regime benchmark/toolkit that effectively standardizes the task.
  • A clinical-time-series foundation model (EHR-FM) ships built-in counterfactual/treatment-effect estimation with uncertainty.
  • A benchmark paper with synthetic ground-truth 'closes' ICU treatment-effect estimation by declaring a settled SOTA.
  • A calibrated-counterfactual method paper subsumes the uncertainty angle across sequential settings.

Gets more feasible if…

  • A new open ICU dataset or a standardized causal benchmark with synthetic ground-truth counterfactuals is released.
  • A library for neural MSM / G-computation with built-in calibration lands (e.g., extension of pyensmallen/CausalML/econML to sequential settings).
  • A well-documented MIMIC-IV treatment-regime preprocessing pipeline is published, cutting the data-engineering cost.
  • Conformal-prediction-for-time-series tooling matures, supplying the calibration machinery off the shelf.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Genetically-Instrumented Causal ML for Precision Prescribing: De-confounding Observational Drug Response with Mendelian Randomization

Causal ML for clinical decision-making
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Use inherited genetic variants as instrumental variables inside a machine-learning framework (deep IV / kernel and neural MR estimators) to recover unbiased heterogeneous treatment/drug-response effects from biobank-scale observational data, where randomised trials are infeasible. The output is a genotype-aware map of who benefits or is harmed by a given drug or drug target, enabling drug-repurposing and precision-prescribing recommendations. Trained and stress-tested on UK Biobank plus Genomics England linked EHR, with target-level validation against known trial outcomes.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.75bang-for-buck
Time to result15months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Genome-wide instruments for nonlinear individual drug response is a sharp idea, but leadership here means becoming a statistical geneticist, not a geometric deep learner.
Verdict: Off his geometric-DL path — his TE/genomics exposure only marginally helps; pursue only if genuinely drawn to statistical genetics, and pitch to biobank/MR labs rather than ELLIS/ETH geometric groups.
Why fundable: Bridges causal ML with statistical genetics — exactly the intersection prized by Wellcome Sanger, Cambridge Centre for Mathematical Genomics and EMBL-EBI, while the clinical/drug-target payoff attracts Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds and Open Phil. Genotype-stratified safety and pharma translatability make it a strong Clarendon/Harding/Gates narrative.
Fits labs:
Chris HolmesNa CaiPedro BeltraoLuke O'ConnorNicola WhiffinNeil Lawrence
Fits funding:
Wellcome Sanger PhDCambridge Mathematical GenomicsEMBL-EBIClarendon
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~24 mo

Strengths

  • Genuinely novel intersection — Mendelian randomization instruments plugged into deep IV / neural causal estimators is far less crowded than either MR or DTR alone.
  • Leverages his genomics background and biobank-scale observational data (UK Biobank, FinnGen), where RCTs are infeasible, giving a strong identification story.
  • Genetic instruments provide a principled de-confounding argument that differentiates it from pure observational ML — attractive to methods and clinical-genetics reviewers.
  • High translational appeal (genotype-aware precision prescribing) with clear stakeholder interest.

Hidden caveats

  • MR's core assumptions (no horizontal pleiotropy, instrument relevance, exclusion restriction) are strong and endlessly contested — instrument validity is the whole ballgame and hard to defend.
  • Weak-instrument problem is brutal: genetic variants explain tiny phenotype variance, so recovering heterogeneous (not just average) drug-response effects needs enormous N and still has low power.
  • Weak fit with geometric-DL/GNN lab focus and his PyG learning path — this is econometrics-flavored causal ML, largely orthogonal to sheaf GNNs.
  • UK Biobank access/approval and compute overhead are real, and drug-response phenotypes are sparse/noisy in biobanks; deep IV is known to be unstable and hard to validate.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A biobank consortium releases an MR-based drug-response / adverse-effect atlas covering the target exposures.
  • A methods paper shows deep IV / neural MR does not beat two-stage least squares or standard MR-Egger for heterogeneous effects.
  • A genotype-phenotype foundation model on biobank data ships instrument-based causal effect estimation.
  • A benchmark standardizes MR-ML evaluation and shows the extra ML machinery adds nothing over classical MR.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open synthetic biobank with genetic instruments and known ground-truth effects is released for benchmarking (removes the no-ground-truth problem).
  • A neural-MR / deep-IV library with genetics support matures (e.g., econML or a dedicated MR-ML package).
  • Precomputed GWAS instrument sets / PGS Catalog releases make instrument selection turnkey.
  • A cloud/TRE workflow (e.g., UK Biobank RAP notebooks) publishes a ready deep-IV pipeline.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Neural Counterfactual Survival Models with Calibrated Uncertainty for Individualized Treatment Timing in Cancer

Counterfactual & treatment-effect estimation in medicine
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Build a Bayesian deep-learning framework that estimates individualized, time-varying treatment effects on survival from observational oncology EHR and registry data, jointly modelling when to treat (dynamic treatment regimes) and quantifying epistemic uncertainty so clinicians know when a counterfactual prediction is untrustworthy. The model combines neural survival analysis with balanced representation learning to control confounding, and outputs calibrated conditional average treatment effects (CATE) with credible intervals. Validation targets held-out RCT arms as counterfactual ground truth (RCT-emulation / target-trial framework).
Appeal3/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.75bang-for-buck
Time to result16months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: He could own calibrated counterfactual survival for oncology, but only after building survival + causal + Bayesian machinery he currently lacks — leadership here means becoming a clinical-ML person, not a geometric-DL one.
Verdict: Off his identity and skill base; pursue only if he wants a clinical-ML pivot, and pitch to biostat/clinical-ML groups, not Lio or ELLIS geometric labs.
Why fundable: Directly hits Wellcome/Cambridge-Trust and Clarendon priorities for clinically translatable AI; uncertainty-aware CATE is a named van der Schaar / Yau research agenda, making supervisor buy-in and studentship top-ups (CCAIM, CRUK) realistic. Precision-oncology + safety framing fits Gates Cambridge 'improve lives' and CRUK/Wellcome disease-impact mandates.
Fits labs:
Mihaela van der Schaar (Cambridge)Yarin Gal (Oxford)Christopher Yau (Oxford)Julia Vogt (ETH)Gunnar Rätsch (ETH)Florian Markowetz (Cambridge/CRUK)
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeCambridge TrustCCAIM studentshipClarendon (Oxford)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~18 mo

Strengths

  • Uncertainty quantification for clinical decisions is a hot, funded direction and 'knowing when not to trust a counterfactual' is a genuinely clinician-facing contribution, not just a benchmark number.
  • Dynamic treatment regimes / treatment timing is under-served by deep learning compared to static ITE, so there is real headroom for a first-author contribution.
  • Large observational oncology substrate exists and is accessible dry-lab (SEER, MIMIC-IV, Flatiron-style registries, TCGA-linked outcomes), so no wet lab needed.
  • Bayesian survival + calibration plays to a probabilistic-ML skill set that complements the lab's GNN focus, giving a differentiated niche.

Hidden caveats

  • Causal identifiability from observational EHR rests on untestable assumptions (no unmeasured confounding, correct treatment-timing model); reviewers in causal-inference venues will attack this and a newcomer underestimates how hard the identification argument is.
  • There is no counterfactual ground truth on survival, so 'calibrated uncertainty' can only be validated on factual predictions or synthetic data — the core selling point is the hardest thing to prove.
  • ITE/CATE estimation is a crowded ML sub-field (many NeurIPS/ICML papers); stacking survival + timing + Bayesian is ambitious and risks being three half-solved problems instead of one strong result.
  • Real oncology EHR access has governance/IRB and data-use-agreement friction that a first-year student without institutional pipelines can badly underestimate.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab releases a clinical/EHR foundation model with built-in calibrated survival or treatment-effect heads (search: 'clinical foundation model calibrated treatment effect survival').
  • A benchmark paper standardizes counterfactual survival / dynamic-treatment-regime evaluation and reports a strong solved baseline (search: 'benchmark counterfactual survival dynamic treatment regime 2026').
  • A widely-adopted library ships calibrated causal survival out of the box (e.g. auton-survival / pycox / econml adding time-varying counterfactual survival).
  • A high-profile RCT-vs-observational emulation paper shows deep ITE-survival models fail to recover trial results, denting clinical credibility of the whole approach.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Open credentialed EHR/oncology datasets expand or get easier access (MIMIC-IV survival extracts, synthetic SEER/registry releases, OMOP-CDM synthetic cohorts).
  • A maintained deep-survival + calibration library lands (extensions to auton-survival, TorchSurv, survival-conformal tooling).
  • A tutorial or reference implementation for neural dynamic treatment regimes with uncertainty is published (workshop code, blog + repo).
  • Conformal prediction for survival / time-to-event matures into a plug-in, removing the hardest calibration engineering.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Causal Structure-Aware Graph Neural Networks for Multi-Omics Treatment-Response Prediction and Counterfactual Perturbation

Counterfactual & treatment-effect estimation in medicine
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Develop GNN-based causal representation models that predict single-cell / patient-level responses to drug perturbations by learning the underlying regulatory/interaction graph, enabling counterfactual 'what if we perturbed gene/pathway X' predictions and individualized treatment-effect estimates grounded in mechanism. The approach fuses causal inference (do-calculus / invariant risk) with geometric deep learning over multi-omics graphs, and is validated against held-out perturbation experiments (e.g. Perturb-seq, LINCS).
Appeal5/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact5/5
Appeal / effort1.25bang-for-buck
Time to result11months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: This is his bullseye: causal structure learning fused with GNNs over regulatory graphs directly extends his sheaf/geometric work and Scanpy base, and owning mechanistic counterfactual perturbation could define a subfield.
Verdict: Strong yes and his single best fit — pitch to Lio, Uhler, Lotfollahi/Theis and ELLIS; the only real risk is closing the PyTorch-Geometric gap fast.
Why fundable: Marries two funder-favourite themes (causal ML + multi-omics precision medicine); mechanistic counterfactuals are exactly the Zitnik/Lotfollahi/Liò agenda, making Sanger/EMBL-EBI and Cambridge Math-Genomics fits strong. Generative-biology angle attracts Open Phil / Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds and Harding.
Fits labs:
Pietro Liò (Cambridge)Mo Lotfollahi (Sanger/Cambridge)Marinka Zitnik (Harvard)Caroline Uhler (MIT)Jure Leskovec (Stanford)Valentina Boeva (ETH)
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeCambridge TrustCambridge Maths of Information/Genomics DTPEMBL-EBI / Sanger PhD
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • Sits directly in the applicant's and Lio lab's core competence (GNNs, message passing, multi-omics), so ramp-up cost is low and supervisor fit is strong.
  • Perturbation-response prediction is a flagship, well-funded direction with abundant public Perturb-seq / drug-screen data (Replogle, sci-Plex, LINCS, Tahoe-100M).
  • Causal representation learning + do-calculus framing gives a mechanism story that is more defensible than pure correlational expression prediction.
  • Counterfactual 'perturb gene/pathway X' outputs are directly experiment-actionable, which strengthens grant and collaboration appeal.

Hidden caveats

  • Extremely crowded and moving fast (GEARS, CPA, scGPT, GEARS successors, Bunne/Theis/Regev-adjacent groups); scoop risk within a PhD timeline is high.
  • GRN / regulatory-graph inference is itself unreliable and contested, so 'learning the underlying graph' can undermine rather than strengthen the causal claim.
  • Genuine causal validation needs held-out perturbations or wet-lab confirmation the applicant (dry-lab) cannot generate, leaving a validation gap versus better-resourced labs.
  • Foundation-scale single-cell models need serious GPU budget; competing on scale against Arc/CZI-funded efforts is not viable, so novelty must be method-side not scale-side.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A virtual-cell / single-cell foundation model demonstrably solves unseen-perturbation response prediction at scale (search: 'virtual cell foundation model unseen perturbation prediction SOTA').
  • A benchmark paper shows causal/graph-structured methods do not beat simpler correlational baselines on perturbation transfer (search: 'benchmark perturbation prediction causal vs baseline single cell').
  • A major lab open-sources a causal perturbation model with counterfactual queries built in (search: 'causal perturbation model counterfactual gene knockout release 2026').
  • scGPT / Geneformer successors ship native cross-perturbation generalization, absorbing the niche.

Gets more feasible if…

  • New large open perturbation atlases drop (Tahoe-100M growth, expanded Replogle/Perturb-seq, scPerturb additions) lowering the data barrier.
  • GEARS/CPA/scGPT-style reference code stays maintained and PyG-compatible, giving reusable message-passing scaffolds.
  • A pretrained cell/gene embedding is released that the applicant can build a lightweight causal head on top of (no full pretraining needed).
  • A causal-GNN tutorial or library (do-interventions on graphs in PyG) lands, cutting the causal-inference engineering.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Mechanism-Constrained Generative Simulators for Privacy-Preserving ICU Trajectories

Synthetic data and simulators for health
Build a hybrid simulator that couples physiology-informed differential equations (cardiovascular/respiratory/renal dynamics) with a deep generative backbone (diffusion or state-space model) to synthesize realistic, shareable ICU time-series that respect clinical constraints and carry formal differential-privacy guarantees. The core contribution is closing the gap between purely statistical EHR generators (which hallucinate physiologically impossible trajectories) and pure mechanistic simulators (which lack realistic heterogeneity), evaluated on downstream train-on-synthetic/test-on-real utility and membership-inference resistance. Deliverable: an open benchmark and simulator that lets researchers develop critical-care ML without touching identifiable patient data.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.6bang-for-buck
Time to result13months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: Leadership needs formal DP, diffusion/state-space generative modelling, and physiology ODEs simultaneously — none of which he holds — so his NIRS physiology exposure is far too thin an anchor to lead here.
Verdict: Skip — furthest from his identity and highest execution risk; only relevant to clinical-DP/synthetic-data groups.
Why fundable: Directly matches funder priorities in trustworthy/privacy-preserving ML for health and reproducibility. CCAIM and van der Schaar's group explicitly fund synthetic-data-for-medicine work; Ratsch and Moor run ICU foundation-model programmes needing shareable pretraining data. Data-scarcity + privacy + open benchmarks is a fundable trifecta (safety, access, reproducibility) mapping cleanly onto MSCA impact criteria and Open Phil's AI-for-good angle.
Fits labs:
Mihaela van der SchaarGunnar RatschMichael MoorMarzyeh GhassemiJulia VogtNeil Lawrence
Fits funding:
Cambridge CCAIM (van der Schaar centre)Cambridge Trust + Gates CambridgeETH salaried doctoral position (Ratsch/Vogt)MSCA Doctoral Networks
