Strengths

  • Shareable, privacy-preserving synthetic health data is a real regulatory and access pain point, so the motivation is durable and grant-friendly.
  • Coupling physiology ODEs with a deep generative backbone is a genuinely differentiated hybrid; pure black-box synthetic EHR generators exist, mechanism-constrained ones are rarer.
  • Formal differential-privacy guarantees give a crisp, defensible claim (unlike fuzzy 'anonymization'), which reviewers and IRBs respect.
  • Substrate is public and dry-lab friendly (MIMIC-IV, eICU, MIMIC waveform), and physiology simulators are open.

Hidden caveats

  • The DP-vs-utility tradeoff is brutal: strong (epsilon) guarantees typically destroy the fine temporal structure the physiology constraints are meant to preserve, and this tension may be the whole result.
  • Combines three hard subfields (mechanistic ODE modelling, diffusion/state-space generation, differential privacy) — a lot of surface area for one student with no prior first-author track record.
  • 'Realism' of synthetic trajectories has no agreed metric, so evaluation can drift into cherry-picked plots that reviewers distrust.
  • Relatively niche audience (synthetic-data + DP + ICU) means fewer natural venues and citations than the perturbation/foundation-model crowd.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A strong open-source differentially-private synthetic EHR / ICU time-series generator is released and adopted (search: 'differentially private synthetic ICU time series release 2026').
  • A benchmark standardizes fidelity+privacy evaluation for synthetic clinical time series and reports a solved baseline (search: 'synthetic EHR time series fidelity privacy benchmark').
  • A clinical time-series foundation model ships with a privacy/synthesis mode, absorbing the use case.
  • A definitive paper shows meaningful DP guarantees make synthetic ICU data clinically useless, killing the value proposition.

Gets more feasible if…

  • MIMIC-IV waveform / high-resolution ICU releases expand, giving richer training signal.
  • Mature diffusion/state-space time-series libraries (e.g. maintained TSGM, diffusion-ts, structured state-space repos) lower the generative engineering.
  • Opacus / JAX-Privacy DP tooling improves for sequence models, making DP training tractable.
  • An open physiology simulator with autodiff-friendly ODEs is published, easing the mechanism-constraint coupling.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Causal In-Silico Cell Simulators: Counterfactual Perturbation Response for Experiment Prioritization

Synthetic data and simulators for health
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Develop a generative 'virtual cell' simulator that predicts single-cell transcriptomic responses to unseen genetic and chemical perturbations by combining latent causal structure with counterfactual generative modeling, then wrap it in an active-learning loop that proposes the most informative next experiments. Rather than only imputing expression, the model simulates interventions (do-operator over regulatory latent variables) so it can extrapolate to unseen perturbation combinations and rare cell states, with calibrated uncertainty flagging where the simulator is unreliable. Deliverable: a simulator that augments scarce perturbation atlases and guides real lab spend toward high-value experiments.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact5/5
Appeal / effort1.25bang-for-buck
Time to result8months @ 20h/wk + AI high AI-leverage
GOAT path: Virtual-cell perturbation simulation is the hottest chair in the field and sits on his Scanpy/single-cell base, so a causal generative version with an active-learning loop could make him a name — if he lands scVI-tools/Pyro fluency.
Verdict: Pursue — near-tie with i=19; pitch to Lotfollahi/Theis, CZI/Arc-style groups and ELLIS, but the generative causal + active-learning stack is genuinely ambitious for him.
Why fundable: Virtual-cell / in-silico perturbation simulators are a flagship priority at Sanger, EMBL-EBI and the CZI-adjacent single-cell community, and align with the Cambridge Centre for Mathematical Genomics remit. Framing it as experiment-prioritization (reducing costly wet-lab screens) gives clear translational ROI that fellowships reward. Causal + uncertainty framing hits the interpretability/robustness themes funders increasingly demand.
Fits labs:
Mo LotfollahiMatt ThomsonJames ZouCaroline UhlerPietro LioJure Leskovec
Fits funding:
EMBL-EBI / Wellcome Sanger PhD programmeCambridge Centre for Mathematical GenomicsBoehringer Ingelheim FondsCCAIM
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • 'Virtual cell' is a flagship, heavily-funded vision (CZI, Arc Institute), so framing a PhD around it rides a strong, well-signposted wave.
  • The active-learning / experiment-prioritization loop adds practical, fundable value beyond pure prediction and differentiates from plain perturbation models.
  • Counterfactual + latent-causal framing aligns with the applicant's GNN/geometric-DL and causal interests.
  • Rich open perturbation data (Replogle, scPerturb, Tahoe-100M) makes the predictive core buildable dry-lab.

Hidden caveats

  • The most crowded and best-capitalized space here — CZI and Arc are pouring compute and staff into exactly virtual-cell perturbation prediction; a lone PhD scoop risk is severe.
  • The active-learning loop's whole point is closing a design-experiment cycle, but a dry-lab applicant cannot run the proposed wet-lab experiments, so the loop is validated only in simulation — a credibility gap.
  • Competing on model scale is hopeless against foundation-model efforts; novelty must be squarely on causal structure or acquisition function, which narrows the contribution.
  • Near-duplicate of project i=19 in substance — pursuing both would fragment effort, and reviewers may see it as derivative of the virtual-cell hype rather than novel.

Gets obsolete if…

  • Arc/CZI (or similar) release a virtual-cell foundation model that predicts unseen genetic+chemical perturbations well (search: 'Arc Institute virtual cell model perturbation prediction release').
  • A community perturbation-prediction challenge/benchmark is declared effectively solved (search: 'perturbation prediction challenge leaderboard solved single cell 2026').
  • An open active-learning-for-experiment-design framework for perturbations ships, commoditizing the loop (search: 'active learning experiment design perturbation single cell open source').
  • A benchmark shows counterfactual/causal simulators don't outperform simpler predictors on prospective perturbations.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Larger open perturbation atlases and standardized splits appear (Tahoe-100M expansion, scPerturb, Perturb-seq compendia).
  • A pretrained virtual-cell embedding is released that a lightweight causal/active-learning head can sit on, removing pretraining cost.
  • Maintained active-learning libraries for genomics (BoTorch/Ax integrations, biological AL toolkits) mature.
  • A wet-lab collaborator or automated-lab partnership (cloud lab) becomes available to actually close the loop.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Deep Gaussian-Process State-Space Models for Uncertainty-Calibrated Disease Trajectory Forecasting from Irregular EHR and ICU Time-Series

Probabilistic ML / Gaussian processes for biomedical data
Clinical time-series are irregularly sampled, multivariate, and noisy, yet current deep models give over-confident point predictions that clinicians cannot trust. This project develops deep GP state-space models (GP-SSMs with amortised variational inference and neural transition kernels) that jointly forecast multi-organ disease trajectories and emit calibrated, decomposable uncertainty, then benchmarks calibration and decision-utility against transformer foundation models on MIMIC-IV/eICU. The core contribution is a scalable inducing-point + normalising-flow variational scheme that keeps GP-grade uncertainty at ICU-scale data volumes.
Appeal2/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.4bang-for-buck
Time to result12months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Deep GP state-space models are a respectable but niche corner of probabilistic ML he has no foundation in, and clinical trajectory forecasting is unlikely to make anyone a field-defining leader.
Verdict: Skip — off-identity, high skill gap, low heat; only for dedicated GP/probabilistic-ML groups.
Why fundable: Directly matches funder priorities in trustworthy/safe ML for health: Wellcome, CCAIM (Cambridge Centre for AI in Medicine), and van der Schaar's programme explicitly fund reliable clinical ML; Clarendon/Gates value societal-impact health AI; requires no wet-lab so cost-efficient. Calibration-for-clinical-safety is a fundable, evaluable deliverable.
Fits labs:
Mihaela van der Schaar (Cambridge, ML for medicine, no bio needed)Neil Lawrence (Cambridge, probabilistic ML / GPs / health)Gunnar Rätsch (ETH, ML clinical time-series / ICU foundation models)Sarah Filippi (Imperial, stat ML)Julia Vogt (ETH, interpretable ML / medical)Yarin Gal (Oxford, Bayesian DL / uncertainty)
Fits funding:
CCAIM PhD programmeCambridge Trust / Gates CambridgeClarendon (Oxford)Wellcome Trust PhD
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~18 mo

Strengths

  • Irregular, noisy, multivariate clinical time series with honest uncertainty is a persistent, clinician-relevant gap that point-prediction deep models genuinely fail on.
  • Deep GP state-space models give principled, calibrated uncertainty by construction — a defensible probabilistic-ML angle distinct from the crowded transformer-forecasting pack.
  • Strong open tooling and data exist dry-lab (GPyTorch/Pyro, MIMIC-IV, eICU), so it is buildable without new data collection.
  • Multi-organ joint forecasting with calibration is a concrete, evaluable target that maps cleanly to a first-author methods paper.

Hidden caveats

  • GP-SSM inference is notoriously fiddly and unstable to train; amortised variational inference with neural transition kernels is a lot of moving machinery for a newcomer.
  • GP scalability to large multivariate EHR is a real bottleneck; naive kernels won't scale and the interesting work may all be in approximations.
  • Neural-ODE / irregular-time-series forecasting is already crowded (Latent ODE, GRU-D, mTAN, neural CDEs), so novelty must be sharp on the calibration/GP side.
  • Calibration evaluation for multivariate temporal forecasts is not standardized, risking non-comparable results reviewers push back on.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A clinical time-series foundation model ships with well-calibrated probabilistic forecasts on irregular EHR (search: 'clinical time series foundation model calibrated probabilistic forecast 2026').
  • A general TS foundation model (TimesFM/Chronos/Moirai successor) adds strong uncertainty quantification and handles irregular sampling, absorbing the niche (search: 'time series foundation model uncertainty irregular sampling').
  • A benchmark declares irregular multivariate EHR forecasting effectively solved with calibrated baselines (search: 'irregular EHR time series forecasting calibration benchmark').
  • A drop-in deep-GP-SSM library makes the method commodity, eroding the methods novelty.

Gets more feasible if…

  • GPyTorch/Pyro/GPJax add maintained deep-GP-SSM or amortised-VI building blocks lowering implementation cost.
  • Larger open irregular clinical time-series releases with standard splits appear (MIMIC-IV derivatives, PhysioNet challenges).
  • Open pretrained TS foundation models (TimesFM etc.) become usable as feature extractors or priors under a GP head.
  • Calibration-for-forecasting libraries and metrics standardize, removing evaluation ambiguity.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Scalable Multi-Output Gaussian Processes for Genotype-to-Phenotype Mapping and Uncertainty-Aware Variant Effect Prediction

Probabilistic ML / Gaussian processes for biomedical data
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Predicting quantitative phenotypes and regulatory variant effects from genomic features demands models that share statistical strength across correlated traits and honestly report uncertainty for rare variants where data is thin. This project builds scalable multi-output/deep kernel GPs (with stochastic variational inference and learned genomic kernels) for cross-trait genotype-phenotype prediction and calibrated variant-effect scoring, benchmarked on UK Biobank and rare-disease cohorts. The GP posterior gives principled credible intervals that flag low-confidence predictions for rare or under-represented variants — a key gap in deep variant-effect predictors.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.75bang-for-buck
Time to result9.5months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: Variant-effect prediction and genotype-to-phenotype is hot and overlaps his genomics/TE HPC experience, but a GP/deep-kernel framing is a harder, less-trendy route to leadership than transformer/foundation VEP models.
Verdict: Plausible backup if he leans genomics — pitch to statistical-genetics/genomics-ML groups, but he must close a real Gaussian-process gap and it is less aligned than the GNN perturbation projects.
Why fundable: Aligns with statistical-genomics and rare-disease funder priorities (Wellcome Sanger, EMBL-EBI, Cambridge Maths of Information/Genomics, Oxford BDI). Uncertainty-aware rare-variant interpretation has direct clinical-genomics translational value, attractive to Clarendon/Gates and to salaried KI positions. No wet-lab, uses established public cohorts.
Fits labs:
Chris Holmes (Oxford, Bayesian stat genomics)Christopher Yau (Oxford, ML omics, BDI)Nicola Whiffin (Oxford, rare-disease / regulatory variants)Pietro Liò (Cambridge, multi-omics / precision medicine)Na Cai (ETH, statistical genetics)Pedro Beltrao (ETH, variant effect)
Fits funding:
Clarendon (Oxford)EMBL-EBI / Wellcome Sanger PhDCambridge Trust / Gates CambridgeCambridge Mathematics of Information / Maths Genomics
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • Genomics + variant effect aligns with the applicant's genomics interest and the lab's remit, giving supervisor fit and a clear thesis narrative.
  • Honest uncertainty for rare/thin-data variants is a real, underserved need that black-box DNA models handle poorly — a defensible calibration niche.
  • Multi-output / deep-kernel GPs sharing strength across correlated traits is statistically principled and well-suited to pleiotropic phenotypes (UK Biobank).
  • Scalable variational GP tooling (GPyTorch SVGP) and large open cohorts make it buildable dry-lab.

Hidden caveats

  • DNA language models now dominate variant-effect prediction (Enformer, Evo/Evo2, and DeepMind's AlphaGenome), so a GP-only approach risks looking outclassed on raw accuracy.
  • GPs scale poorly to genome-wide feature dimensions; the interesting contribution may collapse into approximation engineering rather than genomics insight.
  • Ground truth for rare-variant effects is genuinely thin and noisy, so the very regime the method targets is hardest to validate convincingly.
  • Variant-effect prediction is a crowded, benchmark-driven field (ProteinGym-style, regulatory VEP leaderboards); a newcomer without a first-author paper faces stiff, fast-moving competition.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A calibrated DNA language model at scale ships or is extended (AlphaGenome adding uncertainty, Evo2 successors) covering rare-variant confidence (search: 'AlphaGenome variant effect uncertainty calibration 2026').
  • A benchmark shows transformer DNA-LMs dominate genotype-to-phenotype and VEP including on rare variants (search: 'DNA language model variant effect benchmark rare variant SOTA').
  • DeepMind/Meta/EvolutionaryScale release a variant-effect model with built-in calibrated uncertainty (search: 'genomic variant effect model calibrated uncertainty release').
  • A scalable-GP-for-genomics library appears that commoditizes the method, removing the novelty.

Gets more feasible if…

  • UK Biobank / All of Us / genomics-cohort access and pre-extracted phenotype tables become easier to obtain.
  • Pretrained DNA-LM embeddings (Enformer, Nucleotide Transformer, Evo2) are released usable as GP input features, sidestepping the scaling problem.
  • GPyTorch/GPJax add maintained scalable multi-output / deep-kernel primitives with SVI.
  • Open standardized VEP / G2P benchmarks with uncertainty-aware metrics are published, giving a clean evaluation target.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Amortized Meta-Bayesian Inference for Rapid Structure Learning in Cortical Circuits

Bayesian models of learning (computational neuroscience)
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Animals learn new tasks in a handful of trials by reusing latent structure from past experience, a hallmark of hierarchical Bayesian learning that current normative models capture only with intractable exact inference. This project builds recurrent neural networks trained to perform amortized (learned) approximate Bayesian inference over task structure, then tests whether their internal dynamics and inductive biases match ideal-observer priors and recorded population activity during structure-learning tasks. The result is a mechanistic bridge from normative Bayesian meta-learning to circuit-level computation.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result6.5months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: He could own 'the cortex as an amortized inference engine whose errors are predictions' only if he masters normative Bayesian theory he currently lacks — his RNN-training skills get him in the door but not to the frontier.
Verdict: Pursue only as a deliberate comp-neuro pivot and pitch to Bayesian-brain / meta-RL labs (Gatsby, Sainsbury Wellcome, Tenenbaum-adjacent) — it is off his genomics/Lio trajectory and demands theory he must build fast.
Why fundable: Directly targets the 'normative-to-mechanistic' agenda that CBL, Gatsby, and Wellcome/ERC-style basic-neuroscience funders prioritize; it is theory-driven, hypothesis-generating, and uses existing open behavioral/neural datasets, so it is low-cost and high-ceiling. Frames cleanly as fundamental science of learning for Gates Cambridge/Clarendon 'impact + intellectual ambition' criteria and as sample-efficient learning for AI-adjacent funders.
Fits labs:
Máté LengyelGuillaume HennequinIla FieteSurya GanguliRafal BogaczNeil Lawrence
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeCambridge TrustHarding Distinguished Postgraduate ScholarsClarendon
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~18 mo

Strengths

  • Amortized/meta-learned inference is now standard deep-learning practice, so the intractable-exact-inference bottleneck the pitch names is genuinely sidestepped with compute-modest RNN training
  • Rides a hot wave: directly connects to in-context learning in transformers and meta-RL as Bayesian inference, giving strong normative + ML-relevance framing
  • Pure dry-lab modeling with clear behavioral few-shot-learning benchmarks; fits an applicant with ML tooling and no wet-lab access
  • Simulation-based inference tooling (sbi, NeuroGym task suites) lowers the engineering floor

Hidden caveats

  • Crowded lineage: Wang/Botvinick meta-RL, RL^2, and 'RNNs discover Bayes-optimal strategies' already stake much of this ground — novelty must be sharp
  • Claiming biological relevance needs neural data; a pure RNN model invites the 'just another meta-learner' critique and a validation gap
  • Applicant's background is GNNs/genomics, not systems/comp-neuro — real ramp-up on hierarchical Bayesian cognitive-neuro literature
  • Showing an RNN 'implements' Bayesian inference is an interpretability/identifiability trap that is easy to overclaim and hard to prove

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major paper shows in-context learning in transformers/large RNNs fully accounts for few-shot cortical structure learning, absorbing the contribution
  • A review or benchmark consolidates 'meta-RL as amortized Bayesian inference', closing the open-question framing
  • A DeepMind/Botvinick-style follow-up ships amortized task-structure inference matched to neural data at scale

Gets more feasible if…

  • Public paired behavior+neural datasets of rapid/few-shot task learning released for fitting
  • An open amortized-inference or simulation-based-inference tutorial/library targeted at neuroscience lands
  • Expanded NeuroGym-style structured-task suites with baselines drop, removing task-engineering overhead
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Synaptic Plasticity as Uncertainty-Weighted Bayesian Filtering: Deriving and Testing Adaptive Learning-Rate Rules

Bayesian models of learning (computational neuroscience)
Optimal learning requires modulating the learning rate by uncertainty, exactly what a Kalman/Bayesian filter does, yet biological synaptic plasticity rules are typically fixed-rate and uncertainty-blind. This project derives local, biologically plausible plasticity rules that implement online approximate Bayesian filtering with per-synapse uncertainty (variance) tracking, and tests their signatures, faster relearning after change points and volatility-dependent learning rates, against neural and behavioral data. It reframes plasticity as approximate posterior updating rather than gradient descent.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.6bang-for-buck
Time to result9months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: World-leadership here means deriving new local plasticity rules by hand — pure normative/analytical work that a candidate with no formal math degree and no theory paper is poorly positioned to lead.
Verdict: Skip — this is a math-heavy theory thesis that plays directly into his weakest gap and signals nothing DeepMind recruits on.
Why fundable: Sits at the core of predictive-coding and normative-plasticity programmes that Oxford (Bogacz), Imperial (Clopath), and CBL actively fund, and connects to volatility/uncertainty accounts of learning relevant to computational psychiatry, broadening funder appeal (Wellcome, MRC, Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds basic-biomedical). Cheap, theory-first, and produces falsifiable predictions, ideal for merit scholarships emphasizing rigor and originality.
Fits labs:
Rafal BogaczClaudia ClopathMáté LengyelAldo FaisalPedro MedianoGuillaume Hennequin
Fits funding:
ClarendonImperial President's PhD ScholarshipGates CambridgeCambridge Trust
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~30 mo

Strengths

  • Strong theoretical novelty at a clean interface: per-synapse variance as a Kalman/Bayesian filter giving uncertainty-modulated learning rates
  • Directly transferable to Bayesian deep learning (variational weight uncertainty), so skills and results carry beyond neuro
  • Analytically deep and low-compute — a theory-first derivation is a realistic route to a first paper for someone without heavy resources
  • Timely: surprise/uncertainty-driven learning-rate adaptation is an active theme in learning theory and neuro

Hidden caveats

  • Prior art is close: Aitchison & Latham (synaptic plasticity as Bayesian inference, Nat Neuro 2021) and Kalman-filter plasticity already exist — incremental risk is high
  • Biological validation is the weak point: deriving a rule is easy, matching it to measured plasticity/calcium data is the hard, missing half
  • Biophysics of plasticity is far from the applicant's GNN/genomics base — nontrivial domain ramp-up
  • Theory labs can scoop a derivation quickly; the differentiator must be a concrete testable prediction, not just the rule

Gets obsolete if…

  • A paper derives AND experimentally validates uncertainty-weighted plasticity rules matching physiological data
  • A review consolidates 'Bayesian/uncertainty-aware synaptic plasticity', closing the gap the pitch relies on
  • Experimental work reports synapses do not track uncertainty (negative result undercutting the premise)

Gets more feasible if…

  • Open plasticity/calcium-imaging datasets or standard plasticity simulators released for fitting derived rules
  • Bayesian-deep-learning libraries make per-weight variance prototyping trivial, speeding proof-of-concept
  • A tractable tutorial/framework for online Bayesian filtering as local learning rules lands
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Low-Rank Inhibition-Stabilized RNNs as a Unified Model of Flexible Motor Preparation and Execution

Recurrent neural dynamics / theoretical neuroscience
Motor cortex prepares movements by setting an initial condition in a low-dimensional dynamical state, then lets recurrent dynamics unroll to generate muscle commands, but how a single circuit flexibly switches between many movements without instability remains unresolved. This project builds task-optimized low-rank recurrent networks that are also inhibition-stabilized (tightly balanced E/I), derives analytically how rank, connectivity geometry and stability constraints shape the space of producible movements, and tests the predictions against public monkey/mouse motor cortex population recordings. The goal is a control-theoretic account linking connectivity structure to computed dynamics and to measurable stability signatures.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.6bang-for-buck
Time to result7.5months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: Becoming a leader in low-rank inhibition-stabilized RNN theory requires fluent dynamical-systems analysis and E/I stability proofs — a formal-math frontier that is the wrong bet for his profile.
Verdict: Skip — highest-effort, lowest-transfer option for him; only viable if he first earns a math foundation he does not have.
Why fundable: Basic-mechanism theoretical neuroscience with a clean analytical core plus concrete experimental predictions is exactly the profile Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds, Gates Cambridge and Clarendon reward; it needs no wet lab, only compute, so cost/risk is low and the applicant can show early results before the studentship starts. Strong fit to Cambridge CBL and Gatsby-adjacent priorities in normative circuit theory.
Fits labs:
Guillaume HennequinMate LengyelSurya GanguliIla FieteClaudia Clopath
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeCambridge TrustClarendonBoehringer Ingelheim Fonds
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~24 mo

Strengths

  • Builds on mature, analytically tractable low-rank RNN theory (Mastrogiuseppe & Ostojic, Dubreuil) — solid mathematical footing
  • Inhibition-stabilized network (ISN) framing is well-established; explicitly combining low-rank + ISN is a legible, defensible gap
  • Rich open motor-cortex data (Neural Latents Benchmark, MC_Maze) exists for validation — a genuine plus for a dry-lab applicant
  • Prepare-then-execute dynamics has clear, well-characterized phenomena to model, reducing scope ambiguity

Hidden caveats

  • Very active, strong-lab field (Ostojic, Barak, Sussillo, Shenoy alumni) — high scoop risk on any 'unified model' claim
  • The unification of low-rank and ISN may read as incremental; the flexibility/stability story must deliver a non-obvious result
  • Requires motor-neurophysiology grounding well beyond the applicant's current background
  • Best behavioral+neural data is often monkey/proprietary; fitting and biological anchoring are nontrivial

Gets obsolete if…

  • A paper publishes a unified low-rank inhibition-stabilized motor model first, taking the headline contribution
  • A general task-optimized RNN framework demonstrably subsumes flexible prep/execution switching without the ISN machinery
  • A review resolves how single circuits switch movements without instability, closing the motivating question

Gets more feasible if…

  • Expanded open motor datasets or Neural Latents Benchmark tasks lower the data barrier further
  • Open low-rank RNN training/analysis libraries and tutorials expand
  • New analytical results on ISN stability within low-rank connectivity are published, providing ready tools
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Recurrent Neural Sampling vs. Predictive Coding: Discriminating How Cortical Dynamics Represent Uncertainty

Recurrent neural dynamics / theoretical neuroscience
Two leading normative theories claim cortical recurrent dynamics implement Bayesian inference - neural sampling (trajectories are samples from a posterior, so variability IS the computation) versus predictive coding (dynamics minimize prediction error toward a point estimate). These theories are rarely pitted against each other on the same footing. This project derives recurrent circuit dynamics for both schemes under matched generative models, identifies information-theoretic and variability-structure signatures that distinguish them, and tests which better explains stimulus-dependent noise correlations and transient dynamics in public sensory cortex recordings.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort0.6bang-for-buck
Time to result10months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: A definitive sampling-vs-predictive-coding adjudication would be field-defining, but the win is analytical/information-theoretic and demands exactly the theory chops he is missing.
Verdict: Skip for him — intellectually gorgeous and high-impact if solved, but a theory long-shot given no math degree and no theory track record.
Why fundable: Adjudicating between two flagship theories of cortical computation is a high-impact, fundable question for Wellcome-aligned and blue-skies funders; it is purely computational, reproducible on open data, and directly advances the normative-inference agenda that CBL, Bogacz's group and information-theory neuroscience prioritize.
Fits labs:
Mate LengyelGuillaume HennequinRafal BogaczPedro MedianoSurya Ganguli
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeClarendonCambridge TrustBoehringer Ingelheim Fonds
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~30 mo

Strengths

  • A genuinely open, high-profile theoretical question; a like-for-like sampling-vs-predictive-coding comparison is a real and rarely-filled gap
  • Both frameworks have deep normative pedigree (Fiser/Berkes/Orban sampling; Rao-Ballard/Friston predictive coding), giving rich prior structure to build on
  • Model-plus-data-analysis is fully dry-lab and yields concrete, testable discriminating predictions — good publishable framing
  • Squarely a modeling/analysis problem the applicant can own without wet-lab dependencies

Hidden caveats

  • Discriminating the two on real data is notoriously hard and may be practically unidentifiable — a serious validation risk
  • Both theories have been reformulated to mimic each other's signatures; a newcomer underestimates how slippery the distinction is
  • Requires expertise in trial-to-trial variability / noise-structure analysis that is specialized and outside a GNN background
  • High risk of an inconclusive 'it depends on regime' result that is hard to publish as a clean finding

Gets obsolete if…

  • A decisive experimental paper adjudicates sampling vs predictive coding, settling the question
  • A unifying analysis shows the two are mathematically equivalent limits, dissolving the dichotomy the project rests on
  • A third framework (e.g., distributional/particle codes) reframes uncertainty representation and displaces the two-way contrast

Gets more feasible if…

  • Open datasets with controlled stimulus uncertainty plus trial-to-trial variability released
  • Standard open simulators for both predictive-coding and sampling networks published on equal footing
  • A benchmark task suite for uncertainty representation in cortical dynamics lands
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Connectome-Constrained Latent Dynamics: Inferring Mechanistic Circuit Models from Population Recordings Anchored to EM Wiring Diagrams

Neural population data inference & connectomics
◆ cross-lab workhorse★ flagship
State-space and RNN models fit to neural population activity recover latent dynamics but are structurally unconstrained, so the inferred mechanisms are not identifiable and rarely map onto real circuits. This project develops connectome-constrained inference: recurrent latent dynamical models whose connectivity is regularised or hard-constrained by the FlyWire/hemibrain (Drosophila) and MICrONS (mouse cortex) synaptic wiring diagrams, using low-rank + connectome-prior decompositions and amortised/variational inference to recover cell-type-resolved effective interactions. The output is an identifiable, wiring-grounded generative model that predicts held-out neurons and perturbation responses, turning static connectomes into functional, testable dynamical hypotheses.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result9.5months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: This is the batch's best fit: connectome wiring priors ARE graphs, so his sheaf/geometric-DL instincts map directly — he could lead 'structure-constrained identifiable dynamics' if he pairs PyG fluency with latent-dynamics inference.
Verdict: Pursue as his strongest neuro option — pitch to connectomics/neuro-AI groups (Princeton, Allen, Janelia, ELLIS neuro nodes) framing it as geometric DL on real wiring diagrams, which reuses his graph background.
Why fundable: Directly hits funder priorities for interpretable, mechanistic AI-for-science: combines two flagship open datasets (FlyWire, MICrONS) that funders already invested in, promises reusable methods and open software, and bridges ML with neuroscience in a way that reads as high-impact and low-risk (public data, established labs). Strong fit for basic-science fellowships that reward mechanistic understanding over incremental benchmarking.
Fits labs:
Ila Fiete (MIT)Sebastian Seung (Princeton)Guillaume Hennequin & Máté Lengyel (Cambridge CBL)Surya Ganguli (Stanford)Rafal Bogacz (Oxford)
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeClarendonKnight-HennessyBoehringer Ingelheim Fonds
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~15 mo

Strengths

  • Exceptionally timely: the FlyWire adult-fly connectome (2024) created strong momentum for connectome-constrained modeling
  • Directly leverages the applicant's graph/GNN strength — a connectome IS a graph, so PyG skills map onto the constraint machinery
  • Identifiability of latent-dynamics models is a real, well-motivated methodological gap that hard/soft wiring constraints can plausibly close
  • A defensible new niche rather than a saturated one; connectome-grounded mechanism is a clear differentiator

Hidden caveats

  • The core dependency — paired connectome AND population recordings of the SAME circuit — is scarce and hard to align
  • Fast-moving with capable competitors already active (Turaga/Janelia, Litwin-Kumar, Pandarinath) — scoop risk is real
  • Requires Drosophila neurophysiology ramp-up on top of the connectomics tooling
  • Constraints may overconstrain and fail to improve fit or identifiability — a genuine null-result risk to de-risk early

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab ships connectome-constrained dynamical models over FlyWire at scale, claiming the space
  • Janelia/Turaga release a calibrated connectome-to-dynamics fitting pipeline as a standard tool
  • A benchmark paper shows connectome constraints do not improve identifiability of inferred dynamics

Gets more feasible if…

  • Openly released FlyWire-paired whole-brain activity (e.g., calcium) datasets removing the pairing barrier
  • PyG-compatible connectome loaders/tooling drop, matching the applicant's existing stack
  • A tutorial on connectome-constrained RNN/state-space fitting lands
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

A Cross-Animal Foundation Model for Neural Population Activity with Calibrated Uncertainty and Information-Theoretic Latent Structure

Neural population data inference & connectomics
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Neural recordings are fragmented across animals, brain areas, probes, and tasks, so models are retrained per-session and generalise poorly. This project builds a self-supervised transformer foundation model over spiking population data (tokenising units by their tuning/statistics rather than fixed identity) that transfers across animals and datasets, paired with Bayesian/conformal calibration so downstream decoders report trustworthy uncertainty, and information-theoretic probes (redundancy/synergy, integrated information) to characterise what the learned latent code actually represents. The result is a reusable, uncertainty-aware backbone for population inference and brain-computer interfaces that works few-shot on new subjects.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.25bang-for-buck
Time to result12months @ 20h/wk + AI high AI-leverage
GOAT path: A calibrated cross-animal neural foundation model is the most fundable, most industrially-legible project here — if he builds the transformer + conformal-calibration stack he becomes exactly the profile top labs headhunt, though it is a pivot away from genomics.
Verdict: Strongest DeepMind-ladder bet of the six — pitch to neural-foundation-model groups (POYO/NDT lineage, ELLIS, industrial neuro-AI); leverage his PyTorch/Lightning strength, but be clear it trades his genomics narrative for a neural-data one.
Why fundable: Squarely aligned with AI-safety-adjacent and translational funders: calibrated uncertainty and interpretability are explicit priorities, and cross-subject transfer has clear BCI/clinical payoff. It is a scalable, compute-driven ML project with obvious deliverables (open pretrained model, benchmarks), which reads well to both AI-focused and biomedical funders.
Fits labs:
Aldo Faisal (Imperial)Pedro Mediano (Imperial)Surya Ganguli (Stanford)Ila Fiete (MIT)Gabriel Kreiman (Harvard)Mihaela van der Schaar (Cambridge)
Fits funding:
Open Phil AIKnight-HennessyImperial President's PhD ScholarshipClarendon
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • Neural foundation models are one of the hottest current directions (POYO/POYO+, Neuroformer, NDT), so the topic has strong pull
  • Self-supervised transformer skills transfer directly from mainstream ML; fully dry-lab
  • Cross-animal transfer via tuning/statistics-based tokenization targets a real, unsolved frontier (fixed unit identity is the known blocker)
  • Adding calibrated uncertainty + information-theoretic latent structure differentiates it from pure scaling work

Hidden caveats

  • Crowded AND compute-heavy: pretraining a foundation model needs scale/resources the applicant likely lacks — resource asymmetry vs big labs is severe
  • Well-funded incumbents (POYO team/Mila, IBL, Zador) dominate; scoop risk is high
  • Cross-probe/animal data harmonization is a massive, unglamorous engineering lift that eats the timeline
  • 'Tokenize by tuning' may already be partly covered (POYO-style unit embeddings) — novelty margin is thin

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab releases a scaled, calibrated cross-animal neural foundation model (a POYO successor) with open weights
  • IBL or Allen ships a standardized neural foundation model plus transfer benchmark, setting the SOTA
  • A paper demonstrably solves cross-session/cross-animal generalization, closing the motivating gap

Gets more feasible if…

  • Large standardized open spiking datasets (IBL, Allen, Neural Latents Benchmark) expand, easing pretraining/eval
  • Pretrained neural-foundation-model weights released openly so the applicant can fine-tune rather than pretrain
  • Efficient training recipes / spiking tokenizers published, or accessible compute (grants, Modal-style credits) appears
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Distribution-Shift-Aware Foundation Models for Trustworthy Medical Image Diagnosis: Calibrated Uncertainty Under Real-World Acquisition Shift

Deep learning for medical imaging
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Medical imaging foundation models (CT/MRI/chest-X-ray encoders) collapse silently when scanner vendor, protocol, or patient demographics shift at deployment, yet clinicians get no warning. This project builds a foundation-model adaptation framework coupling Bayesian/ensemble uncertainty with mechanistic distribution-shift detection, so the model abstains or flags out-of-distribution scans instead of confidently misdiagnosing. Core contribution: uncertainty calibrated per-shift-type (acquisition vs pathology vs population), validated across multi-site public cohorts.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result6months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: Owning shift-typed calibrated uncertainty on medical foundation models could make him the go-to trustworthy-deployment person, but that reputation is built on imaging encoders he has never touched, not on his sheaf/GNN edge.
Verdict: Viable but off his track — pitch to a medical-imaging or trustworthy-ML lab, not to Lio/ETH geometric-DL groups; a detour from his genomics story.
Why fundable: Directly hits patient-safety and clinical-translation priorities of Imperial President's PhD, Clarendon, and Wellcome-linked programmes; regulatory trustworthiness of AI diagnostics is a stated funder theme and a live UK/EU MDR/AI-Act concern, making it policy-relevant and translational.
Fits labs:
Ben Glocker (Imperial)Yarin Gal (Oxford)Ana Namburete (Oxford)Pranav Rajpurkar (Harvard)
Fits funding:
Imperial President's PhD ScholarshipClarendon (Oxford)MSCA Doctoral NetworksKnight-Hennessy (US route)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • Clinically urgent and well-documented failure mode: silent collapse under scanner/vendor/protocol/demographic shift is a real, named problem clinicians care about.
  • Regulatory tailwind — FDA good-ML-practice and EU AI Act increasingly demand deployment monitoring, uncertainty, and abstention, making 'trustworthy' framing fundable.
  • Buildable on existing open medical encoders (RadImageNet, medical CLIP, MedSAM) so he does not pretrain from scratch.
  • Sits at the junction of two funded topics (foundation models + calibrated uncertainty/OOD), broadening venue options.

Hidden caveats

  • Extremely saturated subfield — uncertainty calibration + OOD/domain-shift detection has hundreds of papers; incremental 'Bayesian/ensemble + shift detector' combos scoop easily.
  • Needs multi-site imaging with vendor/protocol/demographic metadata; IRB + data-access is a hard barrier for a dry-lab newcomer with no clinical collaborators lined up.
  • Validation gap: credible clinical claims require clinician-in-the-loop or prospective eval, far beyond retrospective AUROC he can produce alone.
  • Off-core for him — no graph/geometric or genomics content, so it burns his sheaf-GNN/PyG comparative advantage and offers little Lio-lab methodological synergy.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab releases a shift-robust medical imaging foundation model with built-in calibrated abstention (search: 'robust medical foundation model abstention release').
  • A WILDS-style multi-site medical benchmark paper shows current FMs are already well-calibrated under acquisition shift, closing the gap.
  • A turnkey conformal-prediction library ships medical-imaging abstention out of the box, commoditizing the method contribution.
  • MedSAM / large radiology FM updates add uncertainty quantification natively.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Release of an open multi-vendor CT/MRI/CXR harmonization dataset with rich acquisition metadata.
  • Pretrained medical FM checkpoints plus lightweight adaptation/PEFT tooling drop, removing the compute barrier.
  • Mature imaging-specific conformal/uncertainty libraries (calibration + coverage guarantees) land.
  • A tutorial or nnU-Net-style framework integrates shift detection, lowering the engineering bar.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Causal Counterfactual Diffusion Models for Medical Imaging: Generating and Auditing 'What-If' Scans to Explain and Debias Diagnostic AI

Deep learning for medical imaging
◆ cross-lab workhorse★ flagship
Clinicians distrust black-box imaging AI because it cannot answer 'what would this scan look like without the pathology, or if the patient were a different age/sex?'. This project develops causally-structured diffusion generative models producing faithful, attribute-controllable counterfactual medical images (disease, demographic, scanner), then uses them as visual explanations and as probes to detect and correct spurious/demographic shortcuts in downstream diagnostic models. Deliverable: a counterfactual-audit toolkit quantifying how much a diagnosis relies on non-causal features.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result12months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Causal diffusion counterfactuals for imaging is a genuinely field-defining direction, but leadership here demands diffusion + SCM fluency and imaging validation he entirely lacks today.
Verdict: Exciting but a long shot for him — only pursue if he first builds diffusion/causal chops; pitch to a generative-vision or causal-ML PI, not a genomics group.
Why fundable: Aligns with fairness, interpretability, and equitable-healthcare-AI priorities central to Wellcome, Gates Cambridge (social-impact framing), and Open Phil AI interpretability funding; counterfactual explanation is a live regulatory demand for clinical AI approval.
Fits labs:
Ben Glocker (Imperial)Marinka Zitnik (Harvard)Kun-Hsing Yu (Harvard)Ana Namburete (Oxford)
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeClarendon (Oxford)Imperial President's PhD ScholarshipOpen Phil AI
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~15 mo

Strengths

  • Distinctive framing — causally-structured, attribute-controllable counterfactual medical images directly answers the 'what would this look like without disease / at a different age' clinician question.
  • Strong dual narrative (explainability + debiasing) that maps cleanly onto grant/ethics funding priorities.
  • Diffusion tooling and pretrained backbones are mature and open, so generation is not the bottleneck.
  • Counterfactual explanation for imaging AI is rising but not yet commoditized, leaving a novelty window.

Hidden caveats

  • Counterfactual faithfulness has no ground truth (you never observe the same patient's alternate scan) — validation is fundamentally hard and reviewers punish hand-wavy metrics.
  • Requires committing to causal-structure assumptions clinicians and reviewers may reject; wrong SCM = plausible but misleading images.
  • Diffusion training/fine-tuning at medical resolution is compute-heavy for a student without a large GPU allocation.
  • Active incumbents (Glocker/BioMedIA-style causal generative imaging groups) can scoop; also off his graph/genomics core.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A lab open-sources a faithful, attribute-controllable counterfactual medical image generator (search: 'open counterfactual medical image generation release').
  • A standardized counterfactual-faithfulness benchmark appears and is quickly saturated.
  • Medical diffusion foundation models ship demographic/pathology conditioning natively (e.g. a medical Stable Diffusion variant).
  • A general causal image-editing SOTA from a big lab generalizes to clinical scans.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Open pretrained medical diffusion checkpoints (chest X-ray / MRI) drop, removing pretraining cost.
  • A causal counterfactual medical-imaging benchmark or paired dataset is released.
  • Structural-causal-model-based counterfactual generation libraries (e.g. deep-SCM toolkits) mature.
  • Tutorials integrating diffusion + causal conditioning land, lowering the entry bar.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

HERON: A Self-Supervised Multimodal Foundation Model for the ICU with Calibrated Uncertainty

Multimodal clinical time-series foundation models
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Pre-train a single transformer-based foundation model on irregularly-sampled ICU streams (high-frequency vitals waveforms, sparse labs, medication events, and free-text clinical notes) using masked multimodal self-supervision, so one backbone transfers to deterioration, sepsis onset, and length-of-stay across hospitals. Every prediction is wrapped in a calibrated, decision-aware uncertainty estimate (deep-ensemble / conformal) so clinicians see 'the model does not know' rather than a confident wrong number. Evaluation is fully retrospective on public multi-centre EHR (MIMIC-IV, eICU, HiRID) with external-hospital generalisation as the headline metric.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result10months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: A calibrated multimodal ICU backbone is fundable and useful, but it is clinical infrastructure work far from the geometric/genomics identity that would actually make him a world leader.
Verdict: Solid but generic clinical-ML — pitch to an ICU/clinical-foundation-model lab; wrong vehicle for his GNN/Lio ambition.
Why fundable: Hits three converging funder priorities at once: AI-for-health translational impact (Wellcome, CCAIM, Imperial President's), safe/trustworthy AI via uncertainty quantification (Open Phil AI, Yarin Gal's group), and foundation-model scale that flagship programmes want to back. HiRID is Ratsch's own ICU benchmark, giving an obvious ETH salaried-PhD route; van der Schaar's CCAIM explicitly funds ML-for-medicine with no wet-lab requirement, matching the applicant's dry-lab profile.
Fits labs:
Gunnar Ratsch (ETH) - ICU foundation models / clinical time-seriesMihaela van der Schaar (Cambridge) - ML for medicine, no bio neededMichael Moor (ETH) - medical foundation models / LLMsNeil Lawrence (Cambridge) - probabilistic ML for healthYarin Gal (Oxford) - Bayesian DL / uncertaintyMarzyeh Ghassemi (MIT) - ML healthcare / fairness
Fits funding:
ETH salaried PhD position (Ratsch/Moor)Cambridge CCAIM (van der Schaar)Gates Cambridge / Cambridge TrustClarendon (Oxford, Gal)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~10 mo

Strengths

  • Data barrier is low — MIMIC-IV, MIMIC-IV-ECG/waveform, eICU are open, so masked multimodal SSL is directly runnable.
  • Masked multimodal self-supervision is a well-established, reproducible recipe with clear precedent to build on.
  • Multi-task transfer (deterioration, sepsis, length-of-stay) from one backbone is a compelling, evaluable story.
  • Calibrated-uncertainty wrapper adds a trust dimension that differentiates from pure predictors and aligns with Lio's multimodal clinical interests.

Hidden caveats

  • Very crowded and fast-moving — multiple ICU/EHR foundation-model papers already exist; low differentiation risks a 'me-too' backbone.
  • Irregular sampling + waveform/text/labs fusion is technically demanding for someone still learning PyG/PyTorch, with high engineering overhead.
  • Cross-hospital generalization claims need data beyond MIMIC (single-institution bias); true multi-site eval is hard to secure.
  • Pretraining a multimodal transformer on high-frequency waveforms is compute-hungry; off his graph/genomics core.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab releases an open ICU/EHR multimodal foundation model covering deterioration/sepsis/LOS (search: 'open ICU foundation model release multimodal').
  • A benchmark paper shows multimodal SSL yields only marginal gains over simple gradient-boosted or unimodal baselines.
  • An industry clinical time-series FM (successor to general TS FMs like MOMENT/Chronos) is extended to multimodal EHR.
  • A shared multi-hospital ICU benchmark standardizes the tasks and a strong published model tops it.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Open pretrained multimodal clinical checkpoints are released for fine-tuning.
  • A new multi-hospital ICU dataset with waveforms + notes goes open-access.
  • Libraries for irregular multimodal SSL (masking, time-aware attention) mature into plug-and-play tooling.
  • A reproducible MIMIC multimodal pipeline/tutorial lands, cutting setup time.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

What-If Vitals: A Counterfactual Foundation Model for Treatment-Effect Estimation on Clinical Time Series

Multimodal clinical time-series foundation models
◆ cross-lab workhorse★ flagship
Build a foundation model that answers interventional 'what-if' questions on multimodal ICU trajectories: given a patient's vitals, labs, and medication history, predict the counterfactual trajectory under an alternative treatment (e.g. earlier vasopressors, different fluid strategy). It marries sequence-modelling pre-training with causal structure (potential outcomes / continuous-time treatment effects) and adjusts for time-varying confounding via balancing representations. Validated retrospectively against held-out treatment policies and, where available, RCT-derived effect sizes.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort0.8bang-for-buck
Time to result14months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Transferable counterfactual treatment-effect models on time series is deep, hot causal territory, but time-varying confounding on irregular streams is a brutal first PhD with no causal-inference or first-author track record behind him.
Verdict: High-ceiling but high-risk — pitch to a dedicated causal-ML/clinical-time-series PI; too far from his current skills to be a safe bet.
Why fundable: Directly serves the funder narrative that AI should support decisions, not just predictions - the flagship theme of van der Schaar's CCAIM and a stated Wellcome/NIHR priority. Causal ML for medicine is precisely fundable at Cambridge (van der Schaar), MIT (Uhler, Ghassemi) and via Knight-Hennessy. Its safety-and-reliability framing (avoiding confidently wrong treatment recommendations) maps onto Open Phil AI and trustworthy-AI calls.
Fits labs:
Mihaela van der Schaar (Cambridge) - counterfactual/causal ML for medicineCaroline Uhler (MIT) - causal inference / generative modelsGunnar Ratsch (ETH) - clinical time-seriesMarzyeh Ghassemi (MIT) - ML healthcare / fairnessChris Holmes (Oxford) - Bayesian statisticsJulia Vogt (ETH) - interpretable ML / medical
Fits funding:
Cambridge CCAIM (van der Schaar)Knight-Hennessy (Stanford, feeds MIT-adjacent US route)Guaranteed US PhD funding (MIT, Uhler/Ghassemi)Clarendon (Oxford, Holmes)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~15 mo

Strengths

  • More novel than prediction-only ICU FMs — interventional 'what-if' trajectory prediction has clearer scientific differentiation.
  • High clinical value (treatment timing/selection, personalization) supports strong funding and impact narrative.
  • Marries sequence pretraining with potential-outcomes/causal structure, a defensible methodological contribution.
  • Builds on open ICU data (MIMIC/eICU) and existing causal-sequence literature (G-Net, TE-CDE, Causal Transformer) for grounding.

Hidden caveats

  • Counterfactual/treatment-effect validation on observational ICU data has no ground truth — confounding and treatment-timing bias make claims fragile and reviewer-contested.
  • Requires combining causal inference and sequence modeling expertise — a steep double learning curve for someone without a first paper.
  • Semi-crowded: Causal Transformer / TE-CDE lineage means novelty must be sharply carved.
  • FM-scale pretraining plus causal machinery is compute- and engineering-heavy; off his graph core (though causal structure could be given graph flavor to reconnect to his skills).

Gets obsolete if…

  • A SOTA causal sequence model (Causal Transformer successor) saturates ICU CATE/counterfactual benchmarks (search: 'causal transformer ICU treatment effect SOTA 2026').
  • A lab open-sources a counterfactual clinical foundation model for treatment effects.
  • A standardized EHR treatment-effect estimation benchmark emerges and is quickly topped.
  • Foundation-model + do-calculus / potential-outcomes tooling ships as a library.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Semi-synthetic ICU causal benchmarks with known ground-truth counterfactuals are released (enabling real validation).
  • Open pretrained clinical trajectory models drop for fine-tuning.
  • Causal-inference-for-time-series libraries (EconML-style extensions to sequences) mature.
  • A reproducible tutorial on counterfactual trajectory modeling on MIMIC lands.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

FedICU-DP: Differentially Private Federated Foundation Models for Multimodal ICU Time-Series

Federated / privacy-preserving ML for hospitals
Train a single foundation model over irregular, multimodal ICU data (vitals, labs, notes, waveforms) held privately at many hospitals, using federated learning with per-hospital differential-privacy budgets and lightweight local personalization heads so no raw patient data ever leaves the site. The core contribution is making DP-SGD usable for large sequence/transformer models on ragged clinical time-series while mapping the privacy-utility-fairness frontier across sites of very different size and case-mix. Deliverable: an auditable federated pretraining recipe plus formal membership-inference and reconstruction-attack evaluations a hospital data-governance board would accept.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.6bang-for-buck
Time to result12months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Making DP-SGD work at foundation-model scale on ragged ICU data is real systems research, but it is privacy-engineering, not the geometric/biological science where he could lead.
Verdict: Avoid — systems/DP heavy, low overlap with his skills and target labs, and not what DeepMind-tier labs recruit on.
Why fundable: Directly matches CCAIM's mission (ML for medicine, privacy, real hospital data) and van der Schaar / Raetsch programme priorities; DP + federated clinical ML is exactly the trustworthy-AI-in-health theme Clarendon, Gates Cambridge, Cambridge Trust, Imperial President's and MSCA doctoral networks fund, and ETH/KI run salaried positions on clinical foundation models. No wet-lab cost and no data-access blocker because data never moves.
Fits labs:
Mihaela van der SchaarGunnar RaetschMichael MoorMarzyeh GhassemiNeil Lawrence
Fits funding:
CCAIM (Cambridge Centre for AI in Medicine)ClarendonGates CambridgeCambridge Trust
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • Targets a genuine deployment blocker — hospitals cannot pool raw patient data — so federated + DP has real-world pull and policy relevance.
  • Combining DP-SGD, federation, multimodal FM, and personalization heads is a hard, clearly publishable stack.
  • Privacy-regulation tailwind (GDPR/HIPAA, EU AI Act) strengthens funding case.
  • Personalization heads give a concrete, tractable sub-contribution even if the full system is ambitious.

Hidden caveats

  • Core scientific bet may be near-unsolvable at the claimed scale — DP-SGD at foundation-model size typically craters utility; the central promise could fail to hold.
  • Real multi-hospital federated infrastructure is hard for a dry-lab student to access; simulated federation is much less convincing to reviewers.
  • Stacks four hard subfields (FL + DP + multimodal + FM) — severe scope/overreach risk for someone without a first paper.
  • Heavy systems engineering and compute; entirely off his graph/genomics core with little methodological reuse.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A lab demonstrates DP-SGD foundation-model training at scale with acceptable utility (search: 'differentially private large model training utility 2026').
  • An open federated clinical foundation-model framework is released.
  • A benchmark shows DP-federated gives little over centralized training on shared synthetic data.
  • A new DP optimizer/mechanism from privacy research closes the utility gap and is packaged in a library.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open federated ICU testbed simulating multiple realistic hospital sites is released.
  • DP training libraries (Opacus successors) improve utility at large scale.
  • Pretrained federated-friendly / DP-compatible clinical checkpoints drop.
  • A Flower-based multimodal ICU federated tutorial lands, reducing setup effort.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

FedCausal: Privacy-Preserving Federated Estimation of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects Across Hospitals

Federated / privacy-preserving ML for hospitals
Most federated hospital ML predicts outcomes; this project instead estimates causal treatment effects (who benefits from which intervention) across hospitals that never share raw records, combining federated CATE estimators with differential privacy and privacy-preserving synthetic-data exchange to overcome site-level confounding and covariate shift. It tackles the hard problem that pooling causal evidence across sites requires reconciling different confounding structures and treatment policies without leaking patient-level data. Output: a federated causal-inference framework with validity guarantees under site heterogeneity plus DP-certified synthetic bridge datasets that let a new hospital calibrate without exposing patients.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.6bang-for-buck
Time to result17months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Federated causal effect estimation is intellectually rich and largely unaddressed, but combining federated + DP + causal inference is three unfamiliar hard fields at once with no paper to anchor credibility.
Verdict: Avoid for him — triple-stacked difficulty far from his geometric-DL/genomics core; only for a dedicated federated-causal lab.
Why fundable: Causal ML for medicine under privacy is a flagship van der Schaar / CCAIM theme and fits funder rhetoric on safe, decision-useful clinical AI; strongly fundable by Clarendon, Gates Cambridge, Cambridge Trust, Imperial President's, MSCA, and ETH/KI salaried tracks. Its policy relevance (evidence synthesis without data pooling) also suits Wellcome/health-mission framing.
Fits labs:
Mihaela van der SchaarJulia VogtNeil LawrenceCaroline UhlerMarzyeh Ghassemi
Fits funding:
CCAIM (Cambridge Centre for AI in Medicine)ClarendonGates CambridgeCambridge Trust
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~17 mo

Strengths

  • Less crowded than federated prediction — federated + heterogeneous treatment effects + DP + synthetic-exchange is a distinctive combination.
  • Tackles a real unsolved problem (site-level confounding and covariate shift across hospitals) with clear clinical/policy value.
  • Causal-federated framing differentiates from the flood of federated outcome predictors.
  • Theory-flavored niche gives longer shelf life and room for a clean methodological contribution rather than a leaderboard chase.

Hidden caveats

  • Triple difficulty — causal + federated + DP simultaneously — is very high risk for an applicant with no first-author paper yet.
  • Validating cross-site causal effects without RCT ground truth is nearly intractable; claims will be contested.
  • Privacy-preserving synthetic-data exchange carries its own fidelity-vs-privacy pitfalls that can undermine the causal estimates.
  • Requires multi-hospital data or a convincing simulation; small specialized community raises venue/reviewer risk; off his graph core.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A paper solves federated CATE under DP and standardizes a benchmark (search: 'federated heterogeneous treatment effect differential privacy benchmark 2026').
  • A federated causal-inference library is released, commoditizing the estimator contribution.
  • A privacy-preserving synthetic-data-exchange method is shown to dominate federated CATE approaches.
  • A major lab ships a privacy-preserving multi-site causal framework.

Gets more feasible if…

  • A multi-site semi-synthetic causal benchmark with known ground-truth heterogeneous effects is released.
  • Federated learning + causal inference tooling integrates (e.g. Flower + EconML-style CATE).
  • Open high-fidelity clinical synthetic-data generators improve, enabling credible exchange experiments.
  • A tutorial on federated CATE estimation lands, lowering the methodological barrier.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Uncertainty-Aware Subgroup Robustness for Clinical Time-Series Foundation Models

Fairness & robustness in clinical ML
◆ cross-lab workhorse
ICU foundation models (trained on MIMIC/eICU/HiRID) degrade silently under distribution shift across hospitals and demographic subgroups, yet are increasingly deployed for early-warning scoring. This project builds a distribution-shift-robust fine-tuning and calibration framework that couples subgroup-conditional conformal prediction with a learned deferral policy, so the model flags when its risk estimate is untrustworthy for a given patient subgroup instead of producing confidently wrong predictions. Deliverables: worst-subgroup calibration guarantees, a selective-prediction ICU pipeline, and a cross-hospital robustness benchmark.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you3/5
10-yr impact2/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result7months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: He could own subgroup-conditional conformal prediction for clinical time-series, but it is a safety-engineering niche, not a field he could tower over from a GNN background.
Verdict: Only a backup — tractable with his PyTorch skills but off his GNN core and DeepMind-irrelevant; pitch to clinical-ML / trustworthy-AI health groups, not ELLIS.
Why fundable: Directly targets Wellcome/NHS-relevant priorities on safe, equitable clinical AI and Gates Cambridge's social-impact remit; van der Schaar and Ratsch both run funded ICU/foundation-model programmes, making it a natural fit for salaried ETH positions and Cambridge Trust/CCAIM support. Health-equity framing plays strongly to Gates Cambridge and Harding.
