Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~15 mo

Strengths

  • Real deployment pain point: ICU early-warning scores silently degrade across hospitals and subgroups, so robustness+calibration has clear clinical demand and reviewer sympathy.
  • Conformal prediction and selective deferral are mathematically clean, publishable in small increments, and match a dry-lab applicant who lacks wet-lab or first-author output.
  • Public benchmarks (MIMIC-IV, eICU, HiRID) allow reproducible cross-hospital shift experiments without new data collection or heavy compute.
  • Sits at intersection of his GNN/geometric-DL background (patient graphs, irregular time series) and a hot fairness+uncertainty topic funders like.

Hidden caveats

  • Crowded and incremental: conformal + subgroup fairness + deferral each have large 2023-2025 literatures; combining them risks reading as engineering, not novel method.
  • MIMIC/eICU credentialing (PhysioNet DUA + CITI training) plus messy irregular-sampling preprocessing eats months before any modeling.
  • Subgroup-conditional conformal has a known coverage-vs-sample-size tension; rare demographic strata may have too few events for honest guarantees, a validation trap newcomers miss.
  • 'ICU foundation model' framing overpromises: few strong pretrained ICU FMs are openly available, so he may end up training weak backbones himself, undercutting the FM claim.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab releases an openly-calibrated clinical time-series foundation model with built-in subgroup robustness (e.g. a MIMIC/eICU-pretrained FM shipping conformal guarantees).
  • A benchmark/survey paper formalizes cross-hospital subgroup-conformal ICU evaluation and reports it as largely solved.
  • Regulatory guidance (FDA/EMA) standardizes uncertainty reporting for early-warning scores, commoditizing the calibration contribution.
  • A high-profile paper shows subgroup-conditional conformal offers no clinical benefit over simpler recalibration, deflating novelty.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open pretrained ICU/EHR foundation model (HiRID/MIMIC-scale) is released with fine-tuning tooling, removing the backbone-training barrier.
  • A maintained library lands for subgroup/Mondrian conformal prediction integrated with PyTorch (like a MAPIE-for-deep-learning).
  • A ready cross-hospital shift benchmark (aligned MIMIC-IV/eICU/HiRID splits) is published, removing preprocessing overhead.
  • A tutorial on learned-deferral / selective prediction for clinical time series drops with reusable code.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Causally-Invariant Representations for Robust and Fair Medical Imaging Across Sites and Demographics

Fairness & robustness in clinical ML
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Medical imaging models exploit spurious shortcuts (scanner make, hospital, demographic proxies encoded in pixels) that inflate accuracy in-distribution but cause fairness gaps and failures on new sites. This project develops a causal, invariant-representation learning approach that explicitly separates disease signal from acquisition/demographic confounders, audited with counterfactual subgroup stress tests and shortcut-detection probes. The output is a deployable framework that certifies which subgroups and sites a model is safe for and quantifies residual demographic shortcut leakage.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.75bang-for-buck
Time to result9.5months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Causal invariance for imaging is intellectually deep, but he has no causal-inference track record and imaging is only tangential to his dry-lab genomics/GNN trajectory.
Verdict: Risky pivot — causal invariance can silently fail empirically for a newcomer; pursue only if paired with a causal-ML co-supervisor, pitch to medical-imaging fairness groups.
Why fundable: Fairness and robustness in imaging is a flagship concern for the NHS AI Lab, Wellcome, and the Imperial President's scheme; Glocker and Rajpurkar lead exactly this agenda and attract clinical-translation funding. Strong equity narrative suits Gates Cambridge/Knight-Hennessy and the Clarendon Fund's excellence-plus-impact criteria.
Fits labs:
Ben GlockerPranav RajpurkarAna NambureteMarzyeh GhassemiFinale Doshi-VelezKun-Hsing Yu
Fits funding:
Imperial President'sClarendonGates CambridgeKnight-Hennessy
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~18 mo

Strengths

  • Shortcut learning (scanner/site/demographic proxies) in medical imaging is a well-documented, high-visibility problem with reproducible failure demos, giving a clear motivating story.
  • Causal / invariant-representation framing (IRM, causal disentanglement) connects to principled theory, appealing for a peer aiming at top-tier methodological rigor.
  • Abundant public multi-site imaging datasets (CheXpert, MIMIC-CXR, CAMELYON, fastMRI, ISIC) enable cross-site generalization studies without new acquisition.
  • Fairness+robustness angle attracts grant/fellowship funding and pairs with his geometric-DL toolkit (equivariance, invariance are geometric notions).

Hidden caveats

  • IRM and invariant-representation methods have a documented reputation for fragility and failing to beat tuned ERM/reweighting baselines, a scoop-yourself risk on negative results.
  • Very crowded: domain generalization for medical imaging is a mature subfield with dozens of yearly papers; genuine novelty is hard to carve.
  • Causal claims require assumptions (known confounders, interventions) rarely satisfied in retrospective imaging, exposing a hidden validation/identifiability gap.
  • Heavier GPU cost than tabular/time-series work; multi-site imaging training is compute-hungry for someone still learning the stack.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A large medical-imaging foundation model (e.g. a successor to RETFound/CXR foundation models) demonstrates that scale alone closes most site/demographic gaps.
  • A benchmark paper shows well-tuned ERM + simple reweighting matches causal-invariant methods on standard multi-site fairness tasks.
  • A widely-adopted debiasing/harmonization tool (image-level scanner normalization) becomes standard preprocessing, absorbing the contribution.
  • A definitive survey declares causal invariant representation learning for imaging saturated or negative.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open multi-site, demographically-annotated imaging benchmark with standardized shift splits is released.
  • A pretrained medical imaging encoder with published site/demographic metadata drops, letting him fine-tune instead of train from scratch.
  • A maintained causal-representation / invariance library for vision (plug-in penalties, disentanglement) matures with tutorials.
  • Cheap fine-tuning recipes (LoRA/adapters for medical vision FMs) lower the compute barrier.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Calibrated Agentic Knowledge-Graph Construction: LLMs that Extract, Verify, and Quantify Uncertainty over Causal Biomedical Claims from Literature

LLMs for biomedical knowledge & literature
◆ cross-lab workhorse★ flagship
Build an agentic LLM system that reads primary literature (PubMed/PMC full text plus preprints) and incrementally constructs a provenance-tracked causal knowledge graph of gene-disease-drug-mechanism claims, where every edge carries a calibrated confidence score and links back to sentence-level evidence. The core research is turning free-text extraction into a statistically calibrated inference problem: distinguishing correlational from causal claims, resolving contradictory reports across papers, and abstaining or flagging when evidence is weak or hallucinated. This attacks the central trust barrier blocking LLMs from real biomedical use.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result5months @ 20h/wk + AI high AI-leverage
GOAT path: Calibrated, causal, provenance-tracked biomedical KGs are a genuine open problem; if he nailed abstention-aware edge confidence he could define the standard, but it demands LLM-agent engineering he doesn't yet have.
Verdict: Hot and high-ceiling but far from his GNN strengths and heavy on LLM-ops he lacks; pitch to LLM-for-science / biomedical-NLP groups if he's willing to pivot hard.
Why fundable: Directly hits funder priorities in trustworthy/safe AI for medicine (van der Schaar/CCAIM agenda, Open Phil AI safety, Wellcome data-for-science mission). Calibration plus provenance is a rigorous, evaluable ML contribution rather than a vague 'apply an LLM', which reviewers reward. Deliverables (a benchmark, an open KG, a calibration method) are concrete milestones that fit a 3-4 year funded timeline.
Fits labs:
Mihaela van der Schaar (Cambridge)Neil Lawrence (Cambridge)Marinka Zitnik (Harvard)Zak Kohane (Harvard)Yarin Gal (Oxford)Michael Moor (ETH)
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeClarendonCCAIM (Cambridge Centre for AI in Medicine)Open Phil AI PhD Fellowship
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~9 mo

Strengths

  • Agentic literature-to-knowledge-graph with calibrated, provenance-linked edges is timely, funder-friendly, and directly leverages his lab's biomedical KG interests (Lio group).
  • Provenance + sentence-level evidence + calibrated confidence is a concrete, evaluable contribution beyond generic 'LLM extracts triples' work.
  • Causal biomedical KGs feed naturally into GNN/graph-reasoning downstream, aligning with his geometric-DL and PyG learning path.
  • Rich free inputs (PubMed/PMC full text, preprints) and existing KGs (PrimeKG, Hetionet) give ready scaffolding and evaluation anchors.

Hidden caveats

  • Extremely fast-moving and crowded: agentic RAG + biomedical extraction pipelines ship monthly, so an engineering-heavy system risks being obsolete before submission.
  • Calibration of extracted-claim confidence lacks gold-standard causal ground truth; building a trustworthy eval set is a large, underestimated annotation burden.
  • Heavy dependence on closed LLM APIs (cost, reproducibility, rate limits) or large local models he may not have compute to run at PMC scale.
  • Reviewers increasingly skeptical of 'LLM builds KG' novelty unless there is a real methodological/statistical core, not just prompt orchestration.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab or company releases a calibrated biomedical claim-extraction / causal-KG model or dataset at scale (e.g. a PMC-wide provenance-tracked KG).
  • A benchmark for evidence-grounded biomedical claim verification with confidence calibration is published and quickly topped.
  • General-purpose agentic RAG frameworks ship built-in citation-faithfulness + uncertainty, commoditizing the pipeline.
  • A frontier model demonstrates near-solved sentence-level biomedical relation extraction with calibrated confidence out of the box.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open full-text mining corpus with gold causal-claim + provenance annotations is released for training/eval.
  • A cheap, strong open-weight long-context model suitable for full-text extraction drops, removing API cost/reproducibility barriers.
  • A maintained agentic-RAG + KG-construction toolkit with calibration hooks lands (LangGraph/DSPy-style) with biomedical templates.
  • PMC/BioC tooling improvements make full-text ingestion and sentence-linking turnkey.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Citation-Faithful Retrieval-Augmented LLMs for Clinical-Grade Rare-Disease Variant Interpretation

LLMs for biomedical knowledge & literature
Develop a retrieval-augmented LLM agent that automates ACMG/AMP-style variant classification by grounding each evidence criterion in retrieved literature and structured databases (ClinVar, gnomAD, regulatory-variant resources), producing an auditable, citation-faithful evidence report for a given rare-disease variant. The research questions are faithfulness (every asserted claim traceable to a real, correctly-interpreted source), handling of regulatory/non-coding variants where literature is sparse, and measuring how often the model fabricates or misreads evidence versus expert curators. Output is a decision-support draft a clinical geneticist can verify in minutes instead of hours.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.75bang-for-buck
Time to result6.5months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: Citation-faithful ACMG variant reports are clinically valuable but a curation-heavy niche — world-leadership here means becoming a clinical-genomics informatics figure, not a geometric-DL one.
Verdict: Weakest fit for him — needs clinical-genetics expertise and RAG engineering he lacks, and it won't ladder to industrial labs; pitch only to genomic-medicine groups.
Why fundable: Squarely aligned with Sanger/EMBL-EBI and Wellcome rare-disease and human-genetics priorities and with Whiffin's regulatory-variant program; addresses a real curation bottleneck with clear clinical impact. Faithfulness/hallucination measurement is a fundable safety contribution. A working pipeline plus benchmark is a defensible thesis with translational appeal that funders and interview panels value.
Fits labs:
Nicola Whiffin (Oxford)Chris Holmes (Oxford)Christopher Yau (Oxford)Bonnie Berger (MIT)Zak Kohane (Harvard)Michael Moor (ETH)
Fits funding:
Wellcome Sanger PhD programmeEMBL-EBI studentshipsClarendonHarding Distinguished Postgraduate Scholars
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • ACMG/AMP variant classification is a concrete, high-value clinical workflow with structured criteria, making faithfulness and auditability naturally measurable.
  • Citation-faithful RAG grounded in ClinVar/gnomAD is exactly the kind of trustworthy-LLM contribution reviewers and clinical funders reward.
  • Rich structured resources (ClinVar, gnomAD, regulatory-variant DBs) plus ACMG rubric give strong, reproducible evaluation scaffolding.
  • Rare-disease framing has strong translational and funding appeal, and dry-lab-only work suits his profile.

Hidden caveats

  • Clinical-grade claim invites a validation gap he cannot easily close: real ACMG assessment needs curated expert gold labels and possibly clinical oversight.
  • Faithfulness/hallucination in citation-grounded LLMs is a saturated topic; distinguishing a genuine method from prompt+retrieval engineering is hard.
  • Existing tools (Franklin, InterVar, tools from Genomics England / ClinGen) already automate much ACMG scoring, threatening perceived novelty.
  • ClinVar label noise and circularity (models learning from the same DB used for eval) is a subtle contamination trap newcomers miss.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major clinical-genomics group or company ships a validated, citation-faithful LLM ACMG classifier (e.g. ClinGen/Genomics England or a foundation-model vendor).
  • A benchmark for auditable, evidence-grounded variant interpretation is released and rapidly saturated.
  • ClinGen expands automated, machine-readable ACMG rule implementations, reducing the room for an LLM contribution.
  • A frontier model demonstrates reliable faithful clinical evidence synthesis, collapsing the faithfulness research angle.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open, expertly-labeled ACMG evaluation set of variants with evidence citations is released.
  • A maintained RAG toolkit with citation-faithfulness metrics and clinical-genomics connectors (ClinVar/gnomAD APIs) lands.
  • Regulatory-variant resources release clean, queryable APIs lowering the data-integration burden.
  • An open long-context biomedical model tuned for evidence attribution drops, cutting cost and improving grounding.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Calibrated Deep Variant Callers: Uncertainty-Aware Genotyping for Rare and Structural Variants Across Ancestries

Statistical genomics & variant calling ML
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Current deep variant callers (DeepVariant, DeepTrio) output miscalibrated confidence scores that collapse on rare variants, low-coverage regions, and non-European ancestries, causing false negatives in clinical rare-disease pipelines. This project builds a Bayesian deep-learning variant caller that emits genuinely calibrated per-genotype uncertainty, propagates it downstream into association testing, and is explicitly evaluated for ancestry-transferable calibration on genome-in-a-bottle plus diverse population panels. The output is a caller whose error bars can be trusted enough to gate clinical reporting.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you3/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result16months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Ancestry-audited, downstream-propagated calibration in variant calling is underserved and sits closest to his real HPC/genomics (PopoolationTE2) experience, so he could plausibly lead the calibration-for-equity corner.
Verdict: Best effort-to-fit ratio here given his genomics/HPC background; solid but not DeepMind-facing — pitch to statistical-genomics / population-genomics groups.
Why fundable: Directly targets health-equity and rare-disease priorities of Wellcome and NIHR-adjacent funders, and the uncertainty-quantification angle maps cleanly onto Yarin Gal's and Neil Lawrence's programmatic agendas, which strengthens named-supervisor fit letters that Clarendon/Gates panels weight heavily. Rare-disease clinical utility gives a concrete impact narrative for Wellcome and Cambridge Trust.
Fits labs:
Yarin GalNicola WhiffinChris HolmesNeil LawrenceJerome KelleherChristopher Yau
Fits funding:
ClarendonGates CambridgeCambridge TrustWellcome Sanger PhD
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~22 mo

Strengths

  • Miscalibration of deep variant callers on rare/structural variants and non-European ancestries is a documented, clinically consequential gap with equity framing funders prize.
  • Bayesian deep learning for calibrated per-genotype uncertainty is a principled, methodologically deep contribution suited to a rigorous applicant.
  • Strong public infrastructure (GIAB truth sets, PrecisionFDA, DeepVariant/DeepTrio open code, diverse-ancestry cohorts like H3Africa/1000G) enables reproducible benchmarking.
  • Ancestry-fairness angle is durable and less trend-driven than LLM topics, giving a longer shelf life.

Hidden caveats

  • Genomics-engineering heavy: BAM/CRAM handling, pileup generation, and variant-calling pipelines have a steep learning curve for someone new to statistical genomics.
  • Retraining DeepVariant-scale callers is compute- and data-intensive; honest calibration on rare variants needs large labeled sets he may lack.
  • Truth-set coverage for structural variants and diverse ancestries is itself incomplete, so the very validation he needs is a moving, contested target.
  • Structural-variant calling is a distinct hard problem; conflating SNV/indel calibration with SV may overextend the scope.

Gets obsolete if…

  • Google/DeepVariant or Illumina/DRAGEN ships a well-calibrated, ancestry-robust caller with uncertainty outputs, absorbing the core contribution.
  • A benchmark paper (PrecisionFDA-style) establishes calibrated cross-ancestry variant-calling metrics and reports it largely solved.
  • Expanded diverse GIAB/pangenome truth sets make ancestry gaps close under existing callers.
  • A pangenome-reference-based calling pipeline demonstrably removes reference-bias-driven miscalibration across ancestries.

Gets more feasible if…

  • A released diverse-ancestry, structural-variant-inclusive truth benchmark with easy tooling lowers the validation barrier.
  • An open, modular deep-variant-caller codebase with pluggable uncertainty heads (Bayesian/ensemble) drops.
  • Pretrained pileup encoders or foundation models for read data are released for fine-tuning.
  • A tutorial/library integrating pysam pileups with PyTorch calibration pipelines matures.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Sequence-to-Effect Foundation Models for Regulatory Variant Interpretation with Population-Scale Statistical Validation

Statistical genomics & variant calling ML
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Non-coding regulatory variants dominate GWAS hits but remain the hardest to interpret, and genomic language models (Enformer-style, DNA LMs) predict molecular effects without honest statistical linkage to disease. This project fine-tunes a sequence-to-function foundation model to predict regulatory variant effects, then validates predictions through fine-mapping and burden/association testing on population biobanks, closing the loop between deep-learning effect prediction and rigorous statistical genetics. The deliverable is a variant-effect prior that measurably improves fine-mapping resolution.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.25bang-for-buck
Time to result20months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Bridging genomic foundation-model effect predictions to fine-mapping and calibrated association power is exactly the frontier AlphaGenome-style labs are chasing — if he built the honest statistical bridge he'd be genuinely field-shaping.
Verdict: Highest ceiling and the one true DeepMind/Isomorphic ladder here — ambitious and compute/stats-heavy for him, but pitch it to genomic-FM and statistical-genetics groups (and it's the only one that rivals his GNN dream).
Why fundable: Sits at the intersection of foundation models and statistical genetics, two areas funders are actively resourcing; the biobank-validation component gives a translational health story for Wellcome/Sanger while the ML-methods core fits Open Phil AI-for-science framing. Strong named-lab fit makes it a credible Clarendon/Gates proposal.
Fits labs:
Anshul KundajeOlga TroyanskayaChristopher YauChris HolmesPietro LiòNa Cai
Fits funding:
ClarendonGates CambridgeCambridge TrustEMBL-EBI
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~10 mo

Strengths

  • Non-coding regulatory-variant interpretation is the acknowledged frontier of GWAS follow-up, giving high scientific importance and a clear unmet need.
  • Coupling sequence-to-function models (Enformer/Borzoi-style, DNA LMs) with honest statistical fine-mapping/colocalization validation is a genuinely differentiated, publishable idea.
  • Population-scale validation (UK Biobank / Open Targets Genetics / eQTL catalogs) provides real ground-truth linkage rather than molecular proxies alone.
  • Directly aligns with his genomics + geometric-DL trajectory and the Lio lab's genomics interests; the statistical-linkage angle is a defensible novelty core.

Hidden caveats

  • Compute wall: fine-tuning Enformer/Borzoi-class models needs large GPU/TPU budgets that a starting PhD without allocation may not have.
  • Sequence-to-function models are known to predict variant effects poorly (low correlation with measured eQTL/allelic effects), a hard, possibly intractable core risk.
  • Fine-mapping + colocalization is a deep statistical-genetics subfield; doing it rigorously demands expertise he must acquire, and getting it wrong invalidates the whole claim.
  • Crowded and fast-moving: DNA LM and enhancer-effect prediction is a hotbed with well-resourced labs, high scoop risk.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab releases a calibrated DNA/regulatory foundation model at scale that reliably predicts variant effects and links to disease (e.g. an Enformer/Borzoi successor with honest effect-size calibration).
  • A benchmark paper establishes standardized regulatory-variant effect + fine-mapping evaluation and reports strong solved performance.
  • AlphaGenome-class or Google DeepMind regulatory models ship population-linked, statistically-validated variant effect predictions.
  • A study conclusively shows current sequence models cannot recover personal/variant effects, killing the fine-tuning premise (or validating a pivot).

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open, pretrained regulatory/DNA foundation model with released weights and cheap fine-tuning recipes (LoRA/adapters) drops.
  • A ready benchmark linking predicted regulatory effects to fine-mapped GWAS/eQTL loci is published with tooling.
  • Open population-scale summary-stat + fine-mapping resources (Open Targets Genetics, eQTL Catalogue APIs) expand with turnkey access.
  • Free/subsidized TPU or GPU access (e.g. via a genomics-model provider) removes the training-compute barrier.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Calibrated Sequence-to-Function Models for Rare Regulatory Variant Interpretation

Regulatory / epigenomics deep learning
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Train and extend Borzoi/Enformer-class sequence-to-function models (DNA sequence -> multi-assay epigenomic and expression tracks) with principled uncertainty quantification so that non-coding variant effect predictions come with calibrated confidence intervals. The core deliverable is a model that flags which rare, personal-genome regulatory variants it can and cannot reliably score, then benchmarks against MPRA and clinical rare-disease cohorts. This closes the gap between impressive average accuracy and the per-variant reliability that clinical genomics actually needs.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result7.5months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: If he owns calibrated per-variant uncertainty tied to clinical rare-variant benchmarks, he becomes the person clinical genomics trusts to say 'this Enformer/Borzoi call is reliable' — a genuinely open lane.
Verdict: Strong DeepMind/AlphaGenome-track bet that needs no PyTorch Geometric; pitch to regulatory-genomics and clinical variant labs, but note it pulls him off his GNN/sheaf identity toward CNN/transformer seq models and heavy compute.
Why fundable: Directly targets rare-disease genome interpretation, a stated priority of Wellcome, health-focused Clarendon/Cambridge Trust projects, and Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds basic-biomedicine remit. Non-coding variant interpretation is an acknowledged unsolved bottleneck in diagnostic genomics, giving a clear translational hook for funders without requiring wet-lab work from the applicant.
Fits labs:
Anshul Kundaje (Stanford)David Gifford (MIT)Christopher Yau (Oxford)Yarin Gal (Oxford)Nicola Whiffin (Oxford)Chris Holmes (Oxford)
Fits funding:
Clarendon (Oxford)Cambridge TrustWellcome (Sanger/Oxford PhD programmes)Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • Targets a genuine, unsolved gap: non-coding variant interpretation is dominated by point predictions with no calibrated uncertainty, so the UQ angle is real methodological whitespace.
  • Builds on open pretrained weights (Enformer, Borzoi) rather than pretraining from scratch, making it tractable for a dry-lab student without a huge compute budget.
  • Conformal / Bayesian UQ maps cleanly onto his theory-leaning GDL background — the novelty lives in a layer he is well-equipped to build.
  • Strong clinical/fundable narrative (rare disease, personal genomes) that reads well to translational funders.

Hidden caveats

  • Enformer/Borzoi are documented to poorly capture cross-individual (personal-genome) expression variation (Sasse 2023, Huang 2023); calibrating a model that is fundamentally miscalibrated for the target task is a hidden validation trap the pitch glosses over.
  • Ground truth for rare regulatory variant effects is scarce — MPRA/eQTL are proxies — so 'calibration is real' is very hard to prove convincingly.
  • Fine-tuning Borzoi-class models over ~500kb contexts is multi-GPU and long-sequence heavy; the compute may still exceed what he actually has despite starting from pretrained weights.
  • Crowded seq2function field (Calico, DeepMind, Kundaje); a UQ wrapper alone may be judged thin novelty without a hard downstream clinical win.

Gets obsolete if…

  • DeepMind's AlphaGenome (announced 2025) or a Borzoi successor ships native calibrated uncertainty for variant effects, absorbing the core contribution.
  • A benchmark paper establishes that current seq2function models cannot generalize to rare/personal variants regardless of calibration — kills the premise rather than the method.
  • A conformal-prediction-for-genomic-sequence-models library becomes a packaged, standard tool, commoditizing the UQ layer.
  • A large calibrated clinical non-coding variant classifier (e.g. from a diagnostics consortium) reaches the literature as de facto standard.

Gets more feasible if…

  • AlphaGenome / Borzoi weights + easy inference tooling released openly, removing the fine-tuning compute barrier.
  • A large saturation-mutagenesis / MPRA benchmark for non-coding variants is released as a public evaluation set.
  • A conformal or Bayesian deep-learning-for-genomics tutorial/library lands (e.g. integrated into scikit or a PyG-adjacent package).
  • Personal-genome eQTL datasets with matched WGS are released at scale, giving a real calibration target.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Single-Cell Epigenome Foundation Models for Enhancer-Gene Programs and In-Silico Perturbation

Regulatory / epigenomics deep learning
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Build a foundation model over single-cell ATAC and multiome data that learns cell-state-specific cis-regulatory grammar and predicts enhancer-to-gene links, then supports in-silico perturbation of transcription-factor motifs and enhancers to forecast accessibility and expression shifts across cell types. The model would be validated against perturbation datasets and CRISPR enhancer screens. This turns static chromatin atlases into a predictive engine for how regulatory programs rewire across cell states and disease.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result13months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: Building the first credible epigenome-native single-cell foundation model with validated in-silico enhancer perturbation could define a subfield — but only a well-resourced lab can fund the pretraining he'd need.
Verdict: The hottest, most fundable idea here but the riskiest for him: foundation-model pretraining plus his unheld scVI-tools/Pyro make it a long shot without a paper first; pitch only to compute-rich single-cell labs (his Scanpy/AnnData helps).
Why fundable: Single-cell multi-omics and perturbation modelling are flagship priorities at Sanger, EMBL-EBI, CCAIM, and the Cambridge Math Genomics ecosystem, aligning tightly with Cambridge Trust/Gates and Imperial President's remits. Cancer-epigenetics framing gives a CRUK/oncology translational angle.
Fits labs:
Mo Lotfollahi (Cambridge/Sanger)Anshul Kundaje (Stanford)Valentina Boeva (ETH)Manolis Kellis (MIT)Florian Markowetz (Cambridge/CRUK)Pietro Lio (Cambridge)
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeCambridge TrustEMBL-EBI (Sanger) PhDCCAIM (Cambridge)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~11 mo

Strengths

  • scATAC/multiome foundation models are much less saturated than scRNA foundation models (scGPT, Geneformer are RNA-first), leaving open ground.
  • In-silico perturbation is a concrete, fundable deliverable with a real validation path (Perturb-seq, CRISPRi enhancer screens).
  • 10x multiome data is increasingly abundant and enhancer-gene linking remains a recognized open problem.
  • Regulatory networks as graphs align naturally with his GNN/PyG skillset.

Hidden caveats

  • The utility of scRNA foundation models is actively contested (multiple papers show simple baselines match Geneformer/scGPT); the same skepticism will hit a scATAC FM and force a hard utility-vs-baseline fight.
  • scATAC is extremely sparse and batch-effect-ridden; pretraining a real foundation model needs a large curated corpus and serious compute likely beyond a single dry-lab student.
  • Enhancer-gene ground truth is noisy and in-silico perturbation validation needs matched perturbation data that may not exist for his target cell types.
  • Well-resourced labs (Satija, Kundaje, Regev-adjacent, big biotech) can scoop a foundation-model effort quickly.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major scATAC/multiome foundation model ships with in-silico perturbation built in (a 'scGPT for ATAC', GET-model successor, or EpiFoundation-style release).
  • A benchmark paper shows foundation models do not beat supervised baselines for enhancer-gene linking.
  • A calibrated non-FM enhancer-gene method (improved ABC model, ENCODE-rE2G) becomes the community standard, reducing appetite for an FM approach.
  • ChromBPNet-style per-cell-type models plus perturbation extensions become the default, undercutting the FM framing.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open pretrained scATAC/multiome foundation model with weights is released, allowing transfer instead of pretraining.
  • A large curated multiome atlas paired with a CRISPRi enhancer-perturbation benchmark is published.
  • Tooling for scATAC modeling lands (scPrinter, ChromBPNet extensions, a PyG-based regulatory-graph library).
  • A public in-silico-perturbation evaluation benchmark for accessibility/expression shifts appears.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Deep generative models of chromosomal instability from shallow whole-genome sequencing for platinum and PARP-inhibitor response in high-grade serous ovarian cancer

Cancer genomics ML (CRUK-fundable)
Existing copy-number/CIN signatures (Drews et al., Nature 2022) are descriptive NMF mixtures that ignore the evolutionary process generating the genome. This project builds a deep generative model (VAE / normalizing flow over allele-specific copy-number profiles from shallow WGS) whose latent space encodes underlying mutational-process dynamics, then uses that representation to predict platinum and PARP-inhibitor response and forecast relapse timing. Trained on TCGA-OV, PCAWG and BriTROC-1/2 and validated for treatment stratification.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you3/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result10.5months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: He could become the CIN-to-therapy-response expert in ovarian cancer, but that is a clinical niche, not a field-defining perch.
Verdict: The most tractable technically (VAE/flow in plain PyTorch, no PyG) and cleanly CRUK-fundable, but clinically-scoped and off the DeepMind track; pitch to cancer-genomics/CRUK labs as a solid, safe PhD.
Why fundable: Direct fit to CRUK priorities: HGSOC is a CRUK strategic cancer, CIN and treatment resistance are named CRUK themes, and shallow WGS is cheap enough for clinical translation, appealing to CRUK Cambridge Institute and CRUK translational calls. Also maps to Cambridge Trust, Gates Cambridge, and Clarendon (Oxford BDI route).
Fits labs:
Florian MarkowetzChristopher YauNiko BeerenwinkelChris HolmesPietro Lio
Fits funding:
CRUK Cambridge Institute PhD studentshipCambridge TrustGates CambridgeClarendon (Oxford BDI)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~20 mo

Strengths

  • Clear clinical/translational hook (platinum and PARP-inhibitor response in HGSOC) makes it strongly CRUK-fundable with a coherent PhD narrative.
  • Shallow WGS is cheap and clinically deployable, so the data modality is realistic rather than aspirational.
  • Genuine methodological gap: current CIN signatures (Drews 2022) are descriptive NMF mixtures; a generative/mechanistic latent model is real novelty.
  • Less crowded than foundation-model spaces — a defensible niche where a first-author paper is achievable.

Hidden caveats

  • The true bottleneck is data access: it needs large HGSOC shallow-WGS cohorts with treatment-response outcomes, usually locked in consortia (BriTROC, TCGA-OV, PCAWG); as a dry-lab student he depends entirely on collaborators.
  • The interpretability claim ('latent encodes mutational-process dynamics') is hard to falsify — risk of an unvalidatable latent story that reviewers distrust.
  • Allele-specific copy number from shallow WGS is low-resolution and depth-limited; the technical validity of the input signal is a real question.
  • Outcome-prediction sample sizes are small, inviting overfitting and weak clinical claims.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A paper delivers a validated CIN-based generative or mechanistic classifier for platinum/PARP response, scooping the mechanism angle.
  • A refined pan-cancer CIN signature framework supersedes Drews 2022 with mechanistic labels attached.
  • An improved HRD/genomic-scar clinical assay becomes standard-of-care for PARP selection, reducing appetite for a new ML predictor.
  • A large trial reports that a simpler existing biomarker already stratifies platinum response adequately.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Public release of a large HGSOC shallow-WGS cohort with linked treatment outcomes removes the data barrier.
  • Open tooling for allele-specific CN from sWGS improves (ASCAT/QDNAseq successors or a new caller).
  • A simulation framework for CIN evolutionary dynamics is released, enabling synthetic training and validation.
  • A generative-model-for-copy-number reference implementation lands to build on.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Graph neural networks for joint inference of spatial clonal architecture and immune evasion from spatial transcriptomics and multiplex imaging

Cancer genomics ML (CRUK-fundable)
◆ cross-lab workhorse★ flagship
Current spatial-omics analyses treat clonal (genomic) evolution and the tumour microenvironment as separate problems. This project develops a heterogeneous GNN over spatial transcriptomics (Visium/Xenium) and multiplex imaging (IMC/CODEX) that jointly infers tumour subclones, their spatial niches, and local immune context, then learns which clone-niche configurations drive immune evasion and predicts checkpoint-blockade response. Inference couples copy-number/expression clone calling with spatial message passing so genomic lineage and TME geography are estimated together.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result12months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: This is the one that fuses his actual assets — GNNs plus microscopy/image analysis — so mastering joint spatial-clonal-immune graph inference could make him a leader at the omics-imaging seam.
Verdict: Best fit to who he already is (GNN-native, leverages his imaging background); pitch broadly to spatial-omics and cancer-immunology GNN labs — the main climb is real PyTorch Geometric fluency on heterogeneous graphs.
Why fundable: Spatial biology and the tumour microenvironment are explicit CRUK strategic investment areas, and immunotherapy response prediction is a top translational priority. Strong fit for CRUK Cambridge Institute and for Imperial President's / EMBL-EBI-linked studentships given the imaging-plus-genomics angle.
Fits labs:
Ben RaphaelFlorian MarkowetzDavid Van ValenPietro LioMarinka ZitnikMo Lotfollahi
Fits funding:
CRUK Cambridge Institute PhD studentshipClarendonCambridge TrustImperial President's PhD Scholarship
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • Strongest methodological fit of the six: heterogeneous GNNs over multimodal spatial data are squarely his skillset.
  • Spatial omics is booming and multimodal integration is a recognized frontier with high publication appetite.
  • Jointly modeling clonal genomics and the microenvironment is genuinely novel — most methods treat them separately.
  • Immuno-oncology framing is CRUK-fundable with a clear therapeutic-relevance story.

Hidden caveats

  • Inferring clonal/genomic subclones from spatial transcriptomics is fundamentally weak — CNV inference from ST (inferCNV-style) is noisy, so the 'clonal architecture' pillar may rest on unreliable signal.
  • Requires paired ST + multiplex imaging + genomic ground truth on the same samples, which is rare and expensive; dry-lab dependence on collaborators is acute.
  • Xenium/IMC/CODEX registration and alignment is a hard preprocessing problem that can consume most of the PhD before any modeling.
  • Fast, crowded space — many spatial GNN methods are appearing, so scoop risk is high.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A method ships joint spatial clone + niche + immune inference (a CellCharter/Squidpy extension or a new spatial-multiomics GNN).
  • A spatial multimodal foundation model handles cell-niche and clone interactions out of the box.
  • A benchmark paper shows CNV/subclone inference from spatial transcriptomics is unreliable, undermining the premise.
  • A spatial-genomics assay (spatial WGS/scDNA) matures, making inference-from-transcriptomics moot.

Gets more feasible if…

  • A public paired ST + IMC/CODEX + WGS/scDNA tumor atlas is released.
  • Tooling for ST-to-imaging registration and heterogeneous-graph construction lands (a PyG-based spatial-omics library).
  • A benchmark dataset specifically for clone-niche inference is published.
  • A robust ST-based CNV caller with validated accuracy is released, shoring up the genomic pillar.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Physics-Informed Generative Models for Continuous Conformational Landscapes from Single-Particle Cryo-EM

Cryo-EM / structural bioinformatics deep learning
→ DeepMind track
Current heterogeneity methods (cryoDRGN, 3DFlex, cryoSPARC) recover latent conformational spaces that are geometrically expressive but physically uninterpretable, so latent coordinates cannot be read as free-energy or reaction coordinates. This project builds an amortized-inference generative model coupling a neural-field volume decoder to a physics-informed prior (Boltzmann weighting, path continuity) and neural-operator dynamics, turning a particle stack into a calibrated free-energy landscape with uncertainty over transition states. Validation is entirely in silico on public EMPIAR stacks of known flexible machines (ribosome, spliceosome, transporters) plus MD-simulated synthetic ground truth.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.6bang-for-buck
Time to result20months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Making cryo-EM latent coordinates readable as free energy is elegant and could own a niche, but he'd be starting structural biology, thermodynamics, and neural operators from near zero.
Verdict: A long shot for him: physics-informed cryo-EM demands domain skills he entirely lacks and no PyG/GNN leverage; only pursue if he deliberately pivots to structural DL and finds a cryo-EM lab to carry the domain.
Why fundable: Directly targets Ellen Zhong's named frontier (interpretable, dynamics-aware heterogeneity) and the neural-operator AI-for-science agenda; funders reward methods that make an expensive modality yield mechanism, not prettier maps. Fully reproducible on open EMPIAR/EMDB data with no wet-lab dependency, de-risking a 3-4 year PhD.
Fits labs:
Ellen ZhongAnima AnandkumarBarbara BraviBrian HieYisong Yue
Fits funding:
Knight-Hennessy Scholars (Stanford)Princeton guaranteed PhD fundingOpen Philanthropy AI / AI-for-scienceBoehringer Ingelheim Fonds
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~16 mo

Strengths

  • Real interpretability gap: cryoDRGN/3DFlex latents are not physically meaningful, and mapping them to free-energy/reaction coordinates is compelling and novel.
  • Physics-informed priors and geometric structure align well with his GDL background.
  • Structural biology plus generative models is prestigious and highly publishable.
  • Bridges cryo-EM heterogeneity with MD/energy landscapes — genuine interdisciplinary novelty.

Hidden caveats

  • Steep domain-entry cost for someone from genomics/GNNs: image formation, CTF, pose estimation, reconstruction — a long time-to-first-result risk.
  • Severe validation gap: there is no ground-truth free-energy landscape for real particles, so 'physical interpretability' is mostly validated on synthetic data and remains contestable.
  • Compute-heavy (neural-field volume decoders, large particle stacks) and requires raw cryo-EM data access plus heavy GPU.
  • The Boltzmann-ensemble-from-single-particle assumption linking density to free energy is physically arguable and reviewers will attack it.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A cryoDRGN successor or 3DFlex v2 ships physically-interpretable / free-energy-aware heterogeneity, absorbing the contribution.
  • MD-emulator / Boltzmann-generator foundation models (BioEmu-style, released 2025) make conformational ensembles obtainable without cryo-EM, reducing the appeal.
  • Time-resolved cryo-EM or other experimental methods deliver kinetics/energetics directly.
  • A physics-informed neural-field cryo-EM method is published as an open standard tool.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open synthetic cryo-EM benchmark with known energy landscapes is released, enabling validation.
  • Pretrained neural-field cryo-EM decoders / differentiable image-formation toolkits are released.
  • Public datasets pairing MD ensembles with cryo-EM maps appear.
  • A cryo-EM-to-MD integration library lands, lowering the domain-onboarding cost.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Sequence-Aware Geometric Deep Learning for Automated Atomic Model Building and Validation in Near-Atomic Cryo-EM Maps

Cryo-EM / structural bioinformatics deep learning
→ DeepMind track★ flagship
De novo atomic model building into 2.5-4 Angstrom cryo-EM density remains a manual, error-prone bottleneck, and existing tools (ModelAngelo, DeepTracer) build backbones but struggle with register errors, ambiguous side chains, and confidence calibration. This project trains an SE(3)-equivariant network that jointly reasons over the 3D density field and a protein-language-model sequence embedding to place, sequence-assign, and self-validate atomic coordinates, emitting per-residue confidence to flag likely modelling errors. It doubles as a scalable auditor that re-scores deposited PDB/EMDB structures for register and rotamer errors.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort0.8bang-for-buck
Time to result16months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: SE(3)-equivariant building + protein-LM priors is squarely Isomorphic/DeepMind flavor and his geometric-DL instinct transfers conceptually, but cryo-EM density and structural validation are a steep new domain.
Verdict: Highest industrial-lab appeal of the structural pair and his sheaf/equivariance background gives partial footing, but the cryo-EM + protein-LM + no-paper stack makes it hard; pitch to structural/geometric-DL labs only if he commits to the domain.
Why fundable: Squarely in the structural-bioinformatics + protein-LM wheelhouse of Sternberg, Minary, Singh and Hie, addressing a concrete community pain point (model bias in the exploding cryo-EM deposition rate) that structural-biology funders prioritise. High-impact tool paper plus a public re-validation resource give clear, self-contained deliverables.
Fits labs:
Michael SternbergPeter MinaryMona SinghBrian HieEllen Zhong
Fits funding:
Imperial President's PhD ScholarshipClarendon (Oxford)Gates Cambridge / Cambridge TrustPrinceton guaranteed PhD funding
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~11 mo

Strengths

  • Concrete, well-scoped deliverable (a tool) with obvious quantitative benchmarks (model-vs-deposited RMSD, sequence recall) — good odds of a clean first paper.
  • SE(3)-equivariant networks are squarely his GDL wheelhouse.
  • Real, community-recognized bottleneck buildable on abundant EMDB/PDB paired data.
  • Confidence calibration on built models is a differentiating angle versus ModelAngelo.

Hidden caveats

  • ModelAngelo is strong, actively developed and open; beating it meaningfully is hard and incremental gains may not clear the novelty bar.
  • Register errors and side chains are exactly what the incumbents are already targeting, so scoop risk from EMBL/Baker/established groups is high.
  • Requires cryo-EM domain onboarding (maps, resolution, coordinate frames) — a moderate but real learning curve.
  • ModelAngelo already uses sequence/HMM reasoning, so the 'sequence-aware' differentiation may be thinner than the pitch implies.

Gets obsolete if…

  • ModelAngelo or DeepTracer ships a major update solving register errors plus calibrated confidence, directly eating the contribution.
  • An AlphaFold3-class model integrates density-guided atomic model building end-to-end.
  • A benchmark paper declares automated model building 'essentially solved' at 2.5-4 Angstrom.
  • A well-funded lab releases a new SE(3) model-building tool that becomes the default.

Gets more feasible if…

  • A curated EMDB-PDB paired benchmark with explicit register-error labels is released.
  • An open SE(3)-equivariant library plus pretrained density encoders lands (improved e3nn tooling or a cryo-EM PyG dataset).
  • ModelAngelo releases modular/open components that can be built on rather than reimplemented.
  • A public confidence-calibration benchmark for built atomic models appears.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Bandit-Guided Perturb-seq: Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Single-Cell CRISPR Screen Design

Reinforcement learning for experimental design (active learning in biology)
Large-scale Perturb-seq screens exhaust combinatorial gene-perturbation space blindly and waste most of their sequencing budget on uninformative guides. This project frames each round of a pooled CRISPR screen as a batched contextual-bandit / RL problem: a policy uses a learned latent model of single-cell transcriptomic response (VAE/GNN embeddings) to pick the next batch of perturbations that maximally reduce uncertainty about the gene-regulatory / perturbation-response map, closing the loop across sequencing rounds. The output is a policy that reaches the same causal map as a full screen at a fraction of the reagent and sequencing cost.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.6bang-for-buck
Time to result11months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: He'd own adaptive-screen design only if he mastered RL/bandits and closed the loop with a real Perturb-seq facility — neither of which he has today.
Verdict: Skip for now — RL is an entire stack he doesn't hold and it pulls him off his sheaf/GNN identity; only pitch to a lab that already runs Perturb-seq and wants a methods PhD.
Why fundable: Directly hits funder priorities for cost-efficient, translational genomics and ML-for-biology: Sanger/EMBL-EBI and CCAIM explicitly fund methods that make functional genomics cheaper and interpretable; van der Schaar and Lawrence groups sit inside CCAIM/health-ML funding streams. Strong Gates Cambridge / Clarendon narrative (democratising expensive experiments, reproducibility, first-gen impact angle). MSCA and Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds reward a crisp method-plus-biology deliverable.
Fits labs:
Mo LotfollahiMihaela van der SchaarPietro LiòYisong YueCaroline UhlerMatt Thomson
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeClarendonCambridge TrustCCAIM
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • Perturb-seq combinatorial space genuinely explodes, so adaptive/active design has a real, quantifiable sequencing-budget-saving story
  • Naturally uses VAE/GNN embeddings of transcriptomic response — directly matches his PyG/GNN skillset and Lio-lab flavor
  • Sits at the intersection of three fundable hot themes (Perturb-seq, single-cell foundation models, active learning)
  • Can start fully in silico on public scPerturb-style data — dry-lab feasible without a wet-lab loop on day one

Hidden caveats

  • A truly adaptive screen needs a wet-lab partner to run iterative rounds; without it the project collapses to a simulation-only benchmark reviewers discount as unvalidated
  • Bandit/RL reward signal in high-dim single-cell space is extremely noisy and batch-confounded — easy to land flat vs a random-guide baseline
  • If the policy is trained and evaluated against his own response model, it is a self-consistent loop (evaluate-on-your-own-oracle) with no external validation
  • Crowded/scoopable: GEARS and several ML-for-Perturb-seq-design groups are already publishing here

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab releases a Perturb-seq response foundation model with calibrated combinatorial prediction good enough that adaptive-selection gains shrink (scGPT/GEARS successor)
  • A benchmark paper directly comparing active vs random Perturb-seq guide selection reports only marginal gains
  • A commercial platform (10x, Recursion) ships adaptive/closed-loop screen design as a product
  • Release of a large public iterative closed-loop Perturb-seq dataset that a well-resourced lab immediately exploits

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open pretrained perturbation-response model plus standardized benchmark (PerturBench-style) drops, giving a fair evaluation harness
  • A validated generative Perturb-seq simulator is released, removing the self-oracle validation problem
  • A closed-loop / active-learning-for-biology library or tutorial lands lowering the RL engineering barrier
  • A cloud pooled-CRISPR screening service exposes an API that makes real iterative rounds accessible
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Active Bayesian Experimental Design for Protein Fitness Landscapes: RL-Driven Directed Evolution In Silico

Reinforcement learning for experimental design (active learning in biology)
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Directed-evolution and deep-mutational-scanning campaigns test far more variants than needed because they cannot prioritise which mutants to assay next. This project builds an RL / Bayesian-optimal-experimental-design agent that, given a protein language-model prior over sequence-function, sequentially selects the most informative batch of variants to characterise, explicitly trading exploration of the fitness landscape against a fixed assay budget and batch/round constraints. The result is a policy that recovers high-fitness variants and an accurate landscape model with markedly fewer wet-lab rounds.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort0.8bang-for-buck
Time to result7.5months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: Protein fitness + BO is a world-leader lane, but leadership there needs a protein-ML track record he has zero of.
Verdict: Fundable and DeepMind/Isomorphic-shaped, but it's a cold start — no protein, no RL, no Bayesian OED yet; pitch only if he first builds a protein-LM demo.
Why fundable: Generative + active biology is a top funder theme (Open Phil AI-for-science, Boehringer Ingelheim, EMBL-EBI, ETH/EPFL salaried protein-ML positions). Clean, benchmarkable, high-impact-for-therapeutics story fits Clarendon/Gates 'transformative research' framing and MSCA mobility. Protein design is a magnet for guaranteed-funding US programmes and Knight-Hennessy.
Fits labs:
Brian HieBarbara BraviDebora Marks-style protein ML (via Marinka Zitnik collaboration)Bonnie BergerAnima AnandkumarYisong Yue

ClarendonGates CambridgeKnight-HennessyOpen Phil AI
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~13 mo

Strengths

  • Clean, well-posed ML formulation (Bayesian optimal experimental design / BO over sequence-function) with an obvious cost-saving value proposition
  • Protein language-model priors (ESM-2, etc.) are freely available, so the prior is a low-barrier drop-in
  • Public benchmarks (ProteinGym, FLIP) make dry-lab evaluation tractable without wet-lab access
  • Strong translational/biotech funding appeal for directed evolution

Hidden caveats

  • Very crowded field — BO+PLM for protein engineering has many active groups and startups; genuine novelty is hard to carve out
  • Offline BO on static DMS landscapes does not honestly test the sequential-design gain (known offline-BO pitfalls)
  • PLM zero-shot fitness is miscalibrated; the agent can exploit oracle errors, inflating in-silico numbers that won't transfer
  • Real payoff needs a wet-lab loop he lacks; reviewers may read it as incremental over existing BO baselines

Gets obsolete if…

  • A calibrated, general protein fitness foundation model ships that predicts well enough that active selection buys little
  • A ProteinGym-scale benchmark paper settles the best active-learning strategy, commoditizing it
  • A self-driving-lab platform (Adaptyv-style) ships automated BO directed evolution, absorbing the niche
  • A new DMS/deep-learning method claims SOTA sample efficiency for variant prioritization

Gets more feasible if…

  • A standardized sequential/active-learning-for-proteins benchmark (not just static DMS) is released
  • Experimental-validation-as-a-service (e.g., Adaptyv Foundry API) lowers the wet-lab barrier enough for a real loop
  • An open BO library tuned for biological sequences with PLM integration lands
  • A calibrated protein-fitness uncertainty toolkit drops, fixing the oracle-exploitation problem
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Uncertainty-Aware Unbalanced Neural Optimal Transport for Single-Cell Developmental Trajectories

Optimal transport methods for single-cell trajectories
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Current OT trajectory methods (Waddington-OT, moscot) give point estimates of cell-state couplings across time but ignore that measurement is destructive, sampling is sparse, and cells are born and die at unequal rates. This project builds a scalable neural unbalanced-OT model that learns continuous-time couplings between temporal scRNA-seq snapshots while emitting calibrated posterior uncertainty over each cell-fate transition, using amortised variational inference and growth/death source-sink terms. The output is a probabilistic trajectory map that tells biologists not just the most likely ancestor-descendant links but how confident the model is, which is essential for downstream fate-decision and lineage-driver claims.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.75bang-for-buck
Time to result9months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: He could become the calibrated-neural-OT person for single-cell if he nails the optimization math and uncertainty layer.
Verdict: Solid realistic fit — sits on his Scanpy/AnnData turf with contained ML risk; pitch to Theis/moscot-adjacent and OT-for-biology groups.
Why fundable: Directly hits health-data and ML-for-medicine funder priorities (developmental disease, cell-fate mapping) while being methodologically deep enough for pure-ML programmes. Calibrated uncertainty is a named priority for Bayesian-DL and trustworthy-AI funders (Gal, Lawrence, van der Schaar), and the single-cell substrate fits genomics-institute funders (EMBL-EBI, Sanger). Cross-cuts a methods flagship and a biomedical impact story, which is what Gates/Clarendon panels reward.
Fits labs:
Mo LotfollahiMihaela van der SchaarNeil LawrenceYarin GalChristopher YauJens Lagergren
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeClarendonCambridge TrustEMBL-EBI PhD Programme
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~18 mo

Strengths

  • OT for single-cell trajectories is a mathematically rich, actively funded area (Waddington-OT, moscot, TrajectoryNet) that suits a theory-leaning dry-lab candidate
  • Uncertainty quantification plus unbalanced (birth/death) growth is a genuine gap over current point-estimate methods
  • Fully dry-lab feasible: public time-course scRNA-seq datasets exist, no wet lab needed
  • Geometry/OT framing aligns tightly with the Lio-lab identity and his geometric-DL background

Hidden caveats

  • moscot (Theis lab) is a strong, well-resourced incumbent expanding fast — high scoop risk on unbalanced + uncertainty
  • Ground-truth couplings are unobservable (destructive measurement), so UQ correctness cannot be directly validated — a deep validation gap
  • Neural OT is numerically finicky; combining scalability, unbalancedness and calibrated uncertainty is a large solo engineering lift
  • No standardized metric for trajectory UQ, so reviewers can question whether improvements are real

Gets obsolete if…

  • moscot or a successor adds calibrated uncertainty and unbalanced OT natively
  • A CRISPR lineage-tracing dataset+method provides ground-truth trajectories, reducing the need for OT-inferred couplings
  • A benchmark paper for single-cell trajectory OT settles the best method
  • A cell-state-dynamics foundation model (flow-matching / CellFlow-style) subsumes OT couplings

Gets more feasible if…

  • A neural-OT library with unbalanced and stochastic support is released (OTT-JAX / TorchCFM extensions)
  • A time-course scRNA benchmark with lineage-tracing ground truth drops, giving a validation anchor
  • A flow-matching-for-single-cell tutorial/library lands, cutting implementation cost
  • Efficient entropic/low-rank neural OT solvers reduce the compute barrier
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Causal Gromov-Wasserstein Optimal Transport for Counterfactual Perturbation Trajectories Across Modalities

Optimal transport methods for single-cell trajectories
★ flagship
Perturbation screens (Perturb-seq, drug time courses) generate unpaired single-cell measurements before and after intervention, often in different modalities (RNA, ATAC, protein), making it hard to ask what a specific cell state would have become under a given perturbation. This project develops a causal Gromov-Wasserstein OT framework that aligns cell populations across modalities and timepoints without shared features, then embeds interventional structure so the learned transport map yields counterfactual trajectory predictions for unseen perturbations. It turns OT couplings from descriptive alignments into interventional predictors of cell-state response.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort0.8bang-for-buck
Time to result16months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Binding Gromov-Wasserstein to interventional semantics is subfield-defining if it works, but it demands heavy OT math and causal theory he lacks formal grounding in.
Verdict: Highest-ceiling, highest-risk — the math gap (no CS/maths degree) makes it a gamble; only for a lab with a strong OT theorist to backstop him.
Why fundable: Perturbation modelling and causal ML for target discovery are top priorities for pharma-adjacent and AI-for-science funders; counterfactual prediction is a clear translational hook (drug response, mechanism). The causal-inference + OT combination is a recognised frontier (Uhler line of work), giving the proposal genuine methodological prestige for elite pure-ML funders while the perturbation-biology payoff satisfies biomedical panels.
Fits labs:
Caroline UhlerMo LotfollahiBarbara BraviPietro LioMarinka ZitnikBen Raphael
Fits funding:
Knight-Hennessy (Stanford)MIT guaranteed fundingHarvard guaranteed fundingGates Cambridge
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~18 mo

Strengths

  • Ambitious, distinctive combination (causal + Gromov-Wasserstein + cross-modal) with a high novelty ceiling
  • Unpaired cross-modal alignment (RNA/ATAC/protein) is a real, actively worked problem
  • GW-OT is mathematically elegant and fits the geometric/theory lab identity
  • Counterfactual-perturbation framing rides the causal-ML wave — fundable narrative

Hidden caveats

  • Scope is overloaded; causal identifiability of counterfactual cell states may not even be well-posed from unpaired observational data
  • GW-OT scales poorly (quadratic-plus); pushing it to single-cell counts is a serious compute barrier he likely lacks
  • Causal claims are very hard to validate here — severe validation gap that skeptical reviewers will target
  • Crowded multimodal-integration space (GLUE, MultiVI, moscot cross-modality) demands strong differentiation

Gets obsolete if…

  • A paired multimodal foundation model or a cheap paired multi-omic assay makes unpaired alignment largely unnecessary
  • moscot or a GW single-cell method adds causal counterfactual perturbation mapping
  • A causal-representation-learning / causal-OT perturbation paper (CINEMA-OT successor) claims the counterfactual crown
  • A benchmark shows GW alignment no better than simpler integration methods

Gets more feasible if…

  • A scalable entropic / low-rank GW library is released, lowering the compute barrier
  • Paired multimodal perturbation datasets with matched pre/post states drop, providing a validation anchor
  • A causal-OT tutorial or reference implementation lands
  • Efficient neural-GW solvers make single-cell-scale GW tractable
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

OmicsMAE: A geometry-aware self-supervised foundation model for single-cell multi-omics that transfers to unseen tissues and perturbations

Self-supervised representation learning for omics
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Pre-train a single masked-modelling + cross-modal contrastive encoder over paired single-cell RNA/ATAC/protein (CITE-seq, 10x Multiome) using a gene-regulatory-network graph prior so the latent space respects known regulatory geometry rather than raw count statistics. The learned representation is then probed zero-/few-shot on tasks it never saw during pretraining, above all perturbation-response prediction (chemical and CRISPR) and cross-tissue cell-state transfer. Core claim: biology-structured SSL yields representations that generalize out-of-distribution far better than batch-correction-tuned embeddings, which is the actual bottleneck in atlas-scale single-cell.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact5/5
Appeal / effort1.25bang-for-buck
Time to result13months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: A geometry-aware GRN-primed SSL foundation model is exactly the intersection — GNN + single-cell + foundation models — where he could become a named leader, if he lands PyG fluency and compute.
Verdict: His #1 — pursue this; it fuses everything he already does (Scanpy, HPC, geometric DL) with the hottest fundable direction, and pitches directly to Lio, Theis, Zitnik and ETH.
Why fundable: Directly hits the priorities of single-cell foundation-model and precision-medicine funders: perturbation prediction is the headline problem at Sanger/EMBL-EBI and CCAIM, and a transfer-generalization story maps cleanly onto 'AI for medicine' framing. Fully reproducible on public atlases, which de-risks a 4-year plan for panels that dislike wet-lab dependency risk.
