Mo LotfollahiPietro LiòMarinka ZitnikAnshul KundajeChristopher YauJulia Vogt
Fits funding:
Gates CambridgeCambridge TrustClarendonEMBL-EBI / Wellcome Sanger PhD
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~9 mo

Strengths

  • Rides the single-cell foundation-model wave (scGPT, Geneformer, scFoundation) — high visibility and citations
  • GRN graph prior is a genuine geometric/GNN angle matching his PyG skills and the lab
  • Abundant public paired data (CITE-seq, 10x Multiome, CELLxGENE) makes the data side dry-lab feasible
  • Multimodal zero/few-shot transfer is a timely, fundable story

Hidden caveats

  • Pretraining a foundation model is compute-heavy — a severe barrier for a compute-limited student who cannot match well-resourced labs at scale
  • Extremely crowded and fast-moving; scoop risk is very high across many multi-omic FM efforts
  • Published critiques already show single-cell FMs often fail to beat simple baselines — the core premise is under active attack
  • GRNs are noisy/incomplete, so the geometric prior may not help, and proving it helps is itself hard

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab releases an open multimodal single-cell foundation model with GRN priors and strong zero-shot transfer
  • A stronger benchmark paper confirms single-cell FMs don't beat simple baselines, deflating the premise
  • A standard multi-omic transfer benchmark gets saturated by an existing model
  • 10x or CZI ships a pretrained multiome encoder as infrastructure

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open pretrained multiome checkpoint plus fine-tuning tutorial drops, letting him fine-tune rather than pretrain
  • An efficient SSL recipe / LoRA for omics reduces the pretraining compute barrier
  • A large curated paired multimodal corpus with a ready data loader (CZI-scale) lands
  • A compute-credit / cloud grant program for single-cell FMs becomes available
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

CalibDNA: Calibrated-uncertainty self-supervised genome language models for regulatory variant effect prediction

Self-supervised representation learning for omics
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Take a self-supervised DNA sequence model (Nucleotide-Transformer / Enformer-style pretraining on masked genomic sequence and epigenomic tracks) and re-architect the head with principled Bayesian/deep-ensemble uncertainty so that predicted regulatory-variant effects come with trustworthy confidence intervals. The central question is not raw accuracy but whether the model knows what it does not know: does calibrated uncertainty flag out-of-distribution non-coding variants and rare-disease regulatory hits where current models are silently overconfident? Evaluate on gnomAD constraint, MPRA saturation-mutagenesis, and ClinVar/rare-disease regulatory variants.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result5.5months @ 20h/wk + AI high AI-leverage
GOAT path: Owning trustworthy uncertainty for regulatory-variant genome LMs is a real gap he could plant a flag in, given his TE/genomics background.
Verdict: Strong #2 — leans on his genomics/HPC strengths and sits in the Enformer/AlphaGenome lineage DeepMind recruits from; pitch to regulatory-genomics and DNA-LM groups, but it's less geometric than his core.
Why fundable: Uncertainty-aware ML for genomics sits at the intersection of Bayesian-DL funders and clinical-genomics funders: overconfident variant calls are a named clinical-safety problem, giving a clean 'trustworthy AI for medicine / AI safety' narrative that Open Phil AI and Clarendon reward, while the rare-disease regulatory angle fits Wellcome/genomics medicine priorities.
Fits labs:
Yarin GalNicola WhiffinChris HolmesAnshul KundajeBrian HieBonnie Berger
Fits funding:
ClarendonOpen Phil AIWellcome Sanger / EMBL-EBI PhDGates Cambridge
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~10 mo

Strengths

  • Calibration/uncertainty angle is genuinely underexplored versus the raw-accuracy race — clear differentiation
  • Builds on open pretrained DNA models (Nucleotide Transformer, Enformer, Borzoi), so no pretraining needed — lowers the compute barrier and fits dry-lab
  • Regulatory variant effect prediction is clinically important and highly fundable
  • Matches his genomics interest; UQ methodology (ensembles/Bayesian heads) is tractable to prototype

Hidden caveats

  • DNA-LM field moves extremely fast (Evo, Borzoi, AlphaGenome) — the foundation can shift under the project mid-PhD
  • No ground-truth calibration targets for regulatory effects, so defining and validating a trustworthy confidence interval is genuinely hard
  • Deep ensembles over Enformer/Borzoi-scale models multiply an already heavy fine-tuning compute cost
  • Reviewers may read a UQ head as incremental engineering unless tied to a downstream decision it demonstrably improves

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab releases a calibrated DNA-LM at scale (AlphaGenome-style) that ships trustworthy variant-effect confidence natively
  • A CAGI-style or dedicated regulatory-variant UQ benchmark settles the best calibration method
  • An Enformer/Borzoi successor ships uncertainty estimates as a built-in feature
  • A clinical variant-effect model with calibrated intervals gets published and adopted

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open calibrated-UQ toolkit or tutorial for genomic models drops
  • A new labeled regulatory-variant benchmark with measured effect sizes is released, giving calibration targets
  • LoRA / efficient fine-tuning for DNA LMs lands, cutting the ensemble compute cost
  • A shared pretrained DNA-embedding API removes the need to run the big base models locally
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Circuits of Regulation: Mechanistic Interpretability of Genomic Sequence-to-Function Foundation Models

Interpretable / explainable AI for genomics
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
State-of-the-art sequence-to-function models (Enformer, Borzoi, and successor genomic foundation models) predict expression, chromatin state, and splicing from raw DNA, but are black boxes whose internal computations we cannot audit. This project ports mechanistic-interpretability tooling — sparse autoencoders, activation patching, and circuit tracing — from the LLM world to genomic transformers, aiming to extract human-readable regulatory grammar (motif syntax, enhancer-promoter logic, distance dependence) as discrete, testable circuits. Extracted circuits are validated against MPRA/CRISPRi perturbation data and used to flag when a prediction rests on spurious features.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you3/5
10-yr impact5/5
Appeal / effort1.67bang-for-buck
Time to result8months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: If he becomes the person who reverse-engineers the internal circuits of Enformer/Borzoi-class genomic FMs, he owns a subfield that literally does not exist yet — mech interp for biology.
Verdict: Pursue hard — his single best fit; pitch to Lio, ELLIS/ETH FM groups, and DeepMind/genomics-FM teams as the interp-for-biology angle.
Why fundable: Sits at the exact intersection two big funders are pushing: Open Phil / AI-safety funders want mechanistic interpretability applied to a high-stakes scientific domain, while genomics funders (EMBL-EBI, Sanger, Wellcome) want trustworthy foundation models. The 'interpretability transfer from LLMs to biology' framing is timely, dual-use-positive, and gives a clean fellowship narrative for interdisciplinary schemes.
Fits labs:
Anshul Kundaje (Stanford)David Gifford (MIT)Olga Troyanskaya (Princeton)Christopher Yau (Oxford)Yarin Gal (Oxford)Bonnie Berger (MIT)
Fits funding:
Open Phil AI (interpretability priority)Knight-Hennessy (Stanford)Clarendon (Oxford)EMBL-EBI / Cambridge Math Genomics
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • Rides two hot waves at once: LLM mechanistic-interpretability tooling (SAEs, activation patching, attribution graphs) is mature and open-sourced, and genomic seq-to-function FMs (Enformer, Borzoi, AlphaGenome-class) are the field's current center of gravity — porting one to the other is a clear, unoccupied bridge.
  • Strong Lio-lab fit: sheaf/GNN and geometric-DL background maps onto circuit/graph structure in model internals, and the group already works in genomics regulatory modeling.
  • Dry-lab and reproducible — pretrained Enformer/Borzoi weights are public, so no wet-lab or new data-generation dependency; ideal for someone without a first-author paper who needs a tractable, publishable first project.
  • Interpretability findings are inherently paper-shaped (each discovered 'motif circuit' or SAE feature is a result), lowering the bar to a first publication.

Hidden caveats

  • SAEs on genomic models may recover only known motifs (TF binding sites already in JASPAR/HOCOMOCO) — reviewers will ask 'what did you learn that ChIP-seq didn't', a real novelty/validation trap.
  • Faithfulness of MI methods is contested even in LLMs (SAE features unstable, activation patching artifacts); importing an unsettled methodology risks building on sand.
  • Compute: training SAEs over Enformer/Borzoi activations across the genome is not trivial GPU-wise, and Borzoi retraining is heavy — he may only be able to probe frozen models.
  • Crowded fast: several genomics + interp groups (Calico/Google DeepMind Genomics, academic labs) can scoop the obvious 'SAE on Enformer' paper within a cycle.

Gets obsolete if…

  • Google DeepMind / Calico or a major lab releases an interpretability report or built-in feature dashboard for AlphaGenome/Borzoi (SAEs, attribution atlas) — would preempt the core contribution.
  • A benchmark or survey paper establishes standard MI-for-genomics protocols and metrics, converting the open frontier into a solved-methodology checklist.
  • A successor seq-to-function foundation model ships that is natively interpretable or replaces Enformer/Borzoi as the target, orphaning tooling built on the older architecture.
  • A high-profile paper shows genomic-model 'circuits' reduce to already-annotated regulatory grammar, deflating the novelty claim.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open SAE/circuit-tracing library (e.g. SAELens-style, TransformerLens-analog) ships an adapter for convolutional/genomic architectures, removing the porting cost.
  • A pretrained-activation dataset or feature dictionary for Enformer/Borzoi is released, so he can analyze without regenerating activations at genome scale.
  • AlphaGenome or Borzoi weights + a Colab/PyG tutorial land publicly, cutting model-access and setup friction.
  • A cheap distilled/small genomic FM appears that fits on a single GPU, making SAE training affordable.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Faithful and Calibrated: Uncertainty-Aware Interpretable Models for Clinical Variant Interpretation

Interpretable / explainable AI for genomics
Rare-disease diagnosis increasingly leans on ML variant effect predictors, but clinicians cannot act on a score without knowing why it was assigned and how confident the model is — especially in non-coding and regulatory regions where evidence is sparse. This project builds variant-effect models whose attributions are provably faithful (via self-explaining/concept-bottleneck architectures rather than post-hoc saliency) and whose predictions carry calibrated, decomposed uncertainty (aleatoric vs. epistemic), so a low-confidence non-coding call is flagged rather than silently trusted. Outputs are designed for direct integration into ACMG-style clinical reporting.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you2/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort1.5bang-for-buck
Time to result6months @ 20h/wk + AI high AI-leverage
GOAT path: Faithful+calibrated variant interpretation makes him a clinical-genomics-ML translator, but the ceiling is impact-in-clinic, not defining a method subfield.
Verdict: A safe, tractable fallback given his gaps — pitch to clinical-genomics/rare-disease PIs and Humanitas-style translational groups, not to DeepMind.
Why fundable: Directly targets the priorities of medical-AI funders (CCAIM, Wellcome, Sanger) and health-focused ML groups: trustworthiness, calibration, and clinical deployability, not just accuracy. The rare-disease and regulatory-variant angle maps onto existing UK genomics infrastructure (Genomics England, 100K Genomes), giving a concrete impact story funders reward.
Fits labs:
Mihaela van der Schaar (Cambridge, CCAIM)Chris Holmes (Oxford)Nicola Whiffin (Oxford)Sarah Filippi (Imperial)Yarin Gal (Oxford)Julia Vogt (ETH)
Fits funding:
CCAIM (Cambridge)Clarendon (Oxford)Gates CambridgeCambridge Trust
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~15 mo

Strengths

  • Clear clinical pain point (non-coding/regulatory variant interpretation with calibrated uncertainty) that funders and translational reviewers reward — strong impact narrative.
  • Self-explaining/concept-bottleneck + conformal-prediction stack is technically well-scoped and dry-lab, buildable on public variant datasets (ClinVar, gnomAD, MAVE/DMS).
  • Fits geometric-DL profile: regulatory-variant effects live on sequence/graph structure, and uncertainty over sparse-evidence regions is a genuine methods gap.
  • Calibration + faithfulness is a defensible niche distinct from the crowded 'bigger VEP model' race — differentiates from pure accuracy chasing.

Hidden caveats

  • Ground-truth for non-coding pathogenicity is scarce and biased, so 'provably faithful and calibrated' claims are hard to validate — the evaluation is the hardest part, not the model.
  • Clinical framing invites regulatory/actionability scrutiny he cannot satisfy in a PhD (no prospective clinical validation), risking a credibility gap between pitch and deliverable.
  • Conformal prediction and concept-bottleneck models are increasingly commodity — novelty must come from the genomics specialization, which reviewers may see as incremental.
  • Competing directly with well-resourced VEP efforts (AlphaMissense-style, EVE, popEVE, Enformer-based scorers) that could add calibration as a feature.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab releases a calibrated, uncertainty-aware variant-effect predictor at scale (e.g. AlphaMissense/DeepMind successor with confidence outputs) covering non-coding regions.
  • A benchmark paper establishes standard calibration/faithfulness metrics for clinical VEPs, turning the open methods question into a leaderboard.
  • ClinGen/ACMG or equivalent issues guidance that fixes how ML uncertainty is used in variant classification, constraining the design space.
  • A large DMS/MAVE or perturbation dataset drops that lets brute-force supervised models close the sparse-evidence gap this project targets.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open conformal-prediction-for-genomics or uncertainty-VEP toolkit/tutorial is released, cutting implementation cost.
  • A new large regulatory-variant benchmark with labels (beyond ClinVar) lands, giving him a real evaluation substrate.
  • Pretrained regulatory FMs expose per-prediction embeddings/logits publicly, enabling last-layer Bayesian/conformal wrappers without retraining.
  • A concept-bottleneck / self-explaining-model library adds genomics examples, lowering the barrier.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Uncertainty-Aware Scalable Oversight: Knowing When a Model Has Left Its Competence Region

AI safety / alignment (CS-core) — scalable oversight + Bayesian deep learning
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
As models surpass human ability to check their outputs, we lose the ability to know when to trust them. This project builds calibrated, distribution-shift-robust uncertainty estimators (deep ensembles, Laplace/last-layer Bayesian methods, epistemic-vs-aleatoric decomposition) that flag when a frontier LLM is operating outside its reliable competence region on a task, giving overseers a principled abstain/escalate signal for weak-to-strong generalization and RLHF reward-model failures. Deliverables: a benchmark of oversight failures paired with an uncertainty-gated oversight protocol that provably reduces confident-but-wrong outputs.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort0.8bang-for-buck
Time to result4.5months @ 20h/wk + AI high AI-leverage
GOAT path: Reframing oversight as uncertainty calibration is a strong idea, but leadership here needs Bayesian-DL depth and an alignment track record he simply does not have.
Verdict: Long shot — hot and fundable, but a big pivot away from his genomics/GNN identity with no CS degree; pursue only if he first ships a Bayesian-DL result.
Why fundable: Directly hits Open Philanthropy's scalable-oversight and 'trustworthy AI' priorities and FLI's high-stakes-reliability agenda; framed as measurable safety-relevant capability rather than pure ML. Bayesian/uncertainty framing is exactly Gal's and Lawrence's funded research line, easing supervisor buy-in and giving a credible technical spine for Clarendon/President's committees.
Fits labs:
Yarin Gal (Oxford — Bayesian DL/uncertainty, primary fit)Neil Lawrence (Cambridge — probabilistic ML/health-scale reliability)Finale Doshi-Velez (Harvard — interpretability + reliable ML)Mihaela van der Schaar (Cambridge — trustworthy ML, no-bio-needed)
Fits funding:
Open Philanthropy AI (career-development/PhD)Future of Life Institute PhD fellowshipClarendon (Oxford, with Gal)Knight-Hennessy (if paired with US move)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~10 mo

Strengths

  • Scalable oversight and calibrated uncertainty are top-priority, well-funded AI-safety agendas (Anthropic/DeepMind/UK AISI), so the topic attracts money and attention.
  • Bayesian deep-learning toolkit (deep ensembles, Laplace/last-layer, epistemic-aleatoric split) is mature with strong open libraries, making a first project technically feasible.
  • Distribution-shift-robust uncertainty is a genuine, unsolved need for frontier-model deployment — clear relevance.
  • Dry-lab, compute-scalable down to small models for method development.

Hidden caveats

  • Far from the applicant's genomics/GNN profile and Lio-lab strengths — this is a lane change with no track record, weak for a first-author debut in a crowded CS-safety field.
  • 'Uncertainty on frontier LLMs' realistically needs frontier-model access/compute he lacks; done on small models it may not convince safety reviewers of frontier relevance.
  • Bayesian-DL calibration is a decades-old field — hard to be novel; the safety framing is the only fresh angle and it is heavily contested.
  • Scalable-oversight benchmarks and definitions are still shifting, so results can be invalidated by a redefinition of the problem.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A frontier lab ships a calibrated-confidence / selective-abstention feature in a deployed model (OpenAI/Anthropic/DeepMind), commoditizing the core capability.
  • A benchmark paper establishes a standard for LLM competence-region / OOD detection, converting the open problem into a leaderboard.
  • A strong result shows verification/debate or process supervision subsumes uncertainty-flagging for oversight, shifting the field's approach.
  • A survey declares Bayesian-DL uncertainty methods insufficient for LLM-scale calibration, undercutting the chosen toolkit.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open frontier-scale model with exposed logits/hidden states plus an uncertainty-benchmark suite is released, giving him a realistic testbed.
  • A last-layer-Laplace/ensembles library adds LLM-scale support and tutorials (e.g. laplace-torch for transformers), removing engineering cost.
  • UK AISI / a safety org publishes an open scalable-oversight evaluation harness he can plug into.
  • A distilled small frontier-like model drops that is cheap enough to iterate calibration methods on.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Detecting Deceptive Alignment via Information-Theoretic Mechanistic Interpretability

AI safety / alignment (CS-core) — mechanistic interpretability + information theory
→ DeepMind track★ flagship
A model that behaves well under evaluation but pursues misaligned goals when unmonitored (deceptive alignment) is the central hard case for alignment. This project develops information-theoretic probes — partial-information-decomposition and causal-mediation measures over internal activations — to detect the signature of a model representing an evaluation-vs-deployment distinction or withholding latent knowledge, and validates them on model organisms trained to exhibit deceptive behaviour. Output: a quantitative interpretability toolkit that scores internal representations for deception-relevant structure.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result16months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Detecting deceptive alignment via PID/causal mediation is a marquee problem, but it demands information-theory and interp maturity that outstrip his current formal training.
Verdict: The biggest reach on the list — glamorous and Anthropic-adjacent, but he lacks the CS/info-theory base to be competitive; skip unless safety becomes his whole plan.
Why fundable: Deception detection and eliciting latent knowledge are named top priorities for Open Philanthropy and FLI; interpretability is currently the most fundable AI-safety subfield. The information-theoretic angle maps cleanly onto Mediano's and Barahona's funded methods, and Doshi-Velez's interpretability programme, giving strong supervisor alignment for competitive scholarships.
Fits labs:
Finale Doshi-Velez (Harvard — interpretability, primary fit)Pedro Mediano (Imperial — comp neuro/information theory, PID methods)Mauricio Barahona (Imperial — graph ML on representation structure)Yarin Gal (Oxford — interpretability/uncertainty crossover)
Fits funding:
Open Philanthropy AI PhDFuture of Life Institute PhD fellowshipImperial President's PhD Scholarship (Mediano/Barahona route)Clarendon (Oxford, Gal route)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~12 mo

Strengths

  • Deceptive alignment is the marquee hard problem in safety — a project even credibly attacking it has high salience and funding pull.
  • Information-theoretic angle (partial-information-decomposition, causal-mediation over activations) is mathematically distinctive and less crowded than generic probing.
  • Rides the well-resourced mechanistic-interpretability wave with a clear conceptual hook (evaluation-vs-deployment representation).
  • Dry-lab, can be prototyped on small models with synthetic deception setups.

Hidden caveats

  • Very far from the applicant's genomics/GNN background — steep ramp with no prior credibility in interp or info-theory, risky for a first paper.
  • Deceptive alignment may not be empirically inducible at accessible scale, so the 'detect the signature' claim can be untestable — a fundamental validation gap.
  • PID and causal-mediation measures are notoriously hard to estimate reliably in high dimensions; the metric machinery could dominate and still be contested.
  • The topic attracts elite, well-funded groups (Anthropic, Redwood, Apollo, academic interp labs) who can scoop or out-resource a solo newcomer.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A leading safety lab (Anthropic/Apollo/Redwood) publishes a working deception/eval-awareness detector or probe suite, occupying the space.
  • A paper demonstrates that current models do have detectable eval-vs-deploy representations with simpler linear probes, making the info-theoretic apparatus overkill.
  • A negative result shows deceptive alignment is not inducible/measurable in studyable models, deflating the premise.
  • A benchmark for 'model organism of misalignment' becomes standard and someone tops it with a non-information-theoretic method.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Open 'model organisms of misalignment' / sleeper-agent-style checkpoints are released for study, giving a concrete substrate.
  • A robust open PID / causal-mediation estimator library for neural activations ships, removing the hardest engineering barrier.
  • TransformerLens-style tooling adds causal-mediation and information-decomposition primitives with tutorials.
  • A safety org publishes an open eval-awareness dataset/benchmark he can build detectors against.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Edge of Stability and the Implicit Regularization of Modern Optimizers (Adam, Muon, Shampoo)

ML theory & optimization (CS-core)
Modern nets train at the 'edge of stability' where Hessian sharpness hovers at the numerical instability threshold, yet the theory explaining why adaptive/preconditioned optimizers (Adam, Muon, Shampoo) still land in flat, well-generalizing minima is missing. This project builds a dynamical-systems + SDE theory of preconditioned gradient flow, deriving the effective implicit regularizer each optimizer minimizes and predicting the curvature it selects. Predictions are validated on controlled small-to-mid-scale networks, turning optimizer choice from folklore into derivable geometry.
Appeal3/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort0.6bang-for-buck
Time to result19months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: An SDE/curvature theory of Adam/Muon/Shampoo could make him an optimizer-theory name, but that requires a math maturity his biomedical/sport-science degrees never gave him.
Verdict: Worst fit for him — heavy dynamical-systems math with no formal math degree; avoid unless he wants to spend a year on prerequisites first.
Why fundable: Sits squarely in Open Philanthropy's 'science of deep learning' priority (understanding training dynamics to make scaling predictable and safe) and UKRI/EPSRC foundational-ML remits. A crisp theory-with-experiments narrative is what Clarendon, Gates Cambridge and Knight-Hennessy reward: high-ceiling, single-author-tractable, no wet-lab dependency, immediate NeurIPS/ICML publishability.
Fits labs:
Surya Ganguli (Stanford)Stefanie Jegelka (MIT)Anima Anandkumar (Caltech)Neil Lawrence (Cambridge)Yarin Gal (Oxford)
Fits funding:
Open Philanthropy AIClarendon (Oxford)Gates CambridgeKnight-Hennessy (Stanford)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~24 mo

Strengths

  • Edge-of-stability and implicit regularization of adaptive optimizers is a live, respected open theory problem with steady top-venue interest.
  • Newer optimizers (Muon, Shampoo) are hot and under-theorized — timely hook that distinguishes it from the older Adam-only literature.
  • Theory project: cheap compute, longevity, and no data/wet-lab dependency; results are durable once proven.
  • Well-defined mathematical target (SDE/dynamical-systems characterization of preconditioned flow) gives a clear thesis spine.

Hidden caveats

  • Heavily math-theory (SDEs, dynamical systems, random-matrix) — a large distance from the applicant's empirical GNN/geometric-DL profile; without strong analysis chops this stalls.
  • Dominated by specialist optimization-theory groups; a newcomer with no theory track record faces a brutal novelty/priority bar.
  • High risk of 'true but incremental' results — EoS has many partial theorems already; carving a genuinely new one is hard and slow.
  • Muon/Shampoo are moving targets; the empirical object of study may shift before the theory matures.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A theory paper delivers a general characterization of preconditioned-optimizer implicit bias / EoS dynamics, closing the headline question.
  • The field converges on a single successor optimizer (e.g. Muon becomes default), making Adam/Shampoo comparative theory less interesting.
  • A widely adopted survey unifies EoS results, raising the incremental-contribution bar sharply.
  • Empirical work shows EoS is an artifact of specific schedules/precision, reframing the problem.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open, well-documented Muon/Shampoo reference implementation with reproducible EoS-regime training scripts lands, easing empirical validation of theory.
  • A tutorial/monograph on SDE approximations of SGD/adaptive methods is published, lowering the math onboarding.
  • A shared small-scale benchmark of sharpness/Hessian-spectrum trajectories is released, giving cheap empirical anchors.
  • Collaboration or co-supervision from an optimization theorist becomes available, de-risking the analysis.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

A Statistical-Mechanics Theory of Scaling Laws and Emergent Capabilities

ML theory & optimization (CS-core)
★ flagship
Neural scaling laws (loss as a power law in data, parameters, compute) are empirically robust but theoretically ad hoc, and 'emergent' capability jumps are poorly understood. This project derives scaling exponents from first principles via solvable models (random-feature and mean-field limits, replica/statistical-mechanics methods) linking data spectrum, task structure and optimizer to observed exponents, and formalizes when smooth loss curves hide discontinuous capability transitions. The theory yields compute-optimal allocation rules and early predictors of emergence, tested on controlled synthetic-to-real curricula.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort0.8bang-for-buck
Time to result23months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: A first-principles theory of scaling laws and emergence is field-defining if it lands, but replica/mean-field methods are a physics-grade barrier he'd have to climb from scratch.
Verdict: Exciting and frontier-relevant but math/physics-heavy beyond his training — a long shot; pitch only to a theory group willing to teary him the stat-mech, otherwise pass.
Why fundable: Directly targets Open Philanthropy's flagship interest in predictable scaling and emergent-capability forecasting (safety-relevant), and appeals to Knight-Hennessy / Gates Cambridge as an ambitious physics-x-CS programme. Cheap to run, theory-heavy, high citation ceiling, aligned with the field-wide push to put scaling laws on rigorous footing.
Fits labs:
Surya Ganguli (Stanford)Anima Anandkumar (Caltech)Stefanie Jegelka (MIT)Yarin Gal (Oxford)Neil Lawrence (Cambridge)
Fits funding:
Open Philanthropy AIKnight-Hennessy (Stanford)Gates CambridgeClarendon (Oxford)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~24 mo

Strengths

  • Scaling laws and emergence are among the most-cited, attention-drawing questions in ML; a principled theory has outsized prestige upside.
  • Solvable-model toolkit (random-features, mean-field, replica/stat-mech) is a proven route that has already yielded partial scaling-law results — credible path.
  • Theory project — durable, low-compute, no data dependency, long half-life.
  • Stat-mech-of-learning is a coherent, well-regarded subfield with clear methodological lineage to build on.

Hidden caveats

  • Requires replica/statistical-mechanics and high-dimensional probability fluency far outside the applicant's empirical genomics/GNN background — a very steep, long ramp.
  • Crowded with strong theorists (Zdeborová, Pehlevan, Bahri/Ganguli-lineage); deriving a genuinely new exponent before them is hard for a newcomer.
  • 'Emergence' is increasingly argued to be a metric artifact, so a whole sub-claim of the project may be attacking a mirage — reviewer risk.
  • Solvable models are often too stylized to match real LLM scaling, leaving a persistent theory-practice validation gap.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A paper derives scaling exponents from data-spectrum/task structure and matches empirics, substantially answering the headline question.
  • A consensus paper reframes 'emergent capabilities' as measurement artifacts, removing that pillar of the pitch.
  • A widely accepted mechanistic/data-manifold theory of neural scaling laws is published, saturating novelty.
  • New empirical scaling results (e.g. data-constrained or distillation scaling) diverge from existing power laws, invalidating the models being generalized.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open lecture series / monograph on replica and mean-field methods for deep learning is released, cutting the math onboarding cost.
  • A public dataset of controlled scaling-law measurements (loss vs data/params/compute across tasks) drops, giving theory something to fit.
  • A solvable-model code library (random-feature/mean-field scaling simulators) is released with tutorials.
  • Co-supervision or collaboration with a stat-mech-of-learning theorist becomes available.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

SynthFlow: Retrosynthesis-Aware SE(3) Diffusion for Synthesizable Structure-Based Drug Design

Diffusion / generative models for molecules
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Most structure-based generative models produce high-affinity but unsynthesizable or physically implausible ligands. This project builds a joint SE(3)-equivariant diffusion model over 3D atomic coordinates and discrete bond graphs, conditioned on a protein pocket, with a differentiable retrosynthesis-aware guidance term so that generated molecules score well on docking, drug-likeness, and buildable synthetic routes simultaneously. The core contribution is coupling continuous-coordinate and discrete-graph diffusion under a synthesizability constraint learned from reaction datasets, closing the gap between in-silico affinity and lab-makeable compounds.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result18months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: If he nailed generation-time synthesizability he'd own the gap every SBDD paper hand-waves — but that crown needs cheminformatics + diffusion fluency he doesn't have.
Verdict: Reach bet: exactly Isomorphic's wheelhouse, but no chem/diffusion/RDKit background makes a strong thesis a steep climb — pitch only if he's willing to retool hard.
Why fundable: Directly hits AI-for-drug-discovery priorities of Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds, Open Phil (safe/beneficial AI for science), and translational-medicine framing for Gates Cambridge, Clarendon, and Knight-Hennessy. Synthesizability is a named industry pain point, making impact concrete for funders wanting real-world therapeutic payoff.
Fits labs:
Jure LeskovecMarinka ZitnikAnima AnandkumarPeter MinaryYisong YuePietro Liò
Fits funding:
Knight-Hennessy (Stanford)Clarendon (Oxford)Gates CambridgeBoehringer Ingelheim Fonds
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~11 mo

Strengths

  • Synthesizability is a widely acknowledged failure mode of SBDD generative models, so the framing lands immediately with reviewers.
  • SE(3)-equivariant diffusion is mature with open reference code (TargetDiff, DiffSBDD, Pocket2Mol, DecompDiff) to build on rather than reinvent.
  • Differentiable retrosynthesis-aware guidance is a defensible novelty hook on top of an otherwise crowded generative stack.
  • Fits a hot dual narrative (equivariant generative models + make-it-real chemistry) that funders and pharma care about.

Hidden caveats

  • Extremely crowded: pocket-conditioned diffusion has many strong incumbents, so incremental deltas get scooped fast.
  • Retrosynthesis engines (AiZynthFinder, ASKCOS) are not truly differentiable; the 'differentiable guidance' term likely collapses to a weak SA-score proxy that reviewers will attack.
  • Dry-lab validation gap: docking-based affinity is reward-hackable and synthesizability claims need wet-lab or CASP-style routes he cannot run alone.
  • Diffusion training over coords+bond graphs is GPU-hungry; compute budget in an RA setting may not stretch to competitive scale.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab (Isomorphic/DeepMind, Genentech, Recursion) releases a synthesizability-aware structure-based generator at scale.
  • A benchmark paper standardizes synthesizability + physical-plausibility evaluation and shows the problem largely closed.
  • A next-version TargetDiff/DiffSBDD ships native retrosynthesis or SA-aware guidance as a built-in.
  • A calibrated, differentiable synthesis-cost / route-scoring model is published and open-sourced, erasing the core novelty.

Gets more feasible if…

  • An open pretrained pocket-conditioned SE(3) diffusion backbone is released with checkpoints.
  • A differentiable or RL-friendly retrosynthesis library lands as a plug-in reward.
  • An improved CrossDocked-style dataset with cleaner pockets/ligands is published.
  • Cheap experimental validation via a foundry API (e.g. Adaptyv-style assays) becomes routine for small candidate sets.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Pheno2Mol: Causal Phenotype-Conditioned Diffusion from Single-Cell Perturbation Responses to Candidate Molecules

Diffusion / generative models for molecules
→ DeepMind track★ flagship
Instead of designing molecules to bind one target, this project inverts the problem: given a desired shift in single-cell transcriptomic state (a phenotype), generate small molecules predicted to induce it. A latent diffusion model over molecular graphs is conditioned on a causal embedding of desired cell-state change, trained by aligning perturbation-response atlases (LINCS L1000, sci-Plex, scPerturb) with chemical structure. This yields phenotype-first, target-agnostic generative design for diseases where the right target is unknown.
Appeal5/5
Effort for you5/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result16months @ 20h/wk + AI low AI-leverage
GOAT path: Inverting binding to phenotype-conditioned generation could define phenotypic AI drug discovery — but it stacks two unsolved hard problems (causal cell-state + molecular diffusion) into one PhD.
Verdict: Highest-ceiling, highest-risk — his single-cell skills help one half but molecular generation + causal conditioning is a double gap; only for a lab that will co-carry the chemistry.
Why fundable: Sits squarely in the ML-for-medicine and precision-medicine remits of CCAIM, Cambridge Trust, EMBL-EBI/Sanger studentships, and MSCA, and appeals to Boehringer's interest in mechanism-agnostic phenotypic drug discovery. The bench-to-bedside phenotype framing is compelling for Gates Cambridge and Clarendon impact criteria.
Fits labs:
Mo LotfollahiCaroline UhlerJames ZouMarinka ZitnikJure Leskovec
Fits funding:
CCAIM (Cambridge)Cambridge TrustEMBL-EBI / Wellcome Sanger studentshipBoehringer Ingelheim Fonds
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~14 mo

Strengths

  • Inverts the target-centric paradigm (phenotype-first design) — a genuinely fresh framing with high-impact upside.
  • Aligns tightly with Lio-lab strengths in causal modeling and single-cell genomics, matching his background.
  • Rides two live waves at once: compound Perturb-seq / large perturbation atlases and latent molecular diffusion.
  • Strong thesis story even if partial: causal cell-state embeddings are independently publishable.

Hidden caveats

  • Severe data-alignment bottleneck: paired perturbation-transcriptome-to-molecule datasets are scarce, and LINCS/L1000 is bulk-ish and drug-centric, mismatched to modern scRNA.
  • The 'causal embedding of desired cell-state change' is under-defined; causal inference from observational scRNA is contested and easy for reviewers to dismiss.
  • Almost no dry-lab validation path — even defining a success metric (did the molecule induce the phenotype?) is hard without wet-lab.
  • Very ambitious scope for someone without a first-author paper; high risk of an unfinishable moonshot.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A well-funded lab (Recursion, Insitro, Genentech, Arc Institute) releases a phenotype-to-molecule generative model.
  • A large paired compound-perturbation single-cell dataset drops and a public benchmark forms around it.
  • A perturbation-response foundation model (scGPT-perturb, Arc's State) adds a generative/inverse-design head.
  • A causal single-cell foundation model is published that supplies the desired-shift embedding off the shelf.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Release of large compound Perturb-seq / Tahoe-100M-style paired perturbation data.
  • An open perturbation-response foundation model with an accessible API/checkpoint.
  • A latent molecular graph diffusion library with pretrained checkpoints lands.
  • Optimal-transport / cell-state-distance tooling matures for aligning transcriptomic shifts.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Calibrated Time-Series Foundation Models for Epidemic Forecasting Under Distribution Shift

Time-series forecasting for epidemiology / public health
Pretrained time-series foundation models (TimesFM, Chronos, Moirai) show strong zero-/few-shot transfer but are untested and poorly calibrated for epidemic surveillance, where regime shifts (new variants, interventions, reporting changes) break stationarity assumptions. This project builds a Bayesian fine-tuning and conformal-calibration layer on top of open foundation models so outbreak forecasts carry trustworthy, decision-ready uncertainty intervals that hold under distribution shift. Evaluation uses public benchmarks (CDC FluSight, COVID-19 Forecast Hub, WHO FluNet) with proper scoring rules (WIS, PIT calibration).
Appeal3/5
Effort for you3/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result4.5months @ 20h/wk + AI high AI-leverage
GOAT path: Calibration for epidemic FMs is useful plumbing, not field-defining, and it pulls him fully off geometric DL / genomics.
Verdict: Off-target for the Lio/ETH dream — tractable engineering but abandons his GDL identity; skip unless he genuinely wants public health.
Why fundable: Directly hits Wellcome/Gates public-health and pandemic-preparedness priorities post-COVID; uncertainty quantification for high-stakes decisions is a named focus of Gal's group and van der Schaar's ML-for-medicine mission. No wet-lab cost, clear societal-impact narrative, and open reproducible benchmarks make a strong Clarendon/Gates Cambridge/Knight-Hennessy statement. MSCA and Open Phil also fund robustness/safety of foundation models.
Fits labs:
Neil LawrenceYarin GalMihaela van der SchaarGunnar RätschChris Holmes
Fits funding:
ClarendonGates CambridgeKnight-HennessyCambridge Trust
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~9 mo

Strengths

  • Builds a light calibration/conformal layer ON TOP of released TSFMs (TimesFM, Chronos, Moirai) — no pretraining, low compute, RA-friendly.
  • Calibration under regime shift is a real, unaddressed gap for epidemic surveillance with clear public-health relevance.
  • Evaluation is clean and reproducible using public epi datasets and backtesting, so a paper is tractable solo.
  • Bayesian fine-tuning + conformal is a concrete, well-scoped deliverable rather than an open-ended moonshot.

Hidden caveats

  • Little to no GNN/geometric content — drifts away from his core skill set and Lio-lab identity.
  • Distribution shift breaks the exchangeability assumption that conformal relies on; the promised regime-shift robustness is exactly the hardest, most contested part.
  • Epidemic forecasting is crowded (CDC FluSight, COVID forecast hubs, many groups) — easy to look incremental.
  • Perceived novelty risk: 'fine-tune a foundation model and wrap it in conformal' can read as engineering, not research.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A benchmark paper comprehensively evaluates TSFMs on epidemic/health forecasting with calibration metrics.
  • TSFM providers ship native uncertainty/conformal calibration in a Chronos/TimesFM/Moirai update.
  • A dedicated epidemic or public-health time-series foundation model is released.
  • A general conformal-under-covariate-shift method is published that solves the non-exchangeable case.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Easier fine-tuning APIs / adapters for open TSFM checkpoints are released.
  • Conformal libraries (MAPIE, TorchCP) add time-series and distribution-shift support.
  • A standardized public epidemic benchmark with fixed splits is published.
  • Tutorials on Bayesian fine-tuning of TSFMs appear.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Spatiotemporal Graph Neural Networks Coupling Human Mobility and Genomic Surveillance for Sub-National Outbreak Prediction

Time-series forecasting for epidemiology / public health
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Most epidemic forecasts treat regions independently, ignoring that spread is driven by a dynamic mobility network and seeded by identifiable pathogen lineages. This project develops spatiotemporal GNNs where nodes are administrative regions, edges are time-varying mobility flows (Meta/Google movement, commuting data), and node features fuse case counts with lineage-frequency time series from open genomic databases (Nextstrain, Pango lineages). The model yields spatially-resolved lead-time forecasts and attributes predicted surges to specific mobility corridors and variants.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result8months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: Fusing mobility graphs with lineage-frequency dynamics is a genuinely underexplored GNN niche where his genomics + GNN combo is rare — but it's epidemiology-bound, not a decade-defining subfield.
Verdict: On-method (his GNNs) but off-domain — needs PyG fluency and heavy data engineering; pitch to computational-epi / spatiotemporal-GNN groups, not a route to ETH-with-Lio.
Why fundable: Genomic-plus-mobility integration matches Sanger/EMBL-EBI and pandemic-genomics funding themes and gives a concrete translational hook for agencies wanting corridor-level early warning. Graph ML on networks is a signature strength of the named labs, making fit letters credible; the interpretability angle (which edges/variants matter) meets funder demand for actionable, non-black-box public-health tools.
Fits labs:
Mauricio BarahonaPietro LiòJure LeskovecMarinka ZitnikFlorian Markowetz
Fits funding:
Imperial President'sCambridge TrustClarendonEMBL-EBI
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~13 mo

Strengths

  • Strong GNN fit — spatiotemporal graphs over regions/mobility directly match his PyG skills and Lio-lab identity.
  • Genuine multimodal novelty: fusing time-varying mobility networks with pathogen lineage/genomic features.
  • Spatiotemporal GNN tooling is mature (PyG-Temporal), making implementation tractable.
  • Clear policy relevance and impact narrative for sub-national outbreak prediction.

Hidden caveats

  • Mobility data access is fragile: Google COVID mobility was discontinued and Meta Data-for-Good feeds are increasingly restricted, threatening both training and reproducibility.
  • Linked mobility + genomic surveillance + case datasets are rare, messy, and privacy-laden; assembling them may dominate the project.
  • Sub-national case data quality varies wildly (reporting artifacts), so label noise can swamp modeling gains.
  • Post-COVID epi-GNN literature is crowded; and true 'prediction' needs prospective evaluation that is hard to demonstrate.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A paper ships mobility + genomic spatiotemporal outbreak forecasting at scale.
  • Major mobility data providers permanently close access, undermining feasibility and reproducibility.
  • A foundation model for epidemic spread on mobility networks is released.
  • A standardized sub-national outbreak-forecasting benchmark is published and saturated.

Gets more feasible if…

  • A new open human-mobility dataset (Meta Data-for-Good successor, GLEAM-style) is released.
  • Integrated genomic surveillance platforms (Nextstrain/Pathoplexus/GISAID) expose better APIs.
  • PyG-Temporal / spatiotemporal GNN tutorials and pretrained models land.
  • A public benchmark linking mobility, lineage, and case counts is released.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Conformal and Selective Prediction for Genomic Foundation Models: Distribution-Free Uncertainty Under Covariate Shift

Uncertainty quantification for scientific ML
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Genomic and single-cell foundation models (scGPT, Geneformer, Enformer-style variant-effect predictors) increasingly guide clinical and experimental decisions, yet they output point predictions with no reliable error bars and degrade silently under batch, tissue, and ancestry shift. This project builds distribution-free conformal prediction and selective-prediction layers on top of frozen foundation-model embeddings, with shift-robust (weighted/Mondrian) coverage guarantees and an abstention mechanism that flags cells or variants where the model should defer to a human or a wet-lab assay. The deliverable is a calibrated, deployable uncertainty wrapper plus theory on when coverage holds under realistic genomic distribution shift.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you2/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort2.0bang-for-buck
Time to result5months @ 20h/wk + AI high AI-leverage
GOAT path: Owning trustworthy UQ for genomic foundation models under ancestry/batch shift is a clean, clinically-load-bearing lane he could plausibly lead — the ceiling is high-utility, not paradigm-shifting.
Verdict: Best tractable pick and fastest path to a first-author paper — works on frozen embeddings, uses his single-cell/genomics knowledge, no PyG or wet lab; pitch broadly as his safe workhorse.
Why fundable: Hits trustworthy-AI-for-health priorities directly: CCAIM is explicitly about deployable, reliable clinical ML; Wellcome and EMBL-EBI prioritize responsible genomics tooling; Open Phil AI treats calibration and abstention as safety primitives. Distribution-free guarantees make it credible to clinicians, the translational angle Gates Cambridge and Clarendon reward.
Fits labs:
Yarin GalChris HolmesMihaela van der SchaarChristopher YauMo LotfollahiGunnar Ratsch
Fits funding:
ClarendonGates CambridgeCCAIMCambridge Trust
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~11 mo

Strengths

  • Very timely: scGPT, Geneformer, and Enformer-style models are widely deployed with point predictions and no error bars — a clear, felt gap.
  • Conformal / selective prediction is compute-light and perfectly suited to solo dry-lab work.
  • Applying distribution-free UQ specifically to genomic FMs under batch/tissue/ancestry shift is a defensible, differentiated angle.
  • Dual methodological + applied contribution raises publishability and clinical relevance.

Hidden caveats

  • Covariate shift violates conformal exchangeability, and batch/tissue/ancestry shift is precisely where guarantees are weakest — the crux is hard theory, not application.
  • Needs scaled access to genomic FM embeddings/outputs, some of which are gated or expensive to run.
  • The conformal-ML community is crowded; without deep genomics anchoring it risks looking like a generic reweighting paper.
  • Ancestry-shift claims touch fairness/clinical validity and need careful data and hedged conclusions plus ground-truth shift benchmarks that may not exist.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A genomic FM provider ships built-in calibrated uncertainty in a scGPT/Geneformer/Enformer update.
  • A benchmark paper on UQ for genomic foundation models under shift is released.
  • Weighted/robust conformal-under-covariate-shift methods mature into a general solution.
  • A major single-cell FM ships native selective prediction / abstention.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Open genomic FM weights with embedding APIs become easier to query at scale.
  • Conformal libraries (TorchCP, MAPIE, Fortuna) add shift-robust / Mondrian methods.
  • Public single-cell benchmarks with labeled batch/tissue/ancestry shift are released.
  • Tutorials on weighted conformal for high-dimensional embeddings appear.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Calibrated Amortized Bayesian Inference for Mechanistic Scientific Simulators: Trustworthy Simulation-Based Inference for Genetics and Neuroscience

Uncertainty quantification for scientific ML
◆ cross-lab workhorse★ flagship
Simulation-based inference (SBI) with neural posterior/likelihood estimators now lets scientists fit intractable mechanistic models (coalescent population-genetic simulators, single-neuron and circuit models), but the learned posteriors are frequently miscalibrated and overconfident, silently corrupting scientific conclusions. This project develops UQ methods that make amortized Bayesian inference honest: post-hoc posterior recalibration, coverage tests (simulation-based calibration, expected-coverage diagnostics) turned into training objectives, and robustness under model misspecification when real data leave the simulator's support. Deliverables are calibrated SBI estimators plus diagnostics that tell a scientist when to distrust inferred parameters.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result10months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: Making amortized SBI honest is a deep, foundational frontier and popgen simulators touch his TE/HPC background — this is the most 'ELLIS-methods' flavored idea, if the math holds.
Verdict: Intellectually the most PhD-shaped and genomics-connected via popgen, but Pyro + normalizing flows + calibration theory is math-heavy for someone with no formal math degree; strong if he commits to the theory, pitch to SBI/popgen ML groups.
Why fundable: Aligns with probabilistic-ML and AI-for-science funder agendas: MSCA and Boehringer Ingelheim Fonds fund methodological ML embedded in real biology; ETH/KI salaried positions reward rigorous statistical genetics and comp-neuro tooling; Open Phil AI values calibration under misspecification as a core reliability problem. Cross-domain applicability (genetics + neuroscience) broadens fundable fit.
Fits labs:
Neil LawrenceGuillaume HennequinMate LengyelJens LagergrenBarbara BraviJerome Kelleher
Fits funding:
ClarendonGates CambridgeMSCA Doctoral NetworksBoehringer Ingelheim Fonds
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~17 mo

Strengths

  • Mature open tooling (sbi/mackelab, BayesFlow, sbibm) lowers the barrier for a solo dry-lab researcher.
  • Miscalibration of amortized neural posteriors is a recognized, real problem (SBC, coverage, TARP) with clear scientific stakes.
  • Methods-heavy and tractable alone; cross-domain (pop-gen + neuro) gives breadth, collaborators, and fits Lio's Bayesian interests.
  • Longer shelf life than foundation-model ideas — theory/diagnostics age more slowly.

Hidden caveats

  • Drifts from his GNN/geometric core; less obvious skill continuity.
  • SBI calibration is an active ML-stats subfield; a well-resourced group could scoop a generic calibration diagnostic.
  • Requires domain simulators plus a non-trivial simulation compute budget to get competitive results.
  • Novelty risk: 'yet another coverage diagnostic' unless the method is genuinely new, and validation leans on synthetic ground truth so real-data impact is harder to show.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A definitive calibration method for neural posteriors is published and broadly adopted.
  • sbi / BayesFlow ship robust calibration (e.g. coverage-corrected posteriors) by default.
  • A benchmark paper systematically resolves SBI calibration across standard simulators.
  • A foundation-model-style amortized inference engine for pop-gen/neuro simulators is released.

Gets more feasible if…

  • sbi / BayesFlow releases add built-in calibration and coverage tooling.
  • Expanded simulator benchmarks (sbibm successors) are published.
  • Pretrained amortized posteriors for population-genetic or neuroscience simulators are shared.
  • Tutorials on conformal/coverage-calibrated SBI appear.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Calibrated genome language models for non-coding variant interpretation in undiagnosed rare disease

Rare-disease genetics & non-coding variant interpretation
◆ cross-lab workhorse
Sequence-to-function foundation models (Enformer, Nucleotide Transformer, Evo, AlphaGenome) predict regulatory effects of non-coding variants but give point predictions with no calibrated uncertainty, so clinicians cannot trust them for diagnosis. This project builds an uncertainty-aware wrapper (deep ensembles / Bayesian last-layer / conformal prediction) around genome LMs, benchmarks predicted effect sizes against experimental MPRA and saturation-mutagenesis ground truth, and outputs calibrated pathogenicity scores that flag which undiagnosed rare-disease genomes to reinterpret. The deliverable is a ranked reinterpretation list for GEL 100,000 Genomes / GUES negative cases with quantified confidence.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you3/5
10-yr impact3/5
Appeal / effort1.33bang-for-buck
Time to result7.5months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: If he owned calibrated uncertainty as the trust layer for genome foundation models he'd be the go-to for deployable clinical variant calls, but calibration is a supporting act and hard to become THE name in.
Verdict: Pursue as his safe workhorse — tractable without deep PyG, pitch to clinical-genomics and trustworthy-ML PIs; weaker fit to his GNN identity.
Why fundable: Directly maps to Genomics England and NHS Genomic Medicine Service priorities on improving diagnostic yield in the ~50% of rare-disease patients who remain undiagnosed after coding-variant analysis; Wellcome and EMBL-EBI explicitly fund open-source variant-interpretation tooling; uncertainty calibration is a named Open Phil / trustworthy-ML priority. Clean patient-benefit + open-data + AI-safety framing that reviewers reward.
Fits labs:
Nicola Whiffin (Oxford) - primary, non-coding/regulatory rare-disease variantsChris Holmes (Oxford) - Bayesian statistical genomics / calibrationYarin Gal (Oxford) - Bayesian deep learning / uncertaintyChristopher Yau (Oxford, BDI) - ML omicsMo Lotfollahi (Sanger/Cambridge) - deep learning on genomics readouts
Fits funding:
Clarendon (Oxford)EMBL-EBI / Wellcome Sanger PhD programmesBoehringer Ingelheim FondsCambridge Trust / Harding (if hosted at Cambridge via Lotfollahi)
Watch fingerprint stays fresh ~14 mo

Strengths

  • Genuine unmet clinical need: sequence-to-function models (Enformer, Nucleotide Transformer, Evo2, AlphaGenome) are being deployed for non-coding variant scoring but ship point predictions with no calibrated uncertainty, which is the exact blocker for clinical adoption in diagnostics.
  • Methodologically tractable for a dry-lab newcomer: uncertainty wrappers (deep ensembles, Bayesian last-layer, conformal prediction) can be bolted onto frozen pretrained backbones, so no need to pretrain a genome LM from scratch (avoids the compute wall).
  • Strong lab fit and scoop-resistance via a differentiated angle: calibration/UQ is under-explored relative to raw model-building, and Lio's group has geometric/uncertainty expertise; conformal prediction has clean theory that suits a first paper.
  • Clear evaluation story with existing labeled data (ClinVar, gnomAD, MPRA/saturation-mutagenesis, eQTL) enabling reliability diagrams, coverage/ECE metrics, and clinically framed decision-curve analysis.

Hidden caveats

  • Calibration is only meaningful against trustworthy ground-truth labels; non-coding pathogenicity labels are sparse, biased toward well-studied loci, and ClinVar is noisy — the validation gap can quietly invalidate the whole calibration claim.
  • Deep ensembles of large genome LMs are expensive at inference (N forward passes over long context); the 'no compute barrier' assumption breaks if he needs many ensemble members or fine-tuning of billion-parameter backbones.
  • Reviewers may see UQ-wrapper-on-frozen-model as incremental/engineering rather than novel; needs a genuinely new contribution (e.g. conformal guarantees under covariate shift across genomic contexts, or calibration-aware training) to clear a top-venue novelty bar.
  • Clinical trust is a sociotechnical claim, not just an ECE number — without a clinician/wet-lab collaborator or prospective case validation, the 'clinicians can trust it' framing is unfalsifiable and weakens impact.

Gets obsolete if…

  • A major lab ships a genome foundation model with built-in calibrated uncertainty at scale (e.g. an AlphaGenome / Evo successor releasing native conformal or Bayesian outputs), collapsing the core gap.
  • A benchmark/leaderboard paper standardizes calibrated non-coding variant-effect prediction (uncertainty metrics + shared splits), turning the open problem into a solved, commoditized task.
  • A large well-labeled non-coding pathogenicity benchmark (big MPRA/saturation-mutagenesis or clinical cohort release) makes plain fine-tuned classifiers accurate enough that calibration stops being the bottleneck.
  • A methods paper demonstrates conformal prediction transfers cleanly to genomic sequence models with distribution-shift guarantees, pre-empting the novel-method angle.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Open release of pretrained AlphaGenome / Evo2 / Nucleotide Transformer weights with inference APIs and embedding access, removing the pretraining compute barrier entirely.
  • A maintained conformal-prediction library integrates with genomic/HF sequence models (or a tutorial lands pairing MAPIE/TorchCP with DNA LMs), lowering implementation cost.
  • Release of a curated, uncertainty-ready non-coding variant benchmark with train/cal/test splits and clinical labels (ClinGen/gnomAD-derived) usable off the shelf.
  • A dry-lab-friendly UQ tutorial or PyG/Lightning recipe for calibrating large sequence models drops, matching his PyTorch Geometric learning curve.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Variant-to-gene graph neural networks linking non-coding Mendelian variants to effector genes via 3D genome and enhancer maps

Rare-disease genetics & non-coding variant interpretation
◆ cross-lab workhorse→ DeepMind track★ flagship
A non-coding variant is only interpretable once you know which gene it dysregulates, but current tools rely on nearest-gene heuristics that are wrong for enhancers acting over long ranges. This project builds a heterogeneous graph neural network over enhancer-gene links (ABC model, Hi-C/Micro-C, eQTL, ENCODE cCREs) that propagates a predicted regulatory perturbation to a ranked list of candidate effector genes, then scores whether that gene explains the patient's phenotype (HPO terms). It transfers regulatory-grammar learned from common-variant/eQTL data to the rare-variant Mendelian setting where data is sparse.
Appeal4/5
Effort for you4/5
10-yr impact4/5
Appeal / effort1.0bang-for-buck
Time to result6.5months @ 20h/wk + AI medium AI-leverage
GOAT path: This is his sheaf/GNN identity aimed at the biggest open problem in regulatory genomics; mastering heterogeneous message passing over 3D-genome graphs could make him a leading voice — if he closes the PyG gap fast.
Verdict: Pursue as his flagship — strongest fit to his GNN/sheaf background and DeepMind-shaped, but effort is high given the PyG gap and heavy multi-omic graph construction; pitch to Lio-style GDL and regulatory-genomics PIs.
Why fundable: Solves the interpretability bottleneck that blocks non-coding variants from entering clinical reporting - a stated goal of Genomics England, CCAIM, and Wellcome. Graph ML + precision-medicine framing sits squarely in CCAIM and Cambridge Centre priorities; the phenotype-linkage angle appeals to health-AI funders wanting mechanistic, auditable models rather than black boxes.
Fits labs:
Pietro Liò (Cambridge) - GNN / multi-omics / precision medicineGos Micklem (Cambridge) - integrative genomicsAnshul Kundaje (Stanford) - regulatory genomics DL / epigenomicsOlga Troyanskaya (Princeton) - DL genomics genotype-phenotypeNicola Whiffin (Oxford) - regulatory rare-disease variantsMauricio Barahona (Imperial) - graph ML / networks

Strengths

  • Attacks a real and well-recognized failure mode: nearest-gene assignment for non-coding/enhancer variants is demonstrably wrong for long-range regulation, and variant-to-gene (V2G) linking is a named open problem in rare-disease genetics and GWAS interpretation.
  • Natural heterogeneous-graph problem (enhancers, genes, cCREs, contacts, eQTLs as typed nodes/edges) that maps directly onto PyTorch Geometric HeteroData and message passing — ideal skill-building vehicle for a PyG learner in Lio's GNN lab.
  • Rich, largely public multi-omic substrate already exists (ABC model, ENCODE cCREs, Hi-C/Micro-C, GTEx eQTL, Open Targets Genetics V2G), so the data-assembly path is clear without wet-lab generation.
  • Strong lab-fit for sheaf/geometric methods: cell-type-specific 3D contact graphs invite a principled heterogeneous or sheaf-GNN treatment, giving a differentiated methodological angle beyond a vanilla GCN.

Hidden caveats

  • Ground-truth enhancer-gene links are scarce and biased — CRISPRi/perturbation-validated pairs (e.g. from a handful of cell lines) are the only gold standard, so evaluation is small, cell-type-limited, and easy to overfit.
  • Heavy crowding and incumbent competition: Open Targets Genetics L2G, ABC, ENCODE-rE2G, and cS2G already do V2G and are actively maintained; beating well-tuned feature-based baselines with a GNN is not guaranteed and is a classic 'GNN underperforms tabular' trap.
  • Cell-type specificity is the crux and the hazard: contact maps and enhancer activity are context-dependent, so a model trained on available cell types may not transfer to the tissues relevant to a given rare disease — a hidden validation gap.
  • Data heterogeneity and leakage risk: genomic-distance features dominate and can leak through graph structure, making it easy to report inflated scores that collapse under distance-matched or held-out-locus splits.

Gets obsolete if…

  • ENCODE-rE2G, Open Targets L2G, or a successor ships a GNN/3D-genome-aware V2G model that becomes the de facto standard, occupying the exact niche.
  • A large validated enhancer-gene benchmark (scaled CRISPRi/Perturb-seq across many cell types, e.g. an IGVF release) is published with a leaderboard, commoditizing the task.
  • A genome foundation model (AlphaGenome-class) demonstrates accurate variant-to-effector-gene prediction end-to-end from sequence, making explicit graph construction unnecessary.
  • A benchmark paper shows simple distance/ABC baselines match or beat GNNs on distance-matched V2G splits, undercutting the method's novelty premise.

Gets more feasible if…

  • Release of a large, harmonized CRISPRi/Perturb-seq enhancer-gene ground-truth dataset (IGVF / ENCODE) with cell-type labels usable as supervised targets.
  • A PyG tutorial or reference implementation for heterogeneous biological interaction graphs (variant-gene-regulatory) drops, cutting graph-assembly and HeteroData boilerplate.
  • Public, ready-to-use processed enhancer-gene graphs or feature tables (ABC + cCRE + eQTL joined) are released, removing the multi-omic data-engineering barrier.
  • Open pretrained embeddings for regulatory elements or 3D-genome contact predictors (e.g. from AlphaGenome/Enformer) become available as node features, boosting a compute-light GNN.
The autonomous run re-checks these against the web and flags on the Advisor radar when one fires.

Application checklist

Tick items as you go — saved in your browser. Reference requests and English tests have the longest lead time; start those first.

Progress0 / 130
